And it's a balanced judgment that you have to make at each budget every year. Okay. Good. It is a very, very important question and bullying in our schools is a hugely important problem. It's a part of the discipline problem which I was talking about earlier. And we have to tackle this if our children are to get the decent chance in life that they need. I'm very lucky. I come from an ordinary family but I went to a school a good state school, and I've benefited enormously from that. And I want every child in this country to have the same kind of chance that I had. So, to come directly to your question, if you give... I'm not sure that you need to ring-fence the money. If you give the head teachers the power to enforce discipline, what happens at the moment is if a head wants to exclude someone from school, someone who's been guilty of bullying, for example, That headteacher can be second-guessed by a panel who take the appeal. They may know little about the school. They will know nothing about the child. And if they override the headteacher... The Tory idea, that, wasn't it? They undermine the headteachers. The Tory idea. And we learn from our mistakes. All right, good. Right, well it's a very serious problem and one of the worries which older people have is that they will have to sell their home in order to pay for the costs of long-term care. OK, well, I misunderstood the question, and you're quite right. There is... I think there are questions to be asked. This is about the dividing line between nursing care and personal care, because nursing care is free and personal care isn't. And I think there are real questions to be asked about the dividing line between the two, and we would look again at the dividing line between the two. There are lots of people who go for private healthcare at the moment who can't afford it. There was an item on the news this evening about someone who had to sell their home in order to get the private care they needed because they had to wait so long for their treatment under the NHS. These are all people who've paid their taxes. They've paid towards the cost of their NHS treatment. We'll be giving half the cost of an NHS operation if someone wants to go private. And these are all people who've paid their taxes. They've paid for the cost of their NHS operation. They're lessening the NHS waiting lists by going into the private sector. I think it's only fair that they should get a proportion of what it would have cost the NHS after all. It's a good deal for the NHS, because instead of having to pay for the whole of the cost of the operation, they're only paying a contribution, a half of it, and that person goes off the waiting list. Would you do the same for schools? People wanting to go private? No. What we would do for schools... Same principle, isn't it? It's not quite, because we're very practical about this, we're very pragmatic. There's a difference between the two. The problem in health is overwhelmingly one of capacity, so we want to encourage more capacity. The problem in education is a bit different. You don't have children on waiting lists in general. I know there are some in London at the moment, and that's a disgrace. But in general, children are not on waiting lists to go to school. It's a question of choice and quality and standards, and so we would encourage... What we say is, if private sector can provide education at the same cost as the state, then children and parents should be able to choose that private school. But only if it's the same cost as the state.
deb_2005_04_28.BLAIR: because we had the Attorney General there at Cabinet, and he was able to tell people his opinion, explain his reasoning, answer any questions that they had. And in the end, remember, for the past few days, it's been said that the Attorney General advised that it was illegal to go to war. He didn't. He advised it was lawful. Now, I totally understand why there will be people in this audience tonight who very, very strongly disagree with my decision to take this country to war in Iran. OK. All I can say to you is this. It's not a matter of the Attorney-General's advice, because it's been shown that he advised it was lawful. Neither is the matter of misusing the intelligence. There have been four inquiries into that. It is, however, a question of a difficult decision I had to take. Was it better to leave Saddam in power or put him in prison? I think it was better to put him in prison. In respect of Parliament, the Attorney General's advice is never published to Parliament. It's been published, I think, three times over the last hundred years. And yet it's now. Exactly. Published to everybody. Exactly, because it's been leaked. And therefore, you might as well have the full opinion. In respect of Cabinet, the reason why we didn't give people the advice is we actually had the Attorney General there in Cabinet. And there isn't any doubt about it. And was all this ground covered? The Attorney General went through his reasoning and explained to Cabinet why, in his view, there was a reasonable case that could be made that the action was lawful. Now, that is what has happened. And look, as I say, in the end, you can frame this any way you want. But the question was, when we were faced with the situation, I think it's important people understand this. Back in March 2003, we were trying to get a second United Nations resolution. We couldn't get one. because France said they would not accept any resolution with an ultimatum to Saddam. In the end, therefore, I had to decide. We had 250,000 UK and US troops down there. It was a tough decision, it was a difficult one. I don't expect people, everyone, to agree with it. I don't disrespect people who take a completely different point of view. But I'm afraid this was an occasion, there was no fence to sit on, I had to take the decision and I took it. But because they actually had the Attorney General there in person. And therefore, in a sense, what was important was that he was able to take them through the reasoning in person and say to them... No, because he was there able to explain it orally himself. And look, in the end, the only question is, did the Attorney General advise that the war was lawful? He did. But in the end, the decision about this It isn't really to do with the Attorney General. It was a political decision that had to be taken. Look, you know, I've been over this ground I don't know how many times in the last couple of years, and I totally understand why some people feel that this was the wrong decision to take. But I happen to believe, for the reasons that I gave, it was the right decision. All right. Let's hear from you, sir. No, no member of the Cabinet asked for it because the Attorney General was there and able to take them through it personally. You, Madam? He didn't... With respect, he didn't change his mind. The 7th of March advice was, which has now been published, was that it was lawful. And when he came to Cabinet, he explained, as he says in his advice, of course it's better to have a second resolution, but if you can't get one, then the action is still lawful for the reasons that he gave. All right, the woman here. Oh, first of all... It wasn't an academic point for the hundreds of thousands of Iraqis who lost their lives under Saddam Hussein. It was important for them. Now, you may say that I took the action simply for America. I didn't. I took it because I believed it was in the interest of this country. If we had backed away and left Saddam in power, stronger because we had backed away, I think that would have been bad for the security of our region and our world and therefore of this country. As I say, you can conduct this debate in whichever way you want, but I took that decision in good faith. Sometimes, when you are sitting in my seat as Prime Minister, you have to take the decision. That is the decision I took. Well that's your view but it's interesting isn't it that today we actually have a democratic government being formed in Iraq. Isn't that something we should actually be proud that we helped bring about? Thank you. You're going to have to make your decision about that. Before you make that decision, though, the interesting thing about the intelligence, and there have been four inquiries into this, all of which have found the same thing, that I acted in good faith in relation to the intelligence. But before you make that decision, you can actually go onto the website, I think it's with the Butler Report, and you can read the judgments of the Joint Intelligence Committee about Saddam Hussein and weapons of mass destruction. Now, I just ask you, before you make that allegation again, go and read that and then tell me there was no evidence at all before me. Do you know how many inquiries we've had? You decide the wording every time. The Hutton inquiry was a six-month inquiry. The Foreign Affairs Committee decided their own terms of reference, so did the Intelligence Committee. We have had four inquiries, we could have another four. And frankly, after that, we could have another four. What I do accept, frankly, in retrospect, is that it would have been better just to have published the Joint Intelligence Committee reports. I think that would have been a better way of doing it, because then you wouldn't have had this argument. And that's why I said to this young man here that... No, I don't mean that. But you accept it wasn't that? What I mean is that Butler said, and I think this is right and I've accepted this, because I'm not saying there haven't been mistakes made and things for which I have to take responsibility. but I think that in the end what would have been better in retrospect is just to have published the raw intelligence and then people could have seen exactly what the Joint Intelligence Committee actually said to me and that's why I say to you and to anybody here or anybody listening to the program you can go and read the intelligence I got and then make up your own mind as to whether I'm a liar or not. You haven't done it. Well, why don't we, instead of dealing with perception, we deal with the reality. And if you doubt the position that I'm saying on the intelligence, as I say, the great thing is you can go and read it for yourself and make up your own mind about that. But I like to think that also there are certain changes we've made in this country that we can be proud of as a government. I don't disagree with that. I think you have made the point. I think that is a point that is worth responding head-on to. In the end, yes, I did have to struggle very hard to get this through. But the reason I did it was because I thought it was the right thing to do. I didn't take this on myself for reasons just because I thought, let's give myself a really hard time for a couple of years. No, that's a reasonable enough point. All I say to you on the other stuff is, if you go back and have a look at what I read, then, you know, drop me a line and tell me what you think about it afterwards. The woman there in grey. Right. APPLAUSE First of all, I don't really know how Brian Sedgmore would know, since I don't think he was ever present at any discussion I ever had with George Bush. In fact, I don't even remember ever discussing it with Brian Sedgmore. But secondly, you've got to make up your mind about it. Look, I've taken my decision. And I would just like to point out to you that in the end, if I'd taken a different decision, that too would have had difficult consequences. If I decided the course that you want me to take, Saddam Hussein would still be in charge of Iraq today. People would have been dying in Iraq. Without television cameras there, it's true, but they were. Don't speak to me about it, speak to Iraqis about it. That is why the decision was difficult. It was difficult to remove him. It was difficult to leave him there. Well, I don't plan any, but... Because I just crept up on you, didn't I? What I would say to you is, this country still remains one of the lowest-taxed countries in the whole of Europe. Actually, taxes as a proportion of national income is lower than most of the years Margaret Thatcher is in. And, yes, it's true, we raised national insurance in the last Parliament. That is absolutely true, to pay for the National Health Service. I don't regret that. I think it's good that we are now making improvements in our National Health Service as a result of that investment. We didn't have to for the commitments that we had, but it's true. We got the report that said, if you want to get your health service spending up to the European Union average, you're going to have to put more money in. Now, even with that additional money, I think it's worth just pointing out that the National Health Service, as a result of that, is getting the largest increase investment it's had since it was created. And I think the National Health Service is improving as a result. And that is surely better than going down the path of forcing people to take out private medical insurance. Right. Do you want to expand on that, or is that it? I take that as not a compliment, so... OK, the man in the grey shirt there, at the very back row. Well, I don't believe it is wasted. Look, I visit schools and hospitals the whole time. I go in there, I see investment in schools, I see it in new school buildings, in computers, in additional teachers. I go into the health services. There's barely a health service site you visit without new buildings being put up, new wards, scanners, extra nurses. I don't I don't misunderstand this at all. There are still big problems that we need to deal with. But I think if you look at our National Health Service today, we can be proud of it again. It's improvements happening in the National Health Service. If you go into the schools, you can see the extra investment in there. That's on the basis of a strong economy, and that's why I want to keep that investment going in. And I don't accept the Conservative charge it's all wasted or it's all going on bureaucrats. Actually, it's going to give our kids a better education and people a better deal on health. OK. Well, I think there is real improvement in the health service. I'm really sorry if you've been waiting an unacceptable period of time. I don't know whether there are other people here who have used the health service recently, but I actually think when you go into our healthcare system, I'm not saying everyone, and I'm sorry that you've had a bad experience, but I think, on the whole, people get a very good service out of our National Health Service today. You, sir. Well, if you're referring to MRSA, it is a real problem and we're tackling it. We're putting in the matrons and the cleaning that's necessary. But let me just say this to you. I don't think that should, as it were, stain the whole of the health service. I honestly believe that our National Health Service, and I probably visit more hospitals and talk to more people in the health service than any Prime Minister before me, I think that of course there are real issues and real difficulties. All healthcare systems have that. But I think when you go into, for example, an accident and emergency department nowadays, you can see a change and an improvement. I think for many people who used to wait, for example, you take cardiac care. people used to wait months and months, years even, to get the treatment that they need. They don't wait that long anymore. If you take cataracts, for example, as a result of the changes we've brought in, big change has happened. In cancer, we now save, literally, I think it's something like 10,000 deaths a year as a result of changes that have been brought in. Now, I'm not saying it's all perfect, don't misunderstand me, but I am saying it's a darn sight better than what we inherited from the Conservatives in 1997. Well, I'm absolutely astonished at that. Look, come on, let's be sensible about this. I don't know about this individual case, obviously, but I would be absolutely astonished if you're saying to your GP, I don't need to see you for four days, and he's insisting he sees you in two. The reason... Is this news to you? I have to say... Hold on, hold on. Is this news to you? That is news to me, that doctors are insisting that you have to come within the 48 hours. The whole purpose of this was that people used to be able, used not to be able rather, to get an appointment with the GP within the 48 hours. Now obviously it shouldn't work in that way because that would be absurd. Anybody else have this experience? Sorry, but there's quite a lot of people who have said this. I understand that, but surely they're not saying to you that you have to have it quicker than you wanted. I will look into that. The purpose of the target, just so you know, is that it used to be the case that people would phone the GP and not be able to get an appointment for a week or two weeks when they wanted it quickly. I agree, the target obviously should not work in that way. That is a perfectly sensible point. I will have a look at it. It obviously should not work like that. The purpose of these targets just so that I explain the situation, is to try and make sure that you get minimum quality and standards and are seen quickly. Now, obviously, it should not work in the opposite way. Well, the plans that we have got are fully costed and taken account of. They were last time round. They were. What happened was, as I just explained, we got the report on healthcare spending that said if you want to reach the European average, you are going to have to put more money in. Should we or shouldn't we now suppose there will be a further increase? I can't sit and write the budget. Yes, of course I know what's in his mind. And what's in his mind is to carry on running the strongest economy in the Western world, and thank goodness he's been doing it. Or to become Prime Minister. Well, that may be there too. But before we get to that stage, David, we've got to win this election, and there's a long way to go. No, I haven't said that either. I've simply said that before anybody has the right to say they're going to be Prime Minister, we've got to get through the next week. I think before we get to that, we better win this election first. I've answered this question 1,000 times and I've always said that Gordon would make an excellent Prime Minister but I'm not getting into selecting the next Prime Minister when I can't even be sure I'm going to be in my own... No, but we're just talking about the leader of the Labour Party. and whether you win the election or not. At some point, you have to stand down in any event, which is why I answered the question. Will you vote for your good friend Gordon Brown? And let us wait and see what happens at a later stage. I've always said, however, he would make an extremely good Prime Minister of this country. But you haven't decided yet. It's not that I haven't decided, it's that I'm not getting in. I'm not getting into those questions until we've decided this election and actually that's for the public to decide. What we've done now is introduce a completely different system of student finance. It's not top-up fees as with the old system where you paid the fees as you went through university. It's important people understand what this proposal is. From now on, people will pay no fees as they go through university. They will then, and there will be maintenance grants, reintroduce £3,000 a year for the poorest students. Then when students graduate, they will pay back into the pot a fair repayment system based on their ability to pay. So, for example, If you stop work, or you're a woman and you start to raise a family, or, for example, for any reason, you become ill or unemployed, you don't pay. And the system is one in which we're able to get more money into the universities, more people able to go to university, without anyone having to pay fees as they go through the system. You didn't say that in the manifesto, did you? No, I didn't say that about maintenance grants being reintroduced for poor students. Where is it? We had legislated... It doesn't exist. No, I'm sorry. It existed because we said we were going not to introduce... When did it exist? The legislation prevented us introducing top-up fees. The legislation we then introduced in this Parliament changes the system entirely. And instead of paying any fees as you go through university, you make a fair repayment after you graduate. Now, the only alternative to that, with the greatest respect, is either the Lib Dem one, which is to impose this 50% top rate of tax and pay for everything through that, or the Conservative one, which is to privatise the student loan book, which will end up with graduates paying a real credit card rate of interest on their student debt. Now, I happen to think our system is fairer. We already, incidentally, in this country... You shake your head, but it's true. Yeah, but, Andrew, when I was going through university, there were 5% of school leavers that went through university. Right? Now it is 40% of school leavers. I put more investment, or you do, actually, as the taxpayer. You put more investment into a student's education at university than you do into a secondary school pupil's or a primary school pupil's. Even under our new system, five-sixths of the money comes from the ordinary taxpayer. I don't think it's too much, instead of taking more money from the ordinary taxpayer, to ask people, when they graduate, to pay something back into the pot. I think that's a darn sight fairer than putting taxes on the ordinary population. I don't quite know who you've spoken to, obviously, but it's... The decision... We're always serious on this show. Look, you're going to have to make up your own mind about that. Look, there's no point in me sitting here and saying, oh, yes, I am a good leader or I'm not. You're going to have to decide who you want to lead this country. And on May the 6th, you're either going to have Michael Howard walk into number 10 Downing Street or me. And if you want to keep the economy strong and the investment in our public services there and the measures to help families in this country that we've introduced in the past few years, you should vote for the leadership that's delivered. Hang on a second. I'll be brief, Mr Blair, if you want. It's entirely up to them. I don't force anyone to go to university. But if people want to go, you've got to have a system of funding universities that's fair. Fair to the student, fair to the taxpayer. A system that is linked to people's ability to pay when they pay nothing going through university is a lot fairer than a system where we prevent, as the Conservatives would do, by a cap on the university numbers, people who want to go to university going there. I think that's a return to the elitism of the past and I don't want it in this country. I debate every week in the House of Commons with Michael Howard. I'm here to answer questions tonight. And I probably do more discussions, not just with audiences like this, but with people when we talk about the issues than any politician before me. Thank you very much.
deb_2005_04_28.QUESTIONER_0: Now, Charles Kennedy for the Liberal Democrats will go first. Then Michael Howard for the Conservative Party. And finally, Tony Blair, Prime Minister for the Labour Party. So, Will our first challenger sign in, please? All right. Our first question, Mr Kennedy, comes from James Gray, please. Gordon Brown had a baby when he was Chancellor of the Exchequer. But you hit people on modest incomes, couples on modest incomes, quite hard. The average... Is it a way to go into an election? 25% of the population are going to pay more tax? But what about in the South East? The ladies from the South East, the economics of the South East are different. What percentage of people in the South East will pay higher tax? No? Done? OK, come to you instead. Come back to you later. Well, that's a different question. Do you want to answer that briefly? Hold on, let's not go to that, because I want to stick on the question you were asked originally. Anybody else got a point on that? Yes, the woman there. We'll move on to another question. Daniel Shaik, please. Daniel Shaik. The question was how you would guarantee the security of the Iraqis if the elected Iraqi government asked British troops to stay. Would you pull them out and say, to hell, we are not interested, wash our hands of all this? Of course, Saddam would still be there if you had your way. We went into Kosovo. But you said without the United Nations agreement. There was no United Nations agreement in Kosovo. The position of the French on Iraq, exactly the same. But not British even wearing blue helmets? The man up there and then I'll come to you. The man there. Can we just hold it? We've got a question about that and I don't want him to go off on that at the moment. You, madam, over there, and then we'll move on. Yes. But isn't Lauren Cox's point that these things are, at present, means-tested, and by making them a universal benefit, you're helping people who can actually afford to pay something towards them. They suddenly won't have to. Is that your point, Lauren? But you said in Scotland you were going to abolish these fees, didn't you? Yes. Oh, I see. Well, what happens... Everybody pays... No. ..£2,000. Sorry, let's just get this straight. The very poorest still now pay £2,000, whereas before means-tested they'd have paid nothing. That's absolutely terrible. Let's move on. Christian, Christian Sanderson, please. No. Hang on. No, there are two questions. The first question was, would you go into a coalition? The second part was... Yeah, all right, well, let me put a supplementary. All right. You don't go into... You don't go... You don't go into a coalition. Who would you prefer to have as Prime Minister? Apart from Charles Kennedy. I looked at your map where you said if everybody who was thinking of voting Liberal Democrat voted, this is what the picture of the country would look like. It was 100 short of a majority, so even you don't believe you're going to form the next government, you don't believe you're going to be Prime You don't believe you're going to be the biggest party? What purpose is the Liberal Democrat Party if you don't do that? All right. All right. Now we hear from the man who took over the leadership of the Conservative Party from Iain Duncan Smith just 18 months ago, Michael Howard. Mr Howard, your first question comes from Gary Horsman, please. And it would depend on whether they'd reach the limit that you wanted, whether they got in or not? Yes, it would. So they might not get in? That's possible. Gary Horstman, does that answer your question? We've got to do what's right for Britain. Mr Horstman, does that answer your question? OK, the man there, can I ask a question? Well, you just said you were up in Scotland and you were talking to them. Lucy Elliott, please. Because you're talking about Mr Blair, and we're here... We're here... We're here... We're here to listen to you talk about your policies, and I want to go back to Lucy Elliott and just ask you what it was you were thinking of, and whether Mr Howard is answering your point. The word liar was what it was about, Mr Howard, not about whether people thought people were telling the truth. It's the use of the word liar, I think, isn't it, that you're objecting to. That's the objection. Is George W. Bush a liar as well as Tony Blair? I'm afraid the truth is... No, I want to go on to somebody else. The man at the back there in the white shirt. Thank you. It's an off-the-wall question, but if you'd like to answer it... It's a very interesting one. Yes, yes, but don't go on too long about it, if you don't mind, because we've got a long agenda here tonight. Let's move on. A question from Ahmad Khwaja, please. Ahmad Khwaja. But Mr Howard, the question was, if you knew now what you didn't know then, would you have gone to war? What you know now is there were no weapons of mass destruction. Are you saying you would have still gone to war against Iraq if you'd known there were no WMD in Iraq? But you can still be a threat. I'll go back to the question. Okay, man down here. And you would have gone, just to get this absolutely clear, you would have gone, if you'd known all the intelligence that was there and all the things that were discovered subsequently, without any WMD, without any immediate apparent threat, simply on the grounds that he hadn't abided by 1441, by the resolution, regardless of whether there was a real genuine threat to this country But it can't be regime change, because Goldsmith has said it can't be, under law. Sorry, what he actually says is regime change cannot be the objective of military action in the thing that was at least released this morning. You could go on the basis that it's a humanitarian catastrophe. You could go on the basis of a humanitarian catastrophe, couldn't you? Well, I think that's... Under the UN. I mean, if you want to argue the UN case, if you want to argue the UN case, that's... You don't want to go? OK, right. And you, sir, yes, and then we'll move on. Let's move on to a question from Alan Revel, please. Alan Revel? Yes. Would, a simple question, if you're returned as a government and you're Prime Minister, would your government increase the percentage of the nation's wealth created each year that's taken in tax, leave it the same or reduce it? You're talking about the tax burden? I'm talking about the amount of money the government receives from all of us here in tax. Yes. Do you have an ideal figure of what you think the tax take should be for a Western economy in the beginning of the 21st century? Do you have a target, since you make a moral issue about tax? As long as you promise not to talk about bullying again, you can have just one quick comment. Bullying is very important. No, no, no, but he's already asked this question before. Sorry about that. OK, no apologies. We learn from our mistakes. That's the point. And you... Just a note, very briefly now, and then we'll move on. Yes. Just quickly, if you would. OK, let me take a quick question from Kirsty MacIver, please. Kirsty MacIver. What do you see? The poorest won't get advantage of this. People really can't afford anything. You'll be giving a bit of money, as I understand it. I have to stop you there, Mr. Howard. I'm sorry, Jack Skillen wasn't able to ask his question, which is, are you thinking what we are thinking? Well, let me just say one thing. No, you can't. I'm sorry, your time's up. Thank you very much indeed. Well, all right. Finally, tonight, the man who wants to lead the Labour Party to a third victory after eight years at number 10, the Prime Minister, Tony Blair. Hello. Mr Blair, your first question comes from Christopher Jones, please. Christopher Jones' question isn't about the Attorney General's final decision. It's about this document, which you have been avoiding publishing for the past two years, and were forced to publish at half past eleven this morning, suddenly. And it's, did this document go before Cabinet? And if not, why not? And did this document go before the House of Commons? With all its hesitations, reservations, explanations, so that your backbench MPs had a chance to study the whole background to the legal decision that he then made. OK. Christopher Jones, you asked a question. You want to come back on that? We've already had one. Mr Blair, you mentioned the Butler inquiry and cited it in your own defence. Can I just quote you from Butler? He said that the intelligence, which as the young man said correctly, was described by the JIC as sporadic and patchy, that the dossier you produced in September went to the outer limits of that intelligence and that by calling it extensive, detailed and authoritative, you may have reinforced that impression. isn't that saying, not that you lied, but that you exaggerated, you over-egged, you misled, you sailed a bit close to the wind when you put across this intelligence to the British public? It doesn't answer my question, that. You said it was extensive, detailed and authoritative. Yes, it was. you off for saying that? And you keep saying perhaps I shouldn't have said anything at all. Do you mean you were wrong to say that about it? I took the decision I thought was right for the country. I want to move on because I have to watch the clock here. I will go to another question now. Of course, you did say in your manifesto that expansion would come without any need to increase money, and you never mentioned one less in your manifesto, and you never said you were going to put up money after the election. You got through that election suggesting to the electorate that you weren't going to increase taxes, let alone put 1% on. The man in the white shirt back there. No, in the back row there, yes. The woman there in black. Yes, that's a good point. And the woman further along in grey. No, please, the man in the green shirt, the dark green shirt. What? They're saying you can't make the appointment until you're within the time frame that allows it to be 48 hours. So you can't make it today, you come back and make it on Wednesday. OK. Just before we started on tax, just one question on tax. At the last election, you were asked on Newsnight whether you thought any reasonable person shouldn't suppose, because there was no commitment not to increase national insurance, that the tax would go up. And you said they shouldn't. suppose. Should or shouldn't we suppose now? You are the Prime Minister and your friend is the Chancellor of the Exchequer. And we've seen him side by side throughout this election campaign, so presumably you know what's in his mind. Oh, right. Oh, so he is going to become Prime Minister? You've said you're going to stand down during this next Parliament. Are you going to vote for him as leader of the Labour Party? No, whether we win... I'm just asking you, when the leadership battle comes, you've announced it's going to come because you said you're standing down. When it comes, will you vote for Gordon Brown? Well I wonder what he has to do to get your vote. Anyway let's go on to another question. Andrew Thwaites please. Andrew Thwaites. You just said you weren't going to introduce top-up fees and had legislated to prevent them. Yes, but where's the legislation to prevent them, Tony? I tried to ask him to give the names. No, I don't, but... You can give the names. Go on, then, name the names. Name them. Serious? Yes. Hang on a second. The woman in cream there. Mr Blair, can I just ask you one last question? We've had Mr Kennedy in that chair, we've had Mr Howard in that chair, why wouldn't you debate directly, face to face, with them? All right. Mr. Blair, Mr. Blair, thank you. Thank you very much. Sorry, hang on. No, no, no, no, no. We've reached the end of our time. I'm sorry. Will you be quiet? Mr. Blair, thank you very much indeed. And... That... That... Sit down, if you would. That ends this special edition of Question Time.

deb_2010_04_15.CLEGG: I believe the way things are is not the way things have to be. Now, you're going to be told tonight by these two that the only choice you can make is between two old parties who've been running things for years. I'm here to persuade you that there is an alternative. I think we have a fantastic opportunity to do things differently for once. And if we do things differently, we can create the fair society, the fair country we all want. A fair tax system. Better schools. An economy no longer held hostage by greedy bankers. Decent, open politics. Those are the changes I believe in. I really wouldn't be standing here tonight if I didn't think they were all possible. So don't let anyone tell you that the only choice is old politics. We can do something new. We can do something different this time. That's what I'm about. That's what the Liberal Democrats offer. Gerard, you talked about a fair, workable immigration system. That's exactly what I want. What's happened over the last several years is almost precisely the reverse. You've had lots and lots of tough talking about immigration from both Conservative and Labour governments and complete chaos in the actual administration of the system. There was a Conservative government that removed the exit control so we knew who was leaving as well as who was coming in. That's what the Labour government then followed up on as well. So what I think we need to do is firstly make sure that we restore those exit controls. We have borders so we know exactly who's coming in, but also when they're supposed to leave. And the second thing that I would do is this. At the moment under the immigration system, if you want to come and work in this country, you have to show two things. Firstly, that you've got a sponsor who's sponsoring your arrival in this country, and secondly, that there's a job for you to do. I want to add a third element, that you also only go to a place, to a region, where you are needed, so that we only send immigrants to those places where they can be coped. I think the truth is that there is good immigration and there's bad immigration. I was in a hospital, a paediatric hospital in Cardiff a few months ago, treating very sick prematurely born babies. I was being shown around and there were a large number of babies who needed to be treated. I went into a ward, it was standing completely empty. It had all the latest equipment, but it was standing completely empty. And I said to the ward sister, I said, what's going on? Why are there no babies here being treated? and she said no, new rules mean that we can't employ any doctors from outside the European Union with the skills needed. That's an example of where the rules are actually stopping good immigration, which actually help our public services to work properly. And that's where I want to see, not an arbitrary cap, I mean, you can't just say a cap, what is it, 10, 10,000, a million, what happens if you reach the cap in the middle of summer and someone wants to come and play football for Manchester United or Manchester City. Do you say they can't come? No. Let's have a regional approach where you only make sure that immigrants who come go to those regions where they can be supported. I think this is partly what's been going wrong for so long. We've had both major parties running governments over the last 20 years talking tough about immigration and delivering complete chaos in the way in which it's run. I'm like anybody else. I just want a fair, workable immigration system that counts people in, counts people out, only make sure immigrants come here if there's jobs for them to do in parts of the country where they don't place unreasonable strain on housing, on public services and so on. I think the regional approach that we're putting forward, which would be a major innovation, they do it in Canada, they do it in Australia, but it would be a major innovation here, which I think would restore public confidence in an immigration system. It was a vision of M62 with a sort of border post. Very easily. They do it in other countries. It basically means that if you are an employer and you are employing someone who has got a work permit, then you will need to make sure that in that work permit they are only able to work in the region where you are offering them work. offer them work when they don't have a work permit which says they're entitled to be in that region, then you are acting illegally. They do it in other countries, we should do it here. Because the truth is that our country has lots of different needs in different parts of the country. That's not being reflected in an immigration system in which the public has lost any confidence. Jacqueline, you asked what can we do to stop burglary happening over and over and over again. Two things. Firstly, quite simply, more police on our streets. This government wants to waste billions of your money on an ID card system so that you have to pay for the privilege of having lots of your own details on a piece of plastic car that you carry around. For pretty well exactly the amount of money, your money, that the government's pouring into that, we could put 3,000 more police officers on the streets. That is the absolute priority for me. Second thing is this. There are too many young offenders who start first getting into trouble with low-level nuisance, antisocial behaviour. who become the hardened criminals of tomorrow. What we've got to do is stop the young offenders of today becoming the hardened criminals of tomorrow. And in my city of Sheffield, where I'm an MP, we've done some great things to do exactly that. That's the way to get burglary and crime down. I think, as I say, it's how do we make sure that the youngsters of today don't become the hardened criminals of tomorrow. It's that conveyor belt from nuisance at the beginning, anti-social behaviour in our communities, you know, yobs on the street corner who then become the hardened criminals of tomorrow. And I think what makes me so angry is that Again, it's a bit like the immigration debate. So much tough talk from different governments of different parties for so long and actually has turned our prisons into overcrowded colleges of crime. I mean, do you know that young men going into prison now on short-term prison sentences now come out and nine out of ten of them re-offend? So they're actually reproducing more crime than actually cutting it. And what I've seen in my city of Sheffield is that you get these youngsters Not when they've done serious crimes, but when they're first starting to get into trouble. To face their victims. Explain why they've done what they've done to their victims. Apologise for what they've done. Make up for what they've done in the community. Cleaning up parks and streets. It has a dramatic effect on their behaviour. I want to change people's behaviour before they become the criminals of tomorrow. Well, it's all very well to say these things, but if what actually happens in practice is that we produce these, as I say, these colleges of crime, where we have now, what, about 4,000 people going into our prisons on short-term prison sentences. They sit around. They learn some extra tricks of the trade from some more experienced criminals and then they go out and nine out of ten of the young men on short-term prison sentences just commit more crime. And it's that, I think that's what Jacqueline's talking about, this desperate feeling, this hopeless feeling, it keeps happening over and over again. I met a young man in London the other day, his flat had been burgled five times. And one of them, would you believe it, Jacqueline, was when he was away at his father's funeral. And all he said to me, he said, why can't this stop? And unless we do something different, not the same old remedies, but do something different to stop the youngsters of today who are getting into trouble from becoming the hardened criminals of tomorrow, all this stuff I don't think will make the difference that they say it will. I'm just slightly surprised that there's any discussion between you two going on about what money you can put into public services, because I read your manifestos this week. In neither of them are you coming clean with people about how anything costs, because you haven't got any figures in your manifestos. We've set out very clearly not only what we will do, but how we will pay for it. And you can only pay for it these days if you're prepared to save money. I don't think that any politician deserves your trust, and you talked about credibility, deserves any credibility, until everybody has come clean about what has gone wrong. Now, there have been some changes to the rules and all that, the changes to the expenses rules, but you know there are still people who haven't taken full responsibility for some of the biggest abuses in the system. There are MPs who flipped one property to the next, buying properties paid by you, the taxpayer, and then they would do the properties up, paid for by you, and pocket the difference in personal profit. They got away scot-free. There were MPs who avoided paying capital gains tax. And of course you remember the duck houses and all the rest of it, but actually it's the people, it's the MPs who made these big abuses, some of them profiting hundreds of thousands of pounds. I have to stress, not a single Liberal Democrat MP did either of those things, but they still haven't been dealt with. But we can only turn around the corner on this until we're honest about what went wrong in the first place. I have to say to both David Cameron and Gordon Brown, what bothers me is when I hear the words, They sound great. But it's not just what you say, it's what you do. Why is it that when I put forward, Liberal Democrats put forward, a law which would have given all of you and everyone watching now the right to sack their MP if their MP is corrupt, The Labour MPs voted against it, the Conservative MPs didn't even bother to vote. Why is it when we supported a deal to clean up the really murky business of party funding, which has affected all parties, you blocked it, you blocked it. You wanted to protect paymasters of the trade union paymasters, you wanted to protect Lord Ashcroft in his offshore haven in Belize. It's not good enough. to keep talking about how we need to change politics, if when you've got an opportunity to change, you actually block it. I think that's a betrayal. I think that's a con. I think you deserve the right to sack your MPs when they're corrupt, but you also deserve a politics where we finally get the big money out of politics altogether. Nick Clegg. Nick Clegg. Hang on a minute. Before we bandy about these things, let's be absolutely clear. We were completely exonerated for that. It was years ago. I'm talking about what's been going on now. Have you paid the money back? And listen, none of this is going to make any difference if we allow this rotten system in Westminster to carry on, where MPs have jobs for life, where they basically only need to get 20, 30% of your votes in their areas, No questions asked. They don't even need to bother until the next time there's an election. And there's a direct correlation between the hundreds of Labour and Conservative MPs who've got these safe seats, these jobs for life, and the levels of abuse in expenses. And neither of you want to clean up the system from top to toe in the way that we've been advocating for years. Thank you, Mr Clegg. Mr Clegg, can you clarify that? Can you clarify that? There's absolutely nothing to support. They did nothing for 13 years. Do you support that? No. What I support is something I've supported all my adult political life, which is a complete clean-up from top to toe of politics, direct elections to the House of Lords. They shouldn't be there just because they've done favours to politicians making the rules that you need to abide by, getting big money out of them. I'm absolutely dismayed by this. I mean, this is something I actually put forward in the House of Commons. We already could have had that law. People already could have had the right to sack corrupt MPs. Labour MPs voted against it. Conservative MPs didn't. It's great we're saying the same thing. You've also got to do the right thing to clean up politics. Well, Joel, I think everyone will recognise what you're talking about, this feeling that you just have to constantly jump through hoops. I mean, the symptoms are everywhere. I think our national curriculum is 600 pages. The curriculum in Sweden, which has generally got a fairly good education system, is 16 pages. I just read the other day that headteachers now, by email over the last year, have received, get this, 4,000 pages of instructions from on high from Whitehall. This is crazy. We've got to let headteachers teach. We've got to let teachers teach. We've got to reinstill a sense of enthusiasm and creativity in the way that you are taught. And that's why what we want to do, the Liberal Democrats, is to put onto the statute book an Education Freedom Act which literally bans government from micromanaging what happens every single minute of the day with every single test in every single classroom in the country. That's what I'd like to see and I think it would make a big difference to you and other people who are at school. I think discipline is important, of course. I think creativity, which I think is the point you're saying, Joel. I'm not allowed to ask you questions, that's against the rules, but just nod if... Yeah, okay, good. I think creativity is important in the classroom. I think freedom for teachers and headteachers. Do you know one thing which I think would really help in all of those things? Discipline, creativity, freedom for teachers is quite simply good, old-fashioned, smaller class sizes. We have 8,000 infants in this country now, between the ages of five and seven, who are in classes which are so big, they're illegal. They're technically illegal. And it's just logical. If you're a teacher, I mean, friends of mine who are teachers just sort of say, they can't really keep an eye on the troublemakers, but they also can't support the brightest children if the classes are huge. And that's why we've got a plan, fully costed, to provide schools with additional resources so that they can bring down the average class size in a primary school for instance down to 20 and the average class size in a secondary school down to 16. You are going to take money out of us. Thank you, Mr Brown. Mr Clegg. I'm not sure if you're like me, but the more they attack each other, the more they sound exactly the same. Let's go back to Joel's question. Joel asked, why are you being tested so much? How can all pupils in our schools feel that they're being supported and getting the best out of education? And I come back to this need to combine two things. Firstly, more freedom for teachers and headteachers. Remember this crazy thing I told you about headteachers getting 4,000 pages of instructions by email? And secondly, smaller class sizes, more one-to-one tuition, Saturday morning classes, evening classes, so that you can help those children in particular who perhaps aren't being supported at home as much as anybody else. Because you know, I certainly know from my two sons who go to a school, a local school, excellent state-funded school in my area, if a whole class can move together, then that enriches all children. I think what goes wrong is when classes get so big and classes actually fall apart. Gordon Brown mentioned spending. Absolutely, too right. I don't think we're really going to get those smaller class sizes, that one-to-one tuition that Joel, I think, agrees is necessary. The catch-up classes, unless we find money from savings elsewhere, we've spelt that out in our manifesto, so we can provide, under our plan, £2.5 billion extra to our schools. Where are you, Robert? I can hear your voice. Ah, there you are, right at the back. Sorry, behind the camera. Now I can see you. Robert, I think we need to just be open with you, straight with you. and we've sought to do that. We have specified, and we're the only party in politics now, in our manifesto, look at the back of our manifesto, which says, here are the figures, this is the way that we would find cuts and savings of £15 billion. How would we do that? By things like removing tax credits for the top 20% of recipients of tax credits, ending the child trust fund, which gives £250 to all 18-year-olds. I'd love to give everybody £250, but I just don't think we can afford it right now. Putting a cap of £400 on any pay increases in the public sector for the next two years. But also some long-term choices. I'm the only leader here who's saying very clearly, I don't think we can either justify or afford the like-for-like replacement of the Cold War nuclear missile system, the Trident missile system, over the next 25 years. It'll cost you, all of us, £100 billion. We can't afford it. These two constantly argue about waste, as if we can fill the black hole in the public finances by saving money on paper clips and pot plants in Whitehall. Of course we can get rid of a bit of waste, but that doesn't really address the big questions we need to ask ourselves. And I think we need to be clear with you, open with you, straight with you, We've tried to do that. We set out £15 billion worth of savings. I've listed some of them. We have one specific tax that we want to introduce to help fill the black hole. We would impose a 10% tax on the profits of the banks. These banks who got us into the trouble in the first place, I think they should pay you back because you, the taxpayer, have bailed them out and use that money to deal with the black hole in the finances. But let's not get obsessed about mythical savings in waste. which is the oldest trick in the book to pretend that you can square a circle like that, or get obsessed about when you deliver these cuts. The crucial thing is, are we going to be open with people, with you, about how we're going to save money in the long term? We all know we've got this great black hole in our public finances. That's obvious. We all know that we're going to have to save money. We all know that we're going to have to make cuts. The question at this election is, who is trying to be straight with you about the scale of those cuts? how long they'll take. As it happens, this is one area where I would like for once to see politicians put people before politics. And what I'm suggesting, I don't know whether Gordon Brown and David Cameron would take up this invitation, is that regardless of the outcome of the general election, that we get the Chancellor and the Shadow Chancellors together, the Governor of the Bank of England, the Head of the Financial Services Authority, to come clean with you about how big this structural deficit is. It's estimated to be somewhere around £70 billion, and straight with you, finally, about how long it is going to take to fill... David Cameron. We've spelt out exactly where that money would come from. We would, for instance, stop this grotesque spectacle of this unfair tax system, which has been built up under succession of Labour and Conservative governments, where right now a greedy banker in the City of London pays a lower rate of tax on their capital gains than their cleaner does on their wages. Gordon Brown. We have a tax system which is grossly unfair. All I would say is this argument, I think, just doesn't address the fundamental issue. There are going to be big things over the next few years, and neither of them will come clean on this with you, that we simply can't afford to do. Trident. I don't think we can afford it. A tax on banks I think is now unavoidable. Tax credits. We need to look at public sector pensions. These are big decisions which we need to take. David Cameron. And I would like us for once to get politicians together. You're right, Nick, they are under-equipped and they are underpaid. I think there's something seriously wrong when you've got 8,000 bureaucrats in the Ministry of Defence who work on communications. When we have too many top brass in some of the services, there are, I think, two admirals for every warship. We have 17 brigadiers for every brigade. And we also, of course, have this consensus from the Conservative and Labour parties that we should spend £100 billion renewing the Cold War nuclear Trident missile system. I say, if we change our priorities, we can provide our brave servicemen and servicewomen, who do the most astonishing job in the most extraordinarily difficult circumstances, we can give them proper pay. I think it's a scandal that someone who starts in the army on a junior rank now gets paid £6,000 less than someone starting as a firefighter or in the police service. I want them to have the same pay and I also want them to have proper body armour, proper helicopters, proper vehicles and you can only do that if you cut out spending elsewhere which isn't being well spent. I think it's also what kind of equipment we provide. I was in a factory in my own city where I'm an MP in Sheffield just a few weeks ago and there's a great British company there, a manufacturing company that produces great metal braces with these huge rollers which apparently are sold to the American army, they attach them onto their vehicles and when the rollers move over mines, the mines blow up but of course they destroy the rollers and not the not the soldiers, and that the American army says that those rollers, designed, manufactured by a great British business in Sheffield, have saved 140 lives. So why is it that they're not being used by the British army? Apparently they don't somehow fit onto the vehicles that our soldiers use. So I think it's not only that we've got to make sure that we don't waste money on bureaucrats in the Ministry of Defence and all the rest of it, and instead spend that on equipment for our brave servicemen and servicewomen. We should also use the know-how and the manufacturing brilliance and expertise in this country to provide our brave soldiers with the equipment which saves lives on the front line. I actually happen to agree, strongly agree, in something I've been calling for for years, that we should have a complete review about whether our military equipment is right for the jobs we're asking our brave soldiers and brave service men and women to do. Because of course the world is changing and the threats to this country are changing with it. What I simply don't understand is that if we hold that review, as I think is going to be likely after the general election, whoever wins that election, Both David Cameron and Gordon Brown want to rule out one of the biggest items of defence expenditure of all, which is the Trident nuclear missile system. This was a system that was designed at the height of the Cold War to flatten St Petersburg and Moscow at the crest of battle. All I'm saying is I don't think we should kid people into thinking that we can either justify or afford £100 billion over 25 years on a nuclear war system, a missile system, which was designed explicitly to flatten St Petersburg and Moscow at the press of a button. I think the world has moved on and I think you too need to move with it. We're not in the Cold War anymore and we shouldn't be spending billions of pounds of people's money, taxpayers' money, on a Cold War missile system when, as Nick said in his original question, we have people on the front line in Afghanistan without the right equipment, without the right protection. It's a question of priorities. Of course, the easy thing is to say how much we all love and depend and rely on the NHS. The difficult question, which is, I think, the one that you're addressing, is how do we protect the NHS, which we all rely on, maternity services, A&E departments, GP services and so on, when money is tight? And I think it's a little bit like the earlier discussion about equipment in the army. The priorities at the moment are all wrong. The last year, under this Government they have just employed 5,000 more managers in the NHS, yet the maternity ward in the NHS hospital where my third son was born just over a year ago is threatened with closure. This Government spent £12 billion on a computer system in the NHS which doesn't work and yet I was in Burnley the other day, I think Jacqueline was saying that you come from Burnley, As you know, Jacqui, they've closed the A&E department there. I think you have to now travel 25 miles to Blackburn. So what is going on? We're closing A&E departments and maternity wards and wasting money on computer systems and bureaucracy. I want to turn that on its head so we can protect the NHS that we all rely on. This is pretending that somehow there are billions and squillions of pounds around that we can continue to pour into our NHS. Every man, woman and child in this country spends £2,000 on the NHS through our tax system. I want to judge the NHS about how it helps me and my family when we're ill and we're sick and we're in need of NHS care, not just by numbers plucked out of thin air. David Cameron, you simply cannot seriously suggest that we should believe that you can cut the deficit immediately as you want, then get a whole blizzard of tax breaks, including a great big tax break for double millionaires in the inheritance tax system, and provide huge lashings of extra money to the public. You might be able to do one of those things. You can't do all three. So I want to say to people, let's be straight with you. We're going to have to find savings in the NHS. I want to see, for instance, strategic health authorities, which is a layer of bureaucracy, stripped away altogether and use that money on the frontline NHS services, which are so important to us. All I would appeal for is just a bit of honesty in this debate. I mean, people know that money is tight. People know that you can't promise something for nothing. You can't say you're going to fill the deficit tomorrow and you're going to give lots and lots of tax breaks to people, inheritance tax breaks for double millionaires, tax breaks for, what is it, one in three hand-picked married couples, and also extra, extra, extra money to the NHS without explaining how you're going to do it. I tell you how we pay for it. We would, for instance, stop the huge unfair loopholes that only benefit the very wealthy at the top of the tax system. At the moment, the top 10% earners in this country get twice as much tax subsidy from all of the rest of you when they make contributions to their pension pot than everybody else. We say give people tax relief on their pension contributions, but make sure that they are the same and use that money so that no-one pays any income tax on the first £10,000 they earn. Thank you, Mr Brown. I think, Alan, this is one of those rare issues where The issue is so big, and the costs are potentially so great, and it affects every family, it affects every individual, that I think this is one of those issues where I would say it's worth the politicians just setting aside their political differences for once and trying to come up with a solution that everybody can agree with. We've all got different ideas. We have ideas, proposals, that there's a contribution from the individual and the state. David Cameron has ideas which help some, but doesn't help people in their home. Gordon Brown has some ideas which help some of the most needy, but not others. Why don't we for once, given this is something which I think is bigger than any other party, actually work together? There are some things, however, that I think we can do immediately. I, for instance, would like to use the money that the government has allocated for its latest, I think slightly flawed proposals, to give respite care to the carers who look after loved ones, those who look after loved ones for the greatest amount of time, give them respite for at least a week every year. That's something we can do now. Let's come up with a longer proposal together, a longer-term proposal together. Of course, I agree with that. There are about a million carers in this country who care, I think now, for 50 hours or more for their loved ones, for members of their family who need care. They are the unsung army of heroines and heroes that keep our society together. They desperately need some time for themselves, time to go on holiday. Under our plans, what we could do immediately is give those million carers who care for the greatest length of time at least a week off. at least a week off, just to have a breather, spend some time on their own, go and visit friends, go on holiday, just have some time to themselves again. But as I say, I think we've all got some ideas, but I don't think any of us, and you don't hear this from politicians very much, I don't think any of us, actually, if we're really honest with you, have got the perfect solution. That's why I think this is so important. Let's, for once, put people before politics and come up with a solution that works for you and your family in the long run. Well, I think everybody would be very surprised that the last question of the evening should actually have flowed into so much consensus. I just think it is one of those issues, a bit like public sector pensions. I also think the scale of the public sector deficit, it's one of those issues where I think if we could go introduce a new kind of politics into this country, not the old style of politics, we could actually come up with a solution that everybody could agree to, because I think you and your family would benefit from it so much. Well, thanks for starters for sticking with us for a full 90 minutes. What I've tried to show you this evening is that there is an alternative to the two old parties. I know many of you think that all politicians are just the same. I hope I've tried to show you that that just isn't true. Whether it's on the questions from Alan on care, Jacqueline on crime, Helen on politics, Joel on schooling, Robert on the deficit, I believe we can answer all of those questions. I believe we can rise to all of those challenges if we say no to the old parties and yes to something new and something different. And that's what I offer, that's what the Liberal Democrats offer. So don't let them tell you that the only choice is between two old parties who've been playing pass the parcel with your government for 65 years now, making the same promises, breaking the same promises, making the same old mistakes over and over again. Because I think, despite all the challenges, all the problems we have, I think we can be really hopeful about the future. I genuinely believe we can have a better, fairer country if we do things differently. So give real change a chance. Trust your instincts. Support fairness. Choose something different. And that will give you and your family a better, fairer life. Thank you.
deb_2010_04_15.CAMERON: I think it's great we're having these debates and I hope they go some way to restore some of the faith and some of the trust into our politics because we badly need that once again in this country. The expenses saga brought great shame on Parliament and I'm extremely sorry for everything that happened. Your politicians, frankly all of us, let you down. There is a big choice at this election. We can go on as we are, or we can say no, Britain can do much better. We can deal with our debts. We can get our economy growing and avoid this jobs tax. And we can build a bigger society. But we can only do this if we recognise we need to join together. We need to come together. We need to recognise we're all in this together. Now, not everything Labour has done in the last 13 years has been wrong. They've done some good things, and I would keep those. But we need change, and it's that change that I want to help to lead. Well, Gerard, what I would say is immigration is simply too high at the moment. It has been these last 10 years and it does need to come down. I think the pressures that we've put on housing and health and education have been too great. And if you look at what's happening with immigration, the difference between the amount of people going to live overseas and those coming here, it's been often as high as 200,000. That's equivalent to 2 million across a decade. It's too much. I want us to bring immigration down so that it's in the tens of thousands, not the hundreds of thousands. How would we do that? Well, I think we need to have not just a point system, but also a limit on migration when people are coming from outside the European Union for economic reasons. I also think when new countries join the European Union that actually we should have transitional controls so they can't all come here at once. It's been too high these last few years and I would dearly love to get it down to the levels it was in the past so it's no longer an issue in our politics as it wasn't in the past. Nick Clegg. Well, I think a cap is necessary because we're not going to control immigration unless we actually take some quite positive and concrete steps. And I think we let down everyone if we don't do this properly. I was in Plymouth recently and a 40-year-old black man actually made the point to me. He said, I came here when I was six. I've served in the Royal Navy for 30 years. I'm incredibly proud of my country. But I'm so ashamed that we've had this out-of-control system with people abusing it so badly. So if we don't address immigration properly, we're actually letting down immigrant communities as well as everybody else. So we do need a cab. As well as border controls that Gordon talks about, and I support strong border controls, I think we need to go one step further and actually have a proper border police force. So we combine at our borders customs, immigration, security and police. So we make the most of the fact that we do have policeable borders and we could stop more illegal immigration that way as well. Nick Clegg. Well, let me... On Nick's point, I mean, of course, we've benefited from immigration across decades in our country. People have come here to work hard, to make a contribution, to bring their special skills, and we see that in the health service, in our schools, all the time. But I do think that, as I say, it's got out of control, and it does need to be brought back under control. I think a lot of people, I think, would ask, though, We have had 13 years of a government that has now only started to talk about addressing this issue. If you look at the numbers, net migration levels before 1997 were never greater than 77,000 a year. Under your government, they have never been less than 140,000 a year. That is a very big number. You are only starting just before an election to take the steps that need to be taken. It is absolutely true that the other side of the coin is proper welfare reform. We have got too many people who could work, who are offered work, but who don't work, and that has actually drawn a lot of people into our country. So we do need to reform welfare. 13 years have gone by when welfare hasn't been properly reformed. Can I just ask one question about this regional approach. I don't quite understand how you can ask people to come to one part of the country and rely on them staying in that part of the country. Well, Jacqueline, the system isn't working properly now. There's no doubt about it. We're not seeing enough police on the streets. We're not catching enough burglars. We're not convicting enough. And then when we do convict them, they're not getting long enough sentences. I went to Crosby the other day and I was talking to a woman there who had been burgled by someone who had just left prison. and he stole everything in her house, and as he left, he set fire to the sofa and her son died from the fumes. And that burglar, that murderer, could be out of prison in just four and a half years. Now, the system doesn't work, but that sort of sentence is, I think, just completely unacceptable in terms of what the public expect for proper punishment. What have we got to do? Well, we've got to get rid of the paperwork and the bureaucracy, and we've got to get the police out on the streets. We need very clear signals from our criminal justice system that if you cross someone's threshold and rob their home, you go to prison, and you go to prison for a long time. Nick Clegg. I think one additional point that I would make is if you look at where so much of the burglary, so much of the car crime is coming from, it is actually coming from people who are addicted to drugs. And I think we've got to be much faster at getting drug addicts off the streets and into treatment. and all too often that doesn't happen. And even when it does today, they actually just get put on a substitute drug. And we're not really dealing with the problem, which is to get these people to confront their problems and lead drug-free lives. I even went to a drug rehab recently in my own constituency and met a young man who told me that he committed a certain amount of crimes so he could get in front of a judge who could then get him a place in a residential rehab centre. We must be mad as a country not to get people into that residential rehab, get them to clean up their lives, so we cut the crime on our own streets. David Cameron! Thank you. Well, let me take on very directly this question of money and public spending, because I think it's going to be a common feature right through these debates about how do we fund the public services that we need. And I think it's really important we start focusing on what we get out of the money that we put in. Because if we think that the future is just spending more and more money, we're profoundly wrong. Mr. Brown. Hold on. I'll get it going between the two of you. Don't worry. Mr. Brown. Yes, of course, but let me give you an example. Will you match our funding on the police? Let Mr Cameron answer your point, Mr Brown. It's answer time, David. What matters is what comes out. I went to Hull police station the other day They had five different police cars, and they were just about to buy a £73,000 Lexus. There's money that could be saved to get the police on the front line. The Metropolitan Police have 400 uniformed officers in their Human Resources Department. Well, our police officers should be crime fighters, not form thinners, and that's what that needs to change. Mr Cameron. On that point, let me take Nick back to his manifesto and one pledge that's in there that worried me a lot. My mother was a magistrate in Newbury for 30 years. She sat on the bench and she did use those short prison sentences that you're talking about. And I've got to tell you, when someone smashes up the bus stop, when someone repeatedly breaks the law, when someone's found fighting on a Friday or Saturday night, as a magistrate, you've got to have that power for a short prison sentence when you've tried all the other remedies. And the idea that you never send someone to prison... Brown on that point, please. Gordon Brown is trying to make you believe he can protect health spending, he can protect education spending, he can protect police spending. He cannot do any of these things because he's given this country the biggest budget deficit of any developed country in the world. I'm going to stop it there. Well, Helen, I'm not surprised you talk about it in your pub because it was just a horrendous episode. As Nick says, it isn't fully finished and sorted out yet. And I know how angry people are in this country. They pay their taxes and they don't pay their taxes for MPs to abuse the system. and I know how angry I was when I heard about the moats and the duck houses and the rest of it and I was determined to do my bit to clean it up, to get my MPs to apologise, to get them to pay back money, all of which they did before the official reviews started to happen. But do you know one thing I think we really need to do as part of the apology? is to say to the British people, we're going to cut the cost of politics. We're going to cut the size of the House of Commons by 10%. We're going to cut ministers' pay by 5% and freeze it for the whole of the Parliament. We're going to cut the size of Whitehall by a third. We're going to get rid of some of these quangos. We're going to make your politics better value for money, as well as cleaner. I think that's part of the apology we really badly need to make. I want to see a reformed House of Lords. I think the House of Lords should be predominantly elected. And Gordon, you've had 13 years to sort out the House of Lords. If there are still hereditary peers sitting in the House of Lords, if you're not happy with the House of Lords, why on earth haven't you done something about it? You've had all this time. and to suddenly now talk about electoral reform, about changing the voting system, which you started doing just weeks before the general election, I think people will see that as a bit of a ploy. But let me defend once again this idea of cutting the size of the House of Commons. Who in business, who in public services, who in their family life hasn't actually had to try and get more for less hasn't had to trim their budgets, hasn't had to work a bit harder. Why on earth should MPs and Parliament be any different? We could quite as well get by with 10% fewer MPs, we could cut the cost for you, the taxpayer, and we could do a better job at the same time. Let me just make one point after all that Nick said, because I thought there was a slight danger of a sort of holier than thou. I think we should all be frank. Politics has been a mess for all of us. We all had MPs with dreadful expenses problems. There are still three Labour MPs in court at the moment. There were Liberal MPs that were criticised. And when it comes to party funding, yes, there's been the union money going into Labour from the Unite Union. Yes, the Conservative Party's been too reliant for too long on rich individuals. And yes, the Liberal Democrats took £2.5 million off someone who's still, I think, a criminal on the run, and the money hasn't been paid back. Well it's rather difficult because Gordon says Nick agrees with Gordon and Nick says Nick doesn't agree with Gordon so let me try and find something we're all agreed on that we could change and will make a difference. I think it is time that when an MP breaks the rules that those constituents should be able to throw that Member of Parliament out of Parliament without having to wait for a general election. I think we all agree about that and whoever wins the next election we can put that in place straight away. I have every sympathy with what you say, because education is important, as well as getting good grades, that actually we're opening young people's minds to all the best things that have been written and all the best things that have been said, and to really excite people about education. And I think there is a danger that our education system has become terribly bureaucratic. We send 4,000 pages of information to schools every year. We spend £300 million on educational quangos. We're not getting enough to the front line, actually following the child into the school. And as someone who's got two children, one of whom started a state school in London and hopefully another one, another child to come. I'm passionate about getting as much money into the school as possible rather than wasting it in Whitehall. In terms of exams, we've got to have good external marking, done properly and high standards. I think that's absolutely key. I wouldn't want to see that change. But let's set the schools free so we trust in the vocation of the teachers who do what they want. They're there because they have a vocation they care about. Nick Clegg. Joel, what I'd say in terms of what I care about most in education with my own children going through the system, I want what every parent in this country wants. And it starts with something that actually doesn't necessarily cost money, and that is good discipline in our schools. You know, in a typical year now, you get 17,000 teachers being attacked by students. We've got a real problem here. There was a case in Manchester once where a child actually produced a knife in a school, got excluded and then the appeals panel put that child back into the school.
