The last five years have been really hard work but we've got a plan now and the economy is improving. What worries me is you're about to put Ed Balls back in as a Chancellor and he called that letter a joke. Now let me tell you, running a business the last few years is anything but a joke and if that's the way your party wants to treat the economy, how can we trust you? I run a marketing business. I employ 76 people here in this city. No it doesn't. That wasn't the question I asked you. I asked you why on earth I should trust a Chancellor who thinks a letter like that was a joke. If he worked in the corporate world he would have been fired and he would not be allowed back to do that job. And you're telling me he can do it. I also think, Mr Miliband, you talk about big businesses. I was today with the biggest private employer in the UK, which is Tesco. They've had a pretty tough time, but they talked about behaving their way out of the problems they've made. And I think that's something that politicians have got to think about too.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_22: Hi. With all the rhetoric about non-DOMs and unpaid tax, if your party were in power, do you think you could ever bridge the gap between the richest and the poorest? It's Shirley. Yeah.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_23: All right. And you, sir, there? global financial crisis. Australia didn't suffer this. Canada didn't suffer this. Some of the other major countries didn't suffer. This country suffered because Gordon Brown sold gold just to prop up the social services. How can you stand there and say you didn't overspend and end up bankrupting this country? That is absolutely ludicrous. You're frankly just lying. You stood there and said you didn't overspend. If I get to the end of the week and I can't afford to buy a pint, I've overspent. It means I haven't got any money left. We've got to the end of a government of 13 years, and during that 13-year period, you spent, spent, you sold gold when it was low. If we still had the gold now, we'd be pretty much better off. You can't stand that and say... Can I just come back on the point that you made? No, I'm sorry, I mean financial services. Financial services actually put millions and millions and millions of pounds into this country. Millions of pounds, which you then spend, and you spend irresponsibly. What are you referring to?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_24: Sure. Nick. Hi Darren. Hiya. Your promise on student loans has destroyed your reputation.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_25: If you can't accept that you overspent in the last government, then why on earth should we trust you not to do it again? If you can't even realise that that's what you did, and they've cut the deficit by half, why should we trust you not to do the same thing if you can't even accept that you overspent? You're saying you don't want people from my age group to be in debt and yet you're committing to borrowing even more.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_26: Why is the Labour Party misleading the country about having to do a deal with the SNP in the event of a minority Labour government? So when other members of the Shadow Cabinet say that they're open to deals, you disagree with that? I do, and I'm the leader. We're not going to have deals. And you wouldn't put forward a Queen's speech on the hope that they'd support it? You just wouldn't go to the Queen and accept her invitation? We do want to put forward a Labour Queen's speech.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_27: In that case, does that rule out a future Labour government? Because you're not going to win the most seats in the UK.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_28: What makes your view more important than the British people when it comes to the EU and an in-out referendum? It's actually Elisha. Yeah, so your view is more important than mine and everybody else here?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_29: So you don't want an EU referendum and all the debate that brings, but a second election because you won't form a majority government? Yeah, but if you don't get a majority, the Conservatives don't get a majority, you're not going to go with the SNP. I want to govern.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_30: The Conservative Party have made cuts to education, adults and further education. How do you propose to undo the damage? Conservative Party have made up to 24% cuts in adults and further education. How do you propose to undo the damage done by the savage cuts to adult education made by this government? Yes, we have. I work in FE. That's for adults in further education, not for universities or primaries.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_31: Hello. Mr Miliband. Hi. Can I just ask you, you know when people ask you a question about forming a coalition, and you say, no, I want a majority, and Mr Cameron said the same. Do you comprehend how much respect you would get from the audience if you were truly honest?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_32: Are you essentially telling us that you're expecting the private sector to fund the gaps in the welfare bill because there's only so much that employers can actually do to, you know, you can't keep expecting, you're talking about zero hours contracts, changes, you're talking about getting people on to working in the private sector after a year. How are employers supposed to afford all of this stuff? Because being an employer is a very, very expensive business as it is.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_33: A ban on zero-hours contracts will prevent me from growing my small business. Isn't it time the Labour Party put business before gimmicks and soundbites? It's a tourism business, so when the sun shines, I've got business. If the sun doesn't shine, I don't, so... The sun always shines in Yorkshire. Hi. What are you going to do differently about immigration?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_34: Yes. Hi, Mr. Clegg. Hi. The public suggests they can't forgive... No. The public suggests they can't forgive that one thing. So if hindsight, would you go into coalition in 2010 again?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_35: Good evening. Is leaking details that you're only privy to as part of a coalition likely to get you invited into a coalition again? So, some details have been leaked today about a policy that was put forward by the Conservatives whilst you were in coalition with them.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_36: But I don't think voters want to hear reasons why not to vote for another party. We want reasons why to vote for your party.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_37: That's all well and good, but you've just told us that in 2010 you had to make a very difficult decision on tuition fees. How do we know that you won't have to make a very difficult decision again around the things that you're promising us tonight?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_38: Yeah, I just wondered if you've got plans for a new job after next week when you become unemployed and your party becomes irrelevant. Charming.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_39: Nick, how do you feel about the huge increase of people driven to use food banks, many of them in work poverty or falling foul of benefit sanctions?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_40: Good evening, Nick. I do think you're an honourable man, but what you're forgetting is that all these sanctions were in place because you put Cameron into number 10. He didn't have a majority, so there'd be no work programme, nowhere near as many benefit sanctions, and you could have made a different choice, and that's why people don't trust you. People like me that voted for you in that election did not vote Liberal Democrat to put Cameron in number 10. Yeah, but... Hang on. What did... Just explain, what did you expect? What did you want? I'm sorry? What did you want? My preference would have been for him to have continued negotiations with Labour, which I think a lot of Liberal Democrat natural voters are people who voted Labour that were protesting. But can I just make... There's just the little matter of democracy. great team and now slugging each other off. Greece our economy and you know it. Our economy was nothing like Greece's and it insults everyone's intelligence to compare the economy of this country to Greece. You're not that stupid.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_41: Your question. Good evening, Mr Clegg. You mentioned earlier about democracy. Do I assume from that, that when the phone rings on Friday morning, next Friday morning, that the first person you will speak to will be the person who has most seats?
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_42: Yes, sir. We've got eight countries that are about to possibly leave the European Union, and we've got still the Eurozone crisis lying underneath. Eight countries about to leave? Yeah, we've got Spain, we've got Cyprus. They're in danger of leaving. There's a chance that they will leave soon. We've got the eurozone debt crisis still underneath the surface, and we've got 30% government bonds, European government bonds, trading at negative interest rates. And Germany, the heart of the EU, 70% of its government bonds are trading at negative interest rates. I want to know how bad does it have to get before you would think, perhaps now we should leave? But it still isn't an answer to my question, which was how bad would it have to get? For example, If Greece was to leave the European Union, and then if France, which looks set to possibly vote in... France could possibly vote in the Eurosceptic National Front, and if they do, they would pull out.
deb_2015_04_30.QUESTIONER_43: With rising tension in Russia and the Middle East, will you support the need for the Trident nuclear deterrent?

deb_2017_05_18.LUCAS: Thank you. Tonight, I'm going to be straight with you. I'm going to say what I really believe, not just what I think you might want to hear. Never in my lifetime has our future felt so uncertain. Brexit, climate change and NHS in crisis. But when people come together and reach for a bigger future, we've shown we can change the course of history. With courage, we emerged from the rubble of the Second World War. With vision, we forged a continent that's lived at peace for the past 70 years. And with ambition, we built a free and universal NHS. And we did this not in the good times, but in our darkest moments. And together, we can make big things happen again. We can end the scandal of four million children living in poverty in one of the richest countries of the world. We can transform our energy system to make it the cleanest and greenest. And we can forge a relationship with Europe which is open and says no to racism. Well, Daryl, I agree with you that we need a safe and beneficial future, but I absolutely don't think we're going to get it through the kind of extreme Brexit that Theresa May is pursuing. I think we need to be really clear that she has no mandate for this kind of Brexit, when we're out of the single market, out of the customs union, putting at risk key social and environmental protections. You know, we were clear that that was not on the ballot paper. The vote was to leave, but leave can look very different. There was no clarity as to what leave actually meant. And that's why the Green Party believes that you, the general public, should have the final say on that final package. Now, if you like what you see, then that's great. Go for it. But we believe that you should have the right to be able to look at the small print and decide if you don't like it, then you should have the right to be able to remain inside the EU. You know, I am passionate about freedom of movement. I'm passionate about staying as close to our European neighbours as possible. And I feel very let down by Labour, which has not only not turned up tonight, but basically left the field empty too when it came to opposition to this kind of extreme Brexit in Parliament. Caroline Lucas, thank you. Paul Nuttall. And Lucas, you wanted to come in on that. I did, because I think I want to say to Paul that the best way to secure the rights of UK nationals in EU countries is precisely unilaterally now to guarantee the rights of EU nationals who have come to this country, who have made their lives here in good faith. And let me just say that I am glad there is one party here tonight who will stand up for free movement. You know, it is a wonderful thing that we can live and learn and travel and be in 27 other member states. I want to fight for that right to stay for my kids and their kids, too. Well, I have sympathy with the point about Labour, very sadly, because it is the case that not only did Labour give the Tories a blank cheque for a hard Brexit, they basically gave them a lift to the bank and helped them cash it in. And that is a real shame, because if we could have had, you know, work between us as opposition parties, not with UKIP, but the others, we could have stood up against not with UKIP. What we could have done was been seriously to have opposed this catastrophic Brexit, frankly, which is going to be out of the single market, ending freedom of movement. But I want to ask Paul in particular, why is he so afraid of a referendum on the final deal? If he's so sure that we're going to get a good deal, why would he be afraid of putting that back to the people? Because if you buy something from the shops, you can look at the small print and you can decide if you don't like it, you can take it back. I think we've got every reason to do that, not least because we have had lies after lies from these campaigners. Well, I want to say to Paul Nuttall that immigrants who come to this country are making a huge contribution, both to our economy, to our communities, to our societies. And the idea that the problem that Paul sees with everything is always immigrants, I think is absolutely outrageous. You know, if you are trying to see a doctor, it's more likely that you will be treated by someone from a EU country, then you'll find them in front of you in the queue, trying to get through to the NHS. So those people in our countries are our lovers, they're our families, they're our plumbers, they're doctors, they make a vital contribution to our society. And the idea that we're going to be a better-off society by closing the door to immigrants I think is just clearly wrong. And it's clear that that's the case for the economy too, because right now you've got plenty of immigration coming from outside of the EU, The government's not stopping that because it recognises it's so crucial for our economy. Well, first of all, Shirley, I want to thank you for all of the work that you've done in the NHS, and I want to say first and foremost that I think we should be properly rewarding all of those amazing people who work in our NHS. They have had pay freezes, they've had an incredibly difficult time recently, and I just want to thank them from the bottom of my heart for all they do in the NHS. I think it's clear that our health service is in crisis, that waiting times are up, people are being treated in corridors, hospitals are closing and it simply can't be right that big business is making money out of that mess. So as well as putting more finance into the NHS, the Green Party is really clear that we would take the private sector out of the NHS. We think it's quite wrong that we have this costly contracting-out process, which is hugely administratively burdensome, hugely costly. We'd give an emergency kiss of life to the NHS using funds that would otherwise be used for our redundant nuclear weapons programme. We think it's a no-brainer to use those funds to look after people rather than to create more weapons of mass destruction. Thank you. Tim Farron. Well, I explained that part of our funding would come from, as I say, our nuclear weapons programme. If you were to get rid of that, you would have £110 billion minimum over the next 30 years. You could certainly be putting £3 billion and more every year into the NHS. And on top of that, clearly, we would also say more progressive taxation. We're very clear, if we want good, world-class public services, we have to be prepared to pay for them. When we put far less into our public services, into our NHS, by proportion of our GDP than many other countries. But I want to say one thing to Tim, if I might, because I'm very glad that he's so proud of the NHS, and rightly so, but I do find it puzzling that his party did support the increasing role of the private sector in the NHS by supporting the Health and Social Care Act when they were in coalition government. And I do wonder if, in his manifesto, he might re-look at that, because it seems to me that that role of the private sector that fact that you can have 49% of hospitals now, 49% of their budget being used on the private sector is deeply damaging. Well, I want to say to him that actually the health professionals themselves are with me on this one, because I piloted through Parliament the health reinstatement bill. We were trying to get the NHS reinstatement bill, which would precisely have rolled back the role of private sector in the NHS. Huge amount of support for that in the NHS itself by the NHS workers. So to call that politicking, I think, is wrong. But just my point, Jim, just to say that it is actually the NHS staff themselves who have been working with me on the NHS reinstatement bill who do say that this, over time, is the right way forward. Well, I think we can learn a lot, actually, from Scotland, where they seem to have things like free personal care, and the Green Party would like to see free social care as well, and we would pay for that by, for example, having a more progressive taxation system, things like a wealth tax. Essentially, this does come down to what kind of country we want to be. And as I've said before, we do want to be the kind of country that makes sure that we look after people all through their lives, including when they get towards the end of their lives. But can I just raise one issue that hasn't been talked about yet, which is really vital, and that is about how we actually prevent ill health in the first place. If we could take more action on things like the air pollution emergency, which is linked to 40,000 early deaths in this country, we could save our NHS a huge amount of money and, indeed, social care too. So let's put that on the agenda. And one last thing, in terms of people who aren't here, can we just say as well to Jeremy Hunt that just because you wear a badge on your suit, that doesn't make you an advocate of the NHS? Let's refocus this. Caroline Lucas. So, Alnus, the story that you've just painted is really... It strikes my heart, and I know many people who've come to my constituency surgeries telling me similar stories. I remember one woman in particular who was distraught because of the bedroom tax, and she just didn't have enough money, and all she wanted was enough money to buy some tights so she could go to a job interview. I mean, that is what we have come to in the fifth biggest economy in the world. I think that is shameful. We can afford to look after everybody in our society if we choose to do so. And do you know what? You know, I remember when I was growing up, we were all told that life was going to get a lot easier, things would be much, much less work we'd all have to do, we were all going to be enjoying ourselves more. Well, it hasn't worked out that way. Who would have predicted that today we would have four million children living in poverty in the UK? Who would have predicted 4,000 people who have to spend their lives on our streets because there's no home for them to go to? Who would have thought that nurses would be going to food banks? So we absolutely have choices here. Our choices would be a progressive taxation system where the rich is put in more, corporations pay more, and people like yourself would have a decent life worth living. Thank you. Nicola Sturgeon. Well, I just want to say it is unfortunate there isn't a single question to which the answer for Paul Nuttall isn't immigration. But on the substance of what we're talking about here... Paul Knuckle says it's going to help people like Alnus to get rid of the so-called green levers. Actually, the green levers are shifting us to much cheaper fuel sources. It is cheaper now to get your fuel from wind and from the sun than it is from fossil fuels. So the idea that that's going to help us is wrong. But let me say something about rent, because you mentioned that too. And we want to see rent controls. Rents are spiralling out of control right now. Private rent people, private landlords, are basically taking all of this money. We want to see rent controls that would stop that, which would mean that people could afford a decent rent, and they could then afford to save up for a deposit. Let me bring in... Right now, it's the opposite. Thank you. And also... Thank you, Caroline. Sorry to conclude you there. I just want to say that when we're talking about money not going so far that isn't happening by accident and it's a great shame that Theresa May isn't here to defend the government's record or to try to because she couldn't. Because what's happening here is a very deliberate policy of targeting some of the most vulnerable people. And I see them in my constituency surgeries. I see people who are desperately trying to fill in the forms to go from disability living allowance to the personal independence payment, and they are absolutely overwhelmed by the bureaucracy and they are also overwhelmed by the fact that there are deliberate targets which actually mean that some of them simply can't get the funding that they deserve. So let no one be under any illusion that this is happening by accident. This is a cruel and callous government, and the sooner it goes, the better. I'm still suffering from the shock of having found one thing I agree with Paul Nuttall about, which is that HS2 is a disaster. Thank you. But that apart, when it comes to what kind of economy we want, then it has to be a green economy. Otherwise, frankly, there won't be an economy at all. We can't simply go on growing and growing with more and more production and consumption in a way that creates more pollution that costs more money to clear it up. What we can do is to invest in the greener economy. For example, why did the government flog off the Green Investment Bank to Macquarie, this company that has a horrendous track record when it comes to things like fracking and bad environmental practice. We need things like a Green Investment Bank to be investing in the new green technologies of the future. They are hugely labour-intensive. They get people back to work. We want to see a Green New Deal. That means investing and insulating in every single home in Britain. It will get hundreds of thousands of people back to work. It will get our fuel bills down, and it will tackle our climate. targets as well and nobody so far I have to say has even mentioned the climate crisis. Thank you very much indeed Caroline Lucas. Well, I think Theresa May's attacks on education are doing real damage, both the way in which she's slashing school budgets and with her obsession with the divisive grammar school system. But coming to Alex's point, I think he's absolutely right to point to demoralisation of teachers right now, because it seems to me that schools should be about nurturing potential and inspiring a love of learning. I remember when I was at school, coming across a poem I loved so much that the teacher basically made it the subject of a whole lesson and had the freedom to do that. Right now, education, I think, can feel a bit like a production line, creating children-shaped pieces to fit the latest gaps in the jobs market. And when we do that, then I think what we do is to stifle the freedom to let children play and flourish and grow. We've replaced that with endless testing and measuring. It demoralises teachers, it adds yet more stress onto our young people. So the Green Party thinks it's time to scrap SATs, to trust teachers. Really, it's quite simple. Let kids be kids and let teachers teach. Paul, this is still an incredibly divisive way forward, and there is no educational evidence that suggests that this is good for the vast bulk of our people. Quite the opposite. To the contrary. And what you're doing, essentially, is making a decision when a child is just 11 years old. You are forgetting and ignoring huge amounts of evidence about how boys and girls develop at different stages, and how many children don't actually develop quite so fast. I have to say, relying on private tutors, which richer people can afford, poorer people can't. Let me bring in Leanne. Thank you, Caroline Lucas. Well, I don't often disagree with Leanne, but I think on this there are plenty of evidence that suggests that, you know, class sizes have an impact. Obviously, the professionalism of the teacher and the teacher assistants that they have alongside them matters too, but the Green Party certainly would try to be reducing our class sizes. We'd be putting money into schools to enable us to do that, because we think that being able to have more of that individual one-to-one process is going to be better. And I think right now, when you've got so many class sizes growing and growing, it's making it very, very hard for teachers to deal with it. Well, I couldn't agree more with that. Yes, we would absolutely lift the pay freeze. It is quite unacceptable that our public sector workers have been taken for granted for so long, essentially, and not being properly rewarded. And that's one reason why many are leaving the profession. But another reason why is because every time we have a new education secretary, they come up with their latest harebrained scheme and impose it on teachers, thinking that teachers don't have the professionalism themselves to make those decisions. So I come back to the point I made before. It's an important one. We need to trust teachers as the professionals that they are. And that has signally failed to happen both under this government and the previous one as well. So continuing exactly that theme, the Green Party absolutely would give young people that chance to look again at the deal that's under negotiation by Theresa May, because we feel that young people have been so betrayed by this government, by previous governments too. And that's the case when it comes to, for example, the loss of freedom of movement, that ability to be able to study and work in so many other countries. That is a precious thing that has just gone. And I feel that you should have had a say on that as young people. I think we would also want to do things like scrapping tuition fees. We want to cancel the burden of debt on young people. Education should be a public good, not a private commodity. We would want to restore student grants and nursing bursaries. We want to make housing more affordable. We would introduce rent controls. We want to see more affordable public transport. We want to see a safer environment. Because one of the things that really concerns me is the environment that we are leaving for you. We are trashing this environment. Last year, the hottest year on record. We need to have a safer environment for you. And finally, votes at 16. Votes at 16 would make sure that you would have your voice over what kind of future you have. Well, what we would be doing is introducing 100,000 new council homes every year. We want to see half a million over the course of the next Parliament. And you'd do that quite simply by enabling a deposit to be put down. You'd put that £80,000 down for each house. The government would do that. you'd then allow councils to borrow, because right now, borrowing for councils hasn't been allowed, even though they've got that housing stock there to borrow against. And were you to do that, well, that would mean you would be able to build these houses cheaply, you'd get the money back in the rent that people would be paying. At the moment, that rent is going to private landlords. In other words, £8 billion and more is going to private landlords every year. Thank you. Just to finish on that, if you were to build those council houses, you'd find that that would actually pay for itself. in a number of years. Why are you cancelling it in your manifesto? Well, I just think it's a great shame that governments and others are basically trying to pit different groups of people against each other. I want to see a good life for people who are older, and that's why we would make sure that they had a proper citizen's pension that was a decent, good pension for them to live on. But at the same time, we need to make sure that our young people are properly set up for life too. And to say that we have to, and again, I come back to the fact that we are the fifth largest economy, to say that we have to make choices between whether it's our older people we look after or whether it's our younger people, I think is completely wrong. We should be doing both. Caroline Lucas. Well, I agree on that last point, a second one, which is a bit scary. But I do have real concerns about the policy that the Conservatives have just announced today. And there are real concerns that if you have dementia, for example, if you might be unlucky enough to have those big costs, then there's nothing really going to protect you from that. Instead of having a system which could have pooled risk, instead that risk is being put onto the individual. On the 8th of June, I'm asking you to vote green, not because this election is about me or indeed about any of the party leaders here tonight. I'm asking you to vote green because this election is about you. It's about our country. It's about what kind of future we want for our children. And it's about standing up for our values of openness, cooperation, and compassion. And we face challenges that we can't possibly pretend to fix in the next hundred days or even the next thousand. We face challenges to our climate, and I'm sorry we haven't had more time to discuss that tonight. We face challenges to our economic future and our security. But we face opportunities, too, to be innovators and pioneers. So as you're watching this at home, I ask you this. Don't just think about who you'd vote for tonight. Think about who you want standing up with you and working alongside you. Together we can build a bigger, more confident and caring future. I'm ready and I hope you'll join me by voting Green on June the 8th. Thank you. Tim Farron.
deb_2017_05_18.WOOD: Although everyone knows that Plaid Cymru is all about Wales, our vision and our values can benefit people in all parts of the UK. Our country faces some real challenges, so we must grasp any opportunities that come our way to give ourselves a voice. Plaid Cymru's positive post-Brexit plan is an alternative to Theresa May's way. And I have a message for the Prime Minister, who I'm sure is watching tonight. You may be too scared to come here tonight for your U-turns to be highlighted, for your cruel policies to be exposed. You want this election to only be about Brexit because that means you avoid talking about the real issues like the NHS, the economy and the cuts you've made to our public services. That's weak leadership. weak and unstable. And I hope all of us here will show you tonight that real leadership means being willing to defend what you stand for, not hide from it. Leanne Wood, thank you. It's difficult to see how it can be better than pre-Brexit. If I held that view, then I would have campaigned to leave, and I was a Remain campaigner. That said, I accept the result, and my party said on the day that the result was announced that we accept it. We've moved on. Without Plaid Cymru defending Wales' interests, we're at risk of trade deals which would see our health service privatised, our agriculture sector decimated by cheap foreign imports and Wales drained of any power. It's only by electing Plaid Cymru MPs will Wales' voice be heard. We've been pretty much ignored since the referendum. Gibraltar has had more attention than Wales has had. So it's vital that we have a strong team of Plaid Cymru MPs to advocate Wales' national interests and to make sure that the Tories don't get away with an extreme Brexit that would cause serious harm for many people in many of our communities right throughout the UK. Leanne Wood, thank you. Nicola Sturgeon. Let's bring in Leanne Wood and then we'll go to Paul Nuttall. We are going to leave the European Union. The question for us now is how we leave the European Union. And Plaid Cymru has said right from the very beginning, the question of jobs and the economy has to be at the centre of all the decision making. We have 200,000 jobs reliant on tariff-free access with a single market in Wales. Those jobs are at risk. if Theresa May walks away without a deal. There are £680 million that come from the EU every year because Wales is one of the poorest countries in the European Union. We've had no guarantees that that money will be available for us beyond 2020. That would mean the end of the Welsh farming industry and the end of many people's jobs. But it's not just about defending and protecting what we've got. We also have to have a post-Brexit plan which creates the conditions for us to be able to develop our economy and create even more better paid jobs. You're saying people voted for 10% tariffs on cars? Paul, Paul, Paul. Leanne Wood first, and then I'll come to Tim Farron. Do you really think people voted to lose their jobs? There are 200,000 jobs in Wales that are reliant upon tariff-free access to the single market. We have an Airbus factory in Broughton in Deeside. They employ 6,500 people. If tariffs are introduced, they will have to pay tariffs on the parts that they import into Wales, and then they'll have to pay tariffs on the wings leaving Wales, because the entire aircraft is built in Toulouse in France. Do you think they're going to stay there? No, of course they're not going to stay there. And that 6,500 well-paid jobs in Wales that you were prepared to just lose down the Swanee. Thank you, Mr. Nutter. But Australia's got much bigger immigration levels than we've got here. We have to prioritise health and social care. There's no doubt that there's a growing population and the system that we've got in place is not able to deal with it, especially into the future. It makes sense for social care services and health services to be operated together. I think that often people can be pushed from pillar to post and given excuses as to why they're not receiving the services that they should be receiving because people in two separate organisations are not taking responsibility. It makes sense for us to properly invest and support social care services because if we don't then beds end up getting blocked in the hospital system. But how we pay for it is the key question. So the wealthiest in society should stump up a bit more so that we can afford to pay for decent health and social care services. Leanne Wood, thank you. It is about priorities, and isn't it interesting how there are always unlimited billions of pounds to spend on foreign wars. Now Plaid Cymru's got a costed plan to increase the number of doctors by 1,000 in the Welsh NHS and 5,000 extra nurses. That money is available within the Assembly budget but the problem with that plan now is that if there are to be restrictions on freedom of movement it's going to be very difficult to attract those doctors. Without doctors and without nurses we have no NHS. Let's turn to Paul Nuttall. And there'd be more refugees then, yeah? You said you wanted to reduce it to nothing. Well, as I've said, health and social care need to be integrated and working together. The social care side of things has been disadvantaged compared to health. Money has gone into health in most areas, whereas local authorities have seen their budgets cut quite severely, and of course social care is provided through local authority budgets. So stop the squabbling in terms of the budgets, make those budgets and departments all come in under one roof and then you can put the patients at the heart of the system instead of the bureaucracy. Thank you. Can I make this point about how social carers are paid? Because so many are on zero-hours contracts and that is a real scourge in our society at the moment. Thank you very much. And I want to make a further point because Labour have claimed that they will get rid of zero-hour contracts. Seven times Plaid Cymru have given Labour in the National Assembly for Wales the opportunity to vote to abolish zero-hours contracts. Seven times they've failed to do that. So what does Labour really stand for in terms of zero-hours contracts? The so-called economic plan was meant to eliminate the deficit by five years, but now we're told we can expect it to take two decades by the Tories. The UK's debt has increased by £555 billion since the Tories took office in 2010, and the UK is now the only advanced economy in which wages have declined despite the state getting richer. So the cuts that have taken place at the bottom are putting real pressure on people but we haven't seen those same cuts to wages at the top of the scale. So that inequality has to be addressed and one way to do that is through introducing a real living wage but we also have to have a proper job creation plan, a proper industrial strategy that enables people to have well-paid work. Because another scandal that's taking place at the moment is in-work poverty. And that rise that you see in the numbers of people going to food banks, so many of those people are in work, not just in work, but working for the public sector. Teachers, nurses, doctors, absolutely outrageous, and it shouldn't be the case in 2017. Thank you very much, Lenwood. Well there is the question of wages and benefit freezes, but also we need to think about how costs can be reduced for people. So the point about rent is a fair one. There's also costs like council tax, VAT on goods could be reduced to make some things easier for people potentially. So we just need to be thinking about how we A, reduce people's costs but also increase people's income and the real living wage, not the joking living wage that the Tories have introduced previously, the real living wage is the way to do that. The industrial strategy is key, and as we leave the European Union, those jobs that we currently have, and some of them are not as well paid as they should be, are at risk. And there are great opportunities in the fields of renewable energy and providing those services that people need. We've lost actually lots of public services and I'm very cherished public servants and a proper industrial strategy could make sure that we take people's full potential and those people can be brought back and make a contribution to our society. In America? With America, with Donald Trump, and have almond-filled beef and chlorinated chicken. Why not? That would be fantastic for the American people. Why not, Sam? Why not, Sam? Education today is the economy of tomorrow and skills, high levels of skills are vital to our economic performance. Applied Cymru will give every child a chance to reach its full potential and we will give everybody an opportunity for a life of education so that they can get to the best place that they can possibly be. I want to bring in Leanne Wood on this. Selection doesn't work. Selection doesn't work for the vast majority of people because then you have winners and you have losers. I believe in a public education system. In Wales, we're not going to have grammar schools, and as long as I draw breath, that will be the case. And the question I have here in England is, who is making the money out of all these free schools? Because somebody somewhere is making a big profit out of this. I'm not aware that there's any evidence to show that smaller class sizes actually deliver better educational outcomes. We have a crisis in teaching at the moment, though. So many people are leaving the profession because of the pressures in the job, and it's not all to do with the size of the classroom. It's more to do with the bureaucracy and the additional tasks they're expected to do. Where is the plan for recruiting more teachers? I believe in the principle of free education, and it's a real shame that there's no representative here from Labour tonight, because one of their proposals is to abolish tuition fees. Thank you. Tim Farron. Older people, on the whole, vote much more in greater numbers than younger people do. If younger people voted, if they... I would advise everybody to look at all the political parties, look at our values, look at our vision, look at our principles, get involved and use your vote. Yes, there should be votes at 16, but that 18 to 25-year-old age group are the group that vote in fewest numbers. And if they increased their voting habits, then I think policy would change to follow that as well. The next few years are critical for all parts of the UK as the Tories win a landslide with UKIP backing. Tonight you've listened to Plaid Cymru's ideas, our values and our principles. And our values are relevant to you wherever you are, but you've also heard Wales be put on the agenda. Plaid Cymru MPs can be trusted to advocate a good deal for our country as we leave the European Union. Neither Theresa May nor the Labour Party will have Wales as their priority. Plaid Cymru will always champion jobs and the Welsh economy. You can rely on Plaid Cymru to defend Wales but also to promote Wales. A vote for Plaid Cymru in this election is a vote to make Wales matter.
deb_2017_05_18.STURGEON: This election really matters for all of us across the UK. For Scotland, now more than ever, we need strong voices at Westminster. We need MPs able to stand up for Scotland and work with others for fair policies across the UK. The SNP will always stand up against Tory cuts because they are damaging our public services and pushing more people into poverty. We will stand up for jobs and against an extreme Brexit that will put living standards and jobs at risk. And we will make sure that Scotland's future is always in Scotland's hands. The next few years will determine the kind of country we become. We need strong opposition, holding a Tory government to account, keeping them in check and standing up for the values we hold dear. Values of social justice, tolerance and community. The more seats the SNP wins on June the 8th, the stronger Scotland's voice will be. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you. this time last year I campaigned passionately for the UK and Scotland to stay within the European Union and I still believe that for all its faults we are better off in the European Union than outside of it and I believe not now but when the time is right at the end of this process Scotland should have a choice about our own future but the priority now is is the Brexit negotiations. Because Theresa May is not just pursuing Brexit, she is pursuing a hard, extreme Brexit. Even today when she published her manifesto, she threatened again to walk away with no deal. That would be an economic catastrophe. That would cost in Scotland alone 80,000 jobs. So my message to people in Scotland, whether you voted remain or to leave, is to vote SNP to strengthen our hand to get a deal that does not sacrifice Scottish jobs and the economy. Because that kind of deal is not just in the interests of Scotland, but in the interests of all of the UK. Well, I think the real danger here is we've gone from a vote for Brexit across the UK, Scotland voted to remain in the European Union, but we've gone from Brexit to this extreme form of Brexit where we are running the real risk of jettisoning many of the benefits that we get from participating in the European Union on an equal basis with other partners. Now, you know, I was in St Andrews University last week, one of the best universities in the world, and they are worried about losing access to research funding, they're worried about losing some of the best academic staff in the world, they're worried about losing students. And those concerns are replicated across so many sectors of our economy. And why are we running the risk of a hard, extreme Brexit? It is around control of immigration. And Theresa may not have had the guts to be here tonight, but her spokesperson in the form of Paul Nuttall certainly appears to be here in her place. And I know immigration is a difficult topic for politicians. People have understandable concerns. But when we talk about European migrants, We're talking about people who work in our National Health Service, we're talking about people who serve us in our restaurants, we're talking about people who make a contribution and it is outrageous that their futures have not yet been guaranteed. 350 million pounds for the NHS. Where's the three and a half million pounds? Nicola Sturgeon, then I'll allow Paul Nuttall to respond to that. Paul Nuttall said people knew what they were voting for. Many people voted for that promise, or should I say lie across the side of the bus, 350 million pounds a week extra for the National Health Service. But I think there was an opportunity missed. after the Brexit vote to try to find compromise, actually to bring people together. The vote across the UK was narrow. Two of the countries in the UK voted to remain, two voted to leave. Trying to find compromise and consensus around the single market would have been the sensible thing to do, and it would have put us in a position of being able to protect jobs, protect businesses, protect the funding that many people benefit from. And I would say this about Theresa May. She called this election, in her own words, to strengthen her hand. She wants to crush opposition so she can strike whatever deal she wants or walk away with no deal. That would be disastrous. So if you don't want Theresa May to have a free hand for a damaging Brexit, you need to make sure there is strong opposition to Theresa May after this election. I am acutely aware that immigration is a really difficult topic for politicians because people do have concerns, and we can't ignore those concerns. But I also believe that politicians have a real duty to be honest with people as well. And the hard reality is our economy needs migration from the European Union. I mean, in Scotland alone, EU migrants contribute £7 billion every year. They are more likely to be in work, they are less likely to be claiming benefits, they are more likely to be university educated. than the population as a whole. One of our biggest challenges in Scotland is growing our working-age population. If we end EU migration, our working population, over the next 25 years, will fall, while our pensioner-age population will be going up by 50%. One last point, Julie. I need to go to Tim Farron. Public services are under pressure, but that's not the fault of migrants. That is the fault of the austerity agenda pursued by the Tories... Thank you. Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon, Tim Farron. My thanks to you as well, Shirley, for all your service to the NHS. The NHS is undoubtedly our most cherished public service. We all love it. We all want it to be there for us when we need it. In Scotland, since my party took office 10 years ago, we've increased the budget by 40%. The number of staff in the NHS is up by 10%. And we've also, unlike other parts of the UK, taken the step of integrating health and social care. So today, our accident and emergency departments are under pressure, but they're the best performing anywhere in the UK. And we abolished car parking charges in 2008. Health services around the world face real pressure, and Scotland's is no different. So we have a commitment that by the end of this Scottish Parliament, the budget will be £2 billion higher than it is today, more than inflation. Money not just for hospitals, but for primary care, mental health services. and community services as well. We won't open it up to private competition, but the most important thing we need after this election is voices arguing against more needless Tory cuts and austerity, because that is putting our NHS at risk. Perhaps what Caroline was trying to do was not so much restructuring, Tim, as renationalising the health service in England, because it should be in public hands. That is where our National Health Service belongs. On the issue of funding, I don't want to see our NHS in Scotland broken up and privatised in any way. One of the first things I did when I was Scottish Health Secretary was bring a hospital that had been contracted out to the private sector back into the NHS. family. The question was about financing. We have a costy plan in Scotland to increase the budget by 2 billion by the end of this Parliament. Health spending per head in Scotland is 7% higher than it is in England and if the other parties were to match that, that would be a significant increase for health elsewhere in the UK. But on this question of tax and austerity, be under no illusion, the Tories are going further than they need to on austerity. If we had a more sensible fiscal plan that still got the deficit and debt down, that would free up more money to invest in our health service. And if the Tories weren't giving a tax cut to the top 10% of earners and planning to cut corporation tax even further, that would be even more money. Paul is talking about taking resources from some of the poorest people in the world. And we contribute through foreign aid, not just to help the poorest people in the world, which I think, as it happens, is the right thing to do, but also to make this country safer. Now, Paul says that's where he wants to get the money. The question he still hasn't answered, if he's going to stop EU migration, where's he getting the staff for our national health services? It's simple. I think actually the best thing we can do for the NHS is to build up social care, because it helps to take the pressure off the NHS. Now, I'm not saying we've got everything right in Scotland. We face the same massive challenges that other countries do. But as I said earlier, we have already integrated health and social care. difficult process, but the right one to do. We're also now doing the equally different thing of trying to take money from the acute NHS and put it into social care, recognising that if we've got more investment in GPs and community services, then that will help to keep people out of hospital. And just on Leanne's point, we also pay the living wage to people who work in social care as well, to value them for what they do. I think this is, and certainly should be, one of the biggest issues in this election. Experts say inequality is now rising at a faster rate than any time since the days of Margaret Thatcher. I was reading a report yesterday that says by 2021 the incomes of the poorest third in society will have fallen poverty. That will take the total to 5 million. The equivalent of the population of Scotland across the UK of children living in poverty. It is obscene. So what do we do about it? Well, Tim is right, we need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. We need to do something about it. make sure we don't damage the economy by coming out of the single market. I would also want to see the minimum wage over the next Parliament start to rise in line with the living wage so it becomes a real living wage. And I tell you what else I would do, I would end the freeze on working age benefits because social security cuts are penalising the poorest in our society, many of them working. Let's bring some decency back to the social security system of this country. Well, money's not going as far because inflation is rising. Inflation is rising partly because the value of the pound has plummeted. The value of the pound has plummeted because of Brexit. So these are the hard realities we've got to face. But we do have to seriously debate how we help people. Interestingly, when Theresa May published her manifesto today, she didn't give any commitment not to increase VAT or national insurance. And I think that is regrettable. So I've already spoken about the real living wage. As First Minister of Scotland, I know there's a real issue about the sustainability or unsustainability of the public sector pay cap. We must address wages in the public sector as well. But, you know, this issue about social security is a really important one. One of the things that I think is quite despicable about what the Tories and some others have done in this country is treat everybody who is reliant on social security as somehow being scroungers. actually so many of the people who rely to some extent on state benefits are working and working hard and Government policy is taking in some cases hundreds and hundreds of pounds of support away for them. Thank you. Nicholas Sturgeon. the economy means doing more to support business. In Scotland, we are taking 100,000 small businesses out of business rates to try to give them more money to employ people. In this election, we will be arguing for exemptions from national insurance for companies who take on more staff. I don't support a headline-cutting corporation tax, but I think things like the investment allowance should be increased to encourage companies to invest in plant and machinery, because improving productivity is a key objective as well. But, you know, supporting some of the key sectors, certainly of the Scottish economy, is partly about making sure we don't pull the rug out from under them. And so many of these key sectors, from food and drink, from our energy sector, will really be hurt by coming out of the single market. And that underlines the point, if we don't want Theresa May to have a free hand to do whatever she wants, regardless of the cost for ordinary people, we need voices in the House of Commons standing up for these kinds of interests. Caroline Lucas, you wanted to come in there. Thank you. Nicola Sturgeon. I mean, nothing is more important to me than education. And as First Minister of Scotland, I've got a big job of work to do to make sure we are really improving education in Scotland. We've got a good education system, but we've also got some challenges that we are working really hard to address. Before I come on to school education, early years is really important. Giving younger children the best start through quality early years in childcare is something we're prioritising. So we're going to double the provision of that over the life of this Parliament. In schools, we're trying to reduce bureaucracy that teachers are working under, because they've told us rightly it is too heavy. So we're trying to take that out. We've got a recruitment challenge in teaching, like many other countries do. We're trying to encourage more teachers into education. But we're also doing something that is new for Scotland. We're giving resources directly to headteachers, so not to councils and then to headteachers, but directly to headteachers and freeing them up to decide how that money is spent, because teachers know best how to improve education in our schools. Schools are organised differently in Scotland than they are in England, so some of these points don't necessarily apply. We've got a challenge in Scotland. We've introduced a new curriculum. It's been the right thing to do. It's been praised by the OECD. But we know we've got work to do to focus on literacy and numeracy, really basic skills, and we're very, very focused on doing that. We also want to free up teachers much more. You know, teachers are best placed to know what works in our schools, but they do tell us they have had too much bureaucracy. So it's about taking that away and putting more resources into the hands of teachers. But it's not just about school. As I said earlier, it's about early years, but it's also then about making sure that there are choices for people leaving school. I want people to have equal opportunity to go to university, but if that's not for them, I want them to have other opportunities too. We want class sizes to be as small as possible, and that's certainly, I think, important. But it's also about focusing on what happens within the classroom and making sure that we have the best teachers freed up to do the best job with the best resources. absolutely what we are trying to focus on just now. I thought, I mean, we've talked a lot about choices tonight, and I'm not an expert on the English education system, so I won't pretend to be, but I thought, I was quite aghast this morning when I heard Theresa May say that she was going to increase funding for schools, almost literally by taking the food out of children's mouths, by abolishing... free school meals. And I don't think you improve education by taking free lunches away from our youngest children. And I'll be proud to protect free school meals in Scotland. Well, I do think it is probably the single most important thing we can do to improve attainment and literally give young people the best start in life. The government I lead have made this a priority over the past few years. We've extended free early years education from 12.5 hours a week to 16 hours a week, and we're doubling that to 30 hours over the course of this Parliament. And unlike in England, that's for every three- and four-year-old and for every vulnerable two-year-old. But with childcare, it's not just quantity that matters, it's quality that matters, so we really need to make sure... Thank you. ..that we've got the quality... OK. ..so that we're genuinely giving people the best start. Thank you. Paul Nuttall, what would UKIP do to support... Well, I think it's a really, really important question. We're already seeking to do many things for the younger generation, not charging them to go to university. For example, make sure their education can be based on the ability to learn. Housing is critical. Across the UK, we need to build more houses. We're working just now to a target of 50,000 affordable houses over the next few years. We need to make sure there are that we need to do to make sure young people have the best opportunities and the best start in life. And we shouldn't take away vital support like housing benefit support from under-20s and under-30s. We need to make sure that young people have the best opportunities and the best start in life. And we shouldn't take away vital support like housing benefit support from under-20s and under-30s. But the other point I would make here is this. I don't think we should be setting young people against older people. At a time when we see pensioner poverty rising again, we shouldn't be removing pension protection. We should end austerity and make sure we can provide these opportunities for people regardless of their age across the country. In Scotland, of course, we have abolished the right to buy, a controversial thing to do at the time, but it helps to ensure that as we invest in building social housing, we're not then losing it later on, and we help people into home ownership in other ways. There's no doubt house building is key to getting the cost of housing down, and we need to build homes that are affordable for people. I was listening to Jeremy Corbyn talk at its manifesto launch the other day with lots of policies that the SNP is already implementing in Scotland, but one of them was restoring council house building. We've done that as well. Now, we've got challenges. I'm not saying we've got everything right, but you have to be prepared to build the houses and you have to be prepared to protect them for the use of the public in the future. I couldn't agree more with Caroline on that. We should not get into... the situation where we are choosing between supporting young people and supporting our pensioners. And coming back to this issue of university tuition, I agree university is not for everybody, and I agree that free tuition in and of itself is not enough to give people access to university, but it is essential. And I take a really simple view. I benefited from a free education as a working-class young person. That's why I can stand here today. I have no right to take that away from another young person. Tonight, I hope you have seen and heard why a strong SNP voice at Westminster matters, and why there needs to be strong and effective opposition to a Tory government that is increasingly in hock to UKIP. We have seen, sadly, that Labour simply isn't strong enough to hold the Tories to account. the SNP can provide strong opposition. My message to those of you watching tonight in England, Wales, and Northern Ireland is that SNP MPs will always work for jobs, fairness, and progressive values. And to those at home in Scotland, my message is this. Now more than ever, as we face big challenges, it is vital that we have strong SNP voices at Westminster. It is only with a strong group of SNP MPs that we will have a strong enough hand to protect our public services from Tory cuts, stand against an extreme Brexit and continue to make our country the very best that it can be.
deb_2017_05_18.NUTTALL: Tonight you will see that there is only one party that is going into this election which is truly committed to the Brexit that people voted for on June the 23rd last year. And that is a Brexit whereby we are back in control of our own laws, of our own borders and of our own money. It is also a Brexit whereby we take back full control of our waters and sign free trade deals all over the world. UKIP is also the only party going into this election with a clear and honest commitment to reducing immigration through a policy of balanced migration. You will also see that we are the only party that is committed to cutting a foreign aid budget that is costing you, the British taxpayer, around £30 million every single day. And we want to reinvest that money into our national health service. And it should be a national health service, not an international health service. Finally, Brexit would never have happened if it wasn't for UKIP. We have taken you so far. Elect UKIP MPs and give us the tools to see this through. Yes. Of course, the deal will be better because it couldn't be much worse than the deal that we've had whilst we've been within the European Union. And I encourage the Prime Minister and David Davis, when you go into these negotiations, be bold, have courage. Promote how great our country actually is and accept that in many ways the European Union needs us far more than we need it. We have a huge trade deficit with the EU. There are 6 million jobs on the continent which are dependent on British trade. And I want to say to the Prime Minister, be bold on immigration, because when people voted to leave the European Union, they didn't just vote to control our borders, they voted to control immigration. Say no to any divorce bill that is put on the table, because since we've been members of this club, we've paid in over £180 billion in membership fee alone. The deal will be better, but only if Theresa May and David Davis have backbone. Paul Nuttall, thank you. I tend to agree with you, Nicola. I do want to see the issue as to whether EU migrants can stay in this country decided and sorted out pretty quickly, just as I want it sorted out for British people who live abroad. However, you're saying I'm the spokesman for Theresa May. I do not have I don't believe that she'll get the best deal possible for Britain. I think she'll begin to backslide. I think she'll backslide on fisheries. I think she'll sell out our fishermen once again like a former Tory prime minister once did in Ted Heath. I think there'll be some sort of dodgy deal over freedom of movement as well. And I think she'll capitulate and we'll pay a divorce bill. The only way you'll get the real Brexit that people want and the real Brexit that people voted for on June the 23rd is by electing UKIP MPs to the House of Commons on June the 8th. Yeah, I mean, we have to leave the single market and it was quite obvious to everyone who voted on June the 23rd that leaving the European Union meant leaving the single market. David Cameron said it, George Osborne said it, Nigel Farage said it, Michael Gove said it, everyone... It says tariffs, it's not just tariffs. Hang on, hang on, hang on, it was also... It was also on the leaflet that the government sent out to every single household in this country which cost the British taxpayer £9 million. To suggest that people didn't know what they were voting for is downright duplicity. People voted to leave the single market. It was clear on the day that leaving the European Union meant leaving the single market. It wasn't. It's all very European Union, isn't it? If you don't get the answer you want in the referendum, you have another one to get the answer that you do want. They did it in Ireland, they did it in France, they did it in Holland. It's what they do. The bottom line is this, the people have voted, they voted to leave the EU, they voted to leave the single market, and Tim can cry about it all he wants. You know, that's just a fact. That's democracy, Tim. You know, but the bottom line here is the single market isn't the be-all and end-all. If we're members of the single market, we can't sign our own trade deals. And there's a big world out there. There's the Anglosphere. There's the Commonwealth, which has over 2 billion people in it. This is where our future lies, not tied within the sclerotic EU bloc. Do you really think people vote to lose jobs? Natalie, we have a huge trading deficit with the European Union. OK? Hang on. We have a huge trading deficit with the European Union. There will be a free trade deal on the table. Split between 27 countries. Because money talks, OK? And it's in their interest. It might be a dodgy organisation, a bullying organisation. stupid organisation and it wouldn't put six million jobs at risk. Yeah, I mean, look, the problem that you've got with immigration at the moment is that there are simply too many people coming to the country. Last year alone, it was a city the size of Newcastle upon Tyne net, OK? We're adding a Birmingham. every three years. It's unsustainable. Even Theresa May said last year in the House of Commons that the immigration level that we have at the moment is economically neutral. Trade unions have said it's led to wage stagnation in working class communities. The Bank of England have admitted that that is the case too. The problem that we have is, quite simply, we're letting too many people come. The only way you solve it is by having an Australian points-based system whereby we have the right to say who comes and who doesn't come. Let's go to Nicola Sturgeon on that. Paul Nutter. Of course, we are a growing population, and as we spoke about earlier, immigration plays a large part on that. And if we continue on the road that we're on at the moment, the population will be 80 million by the middle of this century. It's unsustainable. It puts pressure on the NHS. It puts pressure, of course, on social care. What the NHS needs is more money. And my party is committed to putting six billion extra every single year into the National Health Service. This will fund 20,000 new nurses, 10,000 new GPs and allow us to scrap hospital car parking charges which are nothing more than a tax on the sick. We also will put 1.4 billion a year into social care. And how would we pay for this? Well, we would take that money directly from a foreign aid budget, which is costing us around 13 billion pounds every single year. We would cut that back to 0.2% of GDP, which is exactly the same as it was in the United States under the Barack Obama. Paul Nuttall, thank you. Carolyn Lucas. I'm opposed to privatisation. You know I didn't vote for the bill. Yeah, I mean, I highlighted at the beginning exactly where we would take the money. We would directly transfer that money from the foreign aid budget, which is costing us £30 million every single day. Hang on, hang on. Actually, if we reduce foreign aid to 0.2%, as I said earlier, it'll be the same as the United States under Obama. The point is... No, it's stopping refugees coming in. This is about the law hanging fruit. There's no need to put up taxation to pay for this. We can simply take it from the foreign aid budget and put British people first. Charity begins at home. It's simple. OK? No one is talking about this. Stocking EU migration as a whole. We want a points-based system. If there's a gap in our economy... You said you're immigration sector. No! Hang on, let me finish. Net. Net. OK? One in, one out. A points-based system. If there's gaps... He's a human being. Let's refocus us onto the issue of social care, Paul Nuttall. Yeah, I mean, social care has often been the poor relation, hasn't it, to the NHS? I think that Natalie's absolutely right. What we need to do... Will you call me Natalie? I mean, Leanne, sorry. My fault. Sorry. Accident. Women's names. I've said it twice now. Have I? Oh, I'm sorry about that. Yeah, what we need to do is we need to fully integrate. health care and social care because at the moment the left hand very often doesn't know what the right hand is doing. So you end up in a situation back in January where you know people are stuck in hospital because they literally have nowhere else to go. We as a party will put £1.4 billion extra per year into social care to ensure that the people who've put in most, the elderly, get what they deserve when they're older. What we want to do in UKIP is we want to put more money in people's pockets. We know that there's been a stagnation of living standards in recent years and how we will put money in people's pockets is firstly we would scrap VAT on domestic fuel bills. We would also scrap the green levies, which will save the average household £170 every single year. We'd increase the personal allowance, so you would have to be earning over £13,000 before you paid any tax. That would put more money in your pocket. UKIP will also oppose any rise in national insurance, because we believe as a party that people know how to spend their own money better than any government does on their behalf. We're a party of a small state. We believe that people know what best to do with their own money. Yeah, on fuel bills, as I said earlier, we would take VAT off domestic fuel bills altogether and do away with green levies, which would put more money in your pocket. And one of the reasons that wages have stagnated in recent years, and have probably stagnated since about 2004, is because of the levels of immigration. We have an oversupply of labour. That's the answer to everything, isn't it? We have an oversupply of labour in this country. It is as simple as that. Now, as for Tim talking before about us falling off the cliff edge, It's simply not true. You were the same people who went round talking about project fear before the referendum. You know, let's just look. Unemployment is down. Manufacturing is up. The FTSE's at record levels. We're the fastest growing economy. We're talking about an increase in rents and people not being able to afford to pay rents. That's simply because we're not building enough houses. We're not building enough houses at the moment. You know, we have a problem. We're building 140... Hang on. We're building 140... Hang on. We're building 140... Maybe if we trained enough of our own people we wouldn't. Let me just finish. 140,000 houses per year that we're building at the moment. 80,000 of those are swallowed up by immigration. Alone! Well, firstly, what we would do is we would scrap HS2, which is nothing but a white elephant, which is only there to benefit London. That will save us £50 billion. We would then have an industrial strategy which would promote employment. But let's not forget the opportunities that Brexit will give us once we leave the European Union. We'll be able to sign trade deals all over the globe. Sorry? Why not sign a free trade deal with the biggest economy in the world? No, we're in it now. We're in the biggest economy in the world now. We can sign free trade deals with India, with China. Our economy can boom off the back of trade. That's the way we will get the economy moving. And the only reason we have these opportunities is because the people were sensible enough to vote to leave the European Union last year. I think it's an absolute scandal that 500,000 kids in this country go to primary school and are in classes with over 30 other children in them, considering that Tony Blair gave a commitment that there would be no class sizes over 30 about 14 years ago. I agree with Tim. I would do away with SATs for infants, school-age children. Also, the problem that we have is so many teachers are leaving the profession. And they're leaving the profession because they're overworked. And they're overworked under the deluge of paperwork, schemes of work, individual lesson plans. What you need to do is to strip the paperwork back, strip back the bureaucracy, and let our teachers do what they do best, which is teach our young people to the best of their ability. I believe in academic selection. Always have done. I believe that we don't have enough grammar schools in this country. We have 164 at the moment. We need more. But the problem that you have is that the majority of these grammar schools are in middle class areas. We need to get them into working class areas which then allow working class kids to break out of their communities. It is a disgrace. that there were more working-class kids in Oxford and Cambridge in the 1960s than there is now. However, what we also must do is promote vocational education, which has often been the poor relative of academia. And I would look at the German system for this, because they have something called the dual vocational training system, whereby you attend a vocational school, but at the same time you receive on-the-job training. It's why Germany has kept its manufacturing industry. Tim, you've just made my point. for me when you were talking about school meals. The simple fact is too many of the grammar schools at the moment are in middle class areas. What we need to do is we need to put grammar schools in working class areas. Yes, I think teachers, of course, do deserve a pay rise. I think there is clear evidence that smaller class sizes do benefit not just the teacher, but also the pupil, because teacher-pupil time is premium. students or pupils who are in smaller class sizes do better academically. That is simply a fact and it is a scandal. It's a scandal that 500,000 primary school kids in this country are sitting now in classes with over 30 other people in them. pre-school children in particular. Of course, we've got to give our young people the best start in life. What we need is to have a wrap around care. We need to ensure that there are breakfast clubs, for example, in schools. We need after-school clubs to ensure that the children are looked after, both before and after school. What we need in this country, and let's be utterly blunt about this, is we need to put more money into the education system. It's been lagging behind now for many, many years. If you look at where we are in our position in the world, we're falling down the league tables year after year. We have to do something about it. This is urgent, and UKIP will have a plan which we'll launch in our manifesto next week. We are committed to scrapping tuition fees. I think they are a barrier to many young people going to university, particularly from poorer backgrounds. We would reinstate student grants for those students from working-class areas. What we would also do is improve vocational training. We would create more vocational schools. As I said earlier, we would look at the German system, whereby you have on-the-job training whilst attending a vocational school. This would give many young people in our country a trade which they can go on throughout their life and apply, whether that's being an electrician or a plumber or a bricklayer, they will learn to do things with their hands. Also, we will have a house-building programme 250,000 affordable homes every year. A brownfield revolution which will allow young people to get on the housing ladder. Well, firstly, we would scrap HS2, which would save £50 billion immediately. We would also scrap the Barner formula, which would give fairness to England, and that would save an extra £5 billion. But that's taking money away from Scotland and Wales, incidentally. Well, actually... You know, in Scotland, you're getting £1,700 extra per head than the English. It's all built on English taxpayers' money. That's why you have free prescriptions. Scottish people pay taxes as well. That's why you have free tuition fees. And that's why you have no hospital guard out to charge you. We're paying for it. So the simple fact is there would also be an EU dividend when we leave the European Union. 350 billion per week. Where's your bus? Let us finish. OK. Let us finish. We will save £10 billion a year in membership fee... ..£10 billion in membership fee alone that will easily allow us to build 250,000... It's costing us £290 million a week to leave. All tuition fees do is simply preclude poorer students from going to university. They need to be taken away. We need to go back to a system of student grants. But the point is, and this is someone that nobody else on this platform will admit, that there are far too many people going to university at this present moment in time. Let me finish. Tony Blair... Tony Blair had a policy that 50% of all 18-year-olds should go to university. Last year, 46% of students left university and took a job whereby you didn't need a degree. We need to reduce the number of students going to university, then we could scrap tuition fees and go back to student grants. No, you reduce the number of courses. I mean, there are a number of non-courses in our university, right across the board at the moment, that are nothing to our economy. The question was aimed at obviously how we look after our elderly and the point is the elderly in this country at the moment get a really raw deal. What we need is a flexible state pension that allows people to take their pension younger if they choose. We also should be committed to keeping the triple lock to ensure that pensioners get a fair deal and we will fight tooth and nail the new announcement by the Tories today which basically amounts to a death tax which punishes people for simply living. Well, tonight you've heard it from the mouths of the other party leaders, or at least the ones who bothered to turn up, that they simply don't believe in Brexit. They don't believe we're big enough, We're strong enough and simply they don't believe we're good enough to be a free, democratic and independent state on the world stage. Unlike them, I do. I believe that this is a fantastic country. So if, like me, you believe immigration needs to be reduced, you believe that foreign aid needs to be cut, you believe that our National Health Service needs more investment and that our armed forces need to be bolstered, then you know what to do. If you also believe we need more Bobbies on the beat, and that our elderly need to be looked after, then please go out and vote UKIP on June 8th, because together we really can put the great back into Britain.
deb_2017_05_18.FARRON: I got into politics to fight, to stand up to those who take you for granted. I grew up in Preston in the 1980s.
