I saw what happens when decent people are taken for granted by a heartless Conservative government and I am determined to stop that happening again. The decent Britain I love is under threat. Theresa May, backed by Nigel Farage and Jeremy Corbyn, is going for an extreme Brexit that will damage our futures for generations. Don't give up. The Britain I love is not lost yet. No matter how you voted in the referendum, if you care for our children, don't cut our schools. If you care for our elderly, don't leave them on trolleys in corridors. If you want Britain to lead the world, do not turn your back on the world. A brighter future is still possible. The fight is not over. It is time for some hope. I've got four children. My youngest starts secondary school in a few months' time. And my main motivation, when they're my age, I want to look them in the eye and say I did absolutely everything I could to protect our country's future, to protect their future from the calamitous, extreme Brexit that Theresa May has chosen. She is not enacting the will of the people. She has chosen to interpret it in an extreme way. And that has been backed by Jeremy Corbyn and Nigel Farage as they trooped through the lobbies to back that extreme Brexit will damage future generations. So the deal is this. The Brexit negotiations between Brussels and London over these next few months will lead to outcomes that none of us can predict for the time being. And that means, at the end, you should have the final say on the Brexit deal. Either the politicians will sign it off, or the people. I trust the people. And if you want to reject that deal, you must have the right to remain in the European Union. So when we look at our National Health Service and we realise that a quarter of the people working in the National Health Service are from overseas, the vast chunk of which are from the European Union, we realise that when we refuse to give those folks in the EU the right to settle here with their children, not only are we doing something utterly immoral, we're doing something that is damaging our own future. And this is the problem. with Theresa May's Brexit plans backed by Jeremy Corbyn, is that she is putting together a plan which appeases the Paul Nuttalls and the Nigel Farages, but damages our children's future. That is why you should have the final say on the Brexit deal, not the politicians. But of course, freedom of movement benefits British people. We can move freely across the European Union. And people can come here too. And that leaves massively advantages to our business, to our tourism industry, to our health service. It's about understanding that talking about these issues should be done in the interest of the country, not demonising people who come from somewhere else for short-term political benefit. Nigel Farage and I'm sure your good self over the last decade or two have taught this country saying Britain should be more like Norway and Switzerland. What are Norway and Switzerland? They are out of the European Union and in the single market. Leaving the single market is a political choice and let's remember the impact of that choice. Half of the things we sell as a country go to the single market in Europe. The impact on living standards, on jobs, on our ability to create a future for our children massively depend on Britain being in the single market. For Theresa May, with Jeremy Corbyn's help, to take us out is to damage our children's future. It must be resisted. There speaks the ambassador for Theresa May, so he would know I'm sure. Look, democracy did not end on the 23rd of June last year. If you believe passionately that our children's future is better alongside our neighbours than separated from it, then you keep fighting. And it's a matter of democracy that this deal, that none of us know what the outcome's going to be, what will it mean for all of our families here tonight, you should have the final say. Because somebody will sign off that deal. It will either be the politicians or it will be the people. I trust the people. So imagine your mum has a fall, and then imagine those one in four people in our National Health Service who are from overseas aren't there anymore. And you remember that the people who are working in this country from other countries are hard-working, caring and decent, just like the rest of us. Are there pressures caused by immigration? Yes, there are. Should they be tackled by politicians providing the infrastructure? Yes, of course, they must be. But this is about what kind of a country we are. Setting false low targets, as Theresa May has done, as Paul Nuttall endorses, doesn't put Britain first. It's about appeasing the farages of this world rather than doing what is right by British business, by British health service, by British farmers, by the British economy. Well, Shirley, I'm really grateful for your service in the NHS too and I suspect, like you, my approach to the NHS is personal rather than political. I think about my mum's own journey through ovarian cancer, from the diagnosis to the treatment. to the care, to her passing away on a hospital ward just two floors away from the one where she'd given birth to me all those years previously. I think of the young woman in Kendal who came to see me last week, 19 years of age, eating disorder, told that she could not get treatment because she was not yet sick enough. Are the people who work in our NHS the best in the world? Absolutely. Is our NHS the best funded in the world? Don't kid yourself. It is time politicians were honest with the British people that there needs to be more money and that if there's going to be more money we will all have to pay for it. That is why the Liberal Democrats will put one penny on income tax to raise £30 billion over a parliament to invest in our national health and social care service so you get the treatment you deserve from cradle to grave. 95% of all private sector involvement in the NHS came under a Labour government. But let's point this out, the last thing that Britain needs, more importantly, the last thing that patients and doctors and nurses need is another restructuring. Perhaps the most radical thing any health secretary could do in this country is to let our hospitals and our health care services get on with the job and stop restructuring and instead invest the money. And that is why there is no point politicking and disagreeing on how you might do it. You've got to be honest with the British people. That is why we will say to you it will cost us all one penny in the pound, the equivalent of a cup of coffee every week for most people, to make sure we put £30 billion extra into the National Health and Social Care Service. It's time we were honest with the British people and rescued health and social care. OK, well, Caroline knows I didn't vote for the bill. OK. Your party did. But she also should know the last thing the NHS needs now is restructuring. But that's my point, Tim. Thank you both. What it needs is £6 billion a year additional money, surely. ..then people can come here and work. I mean, Paul is talking about taking money from the poorest people on planet Earth in order to fund his proposals. That stinks, because this election is about deciding what kind of country are we. And I am a patriot. I am proud to be British, and I'm proud of our values. And it shames me when we have people in this country and people in politics who are prepared to attack the poorest people on planet Earth to make a political point, but let's also address the person who isn't here and should be here. See, I have a plan to rescue the NHS by putting a penny on income tax for health and social care. Theresa May is calling this election because she assumes she has already won it. And she assumes that when you vote for her, if you do, on the 8th of June, you will be endorsing her plans to cut social care and health. My grandfather's journey through Alzheimer's, I remember it very well. He was treated well and at times not so well. I remember the stench of the first home he went into and we fought hard to get him out of there and it's a reminder that people who work in our social care service are brilliant people but most of them can earn more stacking shelves at their local supermarket. And if we care about our older people, then we care about the people who care for them. And that is why we're putting a penny on income tax for health, because that is about health and social care. It's about having a plan that William Beveridge, the great liberal, when he drew up the plans for the NHS in the 1940s, for sure, if we'd been living to the ages we are today, then he would have drawn a plan for social care too. Today, in 2017, it's time to finish. Well, Almaz, thank you for your question. And for you and for everybody else in this country, I have a long-term economic plan. It's called Stay in the Single Market. The European single market is where we sell half of our goods to. It is vastly significant to the creation of jobs, well-paid jobs, the ability for us to grow as an economy. Since June last year, let's not pretend that things have not got worse. The value of the pound has crashed. The average family is £500 a year worse off now, and we haven't even left yet. We've seen the inflation rate go up already. So I love my country. I want to do what's right by my country. And that is why, when that deal comes to you in the next 18 months, you should have the right to the final say. Because if we can fight and stay in the single market, we can protect incomes, we can protect jobs, we can keep down prices. Let's do the right thing. And that is the case for so many people. You just need to look at the prices on the shelves, you look at the prices of the supermarkets, and wages have stagnated. For me, in my own personal experience, I said earlier on, I grew up in Preston, a little further north from here, and I remember, in the years following my childhood, realising, I guess, the poverty that I had been brought up in. You only realise, on the mark of good parents, is you only realise that you were poor many years afterwards. And what I, looking back, saw about my community was the way we were taken for granted by a government in London that cared nothing for us. And what I want is a government, and I want to lead a country, I am determined to do that, that makes sure that everybody is decent to one another. And that means fair taxes. That means that everybody pays, but it also means that rich are taxed properly. And it also means having a government that does not take for granted large swathes of this country because it assumes it's going to win a landslide on the 8th of June. This is why opposition is so important in this election. If you want your hospital, your well-being, your daily, your weekly budget to be taken for granted, then a Theresa May majority of Margaret Thatcher proportions is the best way to achieve it. Given you are David Davis's representative on this programme... I want to particularly pay attention to the plight of young people in this country. The fact is housing benefit being cut for those under 25, minimum wage lower than anybody else for those who are young and this is a situation we find ourselves in 12 months since the referendum when we remember that three quarters of our young people voted to remain and it seems to me that our leader in Theresa May and Jeremy Corbyn going along with her are deciding to put together a system which allows our young people's futures to be trashed and taken for granted. That's why the answer surely has got to be to fight that we do stay in a single market, that we do meet the needs of those young people, that we give them the housing benefit they deserve. Well, Alex, as a father of four kids myself, nothing is more important than the education of our children. And it is a reminder that as this election takes place, we are just eight weeks off a time when two out of every three headteachers will be dismissing and getting rid of at least one teaching post in this country. And that is an outrage. That is what Theresa May hopes you don't realise as she seeks to do to our schools what she has already done to our hospitals. And there is nothing more important than developing skills and education and a love of learning from the earliest possible age. That is why, for example, The forced sitting of SATs for young primary school children can often put those young people off learning for life. What I want to do is have a government that does not interfere with our teachers on a daily basis but trusts our teachers to teach our children and to let them get on with the job and give them the £7 billion extra that we will give them to enable them to do so. And choices are what this government is making. It is choosing to spend money on extra free schools and extra grammar schools when it could be spending it to undo some of the £3 billion worth of cuts that are falling on our schools in the next three years. So the average secondary school will lose six teachers, the average primary school two teachers. That is the decision, the choice, Theresa May is making. And let's remember The argument for grammar schools is utterly and totally bogus. The average number of children at school in this country on free school meals is 16%. The average number of children on free school meals in grammar schools is 3%. It's 3%. There is no doubt that whilst many grammar schools do a good job, bringing them up again now is about undoing social mobility. It's about locking people into the 80% that get relegated into secondary models. That's just bad choices, though, isn't it? Because even if... Even if you were to accept, and Paul Nuttall's argument in favour of grammar schools, it's utterly bogus, all the evidence goes against that, but even if he had a shred of evidence on his side, it's a poor choice when there's £3 billion being taken out of our schools and we're wasting money on new grammar schools and new free schools. Free school meals for infant school children was something the Liberal Democrats delivered in government. And we did it for a reason. We did it because all the evidence suggests it boosts attainment. It makes sure that children, particularly from poorer backgrounds, get at least one decent meal a day. And it means that you don't have the stigma of people registering for free school meals if they do not wish to do so. But to get rid of them after the schools have put so much money and effort to build kitchens, to bring in new ways of delivering those free school meals, is an absolute hideous assault. And just to add on the issue of class sizes, it's absolutely right that smaller class sizes do make a difference, but it's also about who is in the classroom. I say the average primary school will lose two teachers, they could instead lose three or four teaching assistants, which will make it that much harder to deliver good education in primary and secondary. Well, first of all, we would lift the pay freeze on public sector pay because teachers and soldiers and nurses and other public sector workers deserve a pay rise. That freeze has gone on for too long and we would end it immediately. But I also think It is important for us to remember that, as somebody who worked for a teacher education institution in the days when I had a proper job, that I worked with people who went into education full of passion, full of desire to help children, and one in four of those teachers, after five years of being qualified in the sector, are leaving the system. What a waste of their dedication. What a waste of our investment. That's why you should pay them more and take the red tape off their back. I live in a part of the world, up in South Cumbria, where one in three young people leave and they don't come back, despite the fact that it's the most beautiful place on planet Earth, because it's so expensive. And that is not unlike many other parts of the United Kingdom. The mismatch between wages and house prices, particularly for young people, is staggering and it is getting worse. That is why we will build every year, 300,000 new homes, the bulk of which will be council homes, and we will allow councils to do it, and we will make sure that the government directly commissions house building if those developers won't do them themselves. But there's something much more going on for young people at the moment. It is bound to be said by people in my position at least, that you've got three quarters of the young people in this country went out to vote in the most important election of their lives last June, and they voted to remain in the European Union. And their dismay, and the dismay of their parents and their grandparents, of the narrowing of their opportunities and of their futures by this extreme Brexit government under Theresa May is something someone has to stand up to. I will stand up to it. I will give you the choice on the final deal. And if you want to remain, you should be able to remain. 10. Me and my mum and sister lived in a two-bedroom house in Preston. My mum's mate separated from her husband, and her and her two kids moved in with us. So six of us in a two-bedroom house. Felt like great fun at my age at the time. But I found out later on, the reason why my mum's mate couldn't get a house for her and her kids was because Margaret Thatcher had sold off those council houses that weren't any available. So what we will do is lift the borrowing cap to allow councils to borrow to build. allow housing associations new access to finance so they can borrow against the value of their stock to build and then you allow the government, you give the government the power to directly commission to buy brownfield sites and you raise the money by the sale of that land at the end. You actually make a profit whilst housing Britain's future. I'm somebody who made a promise back in 2010 that I would not vote to increase tuition fees, and I kept my promise. It's important that when you are in politics, you assume that people also assume that all politicians are scumbags and liars, so don't go and prove them right. What you should do, and what we believe passionately in, is that all access to higher education should be free at the point of use, that we should bring back grants, that we should make sure that maintenance awards... Why are you putting tuition fees in your manifesto? My advice to you, Caroline, and certainly to Jeremy Corbyn if he was here, is don't make promises. Don't make promises you can't keep. And the reason I kept my promise... Caroline, so do I. And here's the deal... The biggest issue facing young people in this country is that their future is about to be trashed by Theresa May, backed by Jeremy Corbyn, taking us out of the single market, out of the European Union, without any kind of vote of the people. Now I want people to trust me that I will give you the final say on the deal. That is the best thing to give our young people a decent future. OK, I'll just raise immigration, just for a change. Yes, it seems to me that there are, of course, individual things we need to do to help young people. We need to make sure they are able to access housing benefit, that we do build the homes that we need. But do you know what? It's more about building a future for our young people. What kind of country do we want to grow up in? I've talked about Brexit being a threat to our country and our young people. Do you know what? Climate change is a bigger threat. And if we don't tackle it by making a kind of joyous national endeavour to, for example, make ourselves energy self-sufficient in green energy, then we'll be letting our children down. We'll also be letting our children down if we turn into the kind of country that slams the door in the face of child refugees. I want to be the kind of country that welcomes those people. That's the kind of country I want my children to grow up in. And they have the choice to do so. They could have followed Dilnot. I believe there is still a decent Britain worth fighting for. The vision of Theresa May and Paul Nuttall is not the only choice. The fact that Theresa May is not here tonight tells you that she is taking you for granted. She thinks she owns this election. She owns the result. She owns your future. Your children's future. You deserve a leader who cares about the things that you care about. A leader who will stand up for you and your family, for your NHS, for your schools, for your children's future. You need a leader who will stop Theresa May's extreme version of Brexit. You don't need a difference of opinion, you need a different option. You need someone who will step up and fight for you. I am determined to offer you the chance to change Britain's future because the Britain I love is open, tolerant and united. This is a country worth fighting for. It's a country I want to lead.
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_0: We'll be taking questions from our studio audience here. But first, a one-minute opening statement from each of the leaders. And first of all, we turn to the leader of the Green Party, Caroline Lucas. Carine Lucas, thank you. Now to Leanne Wood of Plaid Cymru. We turn now to the leader of the SNP, Nicola Sturgeon. Paul Nuttall, Leader of UKIP. Paul Nuttall, thank you. And now to Tim Farron, Leader of the Liberal Democrats. Tim Farron, thank you very much. That then includes the opening statements from each of our party leaders. And the format for tonight is simple. Our audience will put their questions directly to the leaders. Each will then have one minute to answer before we open things up for a free-flowing debate on the question. And our first question tonight is from Daryl White, a 25-year-old youth worker from Newport in South Wales. Good evening to you. Daryl, thank you very much. If elected, how would your party negotiate a safe, beneficial, worthwhile deal with Europe for Britain? And in addition, will this deal be better than pre-Brexit? And for the first statement in response to that question, I'll turn to Leanne Wood. Thank you very much. And next, it's the Caroline Lucas. Tim Farron. So just a reminder of the question, how would your party, if elected, negotiate a safe, beneficial, worthwhile deal with Europe for Britain? In addition, would this deal be better than pre-Brexit? Nicola Sturgeon. Let's first of all pick up on that point with Paul Nuttall and then to Caroline Lucas. One party standing up for free movement, Tim Farron. But forgive me, that policy on free movement, that's the point I was touching on in response to what Caroline Lucas said. On that issue of the single market, Paul Nusselt at the end. Paul Nuttall, on that point, a say on the final deal raised by Caroline Lucas, put to you. Thank you Mr Nuttall, let's go to Mr Farris. Specifically on that image, that instance of the final saying on the deal. There are very many components. There are very many components within this question, issues around the so-called divorce bill from the EU and of course freedom of movement and immigration. Let's just return to that topic for the moment and come to Caroline Lucas on that issue of immigration. Let's go briefly to Paul Nuttall and then to Nicola Sturgeon. Thank you very much indeed, Tim Farron. And we're just going to wrap up our debate on Brexit there, but we know that of course that it is an overarching theme which is no doubt going to touch on some of the other questions. We have tonight, for the moment, party leaders. Thank you very much indeed for that. Well, our next question this evening is on the National Health Service and social care. Now, I should point out, of course, that health is an area where powers are devolved to the Parliament in Scotland and to the assemblies in Wales and Northern Ireland. The Parliament at Westminster, which is what votes the general election, will be electing legislates on health matters in England. And our question comes this evening from Shirley McCall, who is 82 and a retired nurse from Eastbourne. Shirley. Shirley, thank you very much indeed. To the first response to Shirley's question, we go to Leanne Wood. Tim Farron, thank you. Nicola Sturgeon. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you. And thank you to Shirley once again. There are very many areas to talk about within this, but let's start off on the issue of the funding you would see going into the NHS, precisely how you would pay for that and what your priorities are to open, Caroline Lucas. Just very briefly, Caroline Lewis, on the issue of restructuring. Thank you. Let's stay on this issue of privatisation that was raised by Caroline. Let's bring it back to the point that I initially... It is about priorities. Thank you. It is about priorities and it would be good to hear what your priorities would be on the spending. Let's go to Leanne Wood on that and then to Paul Nuttall. If there's gaps in our economy... Thank you. Thank you, Mr Nuttall. Tim Farron. Don't give her that mandate. So I'm just going to pause it there for a moment. Thank you. Just to return, it's quite useful just to reset on the question that Shirley asked us. The second part of her question, not only was she extremely concerned about the funding of the NHS, but she also pointed very carefully to social care too. So some information about where you would go on social care, Leanne Wood. OK, thank you for that point. Thank you. Thank you very much indeed, Nicola Sturgeon, and to all of the party leaders on that issue. the NHS and of course to Shirley too for the question. We're going to take a short break now when we come back the issue will be the economy. Well, welcome back to the ITV Leaders' Debate, where we're joined by five party leaders. The Conservative leader, Theresa May, and Labour's Jeremy Corbyn were invited, but they declined our invitation. Well, our third question tonight comes from Almas Lakho, who's a 43-year-old from Islington. Almas. Amas, thank you very much indeed for that. And as we did before, of course, we go to an opening response to that. And in response to that question, to open this for us is Paul Nuttall. Tim Farron. Thank you. The end. So Almaz was stressing how hard life is at the moment. And a key phrase in her question, Tim Farron, I'll come to you first. Money isn't stretching as far as it used to. Mr. Nuttall, I'll bring you in there. Paul Nuttall on this. This issue about money not stretching as far as it used to, it goes to the heart of so many matters, doesn't it? It goes to fuel bills, for example. It goes to rent. It goes to all sorts of areas on that issue. Thank you, Mr Farron. Let's refrain Caroline Lucas. Thank you, gentlemen. Caroline Lucas. I'm just going to bring in Leanne Wood on the issue of money being stretched as tightly as Alma's was pointing out. These are the issues we should be debating much more in this election. I'm sorry! We are not building enough houses! We are actually going to touch on housing a little later this evening so we're not going to go there. Can I just draw this back to the issue of wages and the cost of living precisely. Let's just reset a bit and remind us of Almaz's question. She was talking about how hard life is at the moment, the price of her weekly shopping continues to rise, bills increasing, wages and benefits have hardly gone up. It also speaks to how the economy might be grown and what your party would do to grow the economy and perhaps improve job prospects. Leanne Wood. On that precise question of growing the economy, Paul Nuttall and then I'll come to the Middle East version. Thank you. And that concludes the discussion on the economy. And thank you to Almas for the question. Our next question is on education, which is also an area where powers are devolved to the Parliament in Scotland and to the Assemblies in Wales and Northern Ireland. The Parliament at Westminster legislates on education matters in England. And our fourth question comes tonight from Alex Rell, 25 years old and from Liverpool. Thank you, Alex. First of all, to Tim Farron. So let's just, a quick reminder of Alex's question. I'm a primary school teacher. The care and education of our young people, very important to Alex. What would you do to support teachers and schools to ensure that every child gets the best start in life, regardless of their background? In terms of how schools would be organised, Paul Nuttall. OK, let's go back to one of the other issues that arises out of that question, particularly about primary education, and that is class sizes. Perhaps, Leanne Wood, a quick word from you on that, and then Nicola Sturgeon. OK. And in terms of free school meals or the way meals in schools are run and organised, Caroline Lucas, perhaps I'll come to you on that first. Let's talk precisely about that issue and also for those teachers watching I'm sure would all be very interested the issue of pay on teachers and perhaps you'd like to take up on that point and we'll go to the others. Thank you. On the issue of teachers' pay and what you would do about it, Tim Farron? Thank you very much, Inder. Just a reminder of the part of Alex's question, which actually looked to very young children. He wanted to know not only what you would do to support teachers, but to ensure that every child gets the best start in life. And that points directly to early years child support. Nicola Sturgeon. Thank you very much indeed. And that is the last comment on that issue. And that question from Alex on the issue of primary school teaching, thank you very much indeed for that. And our final question. this evening. If your arguments, before we go to that, have fired up your political passions and you're not yet registered to vote, the closing date for registration falls on Monday, May the 22nd. You can do it online at gov.uk slash register to vote. And now, as I was mentioning, it's time for our final question. It comes from Paul Bundred, 39-year-old self-employed IT worker from Chester. Paul, thank you very much indeed for that. And first of all, to Nicola Sturgeon. Thank you, Mr. Farron. Time is up, and it's over to Paul Nuttall. Paul Nuttall, thank you very much indeed for those opening statements. Just a reminder of some of the issues that Paul raised within that question. He put the younger generation facing challenges such as rising tuition fees, expensive housing costs. There are a lot of promises being made on housing. Caroline Lucas, I'll come to you first and suggest you could tell us exactly how you might pay for your plans too. And exactly how would you go about paying for some of these in terms of promises on housing and how you'd pay for them, Paul Nuttall? Thank you. Liam Wood, and let's just, as a point of fact, the Barnett formula is the formula by which the Treasury allocates money for public spending in Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland. On that point, on that house building point that Paul Nettle made. Thank you, Leanne Wall, for that. Let's refocus and reset on the question. The younger generation facing the challenges on housing, but also very clearly pointed towards rising tuition fees. What would you do about those, Tim Farron? No, I'm going to go back to the question. Thank you very much. I'm sticking on it. Thank you. Let me just remind you of one of the other issues that Paul flagged right at the beginning of his question. A lot of policies are being directed towards the older generation, that perceived gap that Paul is pointing towards between the younger and the older generation. On that precise issue, Caroline Lucas. Let's wait, but we were touching on the issue. Are the policies being directed towards the older generation? You were touching on the issue of the policies being directed towards the older generation that Paul raised there. Tim Farron? They could have followed Dilnot. Party leaders all for that. very much indeed. And, ladies and gentlemen, it concludes our free-flowing debate. There's been a lot discussed here over the past two hours and a lot for all of us to reflect upon. I'd now like to ask each leader in turn to give their concluding thoughts this evening. First, Paul Nuttall, leader of the UK Independence Party. Nicola Sturgeon, thank you very much. Thank you all very much indeed.
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_1: If elected, how would your party negotiate a safe, beneficial and worthwhile deal with Europe for Britain? Will this deal be better than pre-Brexit?
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_2: Don't worry about it, I'll report on the clarify. I'll report on the clarify. We're going to the health service, are we? Where's your bus? Some of us do keep promises, actually. And so do I. And so do I. Well, your party didn't, right, Tim?
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_3: Having worked in the National Health Service both in hospitals and in the community until I was diagnosed in my early 50s with multiple sclerosis, I am fully aware of care needs for others and myself. I am extremely concerned about the funding of the NHS and social care going forward to make sure that everyone gets the help they need and deserve. What will you do to make sure they do?
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_4: OK. Life is hard at the moment. The prices of my weekly shopping has continued to rise. My rent and bill are increasing. But wages and benefits haven't gone up. Money isn't stretching as far as used to be. And it is hard to balance everything. How is your part going to help people like me? Tony, hold on, let me finish.
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_5: I'm a primary school teacher, so the care and education of our young people is very important to me. What would you do to support teachers and schools to ensure that every child gets the best start in life, regardless of their backgrounds? OK. Thank you. The bus is going to come driving past any minute.
deb_2017_05_18.QUESTIONER_6: Hi. A lot of policies have been directed towards the older generation. I was a mature student and talked to many younger students who felt frustrated. The younger generation are facing a lot of challenges such as tuition fees, job insecurity and expensive housing costs. A lot of feeling that they don't have much hope for the future. If you are elected, what will you do for the younger generation to help them feel more positive about their future?

deb_2017_05_29.CORBYN: It's not about softening our foreign policy. It is about absolutely condemning what happened in Manchester. Innocent lives were taken, young women and girls out enjoying themselves for the evening. That's the society we live in. That's the society I want to live in. And nobody should be allowed to take that away from anybody. The person who committed it did an appalling and abominable atrocious act, as did apparently those that were conspiring with him. The police investigation is ongoing. My point was that we have to have a foreign policy around the world that doesn't leave large areas without any effective government, such as in Libya at the present time, which can become a breeding ground of a place of enormous danger to all of us and indeed any Western country in Europe or in North America. My point was absolute condemnation. My point was that we need more police, not less. That's why we pledged to provide 10,000 more police on our streets and that we wanted a foreign policy that didn't leave large areas of the world ungoverned so that we had a more secure future for all of us. And do not allow this to become an attack on our multicultural society or on the wonderful faith of Islam. This is a perversion of Islam, what was done in Manchester, as indeed other attacks have been. What we need is a strategy that protects us, but a foreign policy that engages with the rest of the world as well, to bring about a safer world for all of us. Well, the coalitions that are dealing with IS in Syria are complex and they're sometimes fighting each other as well. My whole point is that we have to cut off the funds for IS, cut off the arms for IS, cut off their publicity as far as we can but also bring about a peace process in Syria by reconvening the Geneva talks including all the neighboring countries as well as Iran but also bring about a constructive dialogue in Libya so that we don't have huge areas of that country with all its oil wealth underneath it ungoverned and a prey for this kind of thing. There are desperate people in Libya living in refugee camps. Let's give them something positive and some hope by bringing about stability in that country. It's up to all of us to engage with that because if there is ungoverned spaces around the world then actually everybody's under threat. I wanted dialogue in Northern Ireland during the 1970s and 1980s. I did make contact with Sinn Féin when they were not allowed to travel, their leadership was not allowed to travel to Britain, for example. I wanted there to be a process. And that peace process came about by the actions of people such as John Hume, such as Gerry Adams, such as David Trimble, who eventually brought about the ceasefires with both governments, both the Conservative and later with the Tony Blair-led Labour government. And that brought about the Good Friday Agreement, which respects all the historical traditions of Ireland, which is obviously fundamental to bringing about peace. And I think we should all be pleased that we've achieved a great deal through the Good Friday Agreement. And do you know what? That Good Friday process, recognising those differences, has become a bit of a model for peace processes around the world, where people who were not talking to each other, worse than that, now travel the world together in order to promote peace and dialogue in the Middle East, in Colombia and in the past in South Africa. I think we should be pleased about those achievements. None? I think there should be what is necessary in order to save and protect life. I want to live in a society that is safe and secure. Ideally, nobody wants to see armed police officers anywhere. The reality is, at the moment, they are there, and they have been necessary. Just a quick comment, Callum. The commemoration I think you're referring to was a meeting I was at in London where there was a period of silence for everyone who had died in Northern Ireland. The contribution I made to that meeting was to call for a peace and dialogue process in Northern Ireland. It's only by dialogue and process we brought about the peace in Northern Ireland and I think that is a good thing and I think going forward we need to make sure that in the Brexit negotiations there is no return to any kind of hard border between Northern Ireland and the Republic. We wrote this manifesto, which I'm glad you approve of, by consensus within the Labour Party and our affiliated groups and organisations and from the contribution of hundreds of non-affiliated political groups who have very important views to communicate to us. We put those forward. In government, we will carry this manifesto out. This manifesto is about investing in our future. It is about investing in our young people for the future, so that we don't have supersized classrooms, we don't have massive waiting lists in hospitals, we don't have a million people waiting for social care, we don't have thousands of people sleeping on our streets. The choice is quite clearly there. This manifesto, investing for the future, taxing more, a bit, for corporation tax and for the very wealthiest, 95% will pay no more, or you can go down the road of continuing austerity, continuing cuts in all areas of public expenditure and an ever-widening gap between the richest and the poorest in our society. I am very proud of this manifesto, proud to lead this party, and you know what, I'll be very proud to put this manifesto into action by legislation and by government. And I'm looking forward to the chance of doing it. Well I'm sure when John and I get to know each other we'll get along fine and we'll get to like each other, I'm sure we will. John, people have perceptions about each other, but do you know what? In life, I meet lots of people. Some I agree with, some I disagree with, some I profoundly disagree with. But I always want to get to know them. Because everybody I meet, and everybody you meet, knows something we don't know. You should never be so high and mighty you can't listen to somebody else and learn something from them. To me, leadership is as much about using this as using this. When we leave the European Union we accept the results of the referendum, it's taken place, it's happened, we accept it. The priority has to be negotiating tariff-free trade access to the European market to protect jobs in this country. That we will do. It also has to be about the future relationship with Europe because they are, after all, our very close neighbours. On the issue you mentioned of immigration, the free movement is implicit in membership of the European Union, it obviously stops when we leave the European Union. In the future, there has to be managed migration and it has to be based on the needs of this country and our economy and of the rights of family reunion. I'm not going to stand here and put a figure Our Prime Minister has done that now in the third election running and has got nowhere near meeting that figure. I would say we have to have managed migration, we have to have protection of EU nationals living here just as much as there should be protection of British nationals of which there are very many living in other parts of Europe. But we should also remember And if people hadn't migrated to this country, we would have a much worse health service, education system, and transport system than we have. The contribution that's made to your living standards and mine by people who have come here is huge. But I'll tell you what will change. We will not allow companies to bring in whole groups of very low-paid workers in order to undercut often fairly low-paid workers in this country, thus destroying their working conditions. And so that is part of that managed process. At the moment we have some disgraceful undercutting and we have huge impacts on different communities. The other point I just make in finality on this, go and see Faisal's moving his hands, is that We would also reinstate something that Gordon Brown introduced when he was Prime Minister, which was an impact fund for communities that had had an influx of new residents and needed support on vital local services. And so we accept the result. We negotiate with Europe, we have a good relationship with Europe, and we manage our own future. I would have thought, under a managed migration system, it certainly wouldn't go up any more. It would probably probably, but I don't want to be held to this, come down, that's a probability, but I would say we have to recognise that we do have serious skill shortages in this country because we have not invested enough in education and training for a very long time in this country. We need to turn that around. because we have to accept the reality of the result of the referendum. It happened. It was a democratic choice. More people voted in that than any other recorded vote in our history. And so it's there. It's a decision. That doesn't mean we're leaving the European Union. It doesn't mean we're leaving Europe as a continent. It's still there, obviously, and we're going to have to have a relationship with it. I've said, in answer to our friend over there, I said that We would guarantee the rights of European Union nationals in Britain to remain here. And indeed, within days of the referendum, Andy Burnham, who was then our shadow Home Secretary, proposed that resolution in the House of Commons. And it was, in fact, carried by a huge majority, because the Conservatives chose not to take part. There's going to be a relationship with Europe. Our universities must have a close relationship with Europe. We will defend working rights, such as working time directive, paternal leave, maternity leave. all those very important things we've gained from the European Union. We'll protect those in the future and we will also remain part of a number of very important agencies such as policing and intelligence sharing as well as environmental protocols. It's no good one side of a sea polluting it and the other side not. You've both got to agree not to pollute the sea. You have to work together to achieve things. So what I say to you is The result is there. It happened. Let's build a sensible, good, tariff-free trade relationship with Europe. Every car that's made in Britain or made, most of the cars that are made in Germany, the parts come from both sides of the channel. You could say the same with aircraft. You could say the same with an awful lot of other manufacturing industry. That's going to carry on, and it will have to carry on. Otherwise, we'll destroy our own manufacturing industry. But I'll tell you what we won't do. We won't threaten Europe with turning this country into a sort of corporate tax haven with low tax, low wages and low investment. We want high wage, high investment, growing economy with good relations with our neighbours and indeed of course with the rest of the world. Corporation tax was 28% in 2010. This government has reduced it and proposes to reduce it further. We're going to put it back up to 26%. And why are we doing that? Because this country is badly divided between the richest and the poorest. You put corporate tax and tax at the top end down, the division gets greater. Are you happy that so many of our children are going to school with supersized classes, so many of our children are going to school hungry. Are you happy with so many people waiting for hospital operations, a million waiting for social care? You don't address these problems by ignoring them. I appeal to you, as obviously a traditional person who supported our party, recognise that we're all better off when everybody is better off. And your children want to get a home of their own in the future. If we build the homes for them, both to rent as well as to buy, then they've got a better chance. Your children, I'm sure, would like to go to university. I do not believe we should charge fees for our students to go to universities because we all benefit from them graduating and becoming well-qualified citizens. So what we're proposing in this manifesto is transformational because for the first time for many years we're saying this younger generation should not be worse off than we are or ones that went before. They should actually be better off. This is about rejigging the way that we run our economy. But I also say to anyone running a small business I work with small businesses, I talk to them, I listen very carefully to what they have to say. They are often very badly exploited by much bigger business who delay on payments, often don't pay at all and force them out of business. We want a society and an economy that invests and a legal system that protects those that are working really hard. Surely a £10 an hour minimum wage, a living wage, a real living wage by 2020 would mean we actually spend probably a bit less on working tax credits, but it would also make an awful lot of people a lot better off. And do you know what? They'd spend the money in the local economy. It would be something that would be good for all of us. I appeal to you, think again. How did we get our welfare state in Britain? How do we get our National Health Service? We got it because the Labour Party was bold enough in the post-war period to invest in the future. Our Labour government will do the same. I want to live in a world that's free of the danger of a nuclear holocaust. Any nuclear weapon used anywhere in the world is a disaster for all of us. And I've spent my life working with peace organisations, working with the United Nations over the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty in order to bring about global disarmament. I would see my responsibility as our Prime Minister to contribute to a world of peace in the future. That means being very serious about the dangers, very serious about promoting what China is trying to achieve in Korea with the six-party talks, be very serious in what President Obama achieved with his agreement with Iran, but also look at the other threats that we face as well. Cyber attacks are very serious. We saw that with the attack on our National Health Service only a couple of weeks ago. Random acts of terror, such as we saw in Manchester, are a real threat as well. We have to defend our way of life, our society and our country, by an intelligent engagement with the rest of the world. And I want to bring about our contribution to a more peaceful world. I will write the appropriate letter to our commanders, who are obviously very responsible, very loyal naval officers. Listen, this manifesto is a product of the views of the Labour Party, of party conference decisions, and of the views put forward by individuals in the Shadow Cabinet. It was a conference decision by the Labour Party, and as the leader of the party, I accept the democracy of our party. Is that morally right? In answer to the questions put earlier, I made the point that as Prime Minister, I will do all I can to bring about a nuclear-free world because I'm horrified at the very idea. You promised to renew. of a nuclear attack. He promised to win you a nuclear weapon. Is that morally right? Listen, it's there in our manifesto because our conference voted for it. I have to accept that decision. Do you think it's morally right? Can I finish? Can I finish? What I want to see... I'm asking you perfectly simply, do you think it's morally right? What I want to see is a nuclear-free world. Of course everybody wants to see that. But is it morally right? I'm not so sure about that. I'm not so sure about that. If that's what you believe, is it morally right to renew a nuclear deterrent? That is the decision that's been taken. We will work for a nuclear-free world. I note you don't answer. We will work through the Nuclear Non-Proliferation Treaty to achieve that. That surely is something well worth doing. There are some banks that are publicly owned. We will be promoting building societies, cooperatives, and we will be promoting a national investment bank, which won't be a lending bank, it will be an investment bank. And that is an important part of it. You believe in public ownership of retail banks? an important part of directing investment in this country. Of the retail banks, RBS is largely publicly owned. There is a public stake in most of the banks. But it doesn't get into the manifesto, does it? Jeremy, what we seem to be struggling with here is an understanding of a process that brings about a manifesto. No, no. I am not a dictator who writes things to tell people what to do. This is a product of a process in our party. That's why I was elected leader of our party, to give a voice to the members and those that are affiliated to our party. You will? Now what we've said is we will put two billion into the benefit system per year in order to... That's tax credits. In order to reduce the effects we will also end the indignity of the... Will you freeze benefits for three years then? Benefits will be paid, of course. Benefits will be uprated, and will be uprated, of course. And there will be a higher living wage, as I've outlined, which will of course mean that those benefits that are paid for people in work, and indeed the majority of benefits go to people in work, not out of work, will probably reduce. No, they're not going to be frozen because they'll be uprated every year, as they should be. We have made it very clear that we will provide more police. This government has taken 20,000 police officers off our streets. But you don't deny that John Donald said all that. We'll put 10,000 more on our streets. We've also said quite clearly that... How can we believe that when the Shadow Chancellor believes in disbanding MI5 and the special branches? The Shadow Chancellor is the one who signed off that pledge in the manifesto. John McDonnell supports the need for more police officers. Does he still believe in abolishing MI5? John McDonnell also accepts the need for the accountability to Parliament of our security services, which is important. But he also accepts, as I do, the need to have security services that do protect us. Manchester is a good example. And I think when all the inquiries have been completed in Manchester, I think we need to look at those needs. What you're going to see with John McDonnell is a Chancellor that looks at the needs of the country, looks at the needs of the people of this country and invests in our future, not hands money away in tax relief to big corporations and at the top end of our society. Look, there's nothing in there because we're not going to do it. Listen, it's not on anybody's agenda. It's certainly not on my agenda. And do you know what? I had a very nice chat with the Queen. But you don't like her, do you? You don't like what she represents. We got along absolutely fine, perfectly fine, but I don't think she should be brought into political discussion. Look, I believe in a democracy, and we live in a democracy. We have a titular head of state as the monarch, but without political power. Look, the law is there and that's what will prevail. I'm fighting this election, Jeremy, on something very important. That is the levels of poverty in our society. The levels of children that are not supported properly in our society. The question is how that reflects your beliefs. Jeremy, I'm fighting this election on social justice. I'm fighting this election to give us a stronger economy for the future. Jeremy, this manifesto reflects what would be a profound and very good change in our society. Because for the first time you'd have a government that was saying you cannot go on loading our students with debt. You cannot go on overcrowding our classrooms. You cannot go on underfunding our hospitals. You have to invest by all of us for the future of all of us. I don't want to live in a country of food banks. I don't want to live in a country of homeless people. I want to live in a country that actually does seriously care for all. Listen, what they're going to take very seriously is that we would have been elected with a mandate, a mandate to negotiate a tariff-free trade access to the European market, that we will protect European nationals living here. What they will see is a government that is going to protect the protocols, environmental conditions, workers' rights that we achieved through Europe, and a government that wants a serious relationship with Europe but respects the result of our referendum in that we're leaving the European Union. You are prepared to contemplate their no deal? We will make sure there's a deal, and you know what, we won't start... How much of our money are you prepared to give Brussels? We won't start the negotiations by megaphone diplomacy and threatening Europe with some kind of offshore tax haven on the shores of Europe. We'll start those negotiations seriously, seriously, with respect to them. Jeremy, there are going to be negotiations over all of that. I've seen the figures that Michel Barnier has bandied around. I don't recognise those figures. Others may not also. We will obviously negotiate them. Where we have a legal obligation, it must be met. Where the EU has a legal obligation, it must be met. But we have to recognise... So you've done some sums? We have to recognise that we're leaving the European Union. The priority has to be tariff-free trade access because we have a lot of manufacturing industry... Haven't you done any sums? Can I finish, please? Really? Just for a second? How much are you prepared to give to get a deal? Jeremy, I'm trying to explain the basis on which we propose to negotiate. There is no answer to your question because nobody could answer at this stage what I've said. Well, you don't know. I've said what we would do is pay that we are legally required to do. There will have to be an agreement on trade access. Now, it's not a one-way street because all of our manufacturing industry relies on supply chains across Europe, as indeed many European manufacturers supply chains from this side of the channel as well. We had that discussion with the audience earlier on. Free movement... You said probably. Free movement ends when we leave the European Union. We will, as I've said... I've said we'll protect the rights of EU nationals here and we want the same for British nationals across Europe because these are people, they're families... Yeah, and we will stop the undercutting that goes on by importation of low paid workers. There will be migration of managers necessary to sustain our economy. I'm not going to sit here and make promises. Theresa May has done that for the third election running and hasn't got anywhere near meeting her promises in the last two I suspect it would probably be slightly less or less, but... Probably is hardly a commitment, is it? Listen, we require migrant workers to maintain our health service and much of our industry. We have a skills gap because we haven't invested enough in skills training. I wanted there to be a UN-brokered plan of a ceasefire at that time, which President Terry of Peru was trying to pursue. What I wanted was a stopping of that war. I didn't want any young men, British or Argentinian or anybody else, to die in that war. I don't think they should have gone in there, but I also think there should have been an opportunity to prevent that war happening by the UN, which was being attempted at that time. So you do think it was a Tory plot? That was my point to you. No, I think it was important. Why did you say so then? I think it was important that there should be a negotiated solution to that through the United Nations. That surely... If you didn't believe it was a Tory plot, why did you say you thought it was? Jeremy, Margaret Thatcher made a great deal of the whole issue, as everybody could see at the time. I felt that she was exploiting the situation, but there has to be, at the end of every conflict, there has to be a peace process. Why don't we start with the objective of trying to get a peace process and use our councils through the United Nations to achieve that? I would want to know the circumstances, the evidence and what would happen. And also what the effect it would be on innocent civilians around. Killing somebody actually happened outside Parliament a month ago. Yes. And I think you have to look at all the evidence that's there. But you can't answer a hypothetical question without the evidence around it. It's a completely hypothetical question. It's a completely hypothetical question. We have to look at the evidence that's there at the time. And he could have been. You think that was a tragedy? I think the whole Afghan experience is a tragedy and I think he should have been put on trial. It was inclusive language at a meeting in which I was promoting the idea of a two-state solution, in which I was promoting the need for dialogue between Israel and all aspects of Palestine, including Hamas, as well as including Fatah. I think to bring about a peace process, that is important. Do you know what happened last night in Tel Aviv? It was a very large demonstration of people wanting a peace process and a dialogue. I think Those are people you've got to work with. You've got to work with people often you don't agree with and don't like. It was an inclusive piece of language in order to get a meeting underway. I do not agree with them. I do not support them. I want there to be a process. But at the end of the day, there has to be a process in which people talk to each other. You know that.
deb_2017_05_29.MAY: Well Martin, as a serving police officer obviously you'll be aware that we have police and counter-terrorism police. We've been very clear that we have protected the budgets for counter-terrorism policing and we're currently protecting overall police budgets as well. And we're ensuring that we're putting money in to enable the police to address what are actually different ways, as you will know, different types of crime. Crime is changing. And policing will be changing in the future. It's why we're putting more money, for example, into cyber policing, because increasingly we see those as the sort of crimes that are having to be addressed. We did do what was called Operation Temperer, which enabled the military to assist the police following the terrible, horrific attack that took place in Manchester. That's been a well-prepared plan for some time, and that was put into operation and put into operation smoothly and is now being run down. What is important is that we not only ensure we have the counter-terrorism police we need. and the overall police that we need, but also that they have the powers that they need. And that's what I've done as Home Secretary. I ensured that we brought through new legislation to put powers into place, which gave the police what they were asking for in order to be able to deal with counter-terrorism. We agreed that not just the normal armed officers should be increased, but actually the counter-terrorism specialist officers as well. What we had to do when we came into government in 2010 was to ensure that we were living within our means and that was very important because of the economic situation we'd inherited. Now what we need to do in policing terms is ensure it's not just about the numbers of police, people often focus on the numbers of police, it's actually about what the police are able to do and how they are being deployed on our streets. As I say In counter-terrorism policing we have protected those budgets and we're currently protecting police budgets. But crime is changing and the sort of job that the police officers need to do is changing. That's why we're also putting extra money into things like cybercrime because that's today's world. So a government has to ensure that we're keeping abreast with the changes that take place with the challenges that there are out there that we need to deal with. And of course the terrorist threat is severe. That's where it is at the moment. But that's why we ensure that our police, our security service, have that counter-terrorism budget. We are increasing the budgets for our security services as well. But also that they have the powers they need to do the job. Just a quick come back, Martin. Philip, thank you, because you've raised an issue which I know a lot of people are looking at and thinking about. And it's a really important issue because one of the great challenges that we face as a society, and that government faces, is the fact that our society is ageing. We see more people, it's great that people are living longer, but of course that has an impact in terms of the services we have to provide and ensuring people can have the dignity in their old age. Now the situation, as I'm sure you know at the moment is, that if you require care and you have savings of more than £23,000, you have to pay for that care. And if the care you require is residential care, then the value of your house is taken into account. So what happens is people are paying for care People are finding that they are having to sell their house, many people are having to sell their house to pay those care bills and many find that they're not able to leave the money to their families. Now I want to take those risks away and that's what the proposals I've put forward are about. It's about ensuring that nobody is going to have to sell their house to pay for care in their lifetime. It's about ensuring that £100,000 of savings of assets are protected to pass on to the family. We'll put an absolute cap on the level of money that people have to spend on care. And I think what we're doing is ensuring that we can have a sustainable solution for the long term. If I can just give one figure Faisal, in 10 years time there will be 2 million more people aged over 75. If we don't address the issue of our social care system now then it is going to collapse. We need to do it and do it in a way that's fairer to younger generations and that's what my plans are for. There will be a cap, yes. What we set out in the manifesto is the principles of our position. I then saw a lot of scaremongering about that. I heard a lot of scaremongering about it. and recognise that people would be worried as a result of that. 100,000? 500,000? We clarified that there will be a cap. But what we will do, Faisal, is not just put a figure on it. What we will do is publish a green paper, a consultation document. We'll listen to people. We'll listen to voters. We'll listen to charities and organisations working with older people. We'll take people's views on where that cap should be. And I think that's the fairer way to do it. OK. Amanda. Well we have a situation as you know where there's a devolved government in Scotland and they have the ability to make separate decisions on a whole variety of issues affecting people in Scotland and they've been given extra powers in relation to dealing with welfare so it is up to them to make that decision as to how they choose to apply certain welfare benefits. We will, we have said and we will in government means test the winter fuel payment and the reason for doing that is this, at the moment we have a situation where pensions who are better off are getting that help and support where you see working families who might be struggling not getting that sort of support and I think it's right and I think you know I've met pensioners who've said to me that they don't think they should get that winter fuel payment. What we will do with the money that's released is we'll put it into health and social care. So it will be going towards actually ensuring that those systems are providing better quality care for older people and others. Well what we're going to do Faisal, I'm sorry it's exactly the same as I've just said on the social care cap, is once again I think this is an issue where we need not just to pull out a figure in an election campaign, but actually to listen to people, to talk to people, to take people's views and then make a proposal as government. So there's a proper consultation. So we're actually hearing from voters, we're hearing from organisations and charities and others who work with older people about what they think is the right level to set that threshold for winter fuel payments at. But the key thing, the key principle that would drive what we do is that less well-off pensioners will be protected. Nicola, what we will do in government is put further record levels of funding into schools. But we also do want to look at ensuring that the money is being distributed in a fair way. And as you probably know, there are some schools in the country today that may receive twice the amount of funding for a pupil that other schools receive. I think it's important that we see a fair way of funding our schools. What we're committed to is ensuring that as we introduce that, schools won't see any cash reduction in their level of funding. But I actually want to do more than that because I believe that education is absolutely crucial. We want our youngsters to get the best possible start in life. And that's why I want to see a more diverse system of schools available for people. I want to bring in some of the expertise that we see in universities. For example, in supporting schools, there are some good examples of that at the moment. Let's do more of that. Let's make sure that every child has a good school place, and a school place that is right for them. Because that's about ensuring they get the best possible start in life, to be the best that they can. Because I want our society to be one where it's down to your talents and hard work how far you go, and not where you came from or who your parents are. Nobody can guarantee the real-terms per-pupil funding increase. In the Labour Party's manifesto we know the figures don't add up. What is important is that as we look What we need to do is to ensure we will put those record levels of funding into schools. We need to ensure that we get that better spread of funding in terms of the fair funding formula. But I also say this, if you look at what happens in education, it's not just people are focusing on funding, but actually we need to ensure that we do see good or outstanding schools and more good or outstanding places in good or outstanding schools for children. We've seen the number increase over the last seven years. We need to carry on doing that so that there is a good school place for every child. And that isn't just about funding, it's about a whole variety of things that go to make up really good education for children. There were a number of claims made during the referendum campaign on both sides of the argument and there were some passionately held views and some really passionate arguments on both sides. I think what's important now is that we ensure that we get the best possible deal from Brexit. That is about negotiating a really good deal for Britain. It is about ensuring that in future we won't be sending vast sums of money to the European Union every year as we do as members of the EU. And we will be able to look at what, as funding comes back, how we use that funding. But it is important that we get that best possible deal because it underpins so much else of what we want to do. And we can only get that deal if we've got a plan to go in there and really stand up for Britain. And the negotiations will start 11 days after Election Day. So we have to know what we're doing, we have to have a strong hand in the negotiations, and be very clear about getting the best possible deal for Britain. Once again, if we look at the health service, and obviously you work in the health service, so you know the enormity of the task that the health service faces. We're making greater demands on the health service every year. The overall figure is we're spending over the five years up to 2020, we'll be spending half a trillion pounds in total. on the National Health Service. And we do need to make sure that money is being spent well, of course. So we are increasing the funding into the health service and will increase funding into the health service in the future. And we're committed to that in our manifesto, both in terms of buildings and technology, but also in terms of increasing the per head funding for the National Health Service in real terms every year. It's important. We all rely on the health service. We all know people either who work in the health service like yourself, who've been through the health service, who've had great care and attention from the fantastic staff in the health service. That's why we're ensuring that we are putting more money into the NHS and more money for the future as well as what is happening at the moment. We want that first class NHS, but we can only do that if we have a really strong economy to pay for it. And that's where the Brexit negotiations, but so much else actually comes in, because it's making sure we get that right deal for Britain from Brexit, that we can build on the strong economy and the economic progress we've made in the last few years. Because if we do that, then we have that money to fund that first class National Health Service. That's what we're committed to. When I was described in the terms that you've said as a difficult woman, I think it was about because my colleague who used that term saw me as somebody who actually stood by what I thought was right and was willing to fight for what I believe is the right thing to do. And that's what drives me. It's doing what is the right thing by the country. And sometimes you have to be difficult in order to do that. If I look at what's coming ahead to this country, we stand at a very important moment in our history. We have the opportunity now to really change this country for the better for the future. And that is about tough negotiations for Brexit, but it's also about facing up to those domestic challenges, domestic challenges like the ageing society, like the changing face we see in the workplace, the increased technology in the workplace. All of these are things that we need to have a government that recognises it, is open about those issues and is willing to find ways of addressing them. And if in order to address them and do the right thing by the country, it takes being a difficult woman, then that's exactly what I will be. Well, you say, what I'm doing, Faisal, is actually going out across the country asking people, yes, for me to gain their trust so that they will support me at this election. Because I think it is important to have a strong hand in the negotiations for Brexit, to have that strong mandate for those negotiations. But we also have a lot of challenges to face here in this country. And as I've said, there are Brexit is one of the challenges, getting that right, our ageing society is another, the divisions within our society is another and the issue of making sure we have a strong economy and facing the impact of changing technology and the impact that's having on our lives and our workplaces, everybody will know. These are big challenges ahead and the domestic policies that I've set out our policies to address those challenges and some of them will involve difficult choices but I think it's right if I'm saying to people I want a mandate to be Prime Minister and to have the government of this country that we're actually open with people that there will be some hard choices. Well, I'm tempted to ask you, Jeremy, what you think. Do you mean that you're talking about Brexit? Well, of course. Right. Well, there are lots of challenges in our politics at the moment. There are lots of challenges that the government face. I set out my reasons for deciding that we should, on balance, stay within the European Union. I voted to remain. I campaigned to remain. When did you change your mind? The British people were given the choice and the British people decided that they wanted the United Kingdom to leave the European Union. I'm delivering what I believe the British people want their government to deliver. And I think it's very important. I think it's not just an issue about Brexit itself. It's actually an issue to me about trust in politicians. And I also said that the sky wouldn't fall in if we left the European Union. So you have changed your mind, have you? We gave people the choice. I'll answer that in a minute. We gave people the choice, Jeremy, and the British people decided to leave the European Union. And I think it's important for them to see their politicians delivering on that choice and respecting the will of the people. What I am now doing is... Have you changed your mind? I think there are huge opportunities... Have you changed your mind? Jeremy, I know that you have used this tactic and you want... I'm just trying to get an answer. I take the view... that we can make a success of Brexit. I take the view that the British people want us to make that success. But you don't believe in it? Of course. Jeremy, as Prime Minister, I've been out there already ensuring that the negotiations are going to start and I want to ensure that there's a strong hand in those negotiations. I believe in making a success of Brexit for the whole of the United Kingdom. I believe in making a success of Brexit. I voted... Well, there's no gain saying the fact that I campaigned to remain and voted to remain. Sure, but you still think it's a duff idea. No, I think that... You haven't changed your mind. Look, the referendum drew a line under those debates. What the referendum did was took the view of the British people. Now, I think it's right that we do that now, and I think, I genuinely think, that if we get Brexit right, and this is really important for us, if we get Brexit right, we can make a real success of the opportunities that open up for us. But we can only do that if we grasp those opportunities. But you still think it's a bad idea. No, I believe we're doing the right thing in making a success of Brexit. What Jeremy Hunt was talking about and what we referred to in our manifesto was a specific proposal. This proposal was put forward by Andrew Dilnot a few years ago when he was asked by government to look at the social care issue. And if I just explain why we rejected that Dilnot proposal, Jeremy, it was because for two reasons. First of all, because it was going to be paid for out of general taxation. And secondly, because that Deal Not proposal protected wealthier pensioners, but did nothing to protect pensioners on modest incomes. Now the proposals I've put forward actually do that. They do help pensioners on modest incomes. And crucially, they ensure we have a long-term sustainable solution for social care. Well, as I've just answered, I'm not going to give you a different answer from the one I've given Faisal. It's not about not knowing, Jeremy. It's about thinking what... Well, you don't know, do you? It's about thinking what the right approach is to get to that figure. What we want to do is to ask, is to consult, is to listen, and then to determine what that cut will be. I think that's only fair to people, that we actually go through that consultation and say to people and take people's views as we determine that cap. What is helping is ensuring that we don't have a long-term solution. No, no. And I'm answering your question, Jeremy. You're not. You have asked me about the just about managing and how they're being helped. And what I'm going to point out is that if you have a social care system which relies on taxing those just about managing at higher levels, that's not helping them. What we're doing is putting forward a proposal which means that people don't have to sell their house in their lifetime to pay for care, it means they can pass on savings to their children, and it means there will be that cap. But it's fair across the generations. And there are older people today who worry that their children and grandchildren won't have a better future. Yes, I accept that. I think, Jeremy, you'll find that what the people in Brussels look at is the record that I had of negotiating with them in Brussels and delivering for this country on a number of issues on justice and home affairs, which people said we were never going to get. All right. And I got those negotiations. And I said there wouldn't be a general election. And after I became Prime Minister, I felt that the most important thing for the country was the stability and getting on with triggering the Article 50 so that we made sure that we were delivering on that vote on Brexit. What became clear when we were going through that process is that other parties want to frustrate that process of those Brexit negotiations. We have the Liberal Democrats wanting a second referendum. That's all happened since March, has it? We have the... Well, actually, it was March. It was in March that I triggered Article 50 by writing the letter to the European Council. And what became clear as we were looking through those negotiations, as we started those negotiations, started the process of triggering them, what became clear, increasingly clear, as is clear in this election campaign, is the desire of other parties to frustrate the will of the British people.
