That's why I thought it was right to go out for an election. I take responsibility for the decisions I make, Jeremy. I've been in government since 2010, so seven years in government. Net migration? No, we didn't achieve that. Yes, it was 248,000 in the last set of figures. Sorry? Non-EU migrants? Was about 170,000, 175,000. What we saw in immigration figures was it started to come down, then they did go up and now they're starting to come down again. What we will have when we leave the EU is the... Yes. Jeremy, I'm not sitting here saying it wasn't. I'm accepting that what we had to do was to ensure that we were rooting out abuse in the system. But there is no single moment where you take one measure which changes the immigration figures. It is a constant work to ensure that you're dealing with immigration and ensuring there is no more abuse in the system. There's more work to be done. We will, of course, have another tool to do this when we leave the European Union, because we'll be able to introduce rules for people coming from within the European Union. But this will be a constant work. Well, we're working at the moment on what those new rules should be in terms of... No, it's not about that. We have a set of rules for people from outside the European Union. We will look at the requirements for... One of the things we do is ensure that it's possible for people to be brought in where there are skill shortages, where there is a need for people to come to work. I think one of the crucial things we need to do in this country is ensure that actually we are enabling people here in the UK to be skilled up to do jobs. I think sometimes what we've seen is people being brought in from overseas because we haven't done sufficient in this country. That's why I think technical education is so important. It's a policy which will actually... He should know, shouldn't he? It's a policy which ensures that we are recognising the concerns that people in this country have about uncontrolled immigration. Controlling immigration is important. It's important for a number of reasons, but one of the reasons it's important is because uncontrolled migration has an impact on wages at the lower end of the income scale. People feel an impact from that. That's why I think it's so important that we keep working at this and we keep that aim. We will be able to introduce rules for people coming from within the European Union once we've left. That's been a piece of the work on immigration that hasn't been available to us. I don't think it's going to be about paying over money to get a deal. What I think is important is that... You're not prepared to pay anything. We have made clear, I've put it in the manifesto, that we will look for a fair... Would you pay 100 billion to leave the European Union? That we will put a fair settlement, look for a fair settlement of our rights and obligations. There are a number of figures that are being quoted and being around, a number of them coming from the European Union. Have you got the figure in your head? A number coming from the European Union itself. We will look at our rights and obligations. It isn't a question of what it's worth paying to get out. It's a question of what is going to be the right deal for us to leave the European Union, which will stop us from paying huge sums of money into the EU every single year, which will enable that we have control of our money and our borders and our laws. You wouldn't walk out. We will be there to negotiate the right deal, but what I have said is no deal is better than a bad deal. We have to be prepared to walk out. No deal is better than a bad deal. And let me just tell you... That wasn't exactly my question. My question was, are you prepared to walk away... By definition, if we're prepared to say, if I'm prepared to say that no deal is better than a bad deal, I want to get a good deal from the European Union. What I don't want is the sort of bad deal that some people are talking about. If you think about it, some people in Europe are talking about punishing us, and that would be a bad deal for us. Some people here in the UK talk as if they're willing to do anything to sign up to a deal with the European Union, and that would be a bad deal for us as well. As I say, no deal would be better than a bad deal. I think you can take it, Jeremy. No deal would be better than a bad deal. Yes, you've said that repeatedly. I'm not prepared to sign up to a bad deal for the UK. Now, I believe... I think you have to. In negotiations, you have to recognise that you're prepared, that you're not in there to get a deal at any price. We're in there to get the best deal for the United Kingdom. And actually, I'm optimistic about that, because I think that in the European Union, they will want to continue their relationship with us as well. What I will be doing is, as we heard in the earlier session, what I'll be doing is being a difficult woman and ensuring that we're negotiating hard.

At this point, I should point out that the audience members have been carefully selected to provide balance. A third are declared Conservative supporters, a third are for Labour, and a third are undecided. So let's get started with our first question, which comes from Ines. Ines. Is it worth clarifying for Callum, did you vote for the Good Friday Agreement? Yes I did. Absolutely. Let's talk about something. Callum talks about can we trust you on stopping terrorist threats. Let's talk about something practical. Armed police officers. Do you think there should be fewer? Do you think there should be more? The same? OK, thank you, Callum. John Dare. John. Mr Corbyn, John's question was actually, he likes the manifesto, but it was your own leadership he was questioning. Could you just address that to John Corbyn? OK, Roxanne. Moving on to Roxanne. Where's Roxanne? To just help Roxanne out here, you won't give a number, but the current net figure is £248,000. Higher? Lower? The same? We're just trying to get one more... David Gerrard. Can I just keep to the practical implication of David's question, which is in 11 days' time you could be Prime Minister and you would have to write those letters of last resort to the Trident submarine commanders. Are you going to write those letters? OK. Well, that brings us to the end of our first question and answer session of the evening. Thank you very much, Mr Corbyn. Thank you. And thank you very much. Coming next, Mr. Corbyn, head-to-head with Jeremy Paxman. This is May versus Corbyn, the battle for number 10. It's time for our audience and you at home to meet the Prime Minister, Theresa May. OK, well, let's get started with our first question from Martin. Good evening, Martin. Just to clarify for Martin, when you became Home Secretary, the staff numbers of police were 141,850. That's September 10. What are they now? 124,000. So that's down 20,000. That's what he's talking about. And armed officers were nearly 7,000 in March 2010, and now... Well, we're currently having an uplift in the number of armed officers. But they were cut over six years, and you've had to put them back up. Was that a mistake? Okay well let's move on to Philip. Can you just clarify for Philip and people at home, what is the cap? Is it a manifesto policy that there will be a cap? So Prime Minister, we know that Scottish pensioners will keep the winter fuel allowance. Can you tell us how many English pensioners will lose it? The IFS have assumed six million, the Resolution Foundation ten million. It's clearly several million isn't it? OK, all right, let's move on to Nicola. I think Nicola's question was about per-pupil funding in real terms. You've said it's going to go up £4 billion, but that is going down. In real terms, per-pupil, it is going down. Can you clarify that? Okay, next question, Joy. OK, what did you think of that answer? Freya, back to you. OK, let's go to Sandeep. Sandeep. Where's Sandeep? Hello Prime Minister. Prime Minister, the question was about Nazi party applied to domestic policy. And you're asking fairly uniquely for a massive majority. You're already Prime Minister, you want a really big majority. That will also apply to domestic policy. And you voted for things that subsequently you turned on, like tax credits cuts in 2015. Isn't that what will not be able to be turned around when you have a larger majority, if that's what you get? OK, well, that brings us to the end of our second question and answer session of the evening. Thank you very much, Prime Minister. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks again to the audience for their questions. Coming next, Theresa May sits down with Jeremy Paxman.
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_1: Mr Corbyn, in your speech following the Manchester attack last Friday, you stated that no government can prevent every terrorist attack and that we need a foreign policy that reduces rather than increases the threat to this country. Islamic State have clearly stated that anyone who doesn't abide by their ideology is their enemy. So why should we soften our foreign policy if it is blatantly obvious that there is no room for negotiation? I think it's just in terms of foreign policy, you know, are you going to look the other way if there's any military intervention that is needed in that area to support the coalition that are fighting IS?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_2: The horrific events in Manchester are not the first time this nation has faced terrorism. You and two of your senior colleagues openly supported the IRA. How can we really trust you in stopping terrorism? With all due respect, Mr Corbyn, you didn't actually answer my question. You have openly supported the IRA in the past. And I can give you an example. Attending a commemoration for the eight IRA men who were killed at Lochgoll, Now, in your words, it was to honour them. Maybe you can share with the electorate why those IRA men were actually killed. Mr Corbyn, the event was actually to commemorate The people who were killed in Lough Gale. The reason they were killed in Lough Gale was they were on their way to kill British policemen.
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_3: Mr Corbyn, like many voters, I approve of many of the policies in your manifesto. The problem I have is that I don't see you as someone who could effectively run this country. How can you convince me that you're the man for the job?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_4: Hi Mr Colin, I'd like to know for people like myself who voted to leave the European Union and have voted Labour in the past, how are you planning to deal with immigration? The Conservatives have pledged a number, why won't you?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_5: Hi, hello. Hi Miranda, how are you doing? By contrast, I'm a Remain voter and I feel that the Labour response to Brexit has been lacklustre. I don't really see a big difference between the Tory and Labour responses, but it's a really big issue for me. Can you tell me why I should vote Labour instead of Lib Dem as part of the 48%?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_6: I'm a proud Mancunian and a small business owner brought up in a Labour voting household by salt-of-the-earth parents who've gave me their best. Why have you made it impossible for me to vote Labour in this election with your ruthless, short-sighted policies such as 26% corporation tax, the abolishment of zero-hour contracts, £10 an hour minimum wage, and now you want to put VAT on my children's school fees?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_7: David, please, David. Hi. This way. Given your long-standing support for nuclear disarmament, would you be prepared to use the nuclear deterrent, and if so, in what circumstances?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_8: Jeremy Corbyn, in 11 days' time you might be Prime Minister. People are entitled to know about you, aren't they? Yes. I'm looking forward to it as well. Good. Are you frustrated that so many of your core ideas, your basic principles, didn't make it into this manifesto? Oh, come on, this is a really good matter. I'm glad you got it, by the way. It's a really good... I've had to read it, too. Well, I helped to write it. Yeah, but it doesn't include key things that you believe in, does it? But this manifesto fundamentally... You couldn't persuade the Cabinet, the Shadow Cabinet... Can I finish this? No. Oh, come on. Couldn't persuade the Shadow Cabinet to accept your basic principle which you have adhered to for the whole of your adult life. that there should be nuclear disarmament. You're promising this to renew Trident. A number of your other core beliefs do make it into this manifesto, don't they? About nationalising the Royal Mail, for example, or the railways, or energy companies, or water companies. Why not banks? Some of our banks aren't publicly owned, as it is, actually. You said you have always believed that banks should be publicly owned. I would always favour banks being in public ownership. You said that in 2013. You do. Well, I did say that. What we're struggling here with is how much this represents what you believe. All right, let's take another aspect of it. You say you'll freeze benefits for three years. I'm asking you whether they'll be frozen. Thank you. What about money for the security services? How can we take that seriously when your Shadow Chancellor was photographed holding a letter which called for the abolition of MI5, the abolition of a special branch and the disarming of the police? We've made it very clear... So he says one thing one day and one thing another day. So he signs letters which call for the abolition of MI5 and Special Branch and the rest of it, but when it comes to the manifesto, he's perfectly happy to sign off on more police officers and, doubtless, more money for MI5. There is nothing in this manifesto about getting rid of the monarchy, which is another thing you believe in, isn't it? But you do believe in it, don't you? Any human being anywhere can have a nice conversation with anybody, but you do not believe in the institution of monarchy, do you? And when the Queen's reign comes to an end, should we call stumps on the whole thing then? Call it a day. There's a whole series of things that you couldn't even persuade your own party to put in the manifesto that you believe in. You will have to take this country into negotiations with the rest of Europe. When they look at you and they see a man who cannot get his core beliefs into his own manifesto, are they going to take you seriously? There's going to be a deal. There is going to be a deal. Would you leave Europe without a deal? How much money are you prepared to give to Brussels in order to get a deal? No, I'm asking you for a figure. I'm asking you a very simple question. How much are you prepared to pay to get a deal? And you know what? Would a Corbyn Brexit mean lower immigration to this country from outside the EU? So you're not giving any guarantee on immigration? I'm asking you about immigration. So you're not making any promise about whether you would reduce immigration? Do you think there are too many immigrants in this country? Let's look at the security of the state, then. The primary duty of a prime minister, the overwhelming duty, is the protection of the state. And yet the only time in recent history that there has been an invasion of British soil was the invasion of the Falklands. And you recall what you said then, don't you? You said that young unemployed men were being sent to the South Atlantic to die in pursuit of a Tory plot. And as... Did you not think the Argentines should have got out of the Falklands? Because it's quite possible that in a couple of weeks' time, if you're sitting in Downing Street, the Chief of the Defence Staff could come to you and say, we have eyes on a man in Iraq or in Syria or in Afghanistan who is plotting a bombing campaign in Britain. you have 20 minutes to make up your mind as to whether we take him out with a drone strike. Now, if you think, do you think that's a Tory plot as well? It's happened before. You would be prepared to take such a decision? You would be prepared to take such a decision? But it's not really hypothetical. We have to look at the evidence that's there at the time to make that fatal decision one way or the other. Why then did you describe the killing of Osama bin Laden as a tragedy? Because I think he should have been arrested and I think he should have been put on trial. See, what bothers a lot of people is the friends that you have made in your campaign, doubtless heartfully felt for peace. Like Hamas, for example. You know that Hamas have killed civilians, and yet you call them your friends. You're happy to call Hamas your friends despite the fact they kill innocent women and children. Jeremy Corbyn, thank you. Coming next, the Prime Minister Theresa May is here and ready to meet our audience. Theresa May, when did you realise that you'd got the wrong answer to the biggest question of our times in politics? I'm just curious, what was it convinced you that Brexit was so bad for Britain? So you're now stuck with trying to deliver something you believe is bad for this country. Hang on a second. You said in March last year that we would be more secure, more prosperous and more influential, virtually in those words, if we stayed in the European Union. And now you want to take us out of it. You've changed your mind? And what I think... APPLAUSE OK, so you've changed your mind. That's all you can say, yes or no. I haven't changed my mind. Yes, I have changed my mind. But you don't really believe in it, do you? Let me look at it another way. How was it that people like Boris Johnson, Michael Gove, David Davis, Priti Patel, even Andrea Leadsom, they all got it right and you got it wrong? I said it was a balanced... You still think it's a duff idea, don't you? Well, of course you want to make a success of it. Can we look at something else that you've changed your mind on, or perhaps not changed your mind on? Social care. Now, you had a manifesto commitment which said there would be a cap on the cost of social care. Then your latest manifesto dropped that. And for clarification, the health secretary said there will be no cap. And four days later, you said there will be a cap. Okay, you have said earlier that it's very important that people can trust their politicians and their governments. Can I ask you, explicitly, unambiguously, what will the cap be? But you don't know. Have you even thought what the cost to the taxpayer will be? Just at a philosophical level, you said you went into politics to help the just about managing. How is enabling rich people to pass on wealth to the next generation helping the just about managing? That wasn't my question. No, my question was how it helped the just about managing. Can we look at something else on which you've changed your mind? Only this March, in the Budget, you proposed to increase national insurance contributions from the self-employed. Within a week, you backed down from that. You accept that? And we very clearly stated at the time... You see, what one's bound to say is, if I was sitting in Brussels and I was looking at you as the person I had to negotiate with, I'd think, she's a blowhard who collapses at the first sign of gunfire. Can you remind us how many times you told us, after you became Prime Minister, that there would not be a general election until 2020? You did, didn't you? I mean, I got to a grand total of six times on which you or your spokesman said there wouldn't be one. It was in March you told us we didn't need to have a general election because it would destabilise the country until 2020. Prime Minister, are you somebody who takes responsibility for their mistakes? Right. So you have been how many years in government now? In government? Yes. Seven years in government, and you'll recall the manifesto promises on immigration. Indeed I do. You've repeated them, in fact, in this manifesto. Yes, yes. Said the same thing again because you've failed In your capacity, six of those seven years, you were Home Secretary, weren't you? I was indeed. Yeah. Immigration was your main task, to get it down to what you promised in the manifesto, which was 100,000 or less non-EU immigrants per year net. You didn't do that, did you? We did... Can you just tell us what it is now? What we saw was the figures coming... Non-EU migrants? It's 175,000 according to the latest figures, yes. Who won't be able to come to this country under your new rules? So you still don't have any idea? And what's the damage going to be to the economy? Have you worked that out? Your friend George Osborne, or your friend until you sacked him, the Chancellor of the Exchequer, George Osborne, says this policy is economically illiterate. And you say it's much easier. It'll be much easier once we've left the European Union, won't it? How much of our money are you prepared to give Brussels in order to get a deal? I mean, David Davis said he'd walk away at 100,000. 100 million. 100 billion, sorry. Have you got a figure in your head as to how much it's worth paying to get out of this club? But you wouldn't walk out, as David Davis says he would, if there was a request for £100 billion? So you are prepared to walk away from the European Union with no deal. So you are prepared to leave the European Union with no deal? You are prepared to leave the European Union with no deal. So you are prepared to walk away? You can be as optimistic as you like, but you might have to walk away from it. But... Theresa May, thank you very much. Thank you.
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_10: Martin. Good evening, Prime Minister. As a serving police officer, I have been witness to the devastating effects of police cuts during your time as Home Secretary. And now, after the horrendous terrorist attacks in Manchester last Monday, we have seen the need to draft in the military. What promise can you make to increase the number of police officers? And can I ask you to provide us with a specific number of how many extra police officers that will be recruited under your government and how you propose to fund it? I appreciate you're protecting the budgets but we still need the staff to carry out the role of a police officer of keeping the public safe.
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_11: Prime Minister, in my first job 74 years ago, I owned the princely sum of 31 shillings a week, which is £1.55 in current money. I lived in a council house with my four siblings and my parents in the George Orwell's Wigan of the 1930s. Over the years, due to our hard work and the work of my late wife, we came to own our own home, and it is now mortgage-free. I don't like the prospect of being unable to leave it to our family with a greatly eroded value, which I fear is what would happen if you were to introduce your so-called dementia tax. So why, Prime Minister, should we, in my generation, vote for you?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_12: Good evening. Are we dividing England and Scotland by allowing a winter fuel allowance for over-60s in Scotland? Is this fair?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_13: Good evening. As a teacher and mother of two, I am incredibly concerned by your proposed changes to school funding, with my local constituency of Batley and Spen one of the worst affected areas. Prime Minister, despite your manifesto promises, these cuts are clearly unsustainable. Are you prepared to change your current plans to prevent further damage to our schools?
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_14: Theresa May, could you please justify to me why I have leaflets saved at home delivered to me by my local Conservatives before the EU referendum stating clearly that we will save £350 million per week for the NHS if we leave the EU. That lie is the only reason why I, and I'm sure many others here tonight possibly, decided to vote leave.
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_15: Prime Minister, I'm a midwife in Devon and I believe the wealth of a nation lies in the health of its workforce. How do you justify the chronic underfunding of the NHS that I see at work, considering that we now have one of the lowest budgets according to GDP? I see a lot of efficiency savings that are actually cuts. I see hospitals closing. I see staff that are at their wits' end because they can't give the care that they want to give. I'll believe it when I see it.
deb_2017_05_29.QUESTIONER_16: Hello. Prime Minister, describing yourself and being described as a bloody difficult woman may be helpful in Brexit negotiations with the EU, but do you think the same attitude when applied to domestic policy, such as welfare reform and cuts, have reinforced the image of the Conservatives being called the nasty party again?

deb_2017_05_31.WOOD: Theresa May called this election because she's taking you for granted. She won't turn up to these debates because her campaign of soundbites is falling apart. Unlike Theresa May, I'm not afraid to defend my policies, my principles, my values. Plaid Cymru exists to defend and build up our country. In Wales it is Plaid Cymru that stands for fairness and equality and everyone who chooses to call Wales home. For a hundred years Wales has voted Labour. It hasn't stopped Tory rule. Labour in Wales have their own manifesto and they're airbrushing their leader out of their campaign. When Plaid Cymru has tried to ban zero hours contracts and to stop the bedroom tax, Welsh Labour voted against us. It's time we stop doing what we've always done. Let's instead give Wales a voice. Give Plaid Cymru a mandate. Elect a strong team of Plaid Cymru MPs to fight for a successful future for Wales. I'd like to know what kinds of jobs all these extra people are in because we know that the numbers of people on zero hours contracts has gone through the roof. My party Plaid Cymru has tried in Wales on seven different occasions to abolish zero hours contracts only to be voted down by the Labour government in Wales but it's It is a scandal that those people, especially in the public sector, who are on the highest wages get paid pay increases. Politicians have had pay increases, yet those at the bottom have been squeezed. And it's immigrants then who are scapegoated and blamed for those wages being squeezed, when it's Tory austerity that is the real cause of that problem. That wasn't on the ballot paper, though. Hang on. Can I just say something? I've made a lie about holding a lie here. We want to maintain our economy, we want to make sure that... So you need more workers from outside Wales? Well, we're quite happy with the ones we've got. So why do you need a visa system to attract more workers to Wales then? Because Theresa May is going to end freedom of movement and that brings specific risks to the Welsh economy and the Welsh NHS. Well, UKIP has just claimed that people voted to leave the European Union, and in so doing, they also voted to curb immigration. I don't think we can read that into the result. Some people may well have voted to curb immigration, but there was only one question on the ballot paper, and immigration wasn't on it. And I'm afraid that UKIP keep using this issue. They want to whip up people's hatred, division, and fear. And that's why they talk about immigration all the time. I've got to come back to this. Right. Stop lying about immigration. Stop lying. Listen, listen, listen, listen. I'll tell you what, you're the coalition, and I'm the Tory UKIP coalition. Can I tell you what's chaotic? Jeremy, you have a Labour government. in Wales and they aren't doing those things. You have a Labour government in Wales that is charging students to go to university. Why are you saying this here when you have an example of a Labour government and it's not doing that? Are the Labour's different in UK and different in Wales? There does need to be some reviewing going on. We need to look at foreign policy. We need to look at the prevent strategy. It is a fact that policing has been cut by 20%, including when Theresa May was in the Home Office. And it's a question of priorities. We should be investing in police and other public services. Those are the people running into the dangerous situation when everyone else is running away. Well, I think in terms of the cuts to public services, that's had an impact. If you just take one small example, youth work. When you had a well-funded youth service, there were youth workers available to challenge the ideology of young people. I'm saying austerity has cut youth workers and there are fewer people around to challenge the root cause, the ideology that spurs these people on. I used to work as a probation officer. Can I just finish making this point? Of course. I used to work as a probation officer. If we are interested in tackling the root cause of this problem, we have to understand what it is. We have to understand those ideological drivers. And they have to be challenged by people qualified to be able to do that. And by cutting youth workers and the public services, then you are reducing your ability to do that. And that is one of the reasons we are less safe. We have a small amount of time left. Tell him he's wrong. Tell him he is wrong. This is terrible leadership on the part of Donald Trump. It's also appalling leadership. on behalf of the Prime Minister, who can't even be bothered to come here and answer these questions for herself today. But it's no surprise either. He was very clear during his election campaign what it was he would be about in terms of climate change, and he is sticking to his line. But he is wrong, and the world needs to tell him he is wrong. It's bad business. It's bad, bad business. Angus Robertson. We all know about blokes like you. You want a free divorce? It doesn't happen that way. In the real world there's no such thing as a free divorce. You have to pay your dues. I believe leaders should walk the walk and should be prepared to defend their politics and their policies. They should also be prepared to stick to their guns. And it's shocking that Theresa May has u-turned on so many things. There wasn't going to be an election. Now there's an election. There wasn't going to be a tax on the self-employed, or there was going to be a tax on the self-employed, and then it was abolished. And then there's this latest u-turn on the dementia tax. So I'm not one for U-turns. Plaid Cymru will stick to our manifesto promises and Plaid Cymru will lead for Wales. Thank you. Leanne Wood. Tonight you've heard that Plaid Cymru is the only party putting Wales on the agenda during this election. But our MPs will play their part for the whole of the UK. We will stand up for justice and fair play on pensions, on social care and for our cash-starved public services. So I urge people back home to give us the mandate we need to defend Wales. Liking us isn't enough. We need your support and your vote. Don't let our country be invisible in Westminster. Vote Plaid. Vote for Wales.
deb_2017_05_31.LUCAS: On June the 8th, you can lay the foundations for a new kind of politics. Your vote can change everything. Imagine this, a country that leads the world in clean energy, not fracks its way to climate chaos. A country that spends its money on hospitals and schools, not nuclear bombs. A country that welcomes child refugees, not blocks them at the border. A country that's open to its neighbours, not isolated from them. A country that celebrates diversity, not fears it. A country that's friends with the good guys, not the climate criminals and the dodgy dictators. A country that cares for those with dementia, not one that taxes them. We've shown time and again, you don't need the keys to number 10 to open the door to change. On June the 8th, let's unlock that door together. Fearless Green MPs in opposition to the Tories. Independent Green MPs holding Labour's feet to the fire. Pioneering Green MPs to make our economy fit for tomorrow. We decide the future by what we do now. I want to come back to Nicola's point because I think the situation that you described Nicola is something that people feel up and down this country you know and for Amber to say that this is a government that actually cares for those most vulnerable I think is downright insulting to the kind of people that I see in my constituency surgery. Letters from a man who can't actually get into the surgery because he hasn't got his mobility scooter and he hasn't had it for six weeks because the DLA payment going on to PIP has now been delayed. He is just stuck there. You know, I think there is just simply not a recognition from this government about the pain that it is inflicting on some of the most vulnerable people in our society. And it is a matter of political choices, because we are the fifth biggest economy in the world. And yet, we are a country that has four million children living in poverty. We are a country where a million food parcels were given out last year. That is just simply quite wrong. And so the Green Party is quite upfront about saying, We would make those with the broadest shoulders pay more. We would reverse the changes in corporation tax, which has allowed Theresa May to come up with this vision of the country as some kind of bargain basement tax haven. Corporations should be paying their way as well. On the subject of the right of EU nationals who've made their lives here in good faith to stay here, I just think it is unbelievably cruel to be using those lives as bargaining chips in these negotiations. The government right now could be saying, those people should be able to stay right now. But I want to be able to make the case proudly for Free movement. I think free movement has been the most wonderful gift, the ability to travel and work and live and love in 27 other member states, and for them to come here. And I have to say, I am sorry that the Labour Party now doesn't support that, because I think our country is enriched by people coming from other countries. I think that the Britain that I love is a confident, outward-facing country, recognising that migration makes a massive, positive contribution. And I think right now we have people like Paul and his hate-filled rhetoric. will make people feel, that makes people feel as if the reason that they can't get a leg on the housing ladder or the reason they can't see a GP is because of migration. It is not because of migration, it is because successive governments haven't invested enough in our public services. If you are trying to see someone in the NHS, you are more likely to find a migrant actually treating you as a doctor than a head of you in the queue. Jeremy Corbyn, could you respond to the point on giving up on freedom of movement? What is chaotic? What is chaotic, Amber, is you and UKIP arm in arm. It is a thickening prospect. And you stood on a platform just about a year ago saying that you thought that Britain would be safer, stronger and better off in the EU. Now were you wrong then or are you wrong now? Because we can't trust you on this. Well, the first thing to say is that we would stop spending money on things that we simply don't think is a good use of money. And Trident Nuclear Weapons would come pretty close to the top of that list. So we would scrap nuclear weapons. That would give us around 130 billion minimum over the next 30 years. We wouldn't be building HS2. We wouldn't be going ahead giving massive subsidies to Hinkley Nuclear Power Station. So first of all, the thing you do is to stop wasting money. And then you have a principle that says that those people who have more money, who have the broadest shoulders, should be giving more into our system. So essentially what we need to be doing is to be levying things like a wealth tax, looking at issues around, as I said before, corporation tax. I think it's wrong that we're going for this bargain basement tax haven. I think we should be going for a country where corporations pay their tax. But the bottom line is that this country is not a poor country. The money is in the wrong hands. There is vast inequality in this country. And if we were to sort that problem out, then we would have a much better chance of ensuring that public services are properly paid for. Take the NHS, for example. We put far less into the NHS than most other countries of a similar GDP, the similar wealth. We don't put that money in our public services. And therefore, we don't have the world-class public services that we demand. The Greens are really clear that for us, public services should be in public hands. They should be not having the private sector in them. And they should be properly resourced. They want our safety as much as anybody else. Caroline Lucas. Thank you to David for the question and it's an incredibly important question. Let me be very clear to begin with that people who commit the kind of atrocities like the one in Manchester are barbaric and what they stand for is evil. But the best form of defense against attacks like that is intelligence-led policing and community engagement. And the kind of response we've just heard from Paul, which seems to suggest that the violence in Manchester was somehow representative of Islam, I think is completely outrageous. There is no more representative of Islam than from the murderer of Joe Cox. was representative of the wider British public. So listen, I'm deeply concerned about the police cuts we've already talked about and it's interesting that the Police Federation warned that those police cuts could be very dangerous. But I also think it's right that we review our interventions overseas. I think it's a disservice to democracy to pretend there's no link and to close down the debate. The former head of MI5 herself has said that the invasion of Iraq exacerbated the terror threat to the UK and was, in her words, highly significant in terms of homegrown extremists. But what I want to say actually is something to Amber, because we can't solve all the world's problems, but we can at least stop adding to them. And my question is this really, that why is Britain the second biggest arms dealer in the world? Why are we selling to 22 of the 30 countries on the government's own human rights watch list? Why did we make 10 times more in arms sales to Saudi Arabia than we gave to Yemen in aid? Arms sales to Saudi Arabia cannot be justified on the grounds of this being good for industry. No. This is absolutely nothing. Thank you. Caroline Lucas. I just want to thank that so much for that question, because the Green Party has been trying to get climate change onto this election campaign agenda for the past six weeks. It has been impossible. So thank you so much for that. It is a vital question. It is the greatest threat that we face. And I think, first of all, in terms of what we do to President Trump, I'm trying to think of a polite answer. But I think essentially what we need to do actually is ignore him, because the economics behind renewables It's already such that we know that renewables are going to be cheaper than the fossil fuels that they already replace. So that revolution is already happening. But what we need to do in this country is an awful lot more than is being done by this government or, indeed, previous ones. First of all, we need to leave 2 thirds of all known fossil fuel fossil fuels in the ground if we're to have any hope of avoiding catastrophic climate change. That means a massive investment in renewable energy, energy efficiency. Amber Rudd, of course, was the Energy Secretary for a while, but under her we did not see the kind of transition we urgently need to see if we're serious about climate change. So we do need to see keeping fossil fuels in the ground, not giving fossil fuel subsidies. We need to see no fracking, not the kind of frack tracking that is being planned all around the country under this government. Do you think he's doing the right thing, then? I don't know why I'm moving. I think the first rule of leadership is to show up. You don't call a general election. You don't call a general election and say it's the most important election in her lifetime, and then not even be bothered to come and debate the issues at stake. Now, I feel quite unique here tonight as a co-leader, and I'm proud that the Green Party pioneers new ways of doing things. We want to see job sharing in all kinds of aspects of our lives, because we believe that two heads are often better than one, And so I'm very happy to be there with my co-leader, Jonathan Bartley. But what leadership is about, it is about listening. It is about recognizing that all of us have leadership qualities. And in particular, I think it's about trusting the public. And on that issue, I do not understand why this government will not allow the people to have the final say on the EU referendum. Why can we not have a ratification referendum when we see the final deal of what Theresa May or whoever it is brings back from Brussels? Why can we not trust the British people to have that final say? If they like what they see, that's great. But if they don't, the way to have another look at that is by voting Green, because we will say on that ballot paper that you should have the right to remain in the EU if that's what you choose when you see the small print. Because since this referendum was called, We have so many lies told. Tonight, this country stands at a crossroads and the choice is stark. You can stand up for what really matters, values of openness, tolerance and compassion. Or you can turn inward to isolation, division and hate. You know, one more MP from the other parties makes no real difference. But more Green MPs would be truly transformative. On June the 8th, I urge you, don't look back. Choose what kind of future you really want. Vote for change. Vote Green. And together, let's build a caring and a more confident Britain. Jeremy Corbyn.
deb_2017_05_31.RUDD: In eight days, you have a vital choice to make about who you want to lead the country. Do you want Theresa May and her team? A team that has a plan, and it's one that doesn't duck the hard choices about living within our means, but is also a plan that will build on the success we have delivered. Success in cutting the deficit, stabilising the economy, getting more people into employment. It will build on our commitment to having a country that works for everyone. Now, there are seven of us here tonight, and I'm sure you will hear plenty of bluff, bravado, and tempty, shiny election promises. But the only question to consider is who should be in number 10 to steer Britain to a brighter future. Jeremy Corbyn, with his money tree wish list manifesto and no plan for Brexit, or Theresa May and her record of delivery with her clear plan for Brexit and the strong team behind her who can make sure the country gets to that brighter future. And next, it's Jeremy Corbyn. Well, thank you Nicola for the question. My party has presided over making sure that people on lower wages are able to keep more of the wages they earn. We must take it within context. Since 2009 we had, up from the run to 2009, we had one of the largest peacetime recessions. and we've managed to rebuild the economy since then. But my party is focused on making sure that we help people like Nicola, which is why we're going to continue to take people out of taxes, making sure that the amount you can earn before you have to pay tax rises to £12,500 and we're going to live within our means. Some of the offers you will hear tonight are just fanciful. In order to have a strong economy, to support Nicola and others, we need to make sure we have that strong economy. And the way to make sure that we have it is to have a strong negotiating position as we go into leaving the European Union. You're going to hear me say that a lot tonight because the worst outcome would be if we're not able to negotiate a strong deal with the European Union. That changes about political changes. On the direct point of payments to help people who are disabled, we are a party who will always support those in most need and the Welfare Bill for helping people on disabilities has gone up 7 billion in the past seven years and is now at 50 billion. We will always provide that safety net where it's needed. Jeremy, I know there is no extra payment you don't want to add to, no tax you don't want to rise, but the fact is we have to concentrate our resources on the people who need it most and we have to stop thinking, as you do, that there's a magic money tree. You have to be accountable for the money you want to spend. Well, I would add to the long list of criticisms that Caroline has put out before us that there are now 800,000 fewer households that have workless households in them. We have focused on making sure that people can get into their work. But work doesn't pay numbers. Work doesn't pay numbers. Caroline, Caroline, we need to have a system. You cannot ignore the fact that over the past seven years, there have been another 3 million people that have got into jobs. You attack the reductions in corporation tax. You talk about corporations. I talk about jobs. What we need to have is the investment to make sure that people can get those jobs, so they can have the dignity of the jobs, so they can feed their family, and they can work and achieve positive jobs. What kind of jobs? I mean, nice try. I'd like to answer your attack. I'd like to answer your attack. Of course, I have been to food banks. And the way to have people out of food banks, the way to have people not using food banks is to make sure that they can have a good job to go to. I'm sure there will be an opportunity to debate that, but I just have to take on some of Jeremy Corbyn's fantasy economics. I mean, he has this monetary wish list in his manifesto. It's very easy to think about how you spend money. It's much harder to think about how you raise money. And his proposals don't add up. It's as though he thinks it's some sort of game, a game of Monopoly, perhaps, where you ask the banker for the red money to buy the electrics, the green money to buy the railways, and the yellow money to buy the gasworks. Well, it's not like that, Jeremy. This is people's hard-earned money, and we will protect that. We won't roll the dice. Immigration is an important part of building our country, strengthening our economy and supplying the additional support that we need to our schools. But we have to make sure we have an immigration policy that we can control. We have said, the Conservative Party and the government, that we will continue to reduce those numbers. And as we leave the European Union, we'll have more chance to do that and to be able to decide who comes to this country. But be in no doubt, we will always have an immigration policy that, although it continues to reduce, will attract the brightest and the best, and will make sure that people can come here to support our economy and develop businesses here. Well, absolutely untrue. What we're concerned about are the 4 million. So there's 3 million here and there's 1 million UK citizens in the rest of the European Union. We need to make sure that we get a deal to protect all their rights. But as all this squabbling was going on, I was thinking about June the 9th and I was thinking about the different leaders who've had discussions about so-called progressive alliance. And I was thinking how chaotic it would be if they all got together, formed a coalition and tried to run a government. What I'm consistent about, Caroline, is accepting the result of a referendum. I know that for Angus, there's no referendum result. He will accept. Everyone seems to be wrong to him. But the fact is, we had this debate last year. There has been a result. And I want to make sure that we get the right result, that under Theresa May, we will negotiate. Why is she here to defend herself? Well, I would say in answer to that question, judge us on our record. On our record, we have We have cut the deficit, we've reduced taxes for the lowest paid, and we've made sure that we've continued to invest in the NHS, which is getting another £8 billion a year by the end of this Parliament. But the only way we can have that money to invest in our public services, and we know the importance of our public services, our schools, our hospitals, is to make sure that we have a strong economy, which does sometimes main making difficult choices, those sort of choices that no other party here is prepared to face up to. We have to make sure that we control our spending so we have that strong economy. But the sure way to bust our economy, to lead to danger, is to have false negotiations with the EU, to have the sort of leader like Jeremy Corbyn, supported perhaps by the people here, who would create just confusion when trying to deal with the 27 different states. OK, don't give up on me quite yet, Angus. It's only halfway through the debate. Theresa may not be here, but I hope to make a good fist of setting out the Conservative Party policy. We've made a clear decision to make sure that we protect the poorest in our society. Which means that the pensioners will be protected, that winter fuel payment won't be paid to millionaires. No, it'll be focused. So where will the levels be put then? Where will the levels be put? Under a Labour government, the pensioners saw a rise in their pensions of 85p in one year. Under this government, we've seen pensioners' pay rise by over £1,000. Are you going to protect the triple lock? No, we've said very clearly. Are you going to protect the triple lock? Jeremy, have you not read my manifesto? I'm happy to give you a copy afterwards. I'd love to have one, but I'd like the answer now. We have said we will consult on that but I can tell you very clearly that the billionaires will not be given it. But Jeremy has particularly asked me about looking after pensioners and I would like to answer that. We will always look after pensioners, ensuring they have the dignity and security in retirement. And we have made sure, we have committed to making sure, that their pension will always be uprated according with earnings or inflation. That is so different to the commitment they got under Labour. Tim Farron. Thank you. It's a really important question, because the first job of any government is to keep its citizens safe and secure. But I'm going to level with you. Since 2014, we have been at the threat level of severe, which means an attack is highly likely. As Home Secretary for the past year, I've seen the evidence, I've seen the warrants crossing my desk, I spend about two hours a day looking at them, seeing the real damage that some of these terrorists want to do us. The way we try to stop them is by supporting our security services and our counter-terrorism police. The best way to try and intervene in those plots is to make sure that they are well funded and we've increased their budget significantly since 2015 and we hope to further on. No, but the fact is there were reductions in police budget because we had to make changes in order to live within our means after 2010. But the police responded incredibly well. Crime has fallen by a third between 2010 and 2015. And we've protected the police budget going forward. So government can make sure that we live within our means and protect the communities. But particularly on terrorism, if I may, we have to make sure we also have the right legislation. And I am shocked that Jeremy Corbyn just in 2011 boasted that he had opposed every piece of anti-terror legislation in his 30 years. in office. And I really think he must be held accountable for that, because I find it chilling. I will make no apology for being a government that wants to defend this country. We will make sure that our defense budget is well funded. We will do that by having a strong economy. You were selling arms to Saudi Arabia? We have to make sure that we can do that by having a strong industry. I'm going to answer the first question, if I may, about the Paris climate change agreement. And I must tell you, I am disappointed. Caroline is right. I led the UK delegation to get that international agreement of 180 different countries pledging to that reduction. And it is disappointing that the Americans are pulling out. But I hope that we can use our relationship with President Trump and our close relationship with the US to try and influence, to try and make sure they nevertheless take the right step. That's not quite how diplomacy works. The reason why I am optimistic is because there has been such substantial investment in renewable energy. And yes, in solar, it is increasingly doesn't need a subsidy. So because of that investment, it makes good economic sense as well as good renewable energy. Yes, part of being a good leader is having a good strong team around you and I'm proud and delighted to be here representing the Conservative Party and the Prime Minister making that case. Let's face it, Jeremy only decided to come I think late this morning. I was rather hoping Diane Abbott might be here so I could debate with her as well. But the fact is the most important challenge that this government is going to have going forward is getting a good negotiation with the EU. It's going to dictate whether we have a strong economy, whether we can pay good wages to our public servants and support our public services. Theresa May has the support of her team. She has the support of whoever becomes her MPs. Jeremy Corbyn has had a no-confidence vote against him, which four out of five of his MPs wouldn't support him on. How can he go in to negotiate with 27 different countries with such a weak team and weak support behind him? Theresa May will be able to deliver for us. As Home Secretary, she delivered 35 different opt-outs. She knows how to negotiate and deliver for the European Union. She'll be the right leader for us. Thank you, Tim, for that pass. So you've heard the squabbling and discord of disagreement here tonight. You've seen the coalition of chaos in action. But in the quiet of the polling booth, you have a clear choice. A vote for anyone other than Theresa May is a vote for Jeremy Corbyn and that coalition. Our government needs to be at its strongest to take us through Brexit. It is only Theresa May that can deliver that leadership.
deb_2017_05_31.CORBYN: Tonight I am here to debate the future of our country. The question in this election is whether we want a country for the many or just a few. The state of our NHS, our children's schools, social care for older people, our young people saddled with debt, none of that is remotely strong or stable. And now the Conservatives want five more years of cuts to our vital public services to fund tax handouts for the wealthy few. Labour will make very different choices. There will be no tax rises for 95% of taxpayers. But we will ask those with the most to contribute a bit more so no one is held back from achieving their potential. On June the 8th, you have a choice. More cuts in services and living standards with the Conservatives or vote Labour to transform Britain for the many, not the few. Living standards have fallen in the past seven years under the coalition government with the Liberal Democrats and the Tories and now under the Tory government itself. Six million people are earning less than the living wage. A million are on zero-hours contracts. Public sector workers have had a pay cut of at least 14% over the past seven years. We will lift the pay cap on the public sector. We will introduce a living wage of £10 an hour by 2020 and we won't punish workers who want to take their rights to a tribunal where they have to pay charges and fees. We will abolish that. Our determination is that those who produce the wealth of this country should benefit from the wealth of this country. What we've seen is a massive gap growing between those at work and those at the top. It's times 186 between those on the shop floor and those in the boardroom. That has to change and we will ensure it does. Amber, you've tried to take personal independence payments away from people with disabilities and then you turned yourselves around in a few days on that. You're not credible on this issue. Paul, that is complete nonsense, if I may say so. Our corporation tax has been consistently lowered by this government with the support of the Liberal Democrats since 2010. What we're proposing is to put it up during the lifetime of Parliament to 26%. That is less than it was in 2010. And if you put that corporation tax up, you're then in a position to deal with the crisis in social care, the crisis in our NHS, properly fund our schools, and not ask headteachers to collect Jeremy Corbyn. Jeremy. And I would just say this since Amber Rudd seems so confident that this is a country at ease with itself. Have you been to a food bank? Have you seen people sleeping around our station? Have you seen... Jeremy, I need to answer that. Well what we've had is a government for the past seven years that has borrowed more, run up a greater deficit and cut public services at the same time and at the lowest growth rate of any industrial country. The system they have is not working. What we're proposing is to raise corporation tax, yes, in order to make the funding commitments that I've already outlined, but it's also to establish a serious national investment bank to invest in infrastructure all around this country so we do get a sustainable growing economy. We cannot go on giving money away to the very rich. This government's proposing another 60 billion in tax giveaways in the next five years. Instead, I say turn it round and invest in the future of all of our people. What will happen on leaving the European Union is that we cease to be members of the single market and therefore free movement ends. What we're saying is that every EU national that is resident in this country must be given permanent rights of residence here and not blocked for it by this government. And secondly, that we would ask, as we've already done in opposition, that all European governments do the same for British nationals that are living there. And that we recognise the massive contribution made by people to this country have come from all over the world, as well as from Europe, to our health service, our education, our industries, our universities and our science. We will have a fair and managed migration system that is based on the needs of this country and the rights of family reunion. Fair is where you bring people in when they've got jobs to come to or it's necessary for them to be working here or we need them to assist in the economy. That is fair but it's also important that we maintain the links of universities with universities across Europe. This government Is it going to go on? And Theresa May sent vans around Brent telling people to go home as referral to allow refugees and children in. There are people in this city leaving. The problems that we have of exploitation of workers in this country and groups of workers that have been brought in wholesale from lower paid economies in Central Europe is a serious one. Those people are themselves grossly exploited. They're brought here to undermine existing pay and working conditions and create... Hang on, can I finish? That will make the situation worse in any community where that takes place. I was demanding that we have a proper regulation whilst we were still members of the European Union before the referendum. That whole strategy has to stop. There has to be a managed migration that isn't designed to undermine working conditions in this country. I'm very clear that we will negotiate tariff-free trade access to European markets so that our manufacturing industry jobs are defended and supported and we have a growing economy as a result of that. We have made a number of very clear spending commitments in our manifesto. They're set out here in a spending plan. They're also set out in the manifesto itself. The government, the Conservative government, has made a lot of choices. We know what those choices are. Our schools are underfunded. Our hospitals are overcrowded. Our students are saddled with debt. There's a growing housing crisis. And at every single turn, their answer is to further reduce corporation tax and further reduce tax for the very wealthiest in our society. I'm absolutely sure that they add up. absolutely clearly about this. For example, if we want to remove university tuition fees, and we do, so our students are not saddled with debt, and restore maintenance grants, so that all children, whatever their background, have a chance of going to university, that will cost 11 billion pounds. I think that is money well spent. I think that is the kind of investment this country needs. Why should... Why should... Why should... Why should a child's future be determined by the postcode in which they're born? That's why we are determined to bring in free preschool 30 hours a week from two to four-year-olds and make sure that every primary school child gets a free lunch every day so they can learn while they're properly fed. This is a UK government and a UK government policy. It's going to be a UK policy and it will mean that the money is made available there by increasing tax, not for the first 95% of the population, but the other 5% to ensure the funds are there. Because I am fed up with the idea that so many children growing up in poorer households and poorer communities don't get the same chances as others to get to university and get on in life. They lose and we all lose because we lose those skilled, brilliant people that want to contribute to our community. Can you now admit that you're protecting the triple lock? Are you going to protect the triple lock? No, they're going to get rid of it, Jeremy. What happened in Manchester was unbelievably abominable in every way. Innocent lives were taken of mainly young women out enjoying themselves. I want to live in a country that protects the right of people to go out and enjoy themselves at any town or any city in this country. The consequences of that are appalling for those families, and I hope those that are perpetrated this act are all discovered and brought to book. But there's also a question we have to ask about the number of police officers. Under Amber Rudd's time in the Home Secretary and Theresa May before that, 20,000 police officers have lost their jobs. There are fewer police officers around than there were seven years ago, and those cuts are going to continue. I think it's important that we restore that. What I also think, if I may say so, is the point that Angus was alluding to about terror threats and other threats to our security. There are terrorist threats, obviously. There are also cyber attacks threats, as the National Health Service discovered. It's also important that we recognize that leaving large spaces of, for example, Libya, ungoverned, leaves an opportunity for those who wish to do harm to an awful lot of people, a space in which to develop to do that harm. If you intervene somewhere, the consequences go on for a very long time. No, I'm absolutely not saying that. The attack in Manchester happened because somebody decided that they wanted to go and kill a large number of people for some perverted belief in their minds. They have to be dealt with and those people that were under threat have to be protected. What I'm also saying is though, it would be extremely unwise for any government to ignore, any government anywhere in the world, to ignore what is happening in Libya where large areas are left ungoverned and very dangerous forces are arising there. What we need to do is recognise the human rights and justice of people all around the world and be prepared to support them, not just go in and bomb and do nothing about it afterwards. No, I said 20,000 have been cut by the Conservative government. We would put 10,000 more on. What I would also want to add, and I think it's important, is the response of people in this country to what happened in Manchester. I was in Manchester straight after the event took place, and Tim was there as well, and Amber. The most amazing sense of community unity came together. They were not going to allow that individual who killed those people to divide our communities. They were going to be united and responsible. Can I just remind you that in 2005, Theresa May voted against the anti-terrorist legislation at that time. And she voted against it, as did David Davis, as did a number of people that are now in your cabinet, because they felt that that legislation was giving too much executive power. My opposition to anti-terror legislation isn't opposition to protecting us from terrorism, it is simply saying there must be judicial oversight over what is done in our name. You cannot give... I have made that very clear, right from the Prevention of Terrorism Act... You cannot defend your record, Jeremy. The response of the people of Manchester was absolutely magnificent. All communities of all faiths came together and I utterly deplore the language that Paul Nuttall uses and the subliminal attack the whole time on people of Muslim faith in what you say. You invited Hamas to the House of Commons and called them your friends. Paul, if I may just complete a sentence. You invited Hamas to the House of Commons. I think we have to recognize we live in a multi-faith, multicultural society. An attack on any religion is totally unacceptable. Thank you. And we should recognize that we should bring people together and have much stronger communities as a result. We should absolutely adhere to the Paris climate change agreement. And we should urge the American people to press their government, their Senate, and their House, and their president to adhere to it as well. In this country, we are determined to achieve 60% of renewable energy by 2030. We're also determined to reduce emissions. But it's not just about climate change emissions. It's also about our attitude towards the environment, levels of air quality, and air pollution in our cities, which are not being addressed by this government. And also, when we did have a growing and thriving solar power industry in this country, what did they do? cut the feed-in tariffs, which destroyed and damaged a lot of that industry. What about air pollution? Can we take the court over air pollution? Leadership is about understanding the people you represent, is about being prepared to learn, is about not being so high and mighty you can't take advice. It is also about bringing people with you. It is also about ensuring that your responsibilities protect the safety and security of everybody in this country and to lead a government that cares for everybody in the country and doesn't walk by on the other side when there are people that are homeless, there are people that are starving and we have millions of our children living in poverty. It's leadership to lead a government that is prepared to say to our society we cannot go on like this, we have to start putting more money into our public services and our resources. We have to have an economy that works for all and we don't have to have a spiv economy that hands tax relief to the biggest corporations and the wealthiest people whilst ignoring the desperate cries for social help of so many people in our country. 300,000 people elected me to lead this party, and I'm very proud to lead this party. Tonight, we've seen the real choice facing our country. between a Labour government and a Conservative government. This election will decide whether young people are saddled with debt or freed from it, whether we invest in our National Health Service, schools and social care, or they continue to be cut, whether older people get the dignity they deserve or see their incomes fall. On June the 8th, you have the power to decide. Vote Labour for the many, not the few.
deb_2017_05_31.NUTTALL: UKIP will always put the interests of British people first, whether that is prioritising our NHS and social care over a bloated and ineffective foreign aid programme, or protecting those who are most at risk from wage compression because of unskilled mass immigration. UKIP will always stand up for those who have been let down and left behind by the Westminster elites. A government's job, first and foremost, is to protect the public. Our police, our intelligence services, our armed forces and our border force must be given the tools and the resources they need to do their jobs effectively. But money isn't everything. Politicians must show leadership. They must, without fear or favour, ensure that our way of life, our values and our democracy is secure. I believe in our great country. I believe in British values. I believe in our way of life. And as you will see tonight, UKIP believes in Britain. Paul Nuffel. Well, wages have stagnated over the past 10 years. There's a number of reasons for that. Firstly, there was the economic crash. But secondly, we've had an oversupply of labor. And that certainly is the case in working class communities. The Bank of England have admitted that that is so. But what we need to do is we need to put more money in people's pockets. And you don't do that through the politics of jealousy or spite. What we need to do is to reduce taxation. So for example we propose that we would scrap VAT on fuel bills and do away with green levies which will put £170 extra back in your pocket per annum. We would also raise the personal allowance to £13,500. Again, because we believe that people know how to spend their money better than any government. And as for corporation tax, no, we want to see corporation tax reduced, not raised. Because if you raise it, companies will leave this country. If they leave this country, what happens then? There's less jobs. There's less taxation. They're leaving because of Brexit. Therefore, you put people out of work. I'm sorry. They're starting to leave because of Brexit. The politics of spite and jealousy does not work. I'm sorry, if you want to take us back to the 1970s, then, you know, it'll be bad for the economy. Corporation tax in Ireland is £12.5 billion. Businesses will leave this country and if businesses leave the country... They're leaving already because of Brexit, Paul. If businesses leave, there's less tax, there's less jobs and more people will be unemployed. That's what you'll get if you vote Labour. Very easily, because we then can control our own borders. And when people went out last year and voted on June the 23rd, they didn't just vote to control borders, they voted to reduce immigration as well. Because, you know, last week it was announced that a city the size of Hull came to this country. Hang on. Hold on. A city the size of Hull came to this country, net. That's going to be a Birmingham over a five-year period. It's unsustainable. What we need to do is we need to reduce immigration and we do it by having an Australian points-based system. So if you've got the skills that this country needs, yes, please come here and work. But beyond that, we have to get the population under control. Because if we carry on on the roads we're on, we'll have a population of 80 million by the middle of this century. Now you just think what will happen. There'll have to be a huge school building programme. There'll have to be new hospitals, new motorways, a new rail network, new houses. We're already having to build a house every seven minutes simply to keep up with the numbers of people coming to this country. Reduce immigration, have a points-based system. It will be good for the economy, and it will be good for social cohesion. This isn't about immigrants. It's about government policy. And the governments have got it wrong time and time again. Amber, for example, her party, have said in three manifestos now that they're going to get immigration down to the tens of thousands. You know, it's Jack and Ori. It's not going to happen. Jeremy won't give you a figure because under Labour, immigration will go up. There's only one party on this platform tonight who will see real cuts to immigration, which, by the way, is what the majority of British people want. We've seen that. The question was, how would you raise the money? We've been quite detailed about how we raise the money. Firstly, we'd scrap HS2, which is only there for the benefit of one place and one place alone, and that is London. We'd also look at the Barnard formula, which gives the Scots 1,700 pound per head more than us, the English. It needs to be scrapped. We'd also get an EU dividend. And finally, finally, finally, We would slash a foreign aid budget which is costing the British people £30 million every single day. And we would transfer that money to the National Health Service and that's why, because we're prepared Because we're prepared to look at different priorities, we can give the NHS an extra £9 billion a year. £2 billion extra for social care. He said, let's privatise it. And in the areas of procurement, I think I was right. I think the NHS could do better. However, what I will say is we're going into this and the last general election with a clear commitment to keep the NHS in public hands and put £9 billion extra in. And that's taking money from a foreign aid budget, as I say, which is costing us at the present moment £13 billion every single year. Paul Nussel. Well, it's quite clear that the war in Iraq and the bombing of Libya was fundamentally wrong. Because all we were doing is we were removing, yes, evil dictators. But it was quite clear that what was going to come next was going to be even worse. But that isn't, our foreign policy isn't an excuse for what went on in Manchester. And what politicians need to do is at least have the courage to name what it is. It's Islamist extremists. Sorry, OK? It's Islamist extremists. but nobody seems to have the courage to say what it is. How do we solve it? Well we put 20,000 police officers back on the beat, 4,000 more on the border force, we tighten our borders, 7,000 new prison officers because radicalisation is rife within our prisons and I must say I can't believe that we've allowed 350 jihadis to return to our country from Libya and Syria. I'm sorry, if you go out and fight or support Islamic State, you should have your passport revoked and you should never be allowed back into this country. Now, what we've also got to do is look at radicalisation within our own mosques. And I will say, Amber, I think you need to look at Saudi and Qatari funding of mosques in this country. And finally, we need to get the Muslim community itself to sign up to the Prevent programme. Only one out of eight referrals to Prevent come from within the Muslim community. And our best response to terrorists is the defiance of keeping on. I mean, of course. I mean, the vast majority of Muslims in this country are peaceful. They add to our economy. They're great for our culture. But there is a tiny minority, a tiny minority within that community who hate who we are, hate the way we live, hate our democracy. And I've called it a cancer in the past. Radical Islam is a cancer, and it needs to be cut out. If not, there'll be more attacks like that. Well, frankly, I've said nothing should be taken off the table. As far as I'm concerned, you know, when MI5 tell us that there's a possible 23,000 jihadis out there who want to do us harm, I'm sorry, but I would always... Let me finish. Let's not give away our freedom. I would always put British lives over the human rights of any jihadi any day. Let him finish, Paul. Thank you. Well, President Trump is doing what he set out to do. I mean, he is the leader of the free world, whether we like it or not. He said during his election campaign that he would withdraw from the Paris Agreement. What he's doing is he's looking at India and he's looking at China, who've got huge coal-fired power station building programs, which are taking place at this present moment in time. He's looking at the US economy, and he promised to look after the Rust Belt and working class people. And he knows that if they reduce energy bills, and they will do that by withdrawing from the Paris Agreement, then he will protect the American economy. He's putting America first. And frankly, I think Britain should put Britain first as well. Well, you know, we are only to blame for 2% of global emissions. And what's going to happen is if energy prices continue to go up, companies will move to countries with lower environmental standards. Well, I've never changed my stance, pretty much, on anything. I don't flip-flop. I've always been, for example, a Brexiteer. And look, you know, back when I joined UKIP, you don't join UKIP for a career. You join it because you've got principles. And our principle was simply this. We wanted to get our country out of the European Union. And we were very successful on June the 23rd. last year. Now, what we've got to do is we've got to ensure that we get the Brexit that people voted for. A Brexit whereby we control our own borders and cut immigration. We get full control of our waters back and our fisheries. We take full control of our laws and bring them back to Westminster. We leave the ECJ and indeed the ECHR. We're free to sign our own trade deals all over the globe and we're out of the single market. And, most importantly, we pay no divorce bill to the European Union. Because since we've been members, since 1973, we've paid £183 billion net in membership fee alone. I'd be strong enough going into these negotiations. I just hope the Prime Minister is as strong. Good question. We have given that club £183 billion since 1973. We've got £9 billion tied up in the European Investment Bank and indeed we own lots of it. We all know about blokes like you. Listen, don't talk down this country. We are the fifth largest economy in the world. But this, look, look, look, this is all about, listen, listen, this is all about trade. And we have a huge trade deficit with the EU. They need us more than we need them. For God's sake, let's be confident about ourselves. Tonight's been a little bit like Groundhog Day for me because you've heard the same arguments, the same excuses, the same platitudes a hundred times before. UKIP will always be the outsider in British politics. It'll be the one that the Westminster elite and the establishment media want to mock and ridicule. But we have been proven right. Proven right on Brexit. Proven right on immigration. Proven right on grammar schools. Proven right on protecting our police and security services. On June 8th, it's your decision, your country, your vote. Vote UKIP.
deb_2017_05_31.ROBERTSON: Good evening. This election is about the kind of country we want to be. Now, more than ever, Scotland needs strong SNP voices at Westminster. And SNP MPs will work with others to promote fairness across all of the UK. A vote for the SNP is a vote against Tory cuts. that will harm our public services and push many more hard-working families into poverty. It's a vote for jobs and against an extreme Brexit, which will put jobs and living standards at risk. It's a vote to reinforce the right of the people of Scotland to decide their own future. We can't afford a Tory government which thinks it can do anything it likes without being held to account. And we can't give Theresa May a blank cheque to pursue any kind of Brexit, no matter the cost. In Scotland, the SNP is the only party strong enough to stand up to the Tories. So vote SNP for a strong voice for Scotland at Westminster. That voice is needed now more than ever. Well, the first thing I would say is that governments have choices about the kind of economic policy they pursue.
