And the Tories now alone in government and previously working with the Liberal Democrats introduced austerity measures which are hitting those in society with the lowest incomes the most. And frankly, I think the time has come for that to end. And politicians can make different choices. And every SNP MP elected to Westminster will stand up for those different choices and an end to austerity. It is about choices. The Tories have chosen to support those who are wealthiest in society. We don't agree with them. We think those who have the most should pay a little bit more. And that's why we're in favour of the highest earners seeing their taxes rising from 45 to 50p. We're also incidentally, also supportive of looking at changes to the pay restraint that we've seen through these times of austerity. That's going to happen in Scotland under an SNP government. We will press the UK government to do the same in the rest of the UK. Lastly, I want to make this point. Some of those people on the lowest incomes have been massively hit by welfare cuts. And I think the time has come to end punishing disabled people, end a bedroom tax, and leaving people with the lowest incomes with too little to pay for the essentials. Angus Robertson. Can I just say I think this debate shames and demeans us all. I don't think there's anyone in this room or anybody watching this debate from Cornwall to Caithness who does not understand the positive contribution that people have made to this land who've come from the rest of Europe and the rest of the world and demonising those people is totally unacceptable. Scotland's experience, Scotland's problem has never been immigration, it's been emigration. Now, it may well be the case that there are different realities in different parts of the UK, I accept that. But in Scotland, we value the contribution of people who've come to our land. We value refugees who sought refuge in our country. And there is much, much more that we should do to make sure that people are able to stay in Scotland, but we should also be able to protect people who've come there. So the first thing that I would do is make sure that every EU citizen who is here is guaranteed the right to stay. It is totally unacceptable that this Tory government hasn't been prepared to give them the guarantee, the promise that they can stay. I think that's totally unacceptable. If it is perfectly possible to have different immigration policies in countries such as Canada and Australia, who have different policies between different provinces in their country, it's perfectly possible to have different immigration rules for different parts of the UK, and that's what the SNP supports. Well, at the present time, our problem is we're losing people. I don't know how many people saw the reports last week. The issue for us is people are now leaving, and that will have a detrimental impact on our NHS and our public services. And a lot of people make a great deal of money out of the poverty of those people. I am astonished that the Labour Party is now aping UKIP in using the same arguments about immigration. The single European market really matters to all of us right across the UK. And a free movement of people really matters too. Why don't you stand up to the Tories and say we want to remain within the single European market because it matters to jobs of people from Britain and the rest of Europe. Yes, it's actually £128 billion. The different sources are rescheduling the UK's finances and £10 billion, that's £118 billion and £10 billion in funds to be raised through taxation, largely through the £0.45 to £0.50 tax raise change. I'm just interested by Jeremy, following SNP policy and tuition fees, I'd like to congratulate you, Jeremy. It's a shame your party north of the border has opposed the SNP. Can I bring up a really serious issue, which I think has been the biggest so far in the campaign, and that relates to social care and to pensions. And I think when older people are facing the most likely prospect, which is a re-elected Tory government, sadly, and they are promising a dementia tax, an end to the winter fuel payment, and cuts to the pension, I think those people deserve to know by how much. And they haven't been told. Now the Prime Minister didn't have the guts to come along this evening to tell us, so I'd like to challenge... I would like to challenge Amber Rudd to tell us the answer to that question now because they must have costed it. They must know how much money that will bring in. Please tell the pensioners of this country how much they will have to pay to fund Tory austerity. How much? the famous £350 million a week. Well the first thing is I imagine at the forefront of your minds, and I'm sure I speak for all of my colleagues here, at the forefront of all of our minds are all of the families who suffered the devastation to their loved ones and relatives with the attack on Manchester. What happened there was a horror and sadly it's happening far too often, both here and round about the world. So I think the first thing that we need to do is that we do need to take safety and security seriously. We need to invest in the police, not cut them as has been the case in England. In Scotland, we've managed to maintain police numbers. So that's my first step. Let's make sure we back the police, give the resources that they need, back our intelligence services who do a tremendous job. There's been some discussion, has there not, about the link between UK foreign policy and becoming perhaps a terrorist threat. And I think that's a dangerous link to draw. However, I would say this. I think we are right to question our intervention in different parts of the world. In Afghanistan, in Iraq, in Libya. And the lesson I draw from that is not that it is wrong sometimes to intervene. We agreed that helping people in Libya was a good thing. But what was wrong was spending 13 times more bombing that country than helping to rebuild it. on leaving areas of the world. Leaving areas of the world with ungoverned spaces is where terrorism and extremism thrives. So we need to do everything that we can to counter extremist thought, give the police and security service the tools that they require. So not cutting the police would be a first step that I would take from Scotland's experience. And I think we want to be very cautious about how we intervene internationally. And if we're going to do it, we have to think long and hard about how we make sure that those places remain stable and safe for years to come. I remember the question was about terrorism and extremism, and you notice that UKIP went straight for Muslims. We remember it wasn't a Muslim who shot Joe Cox, one of Jeremy's Labour MP colleagues. It was a British right-wing neo-Nazi. Who was it who gummed down kids in Norway? It was a Norwegian white racist neo-Nazi. There are all kinds of threats and we need to combat all kinds of threats. So to answer the question, I'm more than disappointed. I'm appalled that President Trump has walked away from a global approach to one of the biggest challenges that we face at home and abroad. And what he's done is what many of us fear he's going to do in a number of different ways. He's walking away from having a multilateral approach, working together with other countries to deal with serious problems. And that's why if the UK had a special relationship with the United States, and we remember of course the first trip that was made by Theresa May was to see a climate change denier, namely Donald Trump, what kind of influence was used by the Prime Minister then to make sure that he didn't go forward with this disastrous step? Absolutely nothing. This is a government that has got rid of the climate change responsibility at the cabinet table. It is absolutely appalling. So what we need to do is we need to do everything that we can at home. I'm delighted that more than 50% of all electricity generated in Scotland is now through renewables. We would have loved to have gone ahead with carbon capture and storage until Amber and her colleagues scrapped it. So there's much more that can be done. So the Prime Minister and her government need to take climate change seriously in a way that they haven't done in recent years, and we need to impress on all of our international colleagues that we need to work together and get this global challenge sorted. So real leadership is about putting the country before you put your party and I think we all now know that this was a totally unnecessary election and the only reason Theresa May called it was that she thought she was going to have a massive majority as a result of it. whatever happened to strength and stability? Where's strength and stability gone? Weak and wobbly is where we are, not so much the Iron Lady as the U-turn Queen. That is not the leadership that we require. In contrast, In contrast, as leader of the SNP in the House of Commons, the third party in the House of Commons, I am proud that we have led the effective opposition when sadly the Labour Party has too often dropped the ball, asking questions not just about things that matter to people in Scotland but the rest of the UK, in Europe and the world. And I am confident that the SNP will be returned to Westminster as the third party because we need to protect our country from the Tories who sadly I fear are going to win this election. I am working hard to win in every single seat in Scotland, Misha, that is our plan, but I believe people in Scotland realise that it's either the SNP or it's the Tories, and whether they're Labour voters, Liberal Democrats or Greens, they understand that if they vote for those parties they'll be doing nothing but helping the Tories. In this debate, you've heard different views about the kind of country we should be. For those watching in England, Wales and Northern Ireland, the SNP will always work with others who share our belief in fairness of an outward-looking, welcoming society. Now more than ever, we need a strong opposition to hold the Tories to account. The SNP will be that strong voice for Scotland and our pledge is this, to work every day to make Scotland the very best country it can be. Vote SNP.
deb_2017_05_31.FARRON: Where do you think Theresa May is tonight? Take a look out your window. She might be out there sizing up your house to pay for your social care. And why do you think she called this election? She wants five years as prime minister, and she thinks you'll give it to her, no questions asked, literally. even if she brings in a dementia tax, sacks your kids' teachers and nicks their lunches while she's at it. We can all agree that is a rubbish offer. Look, I know we don't all agree on Brexit, but she's off to negotiate a deal for you, for me, for all of us. Imagine if it's a bad deal. I mean, dementia tax bad. I know that to persuade you to vote Liberal Democrat I have to give you some really good reasons. So here's three. One, I'll rescue the NHS and social care by putting a penny on income tax. Two, I'll give you the final say on the Brexit deal, not Theresa May. Three, whatever power you give me, I'll use it to stop her taking you for granted. I mean, to answer Nicola's question, I mean, economics is about people. It's not about figures. It's about people's experience and how they can afford to feed their children and look after them. Like many people, I have known in my life what it's like to not be able to pay the bills. It matters to me that we build a country for all of our children where people are decent to one another. A strong welfare state is essential. I've been helping two people in my constituency with their PIP payments in the last month. both of whom were chief executives of different companies in the not-too-distant past. Everybody out there who thinks that welfare is some kind of a burden needs to remember that any one of us is only one or two steps from being in need at any given time. And just to answer the question specifically, what will we do? We will end the benefit freeze. Since the crash in 2007-08, the public sector pay freeze as well. But since that crash, public sector workers have probably borne the brunt of that. those cuts, whether it be nurses, teachers, doctors, social care workers, but you also need to support people to be able to go to work and have the ability to choose it. That's why we'll invest that money, £3.6 billion, into childcare for two-year-olds, £5.6 billion for two-year-olds, to make sure parents have got the ability to go out to work. We need to tackle Nicola's concerns by being compassionate to the individual and standing up for them, but we also have to get the big choices right. The elephant in the room. The fact is, we need to remain in the single market or else we will not be able to afford the National Health Service, social care or any of the support we're talking about. And if Jeremy cared about having enough money to spend on those who need it the most, to raise living standards, he would not have trooped through the lobbies with the Conservatives and UKIP to trigger Article 50 and to make Britain poorer. Last week, a middle-aged Asian man on his way home from work was abused and called racist names and called a terrorist. He had just finished 50 hours saving lives after the Manchester bomb. He was a doctor. And that is what happens when you demonize immigrants. That is what happens when the language... So, does immigration cause pressures that need to be met? Do we need to invest more in infrastructure to make sure we invest the taxes that immigrants pay to make sure that we provide for everybody? Of course, yes. Do we need, as I'm afraid the Prime Minister has done for the last seven years, first as Home Secretary and then Prime Minister, set completely varmi, bogus targets that she fails to meet every single year, No we don't. What we need is an immigration policy that is good for Britain, good for our businesses, good for our farmers, good for tourism, good for our health service, a wise immigration policy, good for Britain, not a concerted immigration policy written to appease UKIP. Poll after poll shows it time and time again. From the Nuffield study, you're looking at a billion pounds additional cost to our national health service because the government's bungling of the deal with Europe, which means that you'll have thousands and thousands of pensioners from Europe, British pensioners, returning to the United who live in the UK and who speak louder than words. And what we have is lots of rhetoric around here about helping, for example, those EU nationals who live here, who raise their children here, who work in universities like this, who serve in our hospitals. But when it came to the vote in the House of Commons, the Labour Party did not show up. In the Lords, they instruct their people to vote against the Liberal Democrat amendment, which would have guaranteed that right to remain for EU citizens. You are now hitching your wagon to UKIP. You said you wouldn't trust Boris Johnson to drive you home. I don't have answers to your questions. Amber Ruddy's not answering. Yep. You see, what we have is a general election in eight days' time, Theresa May assuming a colossal landslide. That's why she thinks she can ignore it all and not bother turning up tonight. And she's telling you, we will take you, we will take your house off you. And we will allow you to know how much we'll let you keep after you've given me a colossal majority. So if tonight you resolve to vote Conservative on the 8th of June, you are resolving to give Theresa May permission to do what she likes. And that's with education cuts. That's with NHS cuts. Do not give her a blank check. is how you make things add up. The Liberal Democrats have got a fully costed manifesto. I'll tell you what, though. There's a long-term economic plan underlying the whole of the Liberal Democrat manifesto. And that is, don't leave the European single market and throw away 15 billion pounds every single year in revenue. To me, the NHS is personal, not political. My mum, who had ovarian cancer, from the point of her diagnosis, to her treatment, to her care, to her passing away in a ward just two floors away from the one where she'd given birth to me, like everybody out there, our experience with the National Health Service is personal. And let me finish. And the point is simply this. If you look at and you use National Health Services, you know, as we saw in Manchester just last week, how utterly brilliant, caring, dedicated and professional they are. You know I didn't. So what we've got here is you see the professionalism and the decency of those people, but have we got the best funded health service in the world? No, we haven't. We spend less on our NHS than in Portugal, than on Spain, than on Greece. It's important that we put a penny on income tax to fund the National Health Service and the recovery of it. If you look at the security services, what do they need? Do they need more powers or do they need to make use or be given the resources to make use of the powers they already have? The temporary exclusion orders which exist now, only one has been used in the last two years. But undoubtedly, the shadow that we are standing in now is that of Manchester. And Manchester is my capital city. It matters to me. As Jeremy said, I was there the night afterwards. All four of my kids knew people who were in Manchester that night. And it just reminds you of how safe our police and our security services keep us. The countless times that those kinds of outrages had been prevented. So to answer the question, How do you deal with it? How do you tackle that terrorism? Well, we will give an additional £300 million to police to make sure that we deal with the community policing that is so necessary. We will also ensure that we restart engagement with communities where terrorism can be fostered, so you get on the inside to prevent it and nip it in the bud. But there is undoubtedly an international angle to this too. And here's the thing. The terrorists that hate Britain, do you know what? They hate Belgium. They hate France. They hate Germany. They hate our way of life. They hate everything that we stand for. And just as we in Manchester stood together against terrorism, and across Britain we stand together against terrorism, But we must stand together with our neighbours, not just in this continent, around the world, with shared values, to fight it. And that will mean diplomacy. That will mean all sorts of things. But sometimes it will take real, serious action. Sometimes it might even mean military action. You have to do the tough thing to defeat the terrorists. We have to rebuild trust and You know, Paul, that the murder last Monday was reported five separate occasions by the Muslim community. An awful lot of truth in it. I think the thing we need to remember, particularly when everything is so raw just nine days after Manchester, that there's a lot of finger pointing going on. We all have ideas, and there's some of them different, some of them similar. The critical thing we should remember at this point is that knee jerk, new policies, and new laws tend to do more harm than good. More resources for the security service and the police we have already will do a lot more good than harm. Right, we'll talk about Brexit being a huge threat or whatever you think. There's nothing bigger as a threat to our country, to our children's future, than the threat of climate change. You're worried about immigration? You see the mass movement of people as our land is desiccated and deluged by that climate change. So the reality is simply this. If it is simply for hair-shirt, muesli-eating Guardian readers to solve climate change, it ain't going to solve the problem. We're all stuffed. So let's do something that will actually make a difference. And that means a nationwide, indeed a planet-wide, concerted effort to build the future that we need. So for Britain, What can we do? We can make ourselves energy self-sufficient in renewable energy. 95% of the supply chain of energy in this country, of wind, of tidal and marine and coastal energy is British made. Why not rebuild our economy whilst guaranteeing our children's future? Good leaders don't run away from a debate. Theresa May undoubtedly should be here. Whatever we have discussed this evening, her absence is undoubtedly the shadow that hangs over this election. How dare you call a general election and then run away from the debate? We are all products of our upbringing. So I was brought up in Preston during the 1980s. And I saw people around me, what happens when a society, a community, is taken for granted, in that case, by a conservative government with a colossal majority. Half my mates' parents out at work at any given time. And I am determined to build a country for our kids, mine included, where people are decent to one another, where people have enough. We have a national health service that is properly funded. And that means being honest with the British people, saying we will put a penny on income tax to have the best health service in the world. It means stopping Theresa May's plans to do to your kids' schools what she's currently doing to our hospitals. And it also means if you want to lead the people, you have to kind of like them as well and spend time amongst them. And if you trust the people, then you do not impose that Brexit deal on the British people without them having the final say. Either the politicians will sign it off or the people will. Well, I trust the people. Well, look, you've heard my arguments. I'll give you the final say over the Brexit deal. I will put a penny on income tax for the NHS. And I will stand up to the Conservatives over the dementia tax and everything else. Now, Amber Rudd is up next. She is not the Prime Minister. The Prime Minister is not here tonight. She can't be bothered. So why should you? In fact, Bake Off is on BBC Two next. Why not make yourself a brew? You are not worth Theresa May's time. Don't give her yours.
deb_2017_05_31.QUESTIONER_0: We begin with opening statements from all seven politicians, the order of which was decided by the drawing of lots. First, it's the leader of Plaid Cymru, Leanne Wood. And our next opening statement is from Caroline Lucas of the Green Party. Vote Green on June 8th. And the next of our opening statements is for the Conservatives from Amber Rudd. And now the UKIP leader, Paul Nuttall. The SNP opening statement from Angus Robertson. And for our final opening statement, the leader of the Liberal Democrats, Tim Farron. Thank you all for those opening statements. Let's go straight to our first question, and it comes from Nicola Collins. Amber Rudd, your party's presided over this squeeze in living standards. The question is on living standards. Jeremy Corbyn. Angus Robertson, how would you help working people like Nicola? I'd like to bring in some of the other parties. Tim Farron, you've argued for this election being about providing a good and credible opposition. Which of these parties do you think has the right economic formula for living standards? The money is there if people want to. Amber Rudd, when Theresa May became Prime Minister, she talked about the just about managing. What happened to that? I can see why the 70-year-old ones are even made. Hang on, Tim Farron. Two people haven't spoken yet. Leanne Wood and then Paul Nuttall will come to you. Jeremy Corbyn, I'd like you to respond to the point about corporation tax, because that is a risk you're taking. Jeremy Corbyn, I'd like you to answer a specific point about the many, you made many spending commitments in your manifesto. They are going to cost a lot of money. You would also add to borrowing. How does that create the economy that enables more to be spent on working people who need it? I'd like to first get Paul Nuttall on this and then I'll come to you, Tim Farron. Thank you for all your responses to this particular question. It is time for us to move on to our second question of the evening, and this one is from Ghulam Nabi Adam. Thank you. How would we have the workers and skills we need to make the UK a success after Brexit? Paul Nuttall. Jeremy Corbyn, you were talking about immigration today, and you said you would make no false promises about cutting it. Does that mean you will make no promises at all on cutting immigration? Would you make any promise on cutting immigration? What is fair? Leanne Wood, you have campaigned for more immigration to Wales, to get Wales the workers it needs. In your manifesto, Did you not say that you wanted a visa system for Wales so that you can attract the workers you need, which means that you don't feel Wales has the workers it needs at the moment? So you may need more workers to come to Wales in the future from outside? We want to keep the ones we've got. But can I just make this point to you, Kate? How does that square with most people in Wales voting for Brexit? Sorry? How does that square with most people in Wales voting for Brexit? Right, and I asked you about the question about what upper limit you'd put on it, which you haven't answered. I'd like Amber Rudd to respond. Amber Rudd, can you respond specifically to the point that you're using those people, the EU citizens here, as bargaining chips? Why is she here? Why is she not here to defend herself? particular question. Thank you all very much indeed. It's time to bring in our next question and this one comes from Rhiannon Buckley. Thank you. Promises of money for our public services, where does the money come from? How can we trust your plans add up? Caroline Lucas. Thank you, Caroline Lucas. I'm going to turn to Amber Rudd. The question is about trusting plans. And in your manifesto, there was a notable absence of costings. Are you sure? I'll give you an example. I just want to ask, are you sure that your plans add up? I mean, it was only yesterday you couldn't remember the cost of one of your policies. Your manifesto also included major spending promises, an extra £180 billion for public services, UK-wide? They're going to get rid of it. Amber Rudd, Amber Rudd, can I ask you, please? There are two specific points here. One is about the level of the cap on social care and the other is you also haven't said at what level you'd be means testing the winter fuel payment. Just briefly, you have been clear about how you would pay for the extra 6 billion pounds that you would give the NHS. Do you think that would be transformative? Because there are a lot of experts who say that, actually, it wouldn't make that much of a difference. We can't hear him speak. It's not that long ago that you called the NHS a monolithic hangout from days gone by. He did. When did you change your mind? Thank you all for your responses to that question. Just a reminder that if you want to have your say on tonight's debate, you can join the conversation on social media using the hashtag BBC debate. We still have time for more questions from our audience here in Cambridge, and we can go to the next of those questions now, and it comes from David Mansfield. Thank you. How would you make, what are your priorities for making Britain a safer country and the world a safer place? Angus Robinson. Jeremy Corbyn, you have talked about our foreign interventions and our safety here at home. Do you think on the police cuts, without the police cuts, are you saying the attack in Manchester wouldn't have happened? is what happened in London. Just a quick point of fact before I turn to Amber Rudd. Diane Abbott has talked about 10,000 more police officers. You're saying 20,000. What about the ordinary police though? How do the cuts in police numbers make us safer? There is. There is. There is judicial oversight. I'd like to get the other party... Jeremy, you are very good at defending the terrorists. Tim Farron, a specific point for you. In your manifesto, you said you were going to roll back state surveillance powers by ending the indiscriminate bulk collection of communications data. Now that was before Manchester. Do you still stand by that as the right thing to do? Saudi has the right. Leanne Wood. And that point's been repeatedly made. When you say we should look at what we're doing, What else would you do differently in terms of tackling extremism? Are you saying austerity has made us less safe? Can you be brief, Tim Farris? You haven't ruled out locking up suspected terrorists. Sorry? You haven't ruled out locking up suspected terrorists without trial. Would that really make us safer? Thank you. And that's all the time we have on this particular question. Thank you all. Our next question comes from Rebecca Green. Thank you. How would the panellists deal with President Trump wanting to pull out of the Paris Climate Change Agreement? I'm going to ask everyone to deal with this briefly so we can get one more question in before nine o'clock. Tim Farron. We also need to see an end to massive airport expansion. I'm going to stop you, Caroline Lucas, because we're going to... There is lots more to say. I want to turn to Paul Nuttall. So you would want to see Britain doing the same, then? Jeremy Corbyn. We need a different approach. Amber Rudd, the UK's own official advisers say that your government's not on track to meet the pledge of cutting emissions 80% by 2050. Why not? Thank you for all the responses to that particular question. And it's time now for our last question of the evening, and it comes from Neil Plucknett. Thank you. In what way does your leadership have the talent and the character needed to take this country forward? Jeremy Corbyn. Paul Nuttall, your leadership? Amber Rudd, the question is about leadership and the Conservatives have made a lot of noise about Theresa May's leadership, personal leadership. And yet this is a campaign where we saw a major U-turn on social care. OK. Jeremy Corbyn, can you respond to that point about the result of your MPs? What are your qualities of leadership? Thank you to all our speakers for their responses to that final question. And that brings an end to our audience questions for this evening. It's time now for our speakers' closing statements. They are just 30 seconds each. They've drawn lots for the order of these closing statements. And I'm going to ask our audience here in Cambridge, please applaud right at the end of all seven of the closing statements, because we have a limited time left on air. And the first of the closing statements comes from Paul Nuttall. Caroline Lucas, co-leader of the Green Party. Angus Robertson. And for our final closing statement, Amber Rudd. Well, thank you all.

deb_2017_06_02.MAY: Thank you. Thank you, Abigail. And can I first of all say good evening to everybody and thank you for you coming to be an audience at this programme tonight, which is an important part of the election campaign. Let me tell you about some of the things that I did as Home Secretary, Abigail. I said that I would ensure that we were dealing with extremist hate breaches and I excluded more than any Home Secretary before me. I said I would do something about stop and search because I don't think anybody should be stopped and searched on the streets of our country because of the colour of their skin. I said I would be tough on crime and I said I would ensure our police and our security services had the powers they need to be able to do their job and I gave them those in the legislation that I put through. And I made sure that we kept the records of criminals and terrorists on the DNA database, whereas Diane Abbott actually wants to wipe them clean. And I don't think that's a good idea because that helps us catch criminals. First of all, can I just say I'm not refusing to take part in debates because I'm here answering questions from you, this audience, this evening. And that's what I think is important in an election campaign. It's not politicians arguing amongst each other, but actually listening and taking questions from voters. And you mentioned about saying I wouldn't hold an election and now holding one. You're right. I thought we needed a period of stability when I became Prime Minister. But what became clear to me when we went through the Article 50 process to trigger the process of leaving the European Union, to respect the will of the British people, was that other parties wanted to frustrate those Brexit negotiations and wanted to frustrate that will of the people that had been expressed in the referendum vote. And, you know, it would have been easy. I could have said, OK, I'm Prime Minister. There's another couple of years going. Why don't I just stay and hang on in the job? But I didn't do that. I've called an election because of Brexit. I was willing to do that because I think this is a really important moment for our country. We've got to get this right. If we get it right, I'm optimistic for the British people because I believe in the British people. But we need to get it right. Well, you're right. We got the Article 50 legislation through Parliament. That was important. It's triggered the ability for us to start these negotiations. And those negotiations, by the way, start just 11 days after Election Day next week. So whoever comes in as Prime Minister, whoever comes in as a government has got to be ready to actually get the ball rolling and start those negotiations straight away. But it was clear through the discussions that we had around that time that the other parties did want to frustrate us. And you say, you talk about the Liberal Democrats. I mean, we have a situation at the moment where if Jeremy Corbyn was to get into Number 10, he'd be being propped up by the Liberal Democrats and the Scottish Nationalists. You'd have Diane Abbott, who can't add up, sitting around the Cabinet table. John McDonnell, who's a Marxist. Nicola Sturgeon who wants to break our country up and Tim Farron who wants to take us back into the EU, the direct opposite of what the British people want. No, I've been in politics quite a long time and I've always said at every election the only poll that matters is the one that takes place on polling day. And I think the British people, when they're voting, everybody's casting their vote, people here and people watching and others, have a very simple choice and it is about Who do you trust to have that leadership to take us to get the best deal for Brexit, a Brexit deal in Europe? And who's got the will and the vision, not just to take us through Brexit, not just to get on with the job of delivering Brexit, but to take us beyond and build a better future for this country? It would result in the destruction of the lives and communities and environment for millions of people. And so I would be actively engaged to ensure that danger didn't come about. I would also be very aware of other dangers that we face. Cyber attacks hit our National Health Service a couple of weeks ago. The dreadful terrorist attack in Manchester indicates the danger of individual acts of terrorism that can take lives. We have to be secure here. We have to protect our society and our country. But we also have to engage in the rest of the world to make sure we don't have huge ungoverned spaces such as in Libya, which can become the basis of some terrible events that are going to take the lives of many other people. We have to deal with those issues here and globally. That is the function of a responsible government. I'm saying that our party has committed to renewing Trident. That was a decision taken by a party conference and indeed taken by Parliament. I would view the idea of having to use a nuclear weapon as something that was resulting in a failure in the whole world's diplomatic system. There has to be no first use. There has to be a process of engagement to bring about, ultimately, global nuclear disarmament. It's not going to happen quickly. It's not going to happen easily. But we have to have that wish. countenance a world in which we could all be destroyed by nuclear war. The weapon is there and I would say no first use of the weapon and I think that has to be the basis on what we do. Adam, does that answer your question? Fair point. But we have to do everything we can, obviously to protect ourselves, but the best protection is having good and reasonable relationships with the rest of the world so we don't descend into a mentality where there are military blocks starting threatening each other. And I'm utterly determined to do everything I can to bring about a more peaceful world. I've spent a lot of my life with UN and other organisations trying to bring about principle of no first use, principles of disarmament and principles of bringing about peace around the world. That surely is something we can all work for. I think we have to recognize that there is deep inequality and injustice in the world, but the threats are, as I said, cyber security and terrorism. The other threats are actually of environmental disaster around the world and large numbers of people fleeing from environmental disaster, in effect, environmental refugees, which is why I was so disappointed in President Trump's decision particularly in view of what has been achieved by people working together to try and protect and sustain this planet. That is why there has to be a policy of disarmament globally, but through a multilateral policy, not a unilateral policy. I just asked you for a simple answer. No, of course not. You're going to have to do it first place. No, of course not. Of course I would not do that. I think that has to be the right and sensible thing to do. What I think will happen is this, that we have managed migration from outside Europe which is based on family reunion and skill needs or investment. What I think will happen is there will probably be some reduction in European migration because we will also prevent there being recruitment of groups of low-paid workers from Central Europe to come here to undermine existing conditions that are often not very good themselves and damage the life chance of people in this country and be not actually very good for those that have been brought in. So we will end overseas-only recruitment and end those contractual arrangements by which people are brought in, particularly into the construction industry. But we also, if I may say so, just have another issue which we do have to address, and that is the skill shortage and skill problem in Britain, because we have not invested enough in industrial training, we've not invested enough in skill training, we've not invested enough in training more doctors or more nurses in this country. Until we do that, then we've got a big problem. And so our whole approach to our manifesto is about investment for the future. I think it was a big factor in it. I don't think it was the only factor, but it was a big factor. There is also an issue that communities and local government are often not sufficiently funded to deal with groups of people coming in, and so we would restore what Gordon Brown had in 2009, which was a migrant impact fund. When the minimum wage was first introduced by the Labour government after the 1997 election there were a lot of similar concerns and predictions which turned out not to be the case and in fact there was not any substantial number of jobless. We fully recognize that. We would therefore work with them, either to give them tax relief or support, in order to make sure the living wage, the real living wage, was paid, but they didn't close down as a result of it. Because as a society, we have six million people earning less than the living wage. We have a million people on zero-hours contracts. That means those people are loyal to the company, those people know what their income is going to be, and that seems to me a much fairer way of doing it, so you then have some security of what's going on. We have to look at the issues of what kind of economic future we have. We cannot go on being a low-waged economy, underspending on our public services, investing less than any other industrialised country in our future. I would much prefer that we develop a national investment bank and strategy regionally based to improve rail, broadband, road communications, that we improve investment in our industry for the future. We have a lower proportion of manufacturing industry than Germany. We have a lower level of productivity than Germany. We have a lower skill base than Germany. Why is there a difference? Because successive German governments, almost irrespective of which party it is, have been prepared to invest in their infrastructure and their future. I think we should think about that and improve the economy of our own society. And do you know what? That would lead to better economic growth. It would lead to better opportunities for everybody. And I think this is an offer that's important. And I think it's time that we actually invested in our future rather than presided over our decline. Well, actually, the corporation tax level that I'm proposing would actually be less than there is anywhere in the major industrial countries of Western Europe anyway. The crucial thing, and you're right to raise it, has to be gaining tariff-free trade access to the European market as the basis on which they can continue. Because look at it this way. That is the best way of guaranteeing jobs and a growing manufacturing economy in Britain. There are serious issues about maritime surveillance aircraft that are not available, that have to be addressed. There's a crucial issue of cyber security and cyber attacks that are taking place on us. And there's a crucial issue about terrorist attacks. And I don't believe you make a society safer Why university fees? If you look at the process of applications at the moment, the number of working class students is dropping, the number coming from poorer communities is dropping. deb_2017_06_02.QUESTIONER_27: Thank you. Will you rule out doing a deal with Nicola Sturgeon in the event of a hung parliament, because you will be negotiating, as you would expect, with the EU at that time. Thank you. That was the answer I expected, but it wasn't the answer to my question.

My choice, basically, well, the choice of my clients is a Conservative government and a Labour government, and the difference, as you say, 2%, looking at on a European-wide, but it's a 9% difference between what the Conservatives are proposing for corporation tax
deb_2017_06_02.QUESTIONER_30: Is Labour's manifesto a realistic wish list, or is it just a letter to Santa Claus? OK? It's a question of funding it. And I'm thinking of last time Labour were in government, they left a note at the Chancellor's office saying, we've no money left.

Are you saying you will never, ever, under any circumstances, press a red button?

deb_2017_06_05.FARRON: OK, well, first of all, Marco, I mean, you, like everybody else, would have been here last night. But we, of course, rightly postponed to pay tribute to those who died, to remember those who have passed away, and to stand in solidarity with their loved ones and all those injured and affected by the London attacks. And, you know, you think about what you're going to say. before you come on a programme like this, after an event last, as happened on Saturday night. And you remember yourself saying really similar things a fortnight earlier, after the appalling outrage in Manchester. And to my mind, our reactions, maybe they're similar to mine, are heartbreak, the impact on those individuals and their families, and frankly, anger. that this should happen to people in our community, that it should happen at all, such utter wickedness and therefore the desire for something to be done is utterly right. So my sense is, which is indeed, so here we go, do we believe that the reason we have not been able to prevent outrages such as those on Saturday night and two weeks ago in Manchester was because of a lack of surveillance or was it because of a lack of resources? It seems to me, for example, that we have at the moment the ability, the powers, our police have the powers, our security services have the powers to follow and track criminals, to be able to pursue terrorists, to be able to hack into their devices. What we don't have is sufficient pairs of eyes and pairs of hands in our security services and in our police force to be able to pursue them and to catch them. Now I do not know what could be done to prevent three utter murderous cowards in a white van deciding to do what they did on Sunday night, on Saturday night, but I do know that we are much safer if we invest in police and in our security services. The additional £300 million that the Liberal Democrats have put into policing across the country would make us safer. The cuts Theresa May has made over the last seven years as Home Secretary and then Prime Minister have not made us safer. Let's see whether Mr Crowther is convinced by that. So we're talking about the snoopers charter or data surveillance, however you want to describe it. Now, here's the issue. When we're trying to deal with terrorists and suspected terrorists, we need to be able to focus on what they do and who they are talking to. What we have at the moment, if you like, is an ever-widening haystack and we're looking for a needle. And the answer is not to put more hay into the haystack, it is to put more magnets around the haystack so we can actually find what's in there in the first place. So just to be clear, you don't want those powers, you want them rolled up? There are two things here. I have got a practical concern and a principal concern. The practical concern is this. Our security services utterly need the ability to be able to catch people and to trace people. But the widening of powers is not something that the evidence is there to support. So it is a practical problem. There is a principal issue though... Lady in the middle, lady in the middle, Mr Farron. Okay, thank you. Yeah, so Big Stick we were hearing about, is that what you're going to give? So we need to have resources. I mean, Cressida Dick said today that she did not favour and didn't think it would make us a safer country if we armed every officer in the UK. I agree with her. But that doesn't mean we shouldn't have more resources. And of that £300 million the Liberal Democrats would give to our police forces, some of that would, of course, go to ensuring we did have that capability. There's a question about people being allowed back in the country. And that's a really good question, given that we had a question about the powers that we do or don't need. So the government has, the Home Secretary has, the power to issue temporary exclusion orders. In the last two years, she's used one. One. In those two years. And we know of people who potentially could have been stopped. It's not, do we have the powers? It's, do we use the powers? We are the party that wants to back our police to have the resources to actually do something about it. Because at times like this, it's very easy and tempting for a politician to come up with a knee-jerk, sounds-good response. I want to do some good. I realise, at this point, people are seeking answers and want to see action. But, for example, you talked about the anti-radicalisation strategy, and you asked the question before about people we know about and aren't tackling. We know that the murder in Manchester was reported by his community on five separate occasions. That is a reminder that the community out there are desperate to tackle terrorism, and our police and security services do not have the resources to enable them to do it. That's where the priority must be. You can have more money, but you can't have more power. the police and security services, what they want more than anything else is resources to catch people. There's also, though, a point made here. What do the terrorists want us to do? To turn in on ourselves and be divided as a country. What do they want us to do? To give up on our freedoms and our liberties. And those are the things we must not sacrifice, otherwise the terrorists would have won. I always think that if you believe in people having a vote, that's generally quite democratic. I take the view that the result last June, 52-48, as narrow as it was, nevertheless, the government has the mandate to negotiate Brexit. And that's the direction the country is going in. If I'm honest with you, it breaks my heart. I'm somebody who believed that Britain would still be better at the heart of the European Union. Nevertheless, I accept that is the narrow will of the people. The thing is this, though. What happens next? Now, one might argue that Theresa May has called the election as early as she has, despite the fact she already has a clear majority in Parliament because Labour backed her, she's called it now before the details of Brexit become clear. plan, that deal that Theresa May and indeed the European leaders will negotiate, will impact upon all of our lives, the younger we are for the longer, and it will impact upon our prices that we pay at the supermarket, on jobs and everything else. The simple point here, Nick, if I can just finish, I apologise before we come back and you can tell me I'm right or wrong, it's simply this, that that deal we'll have to live with for the next several generations will either get signed off by the politicians or by the people. Now what if it's a bad deal? And I mean dementia tax bad, that kind of bad. And if it's that bad, shouldn't you have the final say and not the politicians in vape-filled rooms in Brussels and London? I don't believe... Well, you're not a Democrat, are you? Because what you say is there was a vote and you want to have another one. Well, we had a general election two years ago and we appear to be having another one. And my... You know, so I... I absolutely accept the results of the referendum. The people I blame for the situation we've got ourselves in are David Cameron and George Osborne who took a chance on our country's future and our children will pay for it. They were on the Remain side. So I blame them if I blame anybody. We are, as a country, learning to deal with our new future and we must do that together. However, that deal that none of us know the contents of will be stitched up behind closed doors by Brussels and London. I simply say that final deal is going to be stitched up, or rather decided, endorsed by someone. Either it is the politicians or it's the people. I think it's ultimately democratic to say that it must be the people. I do want to come back to the question you're asking. And there's a slot on the question. Forgive me, we'll come back to it. The difference between the two positions. Can I be honest with you and say that I'm not an enormous fan of referenda? But I think if you started with democracy, you can't end up with a stitch-up. And I think that we voted for departure last June, but we didn't vote for destination. It wasn't on the ballot paper. The only person I blame for that is David Cameron. Nobody's fault apart from his. But we've started with democracy. We can't now end this process with a stitch-up. And after that, I'll be happy we have no more referenda ever. Woman at the back there. Well, I think Mr. Brown's going to agree with that, aren't you? I do. As a little reminder... It's an absolutely brilliant point. Two things I also want to say. First of all, the majority voted out. That is the result, that's the direction of the country. But all of us, especially our leaders, must not forget that three quarters of our young people voted for Remain and they will have to live with the consequences of this for longer than most of us. And the second point, freedom of movement is often raised, but what about freedom of movement for British people? our ability to live, work and study, love and explore overseas. These things matter too and that's why the content of the deal should be agreed by the people, if not the politicians. So, cliché Claxton number one, the only poll that matters is the one on Thursday. But it's right, isn't it? If people really wanted what you wanted, you'd be a very popular man, wouldn't you? Well, there's a good question. I'm sure that may be so. First of all, doing what you believe in is the right thing to do. Some people may think that we take a calculation over our position on this matter, on democracy, for the final deal. Do you know one major motivation? Is that in 30 years' time, when my kids are my age, I want to look them in the eye and tell them I did everything I could to protect their future. That is what being in politics should be about, not narrow calculations. OK, so the answer to this question starts with me saying something nice about Nicola Sturgeon and the SNP. Wait for it. So nearly three years ago, when Scotland had the chance to vote to leave the United Kingdom, the SNP, in particular the then First Minister Alex Salmond, produced a 650-page white paper showing what leaving the United Kingdom would look like for Scotland. And 12 months ago, Nigel Farage presented us with a lie on the side of a bus. They are not comparable. They are not comparable. So the Scottish people voted in that referendum, knowing not just departure, but destination also. But the argument is, of course... If you're a Democrat, why not give them another go? So I fully respect whatever people feel on both sides of the issue on independence. I'm bound to say, as a Northerner, it breaks my heart the thought that you want to leave us lot saddled with the Tories. Please don't go. But the second thing I'd just say on this, is that we as a party stood in the Holyrood elections, the Scottish parliamentary elections last May on a manifesto committed to Scotland as part of the United Kingdom and so keeping to our mandate it's right that we say that we continue to support the Union and then not being a rerun of the Scottish referendum. Thank you very much. OK. So if the polling is to be believed, one third of the folks in here support the SNP, and enough respect to all of you. And two years ago, you had six MPs. And whether you like it or not, whether I like it or not, you've made a significant difference in that time. So don't write off people because they've got a current number of MPs in single digits. There was a bunch of questions at the back about what happens next and all of the rest of it. My view is simply this. As somebody who is a Northerner, who is committed to my collective British identity, I want us to be a united kingdom. I utterly respect those who believe in independence, but I politely disagree. The issue about there being untruths or wrong arguments on either side, do you know what? I mean, I am most critical about the arguments used by those at the top of the Remain campaign. We are talking about misuse of information. I think I have explained that. You asked me a question about people giving wrong arguments and all the rest of it. Can I point out the moment, I am fairly sure, that Remain lost the referendum last year and it was the moment when George Osborne, who I should now be nice to because he may or may not print my He pointed out that if you leave the European Union, you'd lose your foreign holiday, you'd lose your savings, you'd lose your pension, you'd lose your job. There's a whole bunch of people in this country who thought, I've got none of them, stuff you. And I think we need to remember that the arguments for remain were not the emotionally inspiring ones that should have been used. If they had been deployed, we could well have won. Not in the slightest. I mean, my identity, like most of you in here, is multiple, in the sense I'm a father, yes, I'm a Christian, I'm a northerner, not as northerly as most of you, but I count myself as a... as a northerner and I'm a Liberal Democrat, I can be facetious and say I'm a Blackburn Rovers fan, all these things make up who I am and we are all that blend of different identities and I believe somebody who lives in a society like this which is so diverse and so balanced that I couldn't want to pick another country in the world to be in, to have that kind of freedom. And you've not wanted to answer the question. So no is the answer to that question. But the issue is... No, you don't think it's a sin. No, but the point you... Do you think abortion is wrong, is the other question that's asked? The question is, do you act in a way, because of what you believe sincerely in your heart, in a liberal fashion and defend people's rights? So I joined the Liberal Party, as it was then, when I was 16. One of the very first campaigns I was involved in was trying to abolish and get rid of Section 28. That homophobic piece of legislation was introduced by the Conservative government at that time. And right through my time, passionate for LGBT plus rights, particularly most recently when we were in coalition government, introducing equal marriage. That is massively important to me, but the job is not finished. No, if you're a liberal, you fight for everybody's right to be who they are. And I honestly don't understand the conflict that people think is there. Why was it so hard to answer the question? Because, you know, I'm not somebody who wants to go around talking about my faith all the time. I've never made it... You've had lots of interviews. Or you could actually take the view that I'm not running to be Pope and that you are somebody who believes that I am not... I am a political leader, not a religious one. So I don't judge anybody. What's really key to my faith, for what it's worth, is you treat others as you want to be treated yourself. You do not judge other people. Otherwise, you yourself will be judged. So I am the leader of the Liberal Democrats. I made a promise to my constituents by signing that pledge in 2015 to vote against an increase in tuition fees and I kept my pledge. You can argue it cost me a place as a minister during that time in the coalition government. It's important to keep your promises. It is also important to do what is right by the people that you represent. Whatever one thinks about tuition fees, I can tell you from my experience as a working class lad who got to university in the late 1980s, what helped me to get to university was the simple fact that I got a maintenance award. And that was what made the difference between me being able to afford, and my folks being able to afford, me to go to university. That is what the Liberal Democrats have committed to replacing, bringing in a £7,000 a year maintenance award, so folks like me, and many people in the United Kingdom, can actually afford to go to university. Frank Donnelly, are you convinced by that answer? Well, we're not going to coalition. I made that very, very clear. I mean, first of all, whether one likes it or not, Theresa May is heading for a landslide on Thursday. I wish she wasn't, but she called the election for that very purpose. You just told us you didn't believe the polls, that you shouldn't trust the polls. How on earth do you know she's running for a landslide? There you go. You got me. You got me. So, Nick, you got me. But I think that one assumes nobody calls an election at the point that she did. If she did not assume, maybe she's taking us all for granted that that will be the outcome. Either way, though, we've been very clear we will not be going into coalition with any party after this election. You can still make a massive difference in opposition. Here in Scotland, quite a good example is the SNP government just short of a majority here. The Liberal Democrats are not in coalition or any partnership or pact with the SNP government. We stay in opposition and we make a difference from the opposite side. If you want, for example, to stop the dementia tax, that's in education and in hospitals, then being in opposition gives you the chance to prevent all of those things. No, because it was about trust and not fees. That issue was always about trust and not fees. On the detail of the policy, by the way, as things now are in England, you have to be earning £70,000 a year now before the new system that we introduced is more expensive for you than the one that was replaced by Labour. So it's about making things better. The priority for us is bringing back maintenance grants so under-class kids can go to university in larger numbers. Just time to get in another subject. And I'm going to ask Josh West to ask a question. Well, first of all, we absolutely are proud of saying that we are going to be honest with all of you that the NHS and social care across the United Kingdom is in crisis, and in England especially, I would observe. And that means that either you can have platitudes from people who will tell you that they can solve this problem without any extra money, or we can be brutally honest with you and say that for the price of a cup of coffee a week, we can have the best NHS and social care in the world. My view is this. that there isn't a single person who either themselves or their loved ones uses health and care in this country and doesn't know there is a crisis. In social care you have wonderful loving people, caring people, caring for our older people who could earn more money stacking shelves at the local supermarket. That is an absolute outrage and I'm determined to solve it and we'll solve it by being honest and saying that we'll put a penny on income tax for everybody to make sure we have the best health and social care in the world. Just spell that out a bit more, if you would. How much would your 1p tax rise cost then? Well, an average earner, it would cost £2.50 a week. So that's not nothing. At a time when real incomes are going down. So isn't that all the more important that we're very, very clear that what we're doing is something that's going to make a massive difference. You can do banal offers that you know you're never going to keep, or you make a commitment that is fully... Well, you tax the rich. The 50% best paid people in this country would be paying 95% of the burden. But this is a colossal problem and either we solve it or we don't. And the Liberal Democrats have a plan to solve it and we're going to be honest with you and tell you how we'll pay for it. So there's a great point. And first of all, you are right to say that calling it the dementia tax, Theresa May's dementia tax, does not fully explain the situation. Because people with multiple sclerosis... What, he says it's nothing to do with dementia, he says it's not a tax. I'm afraid we're short of time. It's got everything to do. What Theresa May is planning to do is to say to everybody in this country who gets a long-term condition, whether it be Alzheimer's or multiple sclerosis or whatever it might be, that if you have to get care, then your spouse or your children, that house will have to go on the point of your death. That is not something that is the case now. She says, Theresa May says there'll be a cap, but she says, give me a colossal majority first and then they'll tell you what it's going to be. So we believe there should be a cap in terms of what you pay for care. That £72,000 cap that once upon a time was a cross-party solution under Andrew Dilnot. We believe the dementia tax is an appalling attack on the most vulnerable people in this country. Nine out of ten houses will pay for it. If you have dementia or your loved one has dementia, your house is at risk. And on Thursday you've got the chance to vote to stop it, to vote Liberal Democrat.
deb_2017_06_05.STURGEON: Well it's possibly the question that is uppermost in everybody's mind right now and of course all of our thoughts remain with those affected by that horrific and cowardly attack in the centre of London on Saturday night and of course with the people of Manchester as well. This is one of the greatest responsibilities for any politician. and I think the first thing politicians need to do is be honest. There are no easy answers and often the knee-jerk responses are the wrong ones. Firstly, we've got to tackle and address and challenge extremism wherever we find it. And I believe very strongly that we have to do that with the Muslim community. We mustn't scapegoat the Muslim community, because it's wrong. Most Muslims are as appalled at these attacks as the rest of us. But it would also be counterproductive. because we're going to find it easier to root out extremism if we're working in partnership with that community. Secondly, we've got to make sure we invest in our security and intelligence services, and we've got to make sure we invest in our police. Obviously, one of the big issues of debate today has been the reduction in police numbers in England, 20,000 fewer police officers. In Scotland, we've maintained police numbers and we've increased the numbers of police officers who are trained to bear firearms. So after the Manchester attack, Police Scotland was able to have an increased level of policing from within its own resources without calling on the military, although we're very grateful for the offer of help from the military. So these are the things we've got to do. Two final points I would make are these. I've made one of them already about not scapegoating particular communities. But secondly, we must make sure that in our determination, and all of us share it to keep the population safe, we don't start to undermine our own freedoms and civil liberties, because these are part of what makes us who we are. I think we should always listen very carefully to what the police and security services say. But the police and security services already have fairly wide-ranging powers. I listened to some of your conversations with Tim Farron. We have the ability to tag people who are thought to be extremist, exclusion orders, to keep people who have left and gone to places like Syria, to prevent them coming back here. The police and security services have wide-ranging powers in terms of interception. And what we've got to guard against is the security services having so much data and information that they can't then make sense of it. So there's a number of things we've got to do. And I think we've got to come together and try to make sure that we move forward here with as much consensus as we possibly can. This is, I think, one of the, I'll be perfectly honest about it, it's one of the trickiest balances we've got to strike. We have a police force, not just in Scotland, but across the UK, that is routinely unarmed. And my feeling is that is how the majority of people would prefer that it stayed. But we must make sure we've got sufficient numbers of armed police officers to respond to incidents like the one in London. I mean, frankly, the response of the police on Saturday night was exceptional. I mean, all of us should be full of gratitude for the speed and effectiveness with which they responded. Frankly, the police saved, I think, a lot of lives through their response on Saturday night, and we must make sure we support them to do that. We would study very carefully proposals that came forward. We voted against the Snoopers Charter because we thought that went too far in interfering with people's civil liberties and didn't practically enhance the police powers in the way that we thought was effective. But we would also always look very carefully and study the case for any proposal for additional powers. Look, sometimes in politics, you have to do what you think is right. Don't get me wrong, all politicians make calculations and tactical calculations, but sometimes you have to be guided by principle. And my position on this is reasonably straightforward when you strip it all away. We face not just Brexit, but perhaps a very extreme form of Brexit. that could have implications and consequences for life in Scotland for generations to come. It could see jobs lost, it could see investment into our country fall, it could see the horizons of our young people in terms of freedom of movement seriously restricted. Now, my proposition is simply this. When we get to the end of that Brexit process, and we can see what the implications are for the future of our country, we should have a choice. Do we think that is acceptable and right for Scotland or do we want to choose a different future? Fundamentally, our future should be decided for us, not by us. I don't take a single vote for granted. But I think we have to see that in context. But the point that was made by the gentleman here about the responsibilities of the Scottish Government. In Scotland, we support free personal nursing care so that people have less burden on their personal assets when they need care. Now, less than three years later, we find ourselves facing the prospect of Brexit, with no real understanding yet what the implications are for lots of aspects of our life. I want what's best for Scotland and the alternative to not having a choice over our own future is that we have to put up with Brexit regardless of how damaging that is and that could be narrowed horizons for our young people and lost jobs, tens of thousands of them. Some of the richest and most prosperous countries in the world are countries of a similar size to Scotland, but if that is your view, that is a choice you would make. That is my point, it is a choice for people. But in this election on Thursday, I think we have a more immediate opportunity as a country to make our voice heard more loudly in the Brexit negotiations, to make sure that our interests are not ignored as Theresa May it is Theresa May, takes those negotiations forward. But one of the biggest challenges facing Scotland right now is the need to grow our working age population. If we have the Conservatives putting more and more restrictions on the ability of the best and brightest from around Europe and the world to come here, that will be damaging for our economy, not just now, but for decades to come. And trading across the UK is important for Scotland and for the rest of the UK. Why should we be forced to choose between these two? Why on earth would we stop trading with the rest of the UK. There is no reason whatsoever that we would stop trading with the rest of the UK.
