deb_2017_06_06.ZAHAWI: I'm going to try to cover all three comments. I came to this country in 1978. I was about 11 years old. I couldn't speak English. My parents had to flee Iraq from a dictator, actually not too dissimilar to Abdi's parents. The difference is my parents embraced the country that rescued them, and we integrated. I learned English within six months. I went to university, I've built a great business, I'm now the member, well I used to be, I'm the candidate now, I was the member of parliament for Stratford-on-Avon, the heart of England. This country is a great country, it has incredible values, values of freedom, opportunity, democracy, rule of law, tolerance. I think one of the things that we have not done well is integration, but also we've for too long tolerated people who preached extremism and hatred amongst us, people who preached intolerance to our values, to our way of life, misogyny, all sorts of things. And I think we've got to call those people out in our communities. And I think the Muslim community, the mainstream Muslim community, is equally targeted by these extremists, by these criminals, because they target Ahmadiyya Muslims or other Muslims as well as being kuffar, which is people who are outside the religion, therefore easy game in many ways. But what we've got to do is come together as political parties, as a community, and call out intolerance and say, that's wrong. Our society is not like that. We're proud of what we are. What Manchester did at the concert was incredible, truly incredible. And that should be our role model in many ways of how we deal with this as a political party. I'd like us to come together. Let's not keep We're tearing chunks out of one another. Let's say, look, how can we work together to get the right legislation through Parliament so that we can do this? But it's not just legislation. It's also, how do we come together and call out these extremists? Look, and I agree with you. It isn't right, and I think we should try and just pull back from that. The one thing I would say to you is the bit that you see on Wednesday afternoon, which is sort of the Prime Minister's question time, is not what we do. So if you look at the committee stage of any bill, or the Foreign Affairs Committee, which I sat on, where we got a fantastic report into the intervention in Libya, which has been cited, in fact, on many news programs during the election campaign, as being a really good report. And that was cross-party. We worked together in those committees. And that work is the real work of Parliament. And I hope that young people get to see some of that. Sadly, most of it takes place in the background rather than, as you say, the sort of the yaboo bits of politics. So as you saw on the program, on the clip earlier, the 2015 police budget was protected. That was on the program. I'm not disputing that. I'm just saying that on counterterrorism, the police asked for more funding, and we delivered that. On cyber, another $1.9 billion is going to cyber security. I think the lady in the front mentioned what's happening on social media and so on. And your very good point about the criminal aspect to this, what Theresa May wants to do is work with other countries to get to a place so that actually our security agencies, our security services can work with the online platforms so that we can have a way where your information is secure and you can talk to your friends and everyone else in a secure place. But those people who are using those safe places can't use them anymore. We need to make sure there are no safe places. on the internet for these people. And that's a big area that we want to focus on as well. We want to look at legislation in terms of how do you, if you know someone's about to be radicalised, how do you try and bring them back away from that? How do you curtail their movements? How do you deport those who shouldn't be here in the first place? Those are the sort of things that we want to look at if we are back in government after Thursday. That sounds all very reasonable and wonderfully reasonable until I tell you that when we dropped corporation tax from 28% down to 19%, We actually brought £7 billion more into the Exchequer. Labour obsessed about tax rates. We care about tax take what comes into the Exchequer so we can spend it on our NHS. You need a strong economy if you are going to have a strong NHS. your leaders, some political heroes, a man called Hugo Chavez. Now, if you know anything about Venezuela, that country is one of the wealthiest countries in Southern America. It floats on a lake of oil. It has the second largest oil reserve after Saudi Arabia. Today, it's bankrupt. Why? Because like your leader, Hugo Chavez promised everything to everyone. Today, if you're sick in Venezuela, no matter what class you're from, unless you can leave the country, you're dead because there's no medicine. People are starving. There's nothing moral or decent about crashing an economy. What I'm saying to you is, when you tell people that you're going to raise corporation tax, you also have to tell them it doesn't raise any more money. The independent IFS looked at your figures and said, actually, you won't raise that money, so where is it going to come from? That was the question, too, that was put to you. And you must be honest about these things. We, like the other parties, are saying we're going to invest in the NHS. Well, no, we've said we've put $10 billion in. We've put an additional $2 billion into the challenge around social care. And we're committing another $8 billion by 2022 into the NHS. But you need a strong economy to be able to do that. Can we just nail this once and for all? And that's what Theresa May is saying. So the $11.4 billion that we're putting into mental health if you can identify early enough is that every school will get support to identify if there are mental health issues with young children. Because if you can identify early enough and treat it, you will actually prevent it becoming a much bigger problem later on. Well, we're saying in our manifesto that we'll invest another billion on top of the £11.4 billion into mental health. And, of course, also look at the Mental Health Act to make sure the legislation is fit for purpose, as well as getting people back into work. Our target is a million people with disabilities, whether they're mental health issues or physical, back into the... Jonathan Bartley, what would the Green Party do? Daniel, in the short term, Help to Buy has helped about 350,000 families to bridge that gap in terms of deposit, because you probably can pay the monthly installments on the mortgage, but it's the actual capital that's needed to put down the down payment. In the longer term, I think to Arif's point, We pledge to build a million homes by 2020. We're running at approximately 189, 190,000 at the moment, so I think we'll hit that target by 2020. In the manifesto, we said we'll add another half a million by 2022, and what we want to do is work with councils and housing associations, so we build more council housing, and if people live in those houses for 15 years, they can actually have a right to buy those, because I think that's a good thing to do, to own your home. Tarek, to your point, about homelessness. There's a brilliant colleague of ours, I think we both, both parties support him, Bob Blackman. Shout out to Bob, he's a candidate this time around. He delivered the Homelessness Act, the bill that went through Parliament that had cross-party support and the government supported it. And the government, the Conservative Party in their manifesto, in our manifesto, we've pledged to cut homelessness by half in the next five years. And I hope we can deliver that on that pledge. Let me just answer that quickly, sorry, forgive me. Have you looked at shared ownership as well because that's another thing that you can look at where you pay part rent, but eventually end up sharing the ownership. Housing associations in my area of orbit do a lot of that. They operate in other areas, but it's worth looking at as well. Well, first of all, we introduced the National Living Wage. The Conservative Chancellor did that at £7.50. We've pledged to increase that to 60% of median wage and then continue for it to go up with inflation. I spent a year as the Prime Minister's Apprenticeship Advisor when David Cameron introduced the Apprenticeship Programme where we're basically putting a levy, half a percent of the wage bill of very large corporations. So any company that has a wage bill of three million pounds, half a percent of that is taken in apprenticeship levy. So that's basically going to deliver about three billion pounds of investment into apprenticeships. Now, when I looked at this, The Americans emailed, said, look, can we come and have a look at what you've done? Because we delivered about 2 million apprenticeships in the five years when David Cameron was prime minister. And then we set a target of 3 million for this parliament as well. We were doing something good, yes. It could always get better, I think. And degree apprenticeships, for example, are something that really is pioneering. So a teaching assistant can become a teacher on a degree apprenticeship. A nursing assistant can become a nurse on a degree apprenticeship. and be paid as well as getting that degree apprenticeship. But when you look at countries like Germany, one of the sort of insights that I took from that is that in Germany, businesses were embedded in the education system. So every child had exposure to both the opportunity of going to university or going on an apprenticeship. And the system was pretty stable. And I'm glad that actually Labour worked with us very much on the apprenticeship levy and support the apprenticeship levy on this. On zero-hours contracts, which I think you mentioned, so the problem with zero-hours contracts when we came into office is an employer can say, right, you're on zero-hours contracts and you're exclusive to me. You can't go anywhere else. You've got to sit at home, wait by the phone until I ring you. What we did was we changed the legislation to say, no, it should be equal. If you want me to give you my time, I should be able to go to three, four employers. You have your chance to speak. Relax. So we introduced the idea of actually both ways. The employee can decide to have three, four, five different contracts with different employers, and they can choose. There was a really interesting piece of work that McDonald's did the other day, and our fact checker hopefully will support me on this. So they offered all their zero-hours contract employees to go on a permanent contract. 80% of them said, no, we don't want to go on a permanent contract. We prefer the flexibility, whether they were students or for whatever reason, they wanted that flexibility. So we have to be careful before we sort of tar a hole. section of the workforce by saying, actually, you know what, these are all bad. Actually, people sometimes prefer that flexibility, especially if they're studying and want to work at certain hours, not all the time. You travelled it though. Brian's right, you travelled it. OK, let's stop about travelling. Yes, you can. I don't think there's a thing as a hard Brexit or a soft Brexit. I think it's a good Brexit. And what Theresa May needs is a mandate. What it means is Theresa set out 12 points. for the negotiations. He gave a very important speech at Lancaster House a few months ago. So controlling our borders, making sure that we have the best trade deal possible with our European partners. We remain European. We're just coming out of the EU institutions. The important thing is making sure we've got a trade deal that works for the UK and for Europe. We cooperate and make sure that we deliver defence and security for Europe. And we have 140,000 workers, for example, in our NHS who are European. We've got to secure them and Brits living in Europe.
deb_2017_06_06.CHAMPION: Thank you. First of all, I have to defend my party. We very, very deliberately throughout this campaign have not done the smearing. We've not done the name calling. And so what you're saying about Theresa May, what we're doing is we're looking at her record over the last seven years, six of which she was the Home Secretary, so in charge of police, in charge of our security. And I think it's acceptable to look at policies and look at decisions that politicians make. But I completely agree, it's totally unacceptable to name call. To go back to the bigger picture, I would say in the last five years, it's become much more acceptable to use really despicable language, which I would actually say is getting very close to hate crimes on social media. Politicians are using it, posters that we're seeing. I have to say the Brexit campaign was a particularly vile example of that. I'm glad that it feels in this election that that's calmed a little bit, but we need to have zero tolerance to that sort of thing. To look at the big picture, it's not just about a knee-jerk reaction of bringing in more police, bringing in more legislation. What we need to be doing is, from the youngest age, is teaching people about respect, about tolerance, and embedding that in. and then bringing back things like the youth service. You know, the youth service has been decimated in the last seven years. That's the place where young people can be, you know, just sort of channeled a little bit if they're saying things which are too upsetting, controversial, radical. That system isn't there now. So we're seeing that the schools are under-resourced, we're seeing the youth services disappeared, we're seeing the police, the community police aren't there. So all of those levels, which would have been like our canaries, when things were starting to go wrong, we could have gone, oops, hang on, we've got an issue here, let's put some support around that person. With all that gone, it becomes an inevitability that it's only when someone does something so utterly despicable, and I'm really sorry for the people that are directly impacted on what's happened in these last three, actually, atrocities. We shouldn't just be waiting until that moment. We should be doing something decades before. David, can you understand, though, that when a mainstream party like UKIP is putting out its so-called integration policy, which says that... We need to integrate. Sorry, can I speak? Which says that Muslim women shouldn't be able to wear headscarves... No, we didn't say that. OK. It also says that children, basically non-white children, when they come back to school should be checked to see if they've had genital mutilation. But by humiliating and separating children, do you think that that is an acceptable way forward? Diane put that forward in 2014. So, you know, David, I could possibly speak. Can you see that a mainstream party saying that is actually exacerbating and normalising hate crime, which is why young people feel terrified? This is genuinely the biggest problem that we have at the moment and all of us all of the parties here are coming out with different figures of how much we're going to invest in the NHS and in social care and I just to say that Labour has the biggest figure but it's still a drop in the ocean. But it's still a drop in the ocean. All the independent people are saying that what we need is more investment than we're able to financially commit. So what Labour is looking at is, as well as putting the money in, we're looking at overhauling the service. So we're looking at making sure that staff are both recognised, respected and rewarded, not demonised as they've been in the last couple of years. We're looking at how we can integrate social care and the NHS. so that both of them have parity, because to be quite honest, if you go into hospital for an operation and you can't get home again because there's no one in the community nursing to take care of you, or there's no health worker to come and take care of you, you then get stuck in hospital, which is the worst place for you, and then bed blocking begins. We're looking at very actively. Labour created the NHS, we're still incredibly proud of it. We're looking at making sure that we get back down to the 18 weeks to have your appointment, to have your operation. We're looking at getting back to four hours wait in A&E. And we do that by radically trying to rethink. We do that by talking to the staff. We do that by having an independent regulatory body that looks at the budgeting and that makes the right decisions for the NHS. So whether that's, well you all know, community pharmacy is being cut to the bone now. Whether that's negotiating the best deals on the price of the drugs we get. And to be quite honest, unless we take the politics out of this and get really grown up, we're not gonna have an NHS left. And the one thing that we're particularly passionate about, and I'd love to hear Nadine's views on this, is the Tories are going for something called the Naylor Report, which is looking at selling off land and assets for the NHS. We paid into this. We created this. We own this. It should be us, the population of the UK, that makes the decisions about what we do with our NHS, not just the politicians. You're promising a large amount of money. 30 billion over five years. How is Labour going to pay for that? Well, we all pay our taxes. So we have a pot of money and we've based the projections that we've made on the figures that the government are using at the moment. And then we've made specific sort of recommendations. So one of them is the top 5% earners in this country will pay 5% more. The 95% rest of us won't. And we're also looking at getting corporation tax back to levels that it was before. 2010, it was at 28%. The Tories last year brought it down to 17%? 19%. 19%. We're looking at putting it back up to 26% because, to be quite honest, we all need to be investing in our country. Conservative Party. We've been very clear. I'm not Diane Abbott. Can I answer? One sec, one sec. Sarah, and then we'll bring you in. We've been very clear that we are not looking at borrowing money unless it's for capital investment. What we're looking at is making sure that we are completely within our means on the spending that we're doing on revenue spending. We have had that independently verified. And to be quite honest, the only figures that are in the Tory manifesto are the page numbers. First of all, please don't hate me, but my first house cost £32,000, which you're saying is the average deposit. So I can't actually imagine how you look at having a home of your own. And I think that's one of the saddest things, because to have that security of something that's yours, whether that's a long-term secured rent, whether that's a council house or whether that's somewhere you buy. I mean, that's a fundamental human thing to do. And the flip of that is seeing the homeless levels rise and knowing that's to do with government choices around pay, around accessibility, around building houses. So what Labour is looking to do is basically fundamentally build more houses. It's also about getting young people so that they have the skills to build those houses. It's then about having a million new houses over a five-year period and making sure that half of those go into social housing, so whether that's with housing associations or with councils, so that they're there, they're secure and they're an asset of this country. It's also about making sure that you have landlords who are honourable because the level of rent increases is ridiculous. So we're going to make sure that that's capped to inflation. We're also going to make sure that landlords have to provide a safe home so that all of the checks are done for you. We're going to have to try and also make sure that this just getting rid of tenants when they don't want them, or to get more people in and bigger deposits. That's got to stop and we will bring in legislation to make sure that that stops. But again, with everything that Labour's proposing, what we're trying to do is think big, think how we can help everybody to have a secure home and also to help the economy by putting those skills and the money back in the country. So the final thing, you were talking about foreign investors. If people want to invest in this country, fantastic, we'll take their money, but we also want to make sure that they do invest in this country because they are using our roads, they'll be using our education and they'll be benefiting from the economy that we want to see growing. That's an absolute out and out lie and I think the Tories are outrageous. To be putting lies in there. It's in your manifesto. We are not having a garden tax. It is nonsense. Yeah, we want to ban exploitative ones because we do not think that that is fair. We think that everybody deserves a proper living wage starting at £10 from the age of 18 because, to be quite honest, Prices of a loaf of bread or a bus fare are the same if you're 18 or 28. It's also taking it back a step, so we want to get apprenticeships, good quality apprenticeships, on a par with university degrees, whereas at the moment they seem to be drifting and there's a stigma associated with apprenticeships which is nonsense because particularly businesses are crying out for well-trained people. What we'll do, which is a really big thing, is we will scrap tuition fees because to come out of a degree with £40,000 debt is crazy. What we'll also be looking at doing is finally getting rid of the gender pay gap because I do not understand why it helps anyone and definitely our economy when a woman is earning on average 18% less than a man doing the same job. We've had Labour brought in legislation around equal pay in 1970 and we're still at an 18% pay gap. What we'll also look at doing is getting a national investment fund. We'll have banks regionally so that small businesses particularly can go to that and grow their business to bring in more employment in the area. But what we're looking for particularly is that sort of high earning jobs rather than just about doing jobs. So 3% of our GDP is actually going to go on research and development because that's what we're seeing is the fastest way to grow our economy. Well, we brought in £1,000 as opposed to £9,000. I think there's a big difference between a contribution and £9,000. Perhaps because it's been 10 years since we've seen how it works in Scotland. I've only been here four years. And what we're doing now is looking at what changes we need for the future. I'm good, but I can't change history, I'm afraid. OK, I think it's time to get a reality check. Labour is very clear that we want to maintain an incredibly strong partnership with Europe. We're very aware that the terms of that are going to be changing, but we cannot be going into this all bluster, throwing the baby out with the bathwater. What we need to be looking at is workers' rights, environmental protections and security, as well as forming a really strong trading relationship.
deb_2017_06_06.FORBES: Thank you very much. I mean, the police force is devolved in Scotland, which means it's within the competence of the Scottish Government. And we have been maintaining our police numbers over the last few years. And ultimately, cuts are a political choice. And that's why in the aftermath of the atrocities and the hearts of everybody in Scotland is with Manchester and has been with Manchester over the last two weeks. There are questions to be asked about where we best deploy our resources. And we believe that law enforcement at every stage of the process needs to have the resources to do their job properly. We have legislation there, but they need resources. And in Scotland, we have made the decision over the last, the SNP hasn't been in government since 2007, to increase the number of police on on the beat, as it were, and to make sure that we have the resources we need. So in the aftermath of the atrocities, whilst we were very grateful for the offer of the armed forces to come in and patrol our streets, we did not need to call on that resource and we were able, we had enough capacity within the police force to do that. But I think at election time, a lot of people talk about supporting mental health. And again, it goes back to actually reviewing people's records. And we need to raise awareness. We need to fund it. But we need to put the money where it's most important. In Scotland, we were, under the SNP, we're one of the first governments to establish a minister for mental health. And we have doubled the number of places for child psychologists, for example, going back to the point. about putting the money where it's most critical from a young age, and making sure there is that support. But it comes back to this idea of everything being integrated. So education, health care, we've also integrated health and social care. So that means that we aren't, there isn't the same extent, for example, of bed blocking, because we're able to support people. from the point that they come into contact with social care or with the health service. And there's that streamlined support for people, so that when they ask for help, they're not left hanging for a very, very, very, very long time before they get help. They're able to get help quickly. So you're right that health is devolved but our overall budget is still set by Westminster and if we're seeing cuts under the Conservatives in all the other areas of competences we're also seeing areas we're all seeing cuts to the Scottish budget itself and we made the decision that in order to protect jobs we had to maintain this cap but in our recent manifesto we've said that we will reconsider it and we will ensure that nurses and doctors are paid in line with inflation. If we make it quite simple, we need three things. Simplistically, we need more supply, but we need the right houses, and we need good jobs that pay well. We need more houses. And again, it comes back to looking at what parties' records are, not just what they're saying now in a campaign. And over the last parliamentary term in Scotland, the SNP exceeded their target of building 30,000 affordable homes. And by affordable, I mean houses that actually reflect what people are earning, what the average earnings are in the area. And that can be a variety of different types of home, that can be social rent, council houses, shared equity schemes, but to make sure that there is that increase of supply of affordable homes. But also looking more broadly, whilst housing is devolved, there are certainly policies that are made at Westminster that have a a really negative effect in Scotland. And since 2013, the Scottish government, the SNP government, have been spending about 400 million pounds trying to mitigate the effects of the bedroom tax. And we're also really disturbed to see the scrapping of housing benefit for 18 to 21-year-olds, because that will have a very negative effect on people in Scotland who are in need of housing support. So we're building more. We're building the right types of houses. But we need a bit of help from Westminster. Okay, Kate Forbes, SMB? Yeah, well, I think if you ever wanted an example of how austerity hasn't worked for the last seven years, employment and youth employment is a good place to start. And the Conservatives like to style themselves as looking after the economy and meeting these financial targets, and yet they've singularly failed to do that. And so the economy has not grown at the extent it should have. And austerity has actually kept the economy back, which means you haven't got the same extent of job creation. But it isn't just about people in employment. It's about what they're getting paid as well. And again, we in the SNP feel very, very strongly that people should be paid a fair wage. They should be paid the real living wage, not the rebranded living wage. And the cutoff should not be age 25. Why should somebody age 26 earn something that's a different wage from somebody age 24 who might be doing exactly the same job? So we've said in our manifesto that we're committed to raising the minimum wage in line with the real living wage so that it's over £10 over the next parliamentary term. But it really does come back to ending austerity, making sure that people have money in their pockets that they can spend so there's more money going round the economy and that means that our economy grows, we're more productive and it's better all round. That's where we'll leave it. Kate Forbes, SNP. So we respect across the UK that a majority voted to leave the EU. But in Scotland, 62% voted to remain. And so over the last year, Nicola Sturgeon put forward some compromise solutions for what, in a way that would recognise the majority vote to leave, but also Scotland's position in voting to remain. All those proposals and discussions have been soundly rejected. And so we're now in a position of saying, well, once we know the deal, whenever that might be over the next two years, we will give people in Scotland the opportunity to choose between a hard Brexit that doesn't take into account Scotland's interests and independence in the EU. Jonathan Bartley, the Green Party.
deb_2017_06_06.KURTEN: I'm glad you did mention the word Islamist and Islamism because this is the root of the problem and we have to be honest about that and this is an ideology, it's an idea and there are some writings, Islamic writings that are problematic. Yeah, well, this is a poster, you know, it's about immigration. It's a real picture. It was in The Guardian in October 2015, I believe. I mean Labour seems to have a magic money tree which doesn't exist and they're not going to. Jeremy Corbyn comes out like Santa Claus saying I'm going to give this and I'm going to do this and I'm going to do this but there's no money there to do all of these things. The Conservatives, you know, talk about having a strong economy but they're cutting the NHS to the bone. Well, absolutely, 11 billion pounds. We know where we're going to get it from. We know we're going to get it from Brexit, from not paying our EU membership fees. That's 10 billion a year. And we're also going to cut the overseas development budget back to the useful part of it and not spend the wasteful part of it giving money to countries with space programs and nuclear weapons, rather than spending it here at home on hospitals, on mental health care, and on investing in our doctors and nurses. And we will be able to. re-give out the bursaries to nurses and midwives, and we'll be able to pay back the student fees of students doing STEM subjects, studying medicine, so that if you do study medicine, pharmacy, science, and things like that, you will get your fees paid back for you if you work in the NHS for 10 years after you've graduated. So I think that's a very good deal that we will give. We're the best on the NHS. It won't be privatised by UKIP, we'll keep it in public hands and we're going to be able to fund the bursaries for nurses and midwives. Well, no, absolutely. It isn't, actually. And we are not going to privatise it. And we will, as I said, we're going to give the bursaries to you and we're going to... Can I go back to the original question? In 1996, the average cost of a house was about three times the average salary. They were very, very affordable. But now it's about eight to ten times the cost of an average salary. deb_2017_06_06.PADDOCK: No, I mean, there are two issues here, and it's not about... You've got to have the police there to respond, as you said, when an incident like this happens. Well, cannabis, gives some people, a very, very small proportion of people who have a propensity to schizophrenia to trigger it. But it's a very, very small proportion of people. So what we are planning to do is to promise 300,000 new homes a year. If the private sector, as the Tories rely on, does not deliver that, then the government must directly commission housing where it's needed to make sure we get up to that level. In terms of people who can't afford a deposit, We've got a scheme called Rent to Buy. What it means is that you rent a property from a housing association, you pay market rent, but gradually you'll build up, just by paying the rent, you gradually build up a share in that property. So at the end of 30 years, where you're just paying the rent, you will own that property. And if you want to move, then you take whatever you've built up in terms of the deposit when you move. In Wales, some people who receive treatment because of poor funding in mental health support have to travel to England to receive the support. I think the person I admire most in the world at the present time is the General Secretary of the United Nations, Guterres, trying to bring the world together. Well, I know Mr. Johnson likes a good read. So what I would probably leave under the tree for him would be a Christmas carol by Charles Dickens. And he can then understand how nasty Scrooge was. deb_2019_11_22.CORBYN: No, they should not be frightened of an incoming Labour government because our country's economy relies on many small and medium-sized enterprises that are the motor of our economy that often have great difficulty in accessing capital and accessing support for what they're trying to do. The biggest businesses will be asked to pay a little bit more in corporation tax, but it will be lower than it was in 2010, and indeed lower than the average for most industrial countries. What I want to do is ensure that there is an economy thriving in every part of the country. And so that means the resources. It means an infrastructure system, roads and railways all across the country. It means broadband in every part of our country that's free to everyone to use. And that will mean that businesses can be set up anywhere. It also It is also clear in our manifesto here that what we're keen to do is ensure there is a better trained workforce in our society, so we will be promoting apprenticeships and skilled training at every level and give them the same value as academic education in universities. And so I enjoy working with all the business organisations, setting out clearly our views on public ownership, our views on taxation and our views on investment for the future. The issue of your views on rights and freedoms in our society, I absolutely defend the right of free speech. I supported the introduction of the Human Rights Act. I support the Convention on Human Rights. Freedoms and rights in a democracy are very, very important. I spent my life getting into hot water for defending people because I believe their human rights should not be violated. And that's the kind of government that I intend to lead that does guarantee democracy. Well, I can't understand what everything is going on in your mind that makes you say that, but whatever. Maybe we can talk about it later if you like. I want to live in a society where we do deal with the worst aspects of poverty that exist in our society, we do invest properly in our schools, we do give all our children a real chance and we do have a health service that is really there for all of us at all times. That's the kind of agenda that I've spent my life dealing with. The gentleman that asked the question about businesses, On the questions of public ownership, which you're quite right to raise, our proposals are that we will take our railways, which after all were invested in and built by the rest of us, back into public ownership. We'll take Royal Mail back into public ownership as it's a natural monopoly and our water industry which has been asset-stripped to within an inch of its life by many of these water companies and wastes a great deal of water will come back into public ownership which will be run with consumer and worker involvement. The new announcement we made last week was on broadband because I believe that the government's proposals are insufficient Only 10% of households in the whole of the UK get access to fibre optic broadband. We will make that 100% within 10 years, starting in the areas, the remote areas of the country, that have the least access at the moment. Hence, we can get the business development done in those places. Nobody should... Nobody should suffer any abuse in public life or privately. Nobody should suffer abuse, and many women Labour MPs, Ruth Smith included and others, have suffered the most unbelievable levels of abuse. And it was a Labour MP, Jo Cox, who was murdered because she stood up in public life. I simply say to you that bad behaviour, misogynism, racism in any form, is absolutely not acceptable in any form whatsoever in my party or in our society. I've had many conversations with Ruth Smith since then. She's left the party. The gentleman concerned I'd known for many years, and I did say hello to him as I left the meeting, as you would somebody you'd known as well. Go and look at the video, Mr Corbyn. And also, you don't know what was said between us. It's our process that is being looked at and I'm very happy about that. We've laid ourselves completely open to it. We're the party that after all set up the Equality and Human Rights Commission and in government we will fund it properly and I think it's right that our processes should be examined. We're the only party that actually has a full process to deal with abuse, racism in any form. within our party or our society is simply not acceptable. We have suspended and expelled members that have been guilty of any racist activity. I met Evo Morales in Mexico last year. I had a very long conversation with him about his country and about the economic development that's happened and the numbers of people that have been taken out of poverty. Obviously, I follow what's going on in that country as I do in many other countries in the world. He resigned from the presidency and now has moved out of Bolivia. I ask and I support the other Latin American governments that have asked for a process of peace in Bolivia so that the Constitution can be restored, so that democratic government can come back. That surely is the right thing to do. It's obvious there is civil unrest in Bolivia. There are very strong feelings on both sides about people that feel they've been let down because Evo Morales is no longer president, others that probably don't support him at all. Surely the function now is to try and bring people together. I met Evo Morales last year. I've met him over the years. recognise that Bolivia, before he became president, was probably the poorest country in Latin America. It isn't now. I'm sorry to shut you down. APPLAUSE Thank you. Thanks for your question. The issue of Brexit can be one that divides people enormously and it has and it does. Our whole strategy, my whole strategy has been to try to bring people together however they voted in the referendum in 2016. Right. One, we will negotiate a credible leave deal with the European Union. Let me finish, please. I'm trying to answer the gentleman's question. Secondly, we will put that alongside remain in a referendum. My role and the role of our government will be to ensure that that referendum is held in a fair atmosphere, and we will abide by the result of it. and I will adopt, as Prime Minister if I am at the time, a neutral stance so that I can credibly carry out the results of that to bring our communities and country together rather than continuing an endless debate about the EU and Brexit. This will be a trade deal with Europe or remaining in the EU. That will be the choice that we put before the British people within six months. Any other option will require years of negotiations either with the EU or the USA and put our public services at risk. Well, I think the demands of people to try to, A, some parties want to revoke Article 50. I actually voted to implement Article 50. Some people wanted a second referendum. Some people wanted leaving without any deal whatsoever, which I think would be catastrophic to our economy. People voted remain, they voted leave but they didn't vote to lose their jobs, they didn't vote for deregulation, they didn't vote for damage to our economy and I think it's only sensible and reasonable to put that option back there to the people within six months in order to bring our country and society together to face the future together to bring about a fairer Britain. Thank you. Thank you very much for your question. We face a climate emergency across the whole world. It's very obvious. Unusual and extreme weather patterns have got worse and worse. The poorest and most vulnerable people in the poorest and most vulnerable places suffer first. Others suffer later. We signed up to the Paris Climate Change Accords in 2015. I was there, I thought it was great, but it didn't go far enough and it's got to go much further. So on May 1st this year, I proposed to Parliament that we declare a climate emergency in our country and that's now been replicated in many Parliaments around the world and I'm pleased about that. But it does mean we've got to play our part by reducing emissions to get to net zero emissions. It also means the possibilities of a huge number of jobs and development in our country in dealing with this emergency. It means jobs in retrofitting houses to make them environmentally sustainable, to make them better insulated, to use less energy. It means green energy jobs, wind, wave, solar. It means big projects like Swansea Bay Lagoon. It also means a lot has to be done in protecting our biodiversity, our seas and our rivers. And so our whole strategy is to develop a green industrial revolution which will create hundreds of thousands of jobs in the future, starting very, very quickly. And we will be therefore playing our part in getting to a more sustainable world and a Labour government on the international stage, we'll be working with others to get to net zero and make sure that by our trading policies we don't end up inadvertently exporting pollution by having strong controls here but not insisting on equally strong controls somewhere else. Thank you very much. I'm very excited by everything that's in our green industrial revolution strategy, just as much as I admire those in the USA with the Green New Deal that they're putting forward. OK. Can I deal with the broadband question first? Thank you for it. The issue is, as I said earlier, 95% of people in South Korea have access to broadband free. We have, in this country, 10% access, and it costs people roughly £30 a month. And it's quite selective, the areas... It's not free in South Korea, though, is it? For some people it is, I'm not sure about the whole country, but it is available for the whole country. Our point is that by investing in it, it actually promotes economic growth and brings about a degree of social justice. Listen, everyone in this room probably has a computer and uses access to broadband if they've got it, or some form of internet all the time. It is the normal form of communication. Yet there is a big digital divide between those that can afford the best and those that don't get any whatsoever. I just want to make sure it's equally available for all. And over 10 years, we would make sure it is. And I think that's actually a sensible, up-to-date, modern way of looking at communications within our society. Yes, we can afford it, because if we don't afford it, then what's the effect on areas that don't have access to broadband, on their economic development, on the investments that might come there, on the businesses that won't go there? Listen, talk to a business about investing in, say, Cumbria, or, say, parts of Northumbria. Say, would you open a factory or a shop or a small business here? They'd say, No, there's no broadband, there isn't good train access, there isn't good bus access or whatever else. We have to invest in social infrastructure and transport infrastructure for the whole country. Otherwise, the North-South divide, the inequalities between regions in England and between Scotland and Wales and England will simply get worse as a result of it. This is a serious proposal in order to help bring economic development to all parts of our country. I read the IFS comments very carefully obviously and we have here the grey book that we put forward which actually funds and costs the whole of our programme and manifesto and I was absolutely insistent that we as a party going into election put that forward. It's only right that people should understand it. is 95% of the population will pay no more whatsoever in tax. The top 5% will pay a bit more. Corporation tax will go up to a maximum of 26%, which is lower than it was in 2010, and lower than many countries in other parts of the world, including major industrial economies. and we will then move into a situation where we become more or less mid-ranking in terms of provisions of funding of public services. We'll still be way below all the Scandinavian countries, France and Germany. What's happened in this country for too long is we've rolled back the public realm. We've rolled back on public services and loaded that problem onto individuals, families and they're often paying with it through personal debt. I think we need to rebalance a bit and start investing in people and communities as a whole and that's what our manifesto does. Well if they've got good quality broadband obviously that's a start and clearly there has to be full accessibility to public services online because there is, you're quite correct, a digital divide where public services increasingly, doctors appointments and everything else are done online and there are a significant number of people in the country that don't necessarily have smartphones, probably don't have computers and can't access those things and that becomes a form of social exclusion. So I would always make it a requirement that public services must be available also in the more traditional ways as well so that nobody is left out because there's nothing worse than people that are told, for example, to go online to sort out their universal credit, can't get online because they haven't got a computer or a smartphone. How on earth are they supposed to access universal credit in that sense? That's why we will end universal credit and bring back a serious system. OK, thanks for your questions, and thanks for the way that you've put them. An incoming Labour government will invest a great deal across the UK, including 100 billion pounds will go into Scotland over 10 years. That will be a massive game changer in Scotland, where you have the worst levels of poverty and health inequality in Glasgow. You have serious levels of child poverty and housing stress. You know that as well as I do. So the question is, why will you not support it, or will you support a referendum? And, and I've said that I don't see a priority in Scotland of having an independence referendum. What I see as a priority is investing in the social and capital needs of Scotland. And so, in the early years of a Labour government, we will not be supporting an independence referendum. Instead, we'll be investing in Scotland and working with whoever the government is in Scotland to deliver that programme, deliver that improvement in in business infrastructure, in transport infrastructure and in the social needs of the people of Scotland. Is that year two, year three, year four? The early years, first two years at least. So not in the first two years. I want to deliver a capital investment programme for the whole of the UK. No, no, I heard that. I heard that very clearly. And that will be our focus. And so it will not be in the early part of the new Parliament. So not in the first two years. There are elections in Scotland in 2021, and obviously an expression will come from that. It is, I think, our right as a party to put forward a programme for the whole of the UK, and that is what we're doing, an investment strategy for the whole of the UK, and point out, I point out, that Scotland will get 100 billion of investment under a Labour government which will make a very significant difference to the lives of people all across Scotland. That, I'm sure, is something you would welcome. We are putting forward a proposal which I think will be very beneficial to all parts of the UK, including Scotland. And I think you have to look at the situation of the income and expenditure levels as they affect Scotland. Surely Scotland needs to be able to address the problems it has, and the industrial and green infrastructure investment that it absolutely needs. That's all it means, Scotland, they're telling me. Since 2014 there has been a Tory government in the UK and that is one of the problems. OK. I'll try and deal with some of those questions, if I may. You've got about a minute left, I'm afraid, Mr Corbyn. First of all, we're not doing any deals with any other parties. I'm not trying to form a coalition government. I'm fighting this election to win it for Labour, to win it for the people of this country, to end universal credit, deal with the issues of inequality, injustice and poverty, and deliver an economy that absolutely works for all. And our taxation proposals will affect the richest 5% of the people in this country. We will end the marriage tax allowance because I think it's discriminatory against non-married couples and it ought to be treated equally across the society and individual taxation is a fairer way of doing things. Our manifesto, fully prepared, fully costed, I think this is an opportunity for our country to come together, deal with the inequality, injustice and poverty and actually go forward to the future with some real degree of hope. Thank you very much.
deb_2019_11_22.STURGEON: Obviously, it is a very good and relevant question for everybody across the UK. The SNP may well hold the balance of power in a hung Parliament after this election. Let me say, we will use that influence, if we have it, responsibly and in the interests, of course, of standing up for Scotland, but also in the interests of progressive values for people right across the UK. Let me be very honest, I could not, in good conscience, ever put Boris Johnson into number 10 Downing Street. because I have seen the misery that Tory policies have heaped upon people. Jeremy Corbyn may not be my choice of Labour leader but I don't get to choose the Labour leader. In terms of what I would seek to win from a minority Labour government, obviously I would ask for and expect Jeremy Corbyn to respect the right of the Scottish people to choose their own future. Because it is not for Westminster to decide, it is for the people of Scotland. But I would also want an end to austerity. I would want to see an end to the misery of universal credit. I would want to see an NHS protection bill so that our NHS cannot be put at the mercy of Donald Trump in a trade deal. I would want to see fairer pensions for our older people. The UK has one of the lowest pensions in the OECD. So these are the progressive policies that the SNP would champion, yes, for people in Scotland, but also for the benefit of people right across the UK. Well, you know, firstly, let me explain my position, and then I'll perhaps speculate on Jeremy Corbyn's, if I may. Well, you don't need to speculate, because he told us right here, so would you accept it? I'll speculate on what his position might be after the election, as opposed to what he said here today. Oh, I see. OK. But my position is one of principle. There'll be differences of opinion in this room on Scottish independence. Of course there is a difference of opinion in Scotland on Scottish independence. I would not be asking Jeremy Corbyn to support independence. but simply to accept the principle that that is a question that should be decided by the people of Scotland, not by Westminster. Now, I guess, if I can turn to the position that I think Jeremy Corbyn might take after an election, I've heard some of what Jeremy Corbyn has been saying to you this evening. He spoke with a lot of passion. I don't agree with him on everything, but I do agree with a great deal of what he was talking about, about public spending, health, austerity... But the key thing, as you said, not for the first two years. But that's the point I'm coming on to. I'm asking you, having heard Jeremy Corbyn, do you think he's going to walk away from the chance to end austerity, to protect the NHS, to stop universal credit, simply because he wants, for a couple of years, to prevent Scotland to have the right to self-determination? Jeremy Corbyn supports the right of self-determination for almost every other country in the world. I'm not sure he's going to compromise the chance to have a Labour government for that issue. Look, you're putting it maybe more bluntly, I will be more diplomatic. Just calling it like it is. But the point is, I lead a minority government in Scotland, so I know the compromises that you have to make to govern in that situation. Of course he is putting a manifesto forward, but if he is in the position of needing the support of the SNP, then I think he will choose to do some of the things that are in his manifesto. I don't think he will turn his back on that in order to block the right of the people of Scotland to choose their own future. I would ask you to make your own minds up on that. The man here in the front. I want the Scottish people to have the right to choose their own future. Fundamentally for Scotland, we have put up with... Scotland has not voted for a Conservative Westminster Government for 60 years. For 36 of those 60 years, we have had to put up with a Tory Government. the European Union against our will. I want Scotland to determine our own future. But in answer to your question, I will never put a Tory Prime Minister into power. I grew up in the west of Scotland. I saw the damage. I grew up in the Thatcher years. I saw the damage that Tory governments did to Scotland and to communities across the UK. I will never support a Tory government. But if Jeremy Corbyn wants the support of the SNP, I don't think it should surprise people to hear me say that I would ask him to respect the right of the people of Scotland to choose their own future. And I don't think he'll turn his back on ending austerity in order to block that right. I will try to do it as briefly as possible. On the question, very good question, about are we prioritising independence over other issues, I would say that the evidence says no. Don't get me wrong, Scotland has challenges, all countries have challenges. As First Minister, I face challenges in our public services every single day and my job is to confront those. But if you look at some of the policies... Well, if you look at some of the policies in Labour's manifesto published this week, If you take things like free prescriptions, free university education, restoring the nursing bursary, opposition to fracking, for example, these are all things that my government in Scotland has already done. So we're getting on with delivering those progressive policies, but we want to be in charge of our own future, not to turn our backs on the rest of the UK. We'll always be the closest friends and neighbours, but so that we don't get dragged down paths by Tory governments that we don't want to go down. The second question on if there is a second EU referendum and the UK votes to remain. I hope that happens. I've campaigned and worked as hard as possible, as has the SNP, to try to find a way for the whole UK to stay in the EU, because I think that's the best future. The case for independence doesn't rest solely on Brexit. Brexit is one example, it's a very extreme example of the way in which Scotland is treated with contempt often by Westminster. And we've seen that over the past three years. We've tried to compromise, we've tried to find ways to protect Scotland's interests, and that hasn't happened. So I think Scotland does need to make that choice about taking a future into our own hands. And thirdly, because this is a very good question about, you know, will independence be as difficult as Brexit? And, you know, I... You've probably noticed this. I oppose Brexit. I think Brexit is a big mistake. But what I don't think is that the mess that Brexit has become was inevitable. That was down to the fact that the Brexiteers, if I can be undiplomatic for a moment, told a lot of lies, one of them on the side of a bus. It was down... down to the fact that there was no honesty or frankness about the implications, no planning done in advance, and then Theresa May came out with all these contradictory red lines that could never be reconciled. Contrast that with the independence referendum in 2014. Whether you agreed with the proposition or not, there was a very detailed proposition. We had done the work, we had done the thinking, we had a whole transition programme ready to go had the result gone the other way. I don't think we should allow the Brexiteers, who made such a hash of Brexit, to tar all positive change with their brush. They messed it up. It doesn't mean others have to mess it up as well. And you want a confirmatory referendum on Brexit. That is actually connected to the answer I've just given. Oh, I didn't hear that answer. If you'll let me answer it in my own terms, if I may, because I was drawing the contrast between the mess of Brexit and the different approach taken for independence. I don't think it was inevitable that Brexit was a mess, therefore I don't think it was inevitable that we were in the position of having another referendum. I don't think the position we are in right now with Brexit had to be like this. That was down to the bad planning, the lack of planning, which I don't think the proponents of independence would allow to happen. Can we hear from Rebecca Ferguson? Well, you know, people understandably ask me that all the time, and what I say, and I know it sounds like a typical politician's way of avoiding the answer before I say it is... Oh, no, we don't want that. Well... Not tonight. I know, because I genuinely don't like to answer questions that way, but if I come to that point, then I'll set out how I'll cross that bridge when I come to it. And, yes, I've thought a lot about that, but I don't want to talk about that right now, because that is me conceding that these Westminster politicians actually have a right to block Scotland's ability to choose our own future and actually we're in an election campaign right now and people in Scotland have an opportunity to send a very clear message that they want to be in charge of their own future and that's what I'm asking them to do. But the government does have a right to block it. Your government has the veto on it. I don't think they have a moral or a democratic right to block the right. It has never been legally challenged. I have always accepted and I continue to accept that the transfer of power is necessary and desirable. But I don't believe they have a moral or democratic right. We are in an election campaign and there is an opportunity for Scotland to make that abundantly clear. No I think that's a fair point but what I'm saying to Scotland right now and as I said if I come to that point then I will set out exactly to people in Scotland what I think we should do then but what I'm saying to Scotland now in a general election campaign is we have the ability to to send that message and make that view and that voice very clear. And that's what I hope people in Scotland will do for that reason. But I also hope they'll vote SNP so that we can contribute to all of these other social and economic policies that I've been talking about. OK, there's a few hands up. Can I take the gentleman's questions in turn? There are a lot of questions in there, let me try to take them in turn. Firstly, with the greatest of respect, Europe did not say that in 2014. Europe did not make any comment about what would happen if Scotland voted for independence. It certainly didn't say that Scotland would not be allowed to be a member of the European Union. Some may have taken from their body language, they weren't that keen on it, but they didn't actually say that. Nor has Spain ever said that it would veto the membership of an independent Scotland. What Spain has said is that if it is done constitutionally and legally, they would respect that. and I travel a lot around the European Union and there's been a sea change in attitudes towards Scottish independence since Brexit, both in the institutions and in member states. The European Union, since it was first established, has sought to enlarge itself. I think the idea that it's going to try to kick out or not allow in part of a member state that has been there for 40 years is kind of inconceivable. On the deficit, can I firstly make this point? Exclude London, because London skews a lot of the economic calculations for the UK. Scotland's deficit is not out of sync with the rest of the UK. But the thing is, London is in the UK. OK, but in terms of... Because Scotland is often treated as an outlier, London is actually the outlier and Scotland is in line. But that is... We'll leave that point to one side, because I can see I'm not convincing you. Well, what about you? I mean, it doesn't really matter if it's convincing me. OK, but the other point is Scotland's deficit is falling. It fell by £1 billion in the most recent year. Our onshore revenues went up by £3 billion. But my more fundamental point is this. If the argument against independence is that Scotland has a big deficit, that is a deficit that has been accrued under the Westminster system of governance. And if we've got a big deficit because of that, that's not an argument for Scotland staying the same. Well, I don't control, as First Minister, the macroeconomic policies of the United Kingdom. I wish I did in some respects, but I don't. So Scotland does not take all of the decisions in our own government that govern these things. But the point I'm making is, if you look at independent countries similar in size to Scotland all over Europe, they're richer, they're more prosperous, Why shouldn't Scotland, with all the natural and human and social resources we have, be capable of emulating that if we have the powers to do that within our own hands? Thank you. Look, if Brexit hadn't happened, do I think right now... I would still support independence, OK? I'm not going to pretend otherwise. But do I think right now we'd be standing here talking about having another independence referendum so imminently? Probably not. But circumstances have changed dramatically. And in a democracy... In a democracy, when circumstances change, people have to have the right to change their mind. And if they don't have that right, then I don't believe what we're living in is a democracy. I can't force Scotland to choose independence just as I can't force the UK to stay in the EU. But I do believe we should have that choice so that whatever path we take as a country, whatever kind of country we are going to become, it's one we've chosen ourselves. It's not one that we've had the likes of Boris Johnson decide for us. If by who, sorry? Exactly. Firstly, on the point of EU membership, Scotland, whether we're in or out of the EU, wants to get its deficit down to sustainable levels. Most countries across the Western world have deficits and we've set out plans to reduce that. It is reducing already. It's not a condition of joining the EU to meet those deficit levels. I want to see more investment in Scotland from whoever is in government in Westminster, which is why I've set out the policies that the SNP would want to see pursued. But our experience of Westminster governments over a long time is that that investment doesn't always come. It's always also the case that if you want in Scotland to deprive Boris Johnson of a majority, then the way to do that is to vote SNP, because we're the main challengers in every Conservative seat. On the issue of our grandparents, my grandmother was from the north of England. I've got many family members in England. I believe in independence so that Scotland can be self-governing. But the closest ties of family, of history, social ties between Scotland, England, Wales, Northern Ireland, will always be close. And I don't believe independence for Scotland is turning our backs on that. It's simply making sure that Scotland is in those relationships on the basis of equality. And I think that's a good and positive thing for Scotland. And actually, I think it would have benefits for the rest of the UK as well. Let's take a question now from Stephen Whitelaw. That's a very good question. Firstly, some context for that, as you're probably aware. Scotland, because of our disproportionately high drug use back in the 1980s, there's an ageing cohort of drug users that have other health conditions, which is one, it's not the only reason, but it's one of the reasons for that rising number of drugs deaths. It's a public health emergency. It's not acceptable. What we are doing about it, we're investing more money in drug treatment services. We've established a a task force made up of experts but also people with lived experience of drug use to look at the different, more innovative things that we need to do. Power over the classification of drugs is still held at Westminster. One of the things that Glasgow City Council is wanting to do, and we are seeking the power in order to do that, is to establish in Glasgow what's called a safe consumption room, so that we can try to reduce some of the harm related to drugs. But this is one of the most important issues that we're facing in Scotland, and I think there is a real... I know there is a real determination, but I think it exists across parties to do absolutely that. There is more to do. So some of these policies will be starting to have an effect, but it will take longer in some respects. So I'm not going to stand here and make promises that I can't necessarily deliver in the year timescale that you're talking about. What I can say with absolute conviction is that there is a real determination to tackle this and to do what is required over whatever period is required. I don't want, not just to be First Minister of a country, I don't want to be a citizen of a country in which we've got people dying unnecessarily of drug use. If you take alcohol misuse, for example, which is not the same, we've implemented, for example, minimum unit pricing. So we have a record as a country of doing really courageous things when it comes to public health and we have got to show that same determination here too. Yeah, no, I think that is absolutely right. And... APPLAUSE Given your... Excuse me a second. Given your experience, if you were willing, I would love to talk to you a bit more and perhaps put you in touch with some of the people within the Scottish Government that are working on this, because it's really important that we do listen to people who have experience so that we can learn from that and make sure that the policies we're taking forward properly reflect that. So, if you are willing to leave your details there, I would love to follow that up. Well, that's a very good... Can I just clarify, firstly, not to dodge the question, because I'll come on to it. I don't envisage being in a coalition government with Labour. I think it would be a less formal arrangement than that, if we're in that situation. But, you know, many of the areas of England suffer from much the same neglect as Scotland does and much the same contempt from Westminster as Scotland does. So the SNP will always try to stand up for decentralisation from London. Much of what we have been able to do because we have a Scottish Parliament some of the policies that Labour are now promising in their UK manifesto, I think with more power, with more devolution to some of the regions of England, would be possible there as well. So the SNP will always be a voice for decentralisation and more powers to be spread around. No, we would always respect... So we are responsible in Scotland for health policy and education, for example. I don't think it's right that we would seek to... You know, no more right than it would be for Westminster to dictate to Scotland that we would dictate health policy or education policy in England, except where that affects Scotland, because often the decisions that are taken around Westminster on the health service, for example, have implications for our budget. and one of the big issues in the next Parliament potentially for the health service is trade deals and we all have to come together those of us who don't want our NHS sold off to make sure that that doesn't happen so we will seek to exercise influence of course in Scotland's interest but where it is appropriate in the interests of people right across the UK. In a word, you are right, I agree with the sentiment. If we were to stop oil and gas production tomorrow, we would become more dependent on imports, which would actually increase the carbon footprint. So we've got to have a managed transition and a just transition. That transition is underway. Rightly so, we've got to accelerate it. We've got a net zero target in Scotland, in law already, of net zero emissions by 2045. That's five years ahead of the rest of the UK. So we've got to power on with these things and we're absolutely focused on doing it.
deb_2019_11_22.SWINSON: But I've just started with the easy ones. No, I don't regret it. And I'll tell you why. I think many of you might have watched that debate on Monday night between Boris Johnson and Jeremy Corbyn. And I don't know how you felt about it, but when I watched that, I felt pretty dismayed with the thought that somehow that has to be the choice, that that's all that we get to choose between. And I'm in the position of leading a political party that's standing in more than 600 seats at this election, And I'm proud to say that you don't have to make a choice between Boris Johnson and Jeremy Corbyn. There is a better alternative, because I truly believe that our country deserves a better and brighter future than what either of them is offering right now. And I'm determined, in my role as Liberal Democrat leader, to do everything I possibly can to make that happen. There's still three weeks left in this campaign and all I would say is to anybody that thinks that you can predict the outcome of the election in the middle of the campaign, ask Theresa May how that worked out last time round. I recognise that things have got much more challenging since Boris Johnson and Nigel Farage cooked up a deal to stitch up seats between them. I get that it is a big ask, but I am absolutely fighting for every Liberal Democrat vote and for every Liberal Democrat seat. And we do live in a world where sometimes strange things have happened in politics. I didn't predict that Trump was going to become president. I didn't think Jeremy Corbyn was going to become leader of the Labour Party. So I think we've all made some predictions which have not turned out to come true. I mean, I absolutely recognise the issue that you're raising. And the bottom line is that there are far, far too many people in our country living in poverty and life is too hard. And we did not get everything right. So as I was saying, We didn't get everything right. We also had plenty of fights with the Conservatives. And we won some of those fights, and we lost some of those fights. And I am sorry that we did not win more of those fights in coalition because of the reasons that you are outlining. Now, we did stop the Conservatives cutting 12 billion more from welfare. We did stop them bringing in the two-child cap, which they did, of course, when they were on their own in 2015. But I absolutely think that we have lessons to learn from that. I've learned those lessons. And if you look at what we're setting out, we are identifying six billion extra to put into universal credit to make sure that you don't have that five-week wait, which is hurting so many people, so that we get rid of that two-child cap, that we get rid of the bedroom tax. So I get it. We got stuff wrong. And we, in the future, going forward, are determined to get it right. I absolutely accept the way that you feel, and I might not be able to convince you on this, and I recognise that. I do think that scrapping universal credit on its own isn't some kind of magic bullet. We need to have a system that provides support to people. I think the biggest problems with universal credit is the lack of money that's in it. not the principle that you try to have a simpler benefit system instead of there being multiple different payments that creates more complexity. So I think the principle of making it simpler is a good one, but it needs to have the money behind it, and that's what we're setting out to do. So you've got an unhappy Lever and an unhappy Remainer. OK. What I would say in terms of our policy, look, we are being very straightforward as a party that we want to stop Brexit. You might agree with us. You might disagree with us. I don't think you could accuse us of not being upfront about wanting to stop Brexit. We have been crystal clear about that from the very beginning. Not for one second do I think that that means that you or anybody like you is stupid. I think it means we disagree. When you were in coalition with the Conservatives, you failed to stop them doing a number of things you would now reverse. When the Conservatives were heavily in minority in government just recently, you failed to put your pride to one side, team up with Corbyn and the others, and bring down the government, and then bring forward a referendum. We don't have to have a selection. We could have avoided it.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_47: Quite a lot of it. Mr Johnson, whilst you claim the economy is doing really well, how can you justify, for example in my constituency where I work as a senior leader in a secondary school, how can you justify that 40% of the children in my community are living in poverty. And nationally, many, many more people are homeless. And many people who are working, perhaps on 40-hour contracts a week, are having to use food banks. So how can you justify this? In Stannington, towards Hillsworth, Sheffield.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_48: Hello, Prime Minister. I'd just like to ask you, how important is it for someone in your position of power to always tell the truth? I'm sorry. Is that what you meant? Erm... I'm speaking from a WASPs and you know who the WASPs are and you've made statements to help and take that forward. Are you still in that frame of mind that you're going to help us out? You're going to sort this pension issue?
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_49: Why should you be Prime Minister if you constantly keep on avoiding scrutiny? You've cancelled Parliament for five weeks, you ran away from Channel 4's debate, which is meant to be this Sunday, and you've banned the Daily Mirror from the Conservative bus. The woman here in the blue dress.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_50: You keep saying that leave is the will of the people, but I don't think I've ever heard you address specific issues bringing into question the validity of the referendum. For example, we know that the Leave campaign broke electoral spending rules and privacy laws. We know that leave was not defined and still isn't actually agreed upon today and I'd love for you to address these issues and also actually talk about why you think you've got a mandate to go through with Brexit because I don't think you can say that this general election can be your mandate when Brexit is mixed in with healthcare and climate change and education.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_51: Firstly, Mr Johnson, I'd like to say, whilst you're in South Yorkshire, I hope you managed to stay dry, unlike a lot of the residents in the past month. So... As a working single mother, I rely on benefits to get by and just about manage to sustain myself and my two children. Under the Conservative government, what guarantee do I have of staying on this side of the food banks? You're promising that, yet I'm yet to be moved on to universal credit. How does that work? I'm going to lose money, and yet you're promising more relief in other areas. It doesn't make sense.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_52: Hello Mr Johnson, my name is Dylan, I'm a student at the University of Manchester and I just wanted to ask you, why should a young person between 18 and 25 years old vote for the Conservative Party without using campaign rhetoric? Just straight answer please.
