It's been characterised by complete carelessness, by callousness, the way you treated people through the DWP, the way you treated the victims of Grenfell, the hostile environment. It's completely... You say you visited hospitals and you've spoken to public service workers. But I think you yourself know you haven't been welcome when you've been to speak to them. The way you've conducted yourself when you've been there has shown that you're afraid of the response you're going to get. So I think it's insulting to the people who've suffered under your government. Even if you say you've only been in power for 100 days, you've been part of the government for the last nine years. I think it's completely insulting for you to stand there and talk about all the investment you're going to bring, when it's your austerity policies that have brought...
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_55: Good evening, Prime Minister. In 2015, the Tory Government pledged to recruit and to train an additional 5,000 GPs by 2020. In 2019, there are actually fewer GPs. Why on earth should I now believe yet another pledge that you will recruit an additional 6,000 GPs over the course of the next Parliament? That is the plan. With respect, we do need a lot of extra cash in the NHS and it is through years of underinvestment that we are in the position we are in. I agree with that. It is as much about the conditions as it is about the money. Morale in the NHS is on its knees. The workload is going through the roof. We can't keep the GPs that are close to retirement from retiring because they just see this and they think, I want out. When we're trying to train new GPs to come into the service, they see all of this and they think, do I want to stay in England? We've invested hundreds of thousands of pounds in their training. And they look and think, do I want to commit the next 40 years of my career here? going to go to Australia, New Zealand, Canada, and they go there, they work 40 hours instead of 60-hour weeks, they get better pay, they don't have a political football, they don't get treated like a political football. We need to look at the conditions as well as the money that has been invested.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_56: I'm a physiotherapist, and the problem is across the board in the NHS. Part of the problem was getting rid of the NHS bursary, which stopped a lot of mature students coming back. I wouldn't have been able to train without that bursary, and you've shut a lot of people out with that, so would you reverse that decision to help get people back in?
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_57: I'm a junior doctor in A&E in the NHS. And over the past nine years since you've been in power, I've seen that waiting lists are getting longer. People are in trolleys for hours and hours in A&E. And it's not just inconvenient. People are dying. And I think you're lying to us. Every year, you promise more money for the NHS. But the reality is, in the NHS, people who work there and who use it know that that money isn't getting through. Why should we trust that you're going to get this $26 billion for the NHS when you've got years of cuts and people are dying? One more, the man in the pink shirt.
deb_2019_11_22.QUESTIONER_58: A big part of the election process is all about the trust that you want us to put into you. And even in this sort of campaign over the last week, we've seen Twitter handles that have been changed by the Conservatives. We've seen URLs that have been bought purporting to be from Labour, I think. And you're asking us to put our trust in big decisions, but yet even simple things, you can't seem to get that right. So why on earth should we trust you with anything else?

deb_2019_11_28.BERRY: Tonight, this is what you'll see. Every leader on this stage will agree, with me. They will all work hard to sound a little bit like the Green Party. Some might even sound convincing. It's progress. But as you watch, ask yourself two important questions. Do their plans match the scale of the emergency we face? And do you trust them to get this done without more Greens in Parliament making sure of it? If the answer is no, then vote Green. If not now, when? Thank you. And next Jeremy Corbyn. We have to do what the science demands, not what's politically convenient here. And the key thing about a 2050 target is it leaves us with a 50% chance of tipping over into runaway climate chaos. Now, would you cross a bridge with those odds of getting to the other side? It's incredibly important that we do what the IPCC warned us about, which is get involved in cutting emissions as quickly as possible. Because when we do that, the sooner a target we set, the early action helps in terms of getting people's lives better and equality, and especially global equality as well. If we leave it too late, we end up using technology to solve the problem, some of which we don't already have. So 2030 is the right target. We shouldn't just give up on it and weaken it like Labour have done when things get difficult. Our plans, our Green New Deal, are several times larger than any party who's here. They will achieve that target. We're not weakening. And they put faith in people to achieve it from the bottom up as well as from the top down. We absolutely need to look at every single layer of our society to do this. Our Green New Deal looks at our homes, our industry, our energy, and very importantly, our agriculture, our food, and how we deal with our land. At the moment, we focus far too much of our land on pasture. We don't have enough woods. We need to plant an awful lot more trees. We need to rewild our landscape. What about red leech? And it means some of the landscapes that are currently being used for pasture land for meat need to be changed to being forestry, need to be changed to being absorbing carbon as well. And that's really important. Just explain how that would work. Well, our taxes are all absolutely revenue neutral. Every single penny from the tax that we put on carbon and the tax we put on methane at the level of meat and dairy would go straight back into changing what's going on. We're not about making money. We're about changing how things happen. And so, yeah, we want the industry to change. We don't want any of those taxes to reach people's plates. We want the industry to reduce the amount of meat that they're producing, to produce the greener, healthier choices, and make those the cheapest choices for everybody. That's how the green values always work on these things. We're about changing things. We are one of the only parties here, I think, who want to cancel HS2. And it's the opportunity cost of investing in such a big project when we could be investing in the local sustainable transport that people are crying out for. People are trapped in their cars. They want better buses. They want better local transport. They want to be able to walk and cycle. And that's where we differ on HS2. We like Eurostar. We like high speed rail. But that project at this moment, we could spend that money so much better in other places. Yeah, just on the aviation point, I mean, we are very late, the rest of the parties here, to being against aviation expansion. Just over a year ago, more than half of Labour MPs voted for Heathrow expansion. The Lib Dems voted against, Plaid Cymru voted against, the SNP abstained, and you cannot abstain on climate change. It's good that we're there now saying we need to limit the amount of aviation. The question now is how we share out the flights that we can take more fairly. And that does mean targeting those 15% of people who take 70% of all flights. These are the frequent flyers who need... I will let you answer in a moment. You've got to go in, and you've got to do it street by street in a comprehensive way. Now, at the moment, more than half the emissions from the UK are from our buildings and our homes. And the reason for that is because each successive government we've had has neglected this issue of the homes that already exist, because it's not sexy going around and putting insulation in people's homes. Now, we've got a massive backlog, and we have to make up for it. And I hope you're fact checkers. We'll be checking the amount of money each of us are committing to this big gap that closing this loophole that's been left by everybody so far. Of our 100 billion a year that we're putting into our Green New Deal, 38 billion needs to go into homes because this is the biggest thing we've not cracked so far. We need a deeper retrofit than Labour are proposing. They keep talking about loft insulation and windows. It needs to be much deeper than that. And that's because, and your question again about how can we change everyone's boiler. We can change everyone's boiler to a heat pump, but only if we insulate first, because then the heat pump needs to be smaller and we don't have to change everyone's radiators. And I know how annoying it would be if we went round to everyone's house and said, we need to change your radiators as well as your boiler. So we do think about things more deeply. We are putting in the resources and none of the other parties have anything like our plans for home. It's very old thinking. Yeah, I mean the more diverse a grid is, the more you have different sources, the bigger the grid is, the more you have battery power in people's homes, the more you're investing in long-term storage, the easier it is to do without nuclear power. We cannot be investing in any more nuclear power stations and they take longer than ten years. Well, we're very, very behind on biodiversity and nature. I mean, credit to the movement, first of all, for getting this debate heard and for bringing climate change and new targets forward through so many motions, through councils, starting with Carla Denyer in Bristol, but now there's 389 climate and ecological emergency motions passing through councils. A lot of them were 2030 as their target. But it's the ecological stuff where we're behind. I have far too much trouble, I've spent years trying to argue against road schemes that are going to go straight through. sites of scientific interest. I've got HS2 going through ancient woodland. People have been very, very careless of our natural world. I have too much trouble getting councils off pesticides. We've got resistance from some farmers to using pesticides. We have to think more carefully about the actual world. I'm looking for your actual talk. I mean, Nicola Sturgeon. And this is something where we're very, very far behind. Sian Berry. I'm sorry, I've spent a lot of my life listening to politicians list green things they want to do and even list green policies and it isn't about lists. So many of these pledges end up on a dusty shelf after we stop shining a light on them. And a light's on them today, and they're saying an awful lot of things. But I just cannot sit back and watch this happen again. I mean, Greta Thunberg is only a teenager. She's already sick of hearing broken promises. Think how I feel at my age. Now, in my own life, I'm a private renter. I still do not have the ability to do my own home. So there's not a lot I can do, but I can carry on working to change the system. And it does need to be a comprehensive system change. The science demands it. The emergency demands it. And it isn't about listing things. It's about changing the way things work for good. We've got to be doing these things. Greene's holding you for a count on that in the Parliament. Thank you all very much indeed. Doing a good job.
deb_2019_11_28.CORBYN: This election is our last chance to tackle the climate and environment emergency. The climate movement has changed our politics forever and Labour has listened. Just 100 companies are responsible for 70% of emissions. And you at home must not pay the price of the transition to a net zero economy. Labour will kickstart a green industrial revolution, bringing new wealth to all parts of our country. Labour is on your side and on the side of the environment. It is time for real change. Well, on the government's current target, they won't reach it until 2099 anyway, because they're simply not living up to the demands made of the Paris Climate Change Accord or indeed of their own statements. I think we have to do everything we can to get to a 2030. zero emissions target and we do that by investment in green energy jobs, solar, wind and wave power and we do that by creating jobs in transforming our energy consumption by retrofitting homes to make them environmentally sustainable. We also have to work on the global stage, because at the moment, it's going to be well into the end of this century before there's anywhere near sustainability on a global stage. And by that time, temperatures will have gone up by three degrees centigrade. And so I want to host COP26 next year to go further than Paris so that we can be leaders on the world stage in setting an agenda of achieving this degree of sustainability by 2030. We're not weakening. We're not giving up on it. I'm determined to achieve it. That's why I've put that in our manifesto. we recognise there are huge problems on the way. Adam made the point about Wales being part of a net zero emissions country in the future. I absolutely agree with that. That means very big investment in the Swansea Bay Lagoon. It means big investments all around the UK on wind farms and the solar energy jobs. I'm determined to achieve it. We recognise that we just cannot go on like this, but also we have to recognise we are one country in the whole world, so it's what Britain does on the world stage and particularly particularly at the COP conference next year. It was Labour who put the climate emergency motion to Parliament on May 1st and led the way in the UK. Well, I think meat consumption is falling anyway and more and more people are recognising that you need a balanced diet. It doesn't mean everybody's got to become a vegetarian tomorrow and neither will they, but it does mean we have to look at the balance of our food sources as well. Therefore, more UK production of fruit and vegetables is perfectly possible. Looking at the issues of the damage we do to our environment by but flying vegetables in from all over the world when they could be produced here. There's a whole lot of issues surrounding that. But we also have to recognize that many people in this country are actually obese because of the poor quality food that they eat, or hungry because they can't afford to eat properly. So it is also about food pricing. And with our farms, yes, The farms are absolutely crucial to this. It is about extending biodiversity on our farms. It is about expanding headlands around fields. It is about rewilding part of it in order to strengthen our levels of biodiversity. If we go on damaging our soil at the rate we are, destroying the insect population at the rate we are, the levels of agricultural production will actually start to go down, not up. I voted against it at the time it came up, and we would set any expansion of any airport against tests on noise, on pollution, on sustainability, and on climate change targets. And if we are to reduce the amount of flying going on, then we do have to invest in extending and expanding our rail network, and particularly the rail network into Europe. I mean, I looked at the price of going to Berlin the other week. One airline was offering a £19 single ticket to go to Berlin. To go by train would have cost £240 and the rail infrastructure was totally invested in by the public. There has to be a much more efficient link-up. of the Eurostar rail service with the whole of the rest of Europe in order to reduce the number of short-haul flights that go to Europe and there has to be an expansion of our rail network all across the country if we are to get people to use trains rather than flights and also make the train fares cheaper so that it is actually a real offer, an option for everybody in this country. I think we do it better by offering a sensible alternative which is also, and I'll finish on this point, also about improving bus services all across the country. They're pretty good in London, they're pretty poor in most other parts of the country. There has to be full regulation and demand for provision of adequate bus services all over the country to cut down on air pollution from cars. Jo Swinson says she will pay for low-income homes. When you offer people either an interest-free loan to convert their house or you do it for them in the case of social housing or much in the social rented sector, then I think you've made a massive step forward. We can and do retrofit homes. We can generate electricity from solar wave and wind. We don't have to destroy our countryside with fracking. We don't have to go on in this way. But it does mean a determination and that's why our manifesto quite clearly sets out a very large Green Transformation Fund which will allow that investment in zero carbon homes will create a very large number of jobs in retrofitting those homes and actually give people cheaper electricity as well. Who will qualify for government help? those that are in social housing will get that done because it will be done by their landlords like the local authority or the Housing Association. Those on low incomes will have it done for them. Everyone else will be offered an interest-free loan to ensure that it's done. thousands of pounds on their homes. No, but they will get the benefits of it in lower bills for their gas or electricity or whatever form of heating they do. It is actually possible to construct homes that use very, very little energy. New homes should all be done like that and at the moment they're not. Offshore wind is a huge opportunity. 5% of the UK's electricity needs could be met from the west coast of Scotland alone. Add that to the major projects like the Mersey Barrage or the Swansea Bay Lagoon, you get a huge way down the line of getting totally sustainable electricity production in Britain. You have to plan for it, you have to invest in it, and you have to make sure... We have to make sure that the jobs are there to produce it, such as the BiFab works in Scotland are very much part of that industrial revolution for the future. Well, there has to be a baseline of production of electricity from some totally sustainable source. Otherwise, if you rely solely on wind and solar and wave, it is obviously... could now be like new nuclear power. No, what I'm saying is that when we move into government, if any of us moved into government, we're inheriting a mixed base. I want that to go to 80% or 90% of renewables over time, and that is the direction in which we want to go. Also, everything produced has to be measured against the strike price that could be obtained at the time of its production. Everyone has agreed that wind power has become much, much cheaper. There was a time when it was more expensive. It isn't so. Likewise, solar has also reduced in price. So actually, the future is in sustainable green energy spaces. What we want to do is bring the national grid into public ownership to ensure that you can feed into it as well as out of it in all places and invest in it for the future. Gas is an important part of the energy basis at the moment. Yes, it may well decline over time but it's there at the moment and if you want to change it and also introduce a greater degree of biogas then it obviously helps. If the grid itself is in public ownership which can ensure that distribution and that supply system. I'll leave you a copy of this afterwards. Well, where are you going to put them? Well, I'll leave you a copy of this afterwards, and this sets out in great detail the whole proposals we've made, both on tree planting... You didn't need to print it off, I could have looked online. ...as well as on biodiversity. Yes, that tree planting will take place in national parks, in existing forests, and on farmland all across the country. We have the lowest level of tree cover of almost any country in Europe. It hasn't grown very much over the past 50 years or so, and it should have done. So you will pay farmers to plant trees? We will do exactly that. We'll also be planting in new forests as well as the new national parks that we've proposed. We have to restore the levels of biodiversity in this country. Whole species of birds have completely disappeared. Sparrows, reduced. House martins, reduced. The cuckoo, almost unheard of in many parts of the country. That biodiversity eventually catches up with us and damages food production and farming itself. So it is about restoring peat bogs and salt marshes which have been destroyed. It is about upstream river management and that's why we want to bring the water industry into public ownership to work alongside the Environment Agency so that we do use upstream floodplains as a way of reducing flooding as well as, of course, flood protection. If we don't do this, then we're all damaged. Our plant life is damaged, our natural world is damaged and eventually we're all damaged as a result of that. To do everything I can to improve recycling systems in local authorities and elsewhere. There are, I think, 40 different local recycling schemes across the country, which means that we don't have a great record as a country on the world stage about recycling. Also, do everything I can to reduce the levels of excessive packaging. The waste of plastic with bubble wrap and all that goes with that. What about your life? Well, my life... It's beyond reproach, is it? Nobody's life is beyond reproach. I'm always the last one to turn the heating on. In fact, we turned it on for the first time last week in my house, and that was very low temperature anyway, and I turned it off pretty quickly after that. I'm quite miserable, actually, on this basis, because I don't like to see the waste of energy that goes with it. And also, it is about... Everybody can grow something. If they've got a garden or a window box or whatever, You can grow appropriate plants which do improve biodiversity. So you can plant lavender or something which attracts bees and so on. So I do all of that in my garden and on my allotment and in my life.
deb_2019_11_28.PRICE: As a new father, the climate crisis feels personal now. My son is only one, but one day soon he's going to ask me, you had power, so what did you do? What I want to do is to combine hope with urgency, to make a trinity of zeros, zero carbon, zero waste, zero poverty, our common goal by 2030. To turn Wales into the world's test bed, to make this moment not a tipping point of disaster, but a turning point of change. Well, yeah, and we need the power to do that, you know, so that we can actually use the huge advantages that we have in terms of the green jobs revolution, the fantastic marine energy resources that we have across all over our coastline. But of course, at the moment, we don't have the power to tap into that potential. We want to see that investment. I have to say to Jeremy, of course, it was great to see the Labour motion at your conference coming out for 2030. That's absolutely the right target. But unfortunately, the manifesto says you want to get the majority of emissions by 2030. A couple of countries that are already in the world have gone for 2030. We need to show that same level of ambition. We have the technology now to reach carbon neutrality by 2030. Unfortunately, what's lacking is the political will. That's the monuments to the lack of political leadership that we have either side of us here. We've got to change that. And the people that aren't represented here as well are the future generations. We've got to be their voice and get to 2030 carbon neutrality. No, it's an absolute myth actually that the IPCC report was somehow advocating moving away from meat and milk, the kind of produce that we produce in a temperate, wet, largely wet climate like in Wales, actually animals fed on grassland make a positive contribution because grass is one of the most important stores of carbon that we have. If you lose that, if you move away from that, actually you move to scrub etc, then you actually actually lead to higher emissions as a result of that. The real message on food, right, is that we, all of us, as individuals, public authorities, schools, NHS, etc., we need to be buying more locally. We need to create local food systems producing the whole range, both of plant protein and animal protein, and stop importing it to the level we do. And farmers are an important part of the climate change that we want to create because they are custodians of the environment and we should be involving them as allies rather than decrying them as the enemies of... As Sian rightly says, we would cancel the third runway at Heathrow. We would also cancel HS2. It is a vanity project. It is kind of obscene. The suggestion is that England is going to get a high-speed north-south railway we in Wales haven't got any kind of railway link north to south within our country. We want to rectify that, bring back the Aberystwyth to Carmarthen railway link, take it up all the way to Caernarfon and Ynys Môn and see the proper investment in our transport infrastructure within Wales. We only get 1% of the transport infrastructure investment in the UK, we've got 5% of the population and it's been like that for decades. I have to say, Jeremy, under Labour governments as well. Adam Price. There are two great opportunities for us, and let's be positive about this. So one is powering down, we were talking about that, so that's about energy efficiency in homes, the five billion that we in Wales would want to invest in that, and also transport, decarbonising our transport system. The other one then is powering up. powering up the great renewable revolution. And, you know, after Scotland, I think Wales has got incredible renewable energy resources. And the great thing is, you know, we were the cradle of the last industrial revolution. We could be there at the start of this new industrial revolution as well, if we tap into that potential. And the great thing is, as the entire world is looking at decarbonisation, if we get there earlier, if we get to 2030, if we set that kind of target, of course, there are advantages then. We can reap the benefits of that, because if we solve the world's problems first, then we actually have created a new global industry which is economically useful for us, as well as helping us meet our environmental responsibilities earlier than anyone else. And possibly nuclear. Adam Price. It is a genuine question. I can understand and I support actually taking the energy distribution companies into public ownership, though we'd like to see that within Wales with a national energy company. But why are you nationalising the gas distribution network when you're going to decarbonise? You're going to spend billions of pounds on an energy form that you say you want to get Well, yeah, I think farmers are very, very keenly engaged with this question. And indeed, of course, actually, there's a lot of evidence of the positive benefit of farming because, look, the ecology of much of Wales has grown up with farming. And indeed, that's why the RSPB, for example, has grazing animals on its land. in the next five years? I think the nature crisis is as critically important as the climate change crisis. What we need both nationally and internationally is legislation to tackle the extinction crisis, placing a legal obligation on this and every future government to to act for the recovery of nature. The two crises, which are interconnected, they have different drivers, deforestation internationally, pollution, and hunting in some areas as well, but they need to be now treated with the same level of seriousness, because obviously, as was said in the film, we are facing the mass extinction of a million species. It is what you are going to do about it. I think it's also important to recognise the biodiversity crisis in our oceans as well and off our own coasts. We would want to ban fossil fuel licences for exploration, for instance in Cardigan Bay, and also see the UK signing the UN Global Ocean Treaty, which seeks to create a network of ocean sanctuaries across the whole of the Earth, which will be worth 30% of our oceans. Adam Price. I'm going to start cycling to the Senedd every day. I'm going to shift to an electric vehicle in the new year. And also, I convinced my partner as well, we're going to give a go with reusable nappies as well to make our own small but important contribution. Are you going to wash them? Yes, yes, yeah. I think that's the point. No, I meant you. Yeah, absolutely. I do change, that means no, but the point is, you know, for him and his generation, I want to do this.
deb_2019_11_28.STURGEON: Tackling the global climate emergency is a moral obligation and Scotland is already leading the way. We have almost halved our emissions and now set a new legal target to reach net zero of all emissions by 2045, five years ahead of the rest of the UK. That goes further than the Paris Agreement and beyond what international experts say is needed globally. Our focus now is on taking the action to secure this transition in a fair way, and in partnership with other countries in the EU and beyond. It is a big challenge, but one that we all must meet, and the SNP is determined to do so. It is what the Committee on Climate Change said is at the outer limits right now. That is what we should be aiming for at the very latest. What Scotland has done is pile on the pressure to meet higher targets earlier. We are going beyond what the Committee on Climate Change has said for 2030, a 75% reduction in all emissions by 2030, carbon neutrality by 2040. Now, the IPCC, the international expert, says that the world needs to reach carbon neutrality by 2050 and half emissions, carbon emissions, by 2030. So we are going beyond that. And unlike the other leaders here, I'm actually in government, leading a government that is leading by example. Our targets are the toughest anywhere in the world. They include aviation, shipping and they don't rely on international credits and that's the kind of ambition we need to see all countries take. I don't think it is helping the planet that much for us all to be squabbling with each other here. This is something we should actually be trying to find unity and common purpose on. Now, I'm proud of the fact that Scotland is leading the way. The UN recognises us as a world leader. COP will be in Glasgow next year. And we should be agreeing that Scotland, the UK, should be leading the world. And then we should be putting pressure on other countries. Because right now, the world is not on track to limit temperature rises to 1.5 degrees. We've heard your ambitions. And let's make sure that we focus on the ambitions that we need to I think we all have to think about the balanced, healthy diets that we eat. We also have to think much more carefully about where our food is sourced from. Absolutely, the practices that are used in agriculture and farming have to change, have to become much more energy efficient and environmentally friendly. But of course, again, talking about Scotland, we have the climate and the environment to actually support sustainable, high-quality produce, and we should be supporting our farmers to do that. A lot has already been done just now, but we need to do more of that. Land use, I absolutely agree about land use. I've been listening to all of the parties here today talk about the number of trees they want to plant. Last year in Scotland, 22 million trees were planted, 84% of the UK total. We've got to make sure that the food that we are producing is produced in a way that is more environmentally friendly. There's a lot of work ongoing to do that right now. But for environmental reasons and for health reasons, yes, of course, we've got to be encouraging people to eat balanced diets and to think about getting their food much more locally sourced. That's good for the economy as well as being good for people's health and good for the environment. We don't want Scotland to be left behind in these things, but we want to invest more in public transport. In answer to your question earlier on, do people have to fly less? Yes, I think we do, where we can. Something like half of all international flights are taken by 10% of the population. So we have to invest more. We are investing half a billion pounds to improve bus infrastructure. We need to get the rail services. better. Where flights are necessary, in a country like Scotland, sometimes they are, we have set a target to decarbonise all flights within Scotland by 2040. We have already one of the most comprehensive electric vehicle charging networks anywhere in Europe with a target of phasing out... Are you beseeching people to fly less or are you going to tax them to make sure they fly less? The UK already has one of the highest levels of air passenger duty. My party had a long-standing commitment to cut that, and we decided we couldn't do that, in all conscience, because of the climate emergency. So, you know, I think we need to make sure the alternatives are there and encourage people. But on all of this discussion about individual behaviour, important though it is, somebody made a point about the small number of companies responsible for the majority of emissions. We've got to make sure that industry and government are leading by example. One of the points I wanted to make, going back to the discussion about targets, is the UK government's target right now, the 2050 target, actually excludes aviation emissions from it. Scotland's target includes aviation emissions. And I suspect if the UK was to include emissions from aviation, Heathrow expansion would not align with that. So we've got to make sure that these targets, if they are to be meaningful, are actually including all of the emissions and the biggest emitters along the way. You have to include aviation. Scotland already produces 75% of its electricity from renewable sources. We absolutely now must prioritise transport, which we have spoken about, and heat. We already have a home energy efficiency programme. We are already investing hundreds of millions of pounds in trying to help people insulate their homes and heat their homes better. There is also a role for regulation here. We have given a commitment already to have a new home building standards in place. By 2024, all new buildings will have to have non-fossil fuel boilers in them. There is a big programme of work here. I have just spoken about the home energy efficiency programme which provides financial support, we are going to update that. But people have to pay some money themselves. Absolutely, this is a collective effort but government has a big part to play here. One other point I was going to make, which Scotland cannot do on our own, so we need to have cooperation from whoever is in the UK government, is around decarbonisation of the gas grid. There is not enough priority being given to that by the current UK government and I hope that changes. We are in the transition away from fossil fuels and that transition has to accelerate. Scotland is one of the most renewable energy rich countries in the world. If we were to stop tomorrow, in terms of oil production. We would make ourselves more reliant on imports. The carbon intensity would increase. So that transition has got to be done in a way that cuts our emissions. But the one other thing that's not been mentioned here right now, which is where we've taken action and other countries haven't, is about the justice of the transition. not doing this in a way that leaves people behind, that decimates people's jobs. We have a just transition commission that's looking at how we do this transition fairly and justly. And that's really important as well. I grew up in the west of Scotland in the 1980s. I saw the effects of deindustrialisation and leaving people behind. The legacy of that is still there in too many of our communities and we can't make that mistake again. offshore wind is now half the price of nuclear. I don't know why Labour and the Tories are still supporting new nuclear power. We also need to get more support for onshore wind, which the Tories disgracefully have taken away. These technologies are here now and we have to do much more to support them. and what you are going to do to protect them? Hold on a second, it is not in our manifesto for a Westminster election because it is a responsibility of my government right now. We don't need a Westminster election, we are doing it. There are people outside Scotland as well. I think people would expect me to take responsibility for what I can do directly. We have increased funding for peatland restoration and it is important that we continue to do that. We want to restore by 2030 40% of our degraded peatlands. In terms of tree planting, Scotland is absolutely leading the way. Last year, 22 million trees, more than 11,000 hectares That was 84% of the total number of trees planted across the UK. We need to increase that number. And yes, we have to work with our farmers. I agree with Adam. We should see farmers as allies in this, not as enemies. But peatlands are being burnt still, aren't they? sometimes. We don't deliberately burn peatlands, we are in the business of restoring peatlands. Again, I am not standing here, I agree with others, I think the ecological crisis has to be given the same priority as the climate crisis, they are obviously connected and we have all got more to do here, but I do think that others could look to some of the work we are doing in Scotland here and to some extent learn from it as we continue to seek to go further. I'm already trying to do that. So in terms of how I keep my own home, my energy provider is one that gets all its energy from renewable sources, smart meter installed, trying to fly less myself. Sometimes it's inescapable in the job that I do, but trying to use the train much more when I'm coming to London, as I did today, for example, being much more conscious about what I eat and where I source the food that I eat from. So a whole host of things that I think it's really important that all of us lead by example. If we are asking other people to do these things, it is simply not right that we don't do these things ourselves. We have all got to stop putting more plastics into our oceans and that is why I think banning things like cotton buds, as we have already done, the deposit return scheme that we are introducing in Scotland, increasing the charge for single-use coffee cups, plastic bags, all of these things are important and we have got to keep doing all of them. You know, I've not been talking here tonight about things that I'm simply promising. I'm the only one here in government. The things I've been talking about are the things we're doing. OK. We've got to do more. We've got to leave it there.
deb_2019_11_28.SWINSON: The climate crisis is even more important than Brexit. But Brexit is a climate crime. It is morally wrong to leave our seat at the table and give up our influence to create the change we need right across the world. We have two paths ahead of us. one where our children inherit a poisoned planet, or one where our children breathe clean air, where going green is the easier, cheaper option, where we protect our planet for future generations. Well, we think that it is absolutely possible to get there by 2045, but we should do this as quickly as we can. So you're less ambitious? No, because we should do it as quickly as we can and bring that date forward. But the important thing is not only when we get to net zero, it's how quickly we cut emissions right now, how much carbon we take out of the atmosphere right now. That is what is going to give us the best chance of saving our planet. But can I inject a note of optimism? I noticed in your film at the beginning, the fate of that koala at the end of it perhaps was a bit of a somber note. And often in these climate discussions, that can be how it feels. It can feel impossible. When I was growing up in the 1980s, the big challenge that we were thinking about was the hole in the ozone layer and how we were going to deal with that and all of the negative impacts that had. What gives me hope is that international action was taken, governments got together and agreed. CFCs were cut by 98% and the hole in the ozone layer is now the smallest it has been for 30 years and looks like it will repair. That shows me that we can solve this problem. I totally agree that the role that we play internationally is so important. But surely, you can see, that's why it's so important that we keep our seat at the European Union table. Because we have led the way in Europe on this. Europe has led the world. That's how we got the ambitious Paris Accords through. And turning our back on the European Union is turning our back on our best way of fighting the climate emergency. Yeah. I mean, the international flights, obviously, the biggest amount of carbon released in terms of aviation, and we've said we would start with an international frequent flyer tax for those taking more than three return long-haul flights a year. How much? What it would mean is it would be more expensive for those taking more than three return flights a year. What would you pay? We've got the whole costing set, obviously it's on a scale. So people who are flying very, very frequently, the people that are racking up those long haul flights will pay a lot more. I haven't flown internationally on holiday for a very long time, but I do fly domestically and that is because I represent a constituency of 400 miles from London. And at the moment, we do not have good enough transport infrastructure. We've got Victorian railway infrastructure, and we're in the 21st century. That's why we need the investment in things like High Speed 2 to bring our domestic travel in the UK up to the standards it needs. Hardly anyone would think of flying between London and Paris or London and Brussels these days since the Eurostar came in. That's the kind of 21st century infrastructure that we need. Well, I think the first thing that we can do, which is so important, is actually about retrofitting homes to insulate them properly. And in terms of how we can encourage people to do that, well, that's actually going to be something which is good for people because it cuts energy bills, it helps people save money. And for low-income households, that should be something which we do, that we make sure that that is done by the government so that those homes, within the next five years, are insulated to help cut fuel poverty, cut energy bills. But the other thing we absolutely need to be doing and it's I mean it's madness and it's you know the Conservatives that have dropped this. We know how to build homes that are zero carbon homes. A couple of weeks ago I was in Sheffield eco construction site where they're building these homes. It was you know November. The homes weren't completed yet. I was in the home and even then it was warm because it was properly built in a way that retained heat. So we know how to do this. They've been doing it in Scandinavia for years. We need to make sure that now we have a target for all new build homes to be zero carbon and to have those high standards. And the Conservatives, who can't even be bothered to turn up tonight, scrapped those plans. Absolutely, Adam. I wholeheartedly agree. This is an opportunity for us as a country to take a real lead. And if you look at what the great success story has been in the last few years, if we were having this debate six or seven years ago, people were saying renewables, they're really expensive and they won't be so reliable. there was no offshore wind. We are now in the UK a leader, world leader, in offshore wind. It is now the cheapest way to generate electricity. Because of the support for that industry that was put in by the Liberal Democrats when we were running the climate change department, that has created a great British industry. It's cutting energy bills. That's a great example of what we need to So, I mean, I think about my childhood and those types of birds that you would see in the garden. I think about bringing my children up and I want them to be able to see that kind of wildlife. Hedgehogs, I remember having a little hedgehog box in the garden and putting out food for hedgehogs. They've halved the hedgehog population in the last 20 years. I don't think my five-year-old has actually seen a hedgehog. We need to protect our amazing wildlife and biodiversity. So what do we need to do? strict legal targets in a Nature Act where we have very clear legally binding targets. We need to be able to enforce them. We'd have a new Office of Environmental Protection and we need to invest. So we do need to plant those trees. We do need that to be in national parks. We also need to look at how we do that within our urban spaces and greening our cities as well, which has an obvious benefit in terms of reducing carbon dioxide by taking that out of the atmosphere and public health benefits, making those places greener and more pleasant for people to be in. And that's, I think, one of the key things about this. This is something which makes people's lives better. Of course it's good for the environment, it's good for us in the longer term, but so many of the measures that we can take will make the places we live more pleasant places to be. Well, I try to do all of these small things as well, whether it's taking your keep cup, making sure you're doing the recycling. My campaign bus for this election is the first electric political campaign bus. We do. That's the point of this. And I will say that I think the most important thing that politicians can do is to put in place the policy frameworks, whether it's in terms of getting rid of single-use plastics much sooner, the regulations that are going to make it easier, because what we want to do is to make it easier and cheaper for everybody to do the right thing so that the obvious decision to make is the one that is good for our climate.
deb_2019_11_28.QUESTIONER_0: Let's start with short opening statements from the leaders in alphabetical order, starting with the co-leader of the Green Party, Sian Berry. Next, for Applied Cymru, its leader Adam Price. Thank you. And now the leader of the SNP, Nicola Sturgeon. And now, the leader of the Liberal Democrats, Jo Swinson. Thank you all. So let's begin. Our format is simple. Each leader will get the chance to answer at least one question first, and then we'll freely debate it between everyone. There's a lot to get through, so please keep your answers brief. First, the Conservatives are committed to reaching the emissions target of net zero by 2050. That means the amount of greenhouse gases we are putting into the atmosphere will be balanced by the amount we are taking out. So is 2050 the right target? Jeremy Corbyn. So Labour's aiming at 2030. Nicola Sturgeon, I mean, you're making a big deal of going for 2045. climate change said Scotland would be doing anyway? Adam Price, it's the opposite problem for you. If Scotland has the resources to get there quicker, the Commission on Climate Change says Wales has less and yet you want to go quicker. So you're another 2030. Joe Swinson, where are you? how credible is 2030, which is also your pledge, when the experts on the Committee on Climate Change just say that's too fast? We have heard your ambitions. They range from 2030 to 2045 and 2050 in the case of the Conservatives. But how are you all going to get there? What will it mean for how we live our lives? Let's start with food. It makes up about a fifth of the UK's greenhouse gas emissions. Sean Berry, how do we need to change that? And you want to tax dairy and meat, don't you? Nicola Sturgeon, do we need to go plant-based? I want to talk about the food on people's plates. Do you need to show leadership and say to people, you have got to eat less Aberdeen Angus? And say to your farmers, you're going to have to sell this. Adam Price, are you prepared to tell your sheep and cattle farmers that people are going to have to eat less meat? No. I think you're the only one who's gone plant-based, aren't you? I mean, do the rest of us as well? What's that, sir? Going plant-based in terms of our diet. You've gone there already, basically, haven't you? Do the rest of us have to follow you? Let's talk about another aspect of our lives and how it might have to change. If that one doesn't have to change that radically, it sounds like it. I mean, Jo Swinson, how much do we have to change the amount we fly? You want to tax us according to how we fly. Sian Berry, what would the impact of HS2 be on wildlife? You want to extend HS2 right up to Scotland, don't you? Indeed, Jeremy Corbyn, Heathrow Airport is the single biggest emitter of greenhouse gases. Are you in favour of giving it that third runway? So cheap flights for the mayor have got to end, haven't they? Let's move on from transport. Time is short, we want to get through quite a lot on this. A third of our emissions come from our homes. The Conservatives say they will invest over £9 billion on energy efficiency. You all talk big on cutting that with insulation and zero carbon homes, but how are you actually going to make people change their heating systems? Jeremy Corbyn, how are you going to make everybody change to get rid of their gas boilers and insulate their homes unless you pay for it? Is any of this realistic? I mean if you look at smart meters, which are still only in a few million homes after years, That's just a little box. How on earth are you going to get people to change everything about their home heating? Nicola Sturgeon, this of course takes us to fossil fuels. Oil and gas still provides more than 75% of UK total primary energy, that's houses and cars. What are you going to do in the next five years to get that down? when they will have to do that. What about the oil industry? The Greens say you have to start decommissioning oil rigs. Could you answer Nicola Sturgeon's question about nuclear? I want to move on now to another topic that many of you have already touched on, which is something Britain is surprisingly bad at. We are going to miss 14 out of 19 of our biodiversity targets. One in seven species in the UK are threatened with extinction and we are one of the most nature depleted countries on earth. Adam Price, are you prepared to say to some of your farmers in Wales that they may have to change their farms to have more woodland? But where is your manifesto plan for this? Now the Conservatives, for example, have got a £640 million Nature for Climate Change fund. Sian Berry, what would you do? We've got hardly anywhere. You might have expected the SNP in its manifesto to have something about peatlands. Jeremy Corbyn, you announced 2 billion trees by 2040. Today, that's 100 million trees a year. How are you going to... plant them, where are you going to put them? OK. Huge numbers of people, especially children, are personally alarmed by the climate emergency and genuinely scared for their futures. I want to ask you all now, what is your personal climate change resolution to cut your carbon footprint? Nicola Sturgeon. Your personal resolution, But we all need to do a lot more, don't we? Jeremy Corbyn, your personal resolution for the next generation? What are you going to change about your life? Thank you all very much indeed. After the break, a final thought from three of the country's leading climate academics. What do they make of that debate and our politicians' pledges? Welcome back. Well, making sense of what's been said over the past 50 minutes are three of the country's leading climate scientists. Emily Shuckborough, who heads the University of Cambridge's new initiative, Cambridge Zero, bringing together more than 1,000 academics to help find a path to a zero-carbon future. Professor Rebecca Willis, an academic from Lancaster University's Environment Centre. And Dr Natalie Seddon, who runs the Nature-Based Solutions Initiative at Oxford University. Let's begin with you, Emily. What did you make of it? Did they make any bloopers? I mean, do you feel a taste of it tonight? It gives you any sense that our main politicians have grasped this to the extent they need to? Rebecca, we did talk a lot about land use, farming, diversity. Do they get it? Natalie, thank you. So, Rebecca, talk about the measures they want to take to take the carbon out of the economy. Thank you very much indeed. Well, thank you to all our party leaders who came tonight.
deb_2019_11_28.QUESTIONER_1: No, but I mean, we're sitting here in a studio with melting ice caps. And in the real world, the ice caps are also melting. My colleagues are down in Antarctica at the moment looking at the state of the West Antarctic ice sheet. And to me, that really brings home the scale of the challenge that we face. The really important question to me is what actions are going to be made over the next five years? And do they add up? to the scale of change that's required to ensure that we're on the right trajectory to keep temperatures well below 1.5 degrees. Well, one of the things that I think is so incredibly important that's going to be happening within the next 12 months, and it was mentioned, we are going to be holding in Glasgow, in the UK, the next really big climate conference. That is our opportunity on the world stage to take a leadership position. It is so critical that we do that. We have to inspire the change to turn a nightmare vision of the future into a mission to shape a world that we would be proud of leaving to our children and our grandchildren.
deb_2019_11_28.QUESTIONER_2: I think that's Natalie's question. Yeah, well, I mean, firstly, credit for turning up and competing over climate policies. And that is fantastic. Hasn't happened before. It's where we need to be. I would say, I mean, our research shows that people are willing to play their bit, but politicians need to be consistent. And one thing I've heard tonight is that there isn't that consistency. So you can't tell people how to live their lives if you as a party are supporting really high carbon projects. There's a coal mine being consented down the road from me in Cumbria. North Sea oil and gas extraction is continuing. It's not good enough to say we can't close it down tomorrow. We can't, but we can close it down over a phased process over the next five to 10 years. So all I'd say now is that the challenge I want to set is that you should be asking the carbon question of every single policy. Have that consistency and that way you'll take people with you and you'll cut the carbon.
deb_2019_11_28.QUESTIONER_3: Yeah, that's my question, I think. I'm a biodiversity expert here. I think nature is our ally. It's our major ally in the fight against climate change and other global challenges. Nature is our life support system. And I think you get it. And so the fact that you're all pledging to raise ambition for nature and biodiversity is welcome, but it's more than welcome. It's absolutely essential. But you spend a lot of time talking about tree planting, and this worries me. So three major points on tree planting. One, this is not an excuse to do business as usual with our economies. We need to decarbonize our economies, we need to keep fossil fuels in the ground, and we need to look after nature. It's not an either or. The other thing is that other ecosystems are important. Tree planting, monoculture, big commercial plantations are not going to save us. We need to restore our peatlands, our natural ecosystems. We need to protect what we've got, restore what we've lost, and we need to do careful tree planting, the right species in the right place.

deb_2019_11_29.SWINSON: Before I start, I want to pay tribute to the bravery of our emergency services, and my thoughts are with everybody affected by this afternoon's horrific terrorist attack. My dad taught me always to ask questions, to challenge the way things are and work to change them for the better. And I believe things can be better. That we can remain in the European Union and have more money to spend on improving your life right now and more to invest in our children's future. Imagine a country where you are valued for who you are, no matter what God you pray to or none, how much money you have, the colour of your skin or who you love. where we nurture the bonds in our family of nations, our United Kingdom, where we work with our closest friends to save our planet, where we look after the most vulnerable and our children have the best start in life. If you share my vision for our country, if you want to stop Brexit and build a brighter future, vote Liberal Democrat. Well, I think everybody feels just heartbroken at what we've witnessed today on London Bridge and huge, huge courage shown by those members of the public and the emergency services that should be commended. I think the other thing that I'm struck by when we face these types of challenges as a country, and we've seen these in London, we saw them a few years ago in Manchester, is the way in which the community pulls together is inspiring, and that gives me real hope about how we come together as a country. In terms of security we do need to have more police and we've set out plans to increase police numbers by 20,000. I think it's also important that we look at what the police do and that community policing and one of the threats that many people worry about in their everyday security is how safe they are on the streets and in particular how safe their children are on the streets. Far too many parents have had that horrendous call of a young person being stabbed, being another victim of knife crime, and something which in Scotland has worked really, really well. It started in Glasgow 15 years ago, the Violence Reduction Unit, the police working with public services, with the NHS, with social care, social work, to provide a holistic approach to tackling violence, a public health approach, It has seen the stabbing rate reduced by two thirds nearly in Glasgow and that is something that we should replicate elsewhere. What neither Labour nor the Conservatives are telling you is that their plans don't add up because they both want to leave the European Union and they both know, the government's analysis says and all the respected economic analysis says, that if we leave the European Union We will be poorer. There will be less money. So we won't be able to have these wish lists. And I have to say Peter you ask such a good question. You put your finger right on it. How can you guarantee it. Because both of them are trying to say that you can have this in the case of labor with somebody else paying for it and the case of the Conservatives saying that taxes won't have to go up. Do you know, if you're listening to that and you think in your heart of hearts you just know that's not true, then you need to look for parties that are going to set out where they will get the money from and we're saying very honestly we think the NHS needs more money and we're going to raise income tax by a penny in the pound in order to do so. You have to say where you're going to get the money from. Well, it obviously depends on what their earnings are. Well, I mean, people can make their own calculations based on what income tax they pay. I suppose what I would say, Melissa, is having looked at the last three and a half years, is there anyone seriously who thinks this is going well for our country? It is a mess. And it's a mess because back in 2016, they weren't straightforward about what it would mean. And, you know, we know that remaining in the EU is going to be better for our public services, it's going to be better for our NHS, for our economy, for jobs, for our environment, for workers' rights. And I think it's right that people have the chance to choose a different path now that all of that is so much clearer. And, you know, we're in an election. Liberal Democrats are absolutely standing to stop Brexit. We've led the campaign for a people's vote and we want to stop Brexit. And the final thing I would just say is, you know, you said that we're so far along. I'm sorry... I'm sorry to have to say this, but we are so not far along on this Brexit journey. You know, the idea that Brexit is going to be done, we're like in episode one of a ten-season box set, and if you don't like what you've seen up till now, you don't have to watch the rest. Let's just be really clear about our position. As Liberal Democrats, we want to stop Brexit. I don't think that's news to people. And so we said, if we win a majority Liberal Democrat government, we would revoke Article 50, going from 20 MPs to 326. I think it would be a pretty seismic electoral shift. And therefore, there would be that democratic mandate. Well, I would love to do that job. But if it does happen, then the democratic mandate is there. Yes, I would be prepared to. Obviously, I would assess the situation. Well, I think on the issue of the European Union, I'm a passionate Remainer and lots of people have tried to say that I shouldn't be standing up for what I believe in and tried to say that, you know, it's wrong to argue for a people's vote. We've had the discussion tonight, but I've absolutely stuck to my guns and I will continue to do so to stand up for my principles and you can see that in action. Well, earlier this year, I went along to the Extinction Rebellion protests, and I went along with my five-year-old son, and we sat and we talked, and most polite protest I've ever been to, I have to say, and we talked to different people there, and seeing that crisis through the eyes of a child with that future focus, I have to say, it is just so urgent, and your students are quite right. And this is an issue right now. Tonight, there are families who are living in makeshift accommodation, living out of plastic bags with whatever of their possessions remain, because they have experienced those horrendous floods in Yorkshire and Derbyshire. So this is an issue right now. So some of the things we can do to rapidly cut carbon dioxide, the things we know best are insulating our homes, a rapid program of home insulation. And the new homes that we build should all be zero carbon. We know how to do this. And making sure we take the fossil fuels out of our electricity generation. renewables, we should be so much more ambitious. The cost has already come down significantly. Offshore wind is the cheapest electricity that you can now create, thanks to Liberal Democrats supporting that technology. Absolutely. So people who take more than three return international flights a year, those frequent flyers would pay more under our plans. And it is those people who take the many, many, many flights that we need to target with those. Richard Tyson. If you can cut waiting lists, I think that's a good thing to get more people treated more quickly. But I tell you what, I mean, basically what Rishi is saying about the NHS, you just cannot deliver it. The best way to protect the NHS from us begging the US for a trade deal is by not having to beg the US for a trade deal in the first place by remaining in the EU. And we have tens of thousands of doctors and nurses working in our hospitals as GPs, as carers, And we are already down 5,000 nurses from other EU countries in the last two years. What Rishi wants to do is then charge nurses from other EU countries for the privilege of coming to work in our NHS to use it. He wants to charge them 600 quid a year, a tax on nurses. How is that going to help our NHS get those vital nurses and doctors that we need to have to make sure that we look after people in our country? I mean, I want to remain in the European Union and protect freedom of movement. But I also want us to change the debate that we have about immigration in this country. Because so often it's talked about in sort of hushed tones and some kind of negative and all of the dog whistles that we've seen. And I think we should proudly say, Immigration is a good thing. In fact, immigration is a brilliant thing, that we have people from other countries around the world who want to come to our country and work in our NHS and our schools, work in our farms and our restaurants and bars, be part of our society. You know, we are a nation of immigrants. Whether it was the founder of Marks and Spencers or Rita Ora or Mo Farah, we should not be sort of in some kind of Dutch auction about how harsh we can be in immigration. We should be celebrating immigration. Well I could congratulate him on getting his wish which was of course for him to have Boris Johnson and Nigel Farage come together but of course that's not going to be any good for our country so I would like to tell him that the British public still have the chance to stop him getting what he wants. As you sit on your sofa with your cup of tea, watching this election unfold, you might be thinking, surely we deserve better than this depressing duo of Boris Johnson and Jeremy Corbyn. Boris Johnson, caught lying again and again. He thinks the rules don't apply to him. And frankly, he only cares about himself. He doesn't deserve to win a majority and the Liberal Democrats can stop him. You have the power to stop Brexit and create a brighter future. Vote Liberal Democrat.
deb_2019_11_29.STURGEON: My thoughts, too, are with those attacked on London Bridge today and with all those who responded. This election really matters. At stake is not just Brexit, but what kind of society we want to be. The SNP wants Scotland to be an independent, outward-looking, internationalist country, and we want the people of Scotland to have the right to make that choice. But we also want to play our part in locking Boris Johnson out of Downing Street. If he wins the majority, then the consequences will be dire. Brexit will not, in any sense, be done. Trade talks haven't even started yet. These issues will cause chaos for years to come. In his rush for a trade deal with Donald Trump, Johnson simply can't be trusted not to put our NHS on the table. He also threatens to rip up rules on food standards, environmental protections and workers' rights. To build a better future, we need to stop all of that. And in Scotland, that means voting SNP. Well, firstly, I want to say again that my thoughts are with everybody affected on London Bridge today with those who sustained injuries and I think in particular the two families this evening who are facing up to the loss of a loved one. We witnessed today some exceptional acts of bravery on the part of members of the public and I, like I'm sure all of us are, are full of admiration. It's how all of us hope we'd respond in a situation like that but in truth none of us know until we're confronted by it and hopefully we will not be in that position. In terms of what we do, we have to make sure that we support our security services, that we keep access to the best intelligence, which is one of many reasons that I think leaving the European Union is a mistake. And although the gentleman is right, no amount of police officers can prevent every terrorist attack. the numbers of police officers is important in ensuring the speed of response we saw from the emergency services today. The SNP government in Scotland has over the past decade increased and maintained police numbers. I think it is regrettable that hasn't happened elsewhere in the UK and I hope after this election we will see police officers get the support that they so richly deserve. I was pointing out that the IFS said that neither Labour or the Tories' spending plans We are credible. Let me be candid, my party is not going to be running the Treasury after this election, but we might hold the balance of power and therefore we will want to see investment in the right priorities, investment in the National Health Service, an end to the welfare cuts that have caused misery. more investment in the transition to a net zero economy, pension justice, particularly for WASPI women. And how will we help to ensure that? We'll bring to bear the experience we have in government. In Scotland, the SNP has reformed the income tax system, making it more progressive. Those at the top pay a bit more, those at the bottom pay a bit less. And we'll also argue that we shouldn't be spending money on the wrong priorities. Why should we be investing £200 billion in new Trident nuclear weapons, for example? Jo Swinson then Richard Duff. Nicola Sturgeon. I was just chuckling at Richard saying that he doesn't like manifestos. I think the Brexit Party prefer putting their spending commitments on the side of a bus. He used to say we've got £350 million a week extra for the NHS. It's people like him that the public don't trust. I'm not keen on giving Theresa May personally very much, but that's another matter altogether. The SNP did some work some months back, where we set out five different options to compensate the WASPI women. It went from restoring the pension age to 60 to slowing down the pace of change. We certainly would want to talk to other parties to make sure that that justice is delivered, because this is the question. You know, these big parties, I think we've seen tonight a glimpse of the dreadful choice between these two main parties that people have. But they can always find things for the wrong priorities. Let's make a change and find things for the right priorities in compensating WASP women. I think it's a top one. Nicola Sturgeon... Caroline's right. that point. But can I say, I totally get the frustration. I get that people are, to use a Scottish word, scunnered by this whole thing. And I know I'm in a slightly different position to everybody else here, because I'm the First Minister of a country that voted to remain by an overwhelming majority. And we face being taken out of the European Union against our will. But this get Brexit done is the biggest con of this election. The deal that Boris Johnson has negotiated, I think a bad deal, is only a withdrawal deal. The trade talks haven't started yet. And, you know, if they don't conclude quickly, then we will very soon, next year, face another no-deal cliff edge, and that would be catastrophic. I'm happy to blame them for it. question to Rishi. Say the Tories get their way, I hope they don't, the UK is out, nobody but nobody thinks a trade deal can be negotiated and agreed by the end of next year. That's nonsense, we do. We don't trust you, sorry. I don't trust you. I don't trust you because you don't trust democracy. You don't trust 17.4 million people. 17.4 million people. The Brexit party have got a big responsibility for the mess the UK is in right now. And we're very proud of what we've achieved. He's proud of the mess he's achieved in the UK. But can I finish my question? We haven't achieved the mess. Do you mind if I finish my question? Nobody thinks the deal can be done in a year and you've ruled out extending the transition. So is it the Tory position that they are prepared to countenance a no-deal Brexit with all of the catastrophic effect that will have on jobs and living standards and the economy? I would ask Horatio to rule that out tonight. Answer the point about no-deal Brexit. Are you going to rule out a no-deal Brexit in any circumstance? That's how you negotiate. That's how you get a good deal. Nigel Farage... No, absolutely and emphatically not, because it would lead to the deaths of possibly tens of millions of people and wipe out swathes of our civilisation. So no, under no circumstances would I use nuclear weapons. Less important? I think Trident is both a financial issue, I don't think we should waste £200 billion on it, but it's a moral issue. We should be investing in health and education, not in weapons of mass destruction. Like Adam, I've been campaigning for what I believe in for all of my life, even in the days when it wasn't as electorally popular as it is today. I'm also the leader of a government in Scotland. We're not perfect, we make mistakes, but I'm not asking here to be judged just on my promises. I'm asking people to look at what the SNP government is doing day in and day out. I think people's lifestyles do have to change, but I also think governments and industry has to take the lead, because there's a relatively small number of companies across the world that are responsible for a disproportionate amount of emissions. Now, again, this is one of the areas where I say, look at what my government is doing, not just on what we're promising. We've got the toughest targets anywhere in the world. 75% of Scotland's electricity already comes from renewable sources. Last year, we planted 84% of all of the trees planted in the UK. The reason you can't do it immediately without reducing demand is that you'd make yourself more reliant on imports, which would increase your carbon emissions. But the transition away from fossil fuels is important and is underway. Well, the papers that were leaked the other day do set out what the evidence is, the fact that these discussions have already been happening. And I'm going to be pretty blunt here. When Boris Johnson says that the NHS is not on the table in a future trade deal, I simply do not trust him, and I do not believe him on that point. We propose, well let's put it beyond that, we're proposing an NHS Protection Act so that in law it would not be possible to put the NHS on the table and it would be protected from trade deals. Yeah, I mean, I'm going to just be frank on this, and it may not always be popular. Firstly, I don't believe EU nationals should lose rights that they've got. I think the way in which EU nationals who've made a massive contribution to this country have been treated since Brexit has been absolutely shameful. But I do think we should make it more possible to attract talent from non-EU countries. As you know, I'm the First Minister of Scotland. If you look ahead for 25 years, all of the growth in Scotland's population is projected to come from inward migration. If we make it harder, then the risk is our population will fall and that will have a negative impact on our economy. I also hate the tone of the debate around immigration, the hostile environment demonising immigrants. Imagine if British citizens living in other countries were talked about and treated in the way that immigrants here are sometimes talked about. It's shameful, it's wrong and we need to change it for hard economic reasons as well as for bigger value-based reasons. First of all, there hasn't been unlimited immigration and it doesn't cut wages. I don't want to see a cut in inward migration because it would damage the economy of my country and one of the reasons I want Scotland to be independent is so we can take these decisions for ourselves rather than having damaging decisions imposed upon us. And here's our NHS, is what he would say. Oh, nonsense. It's pathetic. I think you've got form on that. Boris Johnson's Tories are unfit for office, in my opinion. But unless people vote to keep them out, we could be stuck with them and their policies for years to come, with dire consequences for our economy and public services, especially the NHS. In Scotland, the SNP is the main challenger in every single Tory-held seat. So I vote for the SNP on December the 12th as I vote to lock Boris Johnson out of Downing Street, provide an escape route from the mess of Brexit and to put Scotland's future into Scotland's hands so that we can build a better, fairer country.

After careful consideration, yes, if it was essential. Adam Price. Absolutely not. Richard Tice. Well, the truth is that trust has absolutely collapsed in politicians, because people are sick and tired of politicians promising things and then not delivering. And we saw that with Labour Party manifestos. We saw it with the referendum, of course, when the £9 million Conservative government leaflet promised to carry out the will of the people, and it's not yet been done. We have to get it done, and after that, trust can start to be rebuilt. Caroline Lucas. There are two immediate things that we should be doing. There's one more thing that we don't talk enough about, and that is recycling our own waste. Well, and I think we should, yes is the simple answer, because we need to have a system that is fair to everybody. LONG-BAILEY: No, we want a sensible immigration system that works in the interest of business and our economy. LONG-BAILEY: Richard Tice first. Richard Tice briefly. LONG-BAILEY: I'm a bigger man than that, Rishi. Good evening, Mahesh. I'm the son of immigrants. My parents came here 60 years ago. And I'm a British Asian. LONG-BAILEY: We know that if we're still part of the EU, freedom of movement remains, but if not, LONG-BAILEY: We want every single EU citizen who's here in the UK to remain with full rights and protections, and we want that reciprocated for UK nationals in the EU. Easy one, this, for you. ..why should we believe you? Adam Price. Thank you. Rebecca Long-Bailey. LONG-BAILEY: I think we need honesty in politics.
