LONG-BAILEY: And we need to bring honesty back to politics, because it undermines trust. LONG-BAILEY: Every politician becomes involved in politics because they have a vision of society, whether I agree with it or not. Thank you very much indeed. Well, you've all won my vote for keeping sure answers when they're asked. So, relatively sure. Thank you very much. Let's move on to another vital topic now. It is, of course, climate change. More important, some would argue, than any. And the question comes from Tabitha McGuinness in Newport. Tabitha. So, Tabitha's pupils want to see immediate change, not an argument at 20, 30, 40, 50. Immediate change is the question on the issue of climate change. What immediate change, Caroline Lucas, should they see? Give us a couple of examples people at home might think of. In particular, you've targeted aviation, for example, in the Green Manifesto. Give us an example of what would affect people's lives, briefly, if you would. Forgive me, we've all got to move quite quickly to get through all the questions we want to go. Jo Swinson. But the question, remember, is what immediate changes need to be made. Let me ask you what I asked Caroline Lucas, which is your manifesto, like the Green manifesto, has talked about penalising people who fly a lot. So just briefly, and then I'll get other people to respond. Now, forgive me, politicians, but when all politicians agree that something's very serious, and all of them agree that it can be done without much pain, people often think, well, can that really be true? Here is... Who's willing to say, actually, you know what, your lifestyles will have to change, something will have to give? Would you stop all new oil drilling in Scotland? Because people would argue fossil fuels should go. Forgive me, Adam, we've got to keep moving because we've got lots of subjects that we still want to get through. I know it's a huge topic and it's frustrating to move on, but we do want to talk about the National Health Service tonight. Lucy Taylor is with us. Lucy, from Cardiff. Lucy is studying pharmacy. What are you doing? Two-part question to deal with the fact the NHS, in her words, is failing. And what will you do to ensure it is not sold off to the highest bidder? Rishi Sunak. Let's focus on what she said, which is, how will you ensure it isn't sold to the highest bidder? OK. Rebecca Long-Bailey, you've heard him say that. You've heard the Prime Minister say that. Why do you insist on claiming that it is for sale? LONG-BAILEY: Well, I mean, this government has been in place for 10 years now. LONG-BAILEY: We don't trust a word they say on the NHS. LONG-BAILEY: We've got 4.4 million people on waiting lists to have operations. LONG-BAILEY: My husband was one of them, who had his operation cancelled yesterday. LONG-BAILEY: But on the issue of the NHS being up for sale, documents were released... LONG-BAILEY: It's not. LONG-BAILEY: Come on. LONG-BAILEY: I'll be honest with people, it's not up for sale. LONG-BAILEY: Let's only be polite to let me finish. LONG-BAILEY: Stop scaremongering. LONG-BAILEY: Documents were released this week that showed very clearly that the government had been involved in numerous discussions with US trade officials where the NHS services, as it was referred to, were on the table. LONG-BAILEY: Richard Tice. LONG-BAILEY: This is ludicrous. So just to be clear on the point about for sale, what the documents appear to say... Go on then, Rebecca Long-Bailey, you put the question to him. Let Rebecca Long-Bailey put the question. Let her put the question. What were you putting to him? LONG-BAILEY: The discussions that were referred to in the papers released this week showed that NHS pharmacy and drugs that we would be procuring from the US would be on the table. LONG-BAILEY: They talked about extending patents to ensure that prices stayed up and there have been clear indications on White House websites that they're sick of the freeloading of other companies and organizations across the world who were driving US drug prices down. OK, I want to bring some other people in. Nicola Sturgeon, your party has backed Labour in making this claim. What is the evidence? And I'll bring Ritchie Soonack in to answer it in a second. Whose side are you on here? Because you were in government at one stage. Of course, there were plenty of private companies carrying out operations for the NHS. I think at the current time, it's 600,000 operations carried out last year by private companies at no cost to the patients in the NHS. Brief answer. We may move on to the issue of immigration in a second. I want to bring in Adam Price. Rebecca Long-Bailey, what's your answer to Plaid Cymru saying that the NHS here LONG-BAILEY: We talked about trusting politics earlier on, and all we've heard tonight are mistruths. LONG-BAILEY: In Wales, the Welsh Government put £240 million into the NHS. LONG-BAILEY: That was the highest of any nation. LONG-BAILEY: In the UK, with Rishi talking about, you know, his pride of the NHS, but £7.7 billion has been cut from social care alone since 2010. LONG-BAILEY: Your record speaks for itself. LONG-BAILEY: We're going to invest £26 billion, that's a £40 billion cash terms increase to the NHS to bring it well above 4% increase in spending. Just before we do move on, finally, you did raise the issue of untruths in this debate on the NHS. Would you accept that saying that people who have cancer will get a five-figure bill in the future for their treatment is an untruth? LONG-BAILEY: Well, I don't know who you're referring to there. Well, it's a Labour Party email that has gone out to supporters. Imagine what you would feel if you got a five-figure bill for a cancer treatment or if you had to pay to give birth. Are you claiming that there is anybody on this stage who is in favour of having a five-figure bill for cancer treatment. LONG-BAILEY: Certainly if we privatise our NHS, that could be the direction of travel and that's why it's so important to make sure that our NHS isn't on the table in any future US trade deals because that's what Donald Trump wants. LONG-BAILEY: Make no bones about it, he has said publicly, yes, the NHS is on the table. So are you accusing Rishi Sunak's party of wanting to privatise the NHS and charge £5,000 for cancer treatment? LONG-BAILEY: been his party's direction of trouble since the NHS was created. LONG-BAILEY: Let's not beat around the bush. Thank you very much. You both had the chance to answer the controversies in health, and there are other controversies, of course. Immigration is one of those subjects that never raises controversy, but let's see if it does tonight. We've got Mahesh Nayyar here, who comes from near Aberystwyth. Mahesh. Hello. Please, your question. Yeah. Should UK immigration law change so that EU and non-EU citizens are treated the same? Richard Tice. But what we must do... What's the answer to his question, though, about whether they should be treated the same, people from in the EU and outside, which, of course, at the moment, they can't be. So you do think that people should be treated differently as a result? People in the EU get freedom of movement, people in the rest of the world do not. Rishi Sunak, the question, just to remind you, I know you want to talk broader, all of you, about immigration, but is whether EU citizens and non-EU should be treated the same? Thank you, Adam. Most opinion polls suggest that the public do wish to see controls and indeed cuts in immigration, so who wants to... Hold on, hold on, hold on. Who here wants to say that they do want to cut immigration? Are any of you willing to say you want to cut immigration? And could we correct you on that? Understood. The headline figure is for cutting back. You ask different questions, you get different answers. That's perfectly reasonable. Will it be lower under Labour, Rebecca Lombardi? I'm sorry to wind it up. I know there's a lot you could talk about. We want to fit one more question in. You're more than welcome to stay here for another hour and a half afterwards, but we haven't got time while we're on air, at least. Let's move to our last question, and it comes from Trevor Morgan. Where is Trevor Morgan? And you have only got 30 seconds each to answer this, at the most. Nicola Sturgeon? OK. Caroline Lucas, what would you say to Donald Trump? Rishi Sunak? Happy Thanksgiving. Rebecca Long-Bailey? LONG-BAILEY: I'm a good Catholic, so I won't say in public what I probably would say to him, but the most important point I'd make is about the climate emergency and how he needs to change his position. LONG-BAILEY: There is a real climate emergency, and if we don't take action now, we're not going to leave a world or a future for our children. Thank you very much indeed. Let's move then to our closing statements. Each speaker has got just 30 seconds, and as with the opening statements, the order was decided before tonight by the drawing of lots. First, with her closing statement tonight, the Shadow Business Secretary, Labour's Rebecca Long-Bailey. LONG-BAILEY: I want to thank everybody for watching tonight. LONG-BAILEY: And it's easy to be cynical about politics. LONG-BAILEY: But politics makes real change happen. LONG-BAILEY: Labour made the NHS happen. LONG-BAILEY: We made the minimum wage and Sure Start happen. LONG-BAILEY: We'll make the Green Industrial Revolution happen. LONG-BAILEY: A vote for Labour is a vote for hope. LONG-BAILEY: For a government that supports you and invests in your community. LONG-BAILEY: A government that is on your side. LONG-BAILEY: So let's transform our country so that it works for everyone. LONG-BAILEY: Vote Labour on the 12th of December. Thank you very much indeed. And next, the First Minister of Scotland, Nicola Sturgeon, the leader of the SNP. Thank you very much indeed. Next to the leader of Plaid Cymru, Adam Price. Thank you very much indeed. Now to the leader of the Liberal Democrat Party, Jo Swinson. Thank you. For the Conservatives, the Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Rishi Sunak. Thank you very much indeed. Now to the Chairman of the Brexit Party, Richard Tice. Thank you very much. And finally tonight, Caroline Lucas of the Green Party. Thank you very much indeed. And ladies and gentlemen, could you thank all the politicians from the seven parties that are here tonight?
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_1: Tragically, no amount of extra police officers will prevent terrorist attacks like the one in London today. What do the panel members propose to do to tackle security issues in the UK in future? Thank you.
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_2: Yeah, I mean, it's difficult to find the words. I can't imagine what the families and the loved ones, those that lost their lives, are going through. And, you know, I extend my condolences to them. It's an area... I live around the corner from Tooley Street. It's an area I know very well. And, you know... What can we do? Clearly, there has to be a multiplicity of answers, because no one answer will keep us safe.
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_3: With all the parties promising to spend more money during the campaign, what guarantees can you give that these spending plans will be delivered when you get into government?
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_4: I was a Remain voter but feel that we're so far along the Brexit journey now that it has to go ahead. How do those of you here this evening feel about talk of a second referendum or revoking Article 50? Is this not going against the wishes of the 52% of people that voted to leave and a waste of a huge amount of time and money that has already been spent to enable this process to take place?
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_5: I'm a teacher, and many of my students rate climate change as one of their top priorities, even attending climate strikes. What changes would you propose that would help them to see the immediate change they would like to see?
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_6: I'm studying pharmacy and I'm genuinely worried about what my future will look like. The NHS is failing. What are you going to do to stop this and make sure it's not sold to the highest bidder?
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_7: UK immigration laws don't treat EU and non-EU citizens equally now. Should this change, no matter whether we leave or remain?
deb_2019_11_29.QUESTIONER_8: Good evening. Donald Trump has recently confirmed his visit to the UK. If you had 30 seconds with him, what would you say?

deb_2019_12_01.SWINSON: Jo Swinson. Boris Johnson can't be bothered to talk to you tonight. He's leaving it to his cheerleader, Nigel Farage. And if they win on the 12th of December, you lose. Boris Johnson will start years of Brexit talks, stitch up deals with Donald Trump, pick on people weaker than himself and stoke divisions in our country. And that's why Nigel Farage is happy to help him win. I'm determined to stop them. We deserve better than the Farage-Johnson-Trump nightmare. Their plans for Brexit are not only about our place in Europe. They're about the kind of country we are. I love our country. Our United Kingdom. Open, fair and generous. We are stronger working with our friends than standing alone. Tackling the climate crisis, helping the vulnerable, celebrating the richness of our diversity. If you want that brighter future, vote Liberal Democrat. Rishi Sunak. Well, Darren, in answer to your question, obviously it's wrong that that was able to happen. No terrorist should be released without it being properly assessed whether or not they still pose a risk to the public. So somebody like that just should not have been on our streets. clearly these questions need to be asked. We need to learn the lessons of this instance. I think we also need to listen very carefully to the loved ones because there are families who are grieving and mourning and the father of Jack Merritt I think was very powerful in what he said which was His son wouldn't have wanted this to be used as a pretense for some kind of draconian change in the law and I think we do need to listen to those loved ones too. Well, we need to resolve the chaos of Brexit. We need to end the chaos of Brexit. We've seen for the last three and a half years it's costing more than people ever thought, it's taking longer, and it's not anywhere near done. There are so many problems in our country that do divide our country. the inequality that for too many people it feels like it's just too hard to get by to make ends meet and we need to resolve those things. But we know from the government's own analysis, from all of the economic analysis, that remaining in the EU will mean we have more money, we can invest more in our public services, we will be better able to improve people's lives and help to resolve that division. Richard, by taking the position that he is, Jeremy Corbyn shows that he's a bystander, not a leader. But can I ask you this? Do you think it is possible for Jeremy Corbyn to negotiate a Brexit deal that is better for our country than remaining in the European Union? We're standing in an election and saying what we would do if we won the election. I think that's democratic. But you haven't answered the question whether you think it's possible that there could be a Brexit deal that is better than remaining in the European Union. I mean what was negotiated was even much less close agreement with the rest of the European Union than what Theresa May put forward because you watered down any of the what were inadequate protections on workers' rights, on environmental protections and the only possible conclusion can be is that's because a Conservative government would want to rip up workers' rights. It's absolutely the case that that's what the Conservatives want to do and that's why it's so dangerous for people in this country who are wanting to make sure that their hard won rights are not going to be sold down the river. Nicola tended to stand up for the United Kingdom too. And now we have a Labour party that is saying they're going to have a second independence referendum, and a Conservative party that has quite shockingly said that they will put a border down the Irish sea, despite all of the promises and pledges that we heard from Boris Johnson that no Conservative prime minister should ever agree to that. And they've gone ahead and done it. So neither of those parties can truly be saying they're standing up for the United Kingdom. Well, it seems like there's three people in the special relationship, Boris Johnson, Nigel Farage, and Donald Trump. We obviously have a hugely important relationship with the United States, but make no mistake, the current occupant of the White House is not somebody who shares our values. This is someone who has boasted about sexually assaulting women, whose policies are discriminatory against different people from different religious backgrounds, such as Muslims, and is separating parents from their children at the border. And we should be very careful with that relationship. The last thing we should have done was rule out the red carpet for a state visit. And I think it's shocking that that's the way the Conservatives have chosen to develop that relationship with Donald Trump. Nigel Farage. Look, Nigel, you're just trying to defend Donald Trump. You're saying these things aren't what he said, when we've all seen the footage of him talking and boasting about sexually assaulting women. Have you seen the footage of him boasting about sexually assaulting women? I think we should keep a nuclear deterrent. I don't think we need to replace it exactly as it is at the moment. But I do think that we should have a nuclear deterrent in a very uncertain world while working hard for multilateral disarmament because that is so important to create a safer world. Well, Liberal Democrats are being really clear about where the money's going to come from. So we're saying corporation tax will go up a bit and that there will be some changes to capital gains tax, and that will enable us to make sure we have free childcare for people from the point at which they're going back to work from the nine months onwards. We're also saying that by staying in the EU we know that the economy will do better, there will be more taxation revenue and as a result there'll be more money for schools and to put into universal credit to help the most vulnerable. But we're also saying that there isn't any easy shortcut to this. that we all have to pay more if we want better services. So we'd put a penny on income tax for the NHS and for those who are promising a wish list of policies without saying that we'll all actually have to pay a bit more for it. They're just not being credible or honest with you. There's almost no capital investment in your plans. There's £130 billion of capital investment. I mean, I think, you know, the Independent Institute for Fiscal Studies has said that the Liberal Democrat proposals are radical. £130 billion of investment in tackling the climate emergency, something we agree on, is very significant. I want to talk about a particular part of our NHS that hasn't been mentioned yet. One in four of the people in this room tonight will, at some point this year, face a mental health problem. And imagine if you went to hospital and you had a broken leg, and you were told you were going to have to wait six months to get it seen to, or you were going to have to travel hundreds of miles to get it sorted. You would be outraged, and rightly so. Now imagine being a teenager in mental health crisis, and when you need that help, you're told that you have to travel hundreds of miles, or it's going to have to wait weeks and weeks or months and months. It's not good enough. We've not given it the resource it needs. And that's why Liberal Democrats have identified £11 billion to invest in more mental health services over the next Parliament, to make sure that we can have those talking therapies when people need them, and make sure that we're properly dealing with mental health with the seriousness that it deserves. Thank you. I think one of the things about this election is it feels incredibly polarised and like everybody's just at each other's throats, but there's actually cause for optimism. So Adam and Sian and myself, our parties are all working together in this election on the Unite to Remain Alliance. We've been working together on stopping Brexit and although Nicola and I have our robust differences on Scotland's rule in the UK. We've also worked together on stopping Brexit. I've worked with Rishi's colleague Andrea Ledsom, who I disagree with on most things, but I've worked with her on how we can try and stamp out harassment and bullying for staff working in Parliament. And Richard's colleague Stella Creasy have joined on many a feminist campaign and trying to protect women in terms of rights over their own bodies. So I think there's a lot that we can be positive about where there's actually a lot of cooperation in politics, which can help us to work together on those big issues, including climate change and social care. Rishi, I have to pick you up on your record on this, because Liberal Democrats in government did make massive investments and change the industry for offshore wind in particular, which has helped to make us a world leader in offshore wind, but you reeled off a set of promises. You said that there's a net zero target but the Conservative government have got no plans on how that's going to be met and let's look at your record. You sold off the Green Investment Bank, you stopped more onshore wind happening and you abolished the Department for Climate Change. That shows the lack of priority you're putting on it. Jo Swinson. Well, this is exactly the kind of issue that does need to have cross-party working. And that has been set up in the past and then fallen apart. And the Conservative government says they want to work cross-party, but you have delayed again and again and again the social care white paper and are not bringing it forward. So it's all very well to now say you want to work cross-party on this basis, but Boris Johnson hasn't been bringing forward his plan on this. So what will Boris Johnson do if he wins a majority? He'll ram through his bad Brexit deal, starting years and years of more negotiations. He won't be there when the redundancy notices are handed out, or when you're paying more for your supermarket shop, just like he isn't here tonight. Get Brexit done is just his latest lie. But there is still time to stop him. The Liberal Democrats are the only party that can win a significant number of seats from the Conservatives and stop Boris Johnson. And every vote for the Liberal Democrats is a vote to stop Brexit, a vote to build a brighter future, to save our planet, protect our NHS, and ensure you and your family can thrive.
deb_2019_12_01.FARAGE: There has never been a time when confidence in politics, politicians and our institutions has been lower than it is right now today. And that's because three and a half years ago, all the parties on this platform told you to vote Remain, but you didn't. You said, we want to leave. And three and a half years on, we find most of the parties here still don't want to honour the vote, want you to vote again, some even want to revoke Article 50, and the Conservatives have had three and a half years to sort this out, yet they sort a half-in, half-out fudge. I formed the Brexit Party because I want to get Brexit done properly. I want us to become an independent nation. That is what the vote was for, to leave the institutions of the EU and to be self-governing. Once we've done that, that's just the beginning of a political revolution that needs to happen. The Brexit Party are the new radicals. We've got to reform our rotten institutions, the House of Lords, our voting system, corruption with postal voting. Let's get back to being a proper, functioning democracy. We all express our condolences, but nobody apologises. Apologises for the fact the liberal elite have given us a ridiculous sentencing system. I don't care whether these people were in jail for six years or 12 years. If you have committed mass murder, or plan to commit mass murder, you're not just an ordinary criminal, you've got the virus of jihadism, and I think these people, Darren, should never, ever be let out of prison unless we're absolutely convinced they do not have the jihadi virus but of course political correctness stops us from doing this. The Conservative government have been very very weak on sentencing over the last 40 years just as Labour have been. re-offending. There are 74 convicted terrorists out on our streets. There are 400 returning jihadi fighters on our streets who've come back from Syria. Surely whether they serve six years or 12 years isn't the point. Why should they ever be let out again into society? Is anybody else on the platform concerned that there are 74 convicted terrorists out on our streets and 400 returning jihadis? Or do we just ignore it and pretend it's going to go away? Surely, surely we need... This is not just an ordinary criminal. These people want to destroy our civilisation. Surely they should be put in prison for life. Silence is golden. Well, Sean, you beautifully illustrate why there's division in the country, because the political parties, and most of Westminster, don't accept the result of the people. Losers' consent is the principle by which civilised democracy works. You fight elections, you lose them, you accept the result. That is how our system works. We have had three and a half years, and you're still doing it. You don't accept the result, and now you want a second referendum. just to give even more division and acrimony in the country. And I've no doubt that when we vote to leave again by an even bigger margin, you still wouldn't accept it. There's no perfect answer, given where we are. But we have to deliver Brexit. We have to become an independent country or our democracy in this country is, frankly, dead. It's going to give us independence. I thought you liked independence. We're going to be a free nation. You can't be independent of me, innit? But isn't the real key here? Isn't the real key here? Labour's referendum would give us a choice, would it not, Richard, of remain versus remain. You don't even intend to give the voters a credible leave option, and that surely is a betrayal of five million of your own voters who voted to leave. As it is, it doesn't work. As it is, if it goes through... If it's oven-ready and it goes through as it is, we're headed for years of misery. Now, Boris Johnson said, after the election was called, that we would go back to Brussels and negotiate a free trade deal in line with the Canada plus plus agreement. If you stick to that and you amend the political declaration, then we will get something like Brexit. If you carry on with the deal as it was, it is not Brexit, it will take years to deliver and millions of people will be very upset and disappointed. So the question is, is it oven ready or are you going to go for a free trade deal? Brexit is leaving, which would mean in Wales we could help the steel industry. In Wales you could help the fishing industry. So much better we could do outside. Leaving means we do not have the European Court of Justice ruling over our country. The problem with the deal on the table is that it would. That needs to be amended. If it is, it'll look like Brexit. If you leave it, Rishi, as it is, it's not Brexit. Nigel Farage. The population of our country has risen by 8 million since Tony Blair came to power in 1997. And you can argue about people coming and working hard in coffee shops and all the rest of it. The fact is, this massive increase in population has made our access to public services difficult, has compressed people's wages, and not improved our quality of life. And frankly, I'm pleased you picked up the Australian-style points system from me, but I've got no confidence that anybody here genuinely wants to reduce the numbers. Isn't that terrible? Why aren't we training our own people? Why don't we train our own people? What's wrong with us? When we talk about the special relationship, we're particularly talking about foreign policy, we're talking about defence, and indeed we're talking about security. And I would argue that the special relationship is more important now than ever, because NATO itself is now directly threatened by a European Union that wants to do defence on its own. You've only got to listen to what President Macron has said over the course of the last few weeks, what Mr Juncker's been saying, what Mr Verhofstadt's been saying. The European Defence Union effectively has been set up to replace NATO, and we play a key role in this. Boris Johnson needs to make a big decision in the next few days. Are we staying with the European Defence Union, or are we staying with NATO and America? And I hope we choose the latter. Well, Obama brought him a so-called Muslim ban, but no one criticised him. He was the one that named the seven countries where a total stop on anyone coming into America should come from. Trump implemented it. And so these really, in many cases, are very, very false arguments. Look, Donald Trump's style is very American. It may not be to everybody's taste, but just think about this. He is our most important friend in the world. And at a time, at a time of huge global uncertainty, we should think about not just trade with America, but security, making sure that NATO is not destroyed by the European Union. These things really matter. And can I just say, the biggest lie of this entire election campaign is that Trump wants to buy the NHS. He does not. And he will say so, I'm sure, this week when he's here. I was talking about Obama, and you're so Muslim about it. I have, I have. And it was crass, and it was crude, and it was wrong. And men say dreadful things sometimes. But the point here... That's just all right then. No, they do. They do. But you know something? Some men in particular. If all of us were caught out for what we'd said on a night out after a drink... Listen, American politics is in fact more divided in many ways than our politics. But all these false claims, you may not like him, he is president of the USA and that relationship matters. You are so anti-American, you're prepared to put your hatred of Trump above our national interest. And that's a big mistake. Just like Obama before him. Obama brought the ban. You never attacked Obama, did you? Well, in a world that has barbarous countries like Iran rushing as quickly as they can to get their own nuclear weapons, it would be madness to unilaterally get rid of ours. And so, yes, we have to have it. And with reluctance, yes, there may be circumstances in which we'd have to use it. Well, James, you work in manufacturing, and if there is a green jobs revolution, as they all call it, you will lose your job in manufacturing, and perhaps we'll come back to that later on in the programme. Look, I do agree with the point about the regions being left behind. Many parts of the UK feel as though they've been ignored for decades, taken for granted, and the argument for better infrastructure, the argument for access to broadband, even mobile phone connectivity in parts of this country is absolutely overwhelming. The question is, how do we pay for it? Well, Labour clearly are going to borrow vast amounts of money. The Conservatives are going to borrow some. We suggested cutting things like HS2, which cost £100 billion to benefit just a few thousand. You could help millions with £100 billion. Can we stop this? We had it with Labour governments, we've now got it with Conservative governments, just lying to everybody. You are not cutting the debt. You are cutting the deficit. When you came to power, the national debt was £650 billion. It is now £1.7 trillion. The debt has gone up massively. Well, even more hugely under Labour, I accept that, but please tell people the truth. Our national debt now, the interest payments on it, are £40 billion a year and going up. I didn't hear it. Well, I've used the NHS a lot as an emergency patient. through my life and it can be fantastic and brilliant. But let's stop the lies, shall we? Let's stop that everyone wants to privatise it, Donald Trump wants to buy it. He's not going to buy it, Richard. Nobody else wants to privatise it. Actually, we all want the same thing. I know your party gave us PFI deals, but if we all want the same thing, could this possibly be one issue that shouldn't be a political football and where we could perhaps work together? I'm not on You're on the record for not saying you want to. Thank you gentlemen. Joe Swinson. Lie, lie, lie, lie, lie. Nigel Farage. Well, Carly, politics is about choices. So inevitably, people will have differing views. And the electors decide who they want to carry out that policy for the next four or five years. But I did in a previous question talk about the National Health Service. There are areas where it seems everybody wants the same thing. And when it comes to resolving the care crisis, when it comes to the National Health Service being free at the point of delivery, there is actually quite broad agreement across all the parties. So there should be areas in which parties can work together. Whilst everybody here agrees on climate change, I've got a slight issue with that, in that what we're doing is putting a lot of people out of work and making the poor give lots of money to the rich. Yes, we want a cleaner planet, but I don't think the way we're currently doing it is right. So there'll always be disagreement. Just look at what's happened. I mean, since we started putting in place green policies, and this country's done it worse than any other country, we've finished up with electricity prices 60% higher than most of our European competitors. So no wonder our aluminium supply has dropped. I challenge the idea that we've... No wonder. No wonder. We've got more policies in place.
