What will solve this problem is going to be new technologies and innovation, whether it's more powerful offshore wind turbines, cheaper car batteries that drive faster, or cleaner, greener homes. But the only way you can bring those new technologies to life is through a strong economy, which is why that strong economy has to be at the foundation of our greener future as a country. I'm actually very proud of our record. We are the country that has decarbonised faster than any other major economy. We're leading the way in banning the use of single-use plastics. And this year, we passed a very important milestone. For the first time ever, we generated more of our power from carbon-free sources than fossil fuels. So that actually is a record. I'm very proud to stand on it. Because you've taken it away completely. It's about moving the support to the technologies which are new, which need the support. Well, we have a three-point plan to try and tackle this problem. The first of it is to invest a billion pounds a year extra to deal with the immediate pressures in the system. The second thing is explicitly to work on a cross-party basis to try and find consensus the way we have with the NHS. And the third thing is to ensure that when it's reformed, no one will have to sell their home to pay for care. I think, Julie, on that, look, this is a difficult issue. As you said, 20 years, different governments of all stripes haven't been able to solve this problem. We need to work together, we need to try and solve the problem. But I can say this, if you think about next year, should we spend next year focusing on this kind of problem, social care or climate change, or should we spend next year arguing about Brexit with more referendums and years more gridlock? The choice to get on to these issues is to get Brexit done and then we can start focusing on this kind of stuff. Thank you. Ask yourself this. Do you want Brexit sorted in weeks or more referendums taking years? Focus on the NHS, schools and police or more arguing about Brexit? To move on or to stay gridlocked? This is the choice you have. Boris Johnson delivering Brexit by the end of January and focusing on your priorities. Or Jeremy Corbyn ridiculously stuck in neutral on one of the biggest questions our country has ever faced. Only the Conservatives can break this deadlock and set Britain free.
deb_2019_12_01.BERRY: We are in dark and dangerous times, and this election is about trust. I know how many casually broken promises you've heard from politicians. I joined the Greens after hearing too many of these myself 18 years ago. I know you want real change this year, and things can change dramatically when you use your right to vote. I'll be honest and say that Brexit would change things for the worse. And I'll be hopeful and say that I believe you want that final say. And that you and your children are crying out for real action to stop climate chaos. That's what I stand for today. For good ideas that build up communities for the common good. For the Green New Deal we need to prevent disaster. Our efforts together to do politics differently mean you have the chance now to elect more Green MPs, a louder voice for you and future generations, adding to the volume and the clarity that Caroline Lucas has been providing for a decade. You can make history, shine a light in the darkness with your vote. If not now, when? First of all to say that the way to really prevent and detect these offences is with a community who doesn't feel discriminated against working alongside the police in a cooperative way and we do need intelligence-led stronger policing in the community. I work in London in the London Assembly I know that police cuts are real the police do not have time to get out of their vans into the community and and work with people in a way that would really prevent this. Now when it comes to people who've been recruited, who've been exploited, who are vulnerable to this kind of political ideology, we do need to have proper rehabilitation for them while they're in prison. It's no good just leaving people to fester for longer in an overcrowded prison system. We need real rehabilitation and it breaks my heart that Jack Merritt, who was murdered was actually working on this exact thing of education and rehabilitation. Those are the things we need to be funding more of, as well as real policing in the community that doesn't just alienate people. OK, and Adam Price? Thank you. Sian Berry. I understand completely the motivation behind the question, because we're all really just hoping that the country will stop being so divided. And I have to say, I really, truly believe that the best way to finish off Brexit, to finish off this process, is with more democracy, with a people's vote. And I think, I mean, looking around this panel, we've got a majority of parties here who are committed to putting back the Brexit question in a people's vote. I do believe that's the best way to finish it off. And I do believe that Brexit would be a disaster. The reason I want to get a people's vote done is so we can get back to fixing the country, to dealing with the problems that austerity caused. And I don't think we can trust the Conservatives with another vote in this election, another government, because they've had three years, they've made a hash of Brexit, and they cannot be trusted again. Thank you. Nigel Farage. Thank you. The Conservatives keep shouting out, let's move on and we got a deal. And it's move on to the next cliff edge, they haven't got a deal, they've got a transition agreement and then we've never negotiated a trade deal in less than about five, seven years. It took seven years to negotiate a deal with Canada. These are more lies and more slogans and people won't be fooled again. Greens have been completely consistent in saying we need a people's vote, we need a ratification referendum and we will campaign for Remain. I think that makes us represent the majority of people in this country. Okay, Adam Price. OK, let's bring in Sean Barry, who wants to come in here. The NHS is staffed by people from around the world. Immigrants from around the world but also 60,000 people who come from other EU countries. You're far more likely to find a doctor treating you in the NHS than you are to find an immigrant ahead of you in the queue. This is one of the worst lists we've had to fight over the years. We should train nurses and doctors, shalln't we? I am proud to stand up for free movement. I'm proud to stand up for the free movement we already have and I'm proud to stand up for the principle too, that the thought of an Australian style point system strikes fear into my heart because I know what it's like in Australia for immigrants. Trump's rise obviously concerns me hugely. It's not just his rhetoric, it's not just the use of populism, the use of hatred in his words, it's also his policies. When you look at what's actually happening to children in his detention. centres, I almost cannot cope with the thought of that. When it comes to our relationship with America, I just hope that after next year, when Donald Trump is gone, we can fix that and build something new. Because when I think about what we could do with that special relationship, with NATO's organisation, with its rapid reaction forces, and its logistics, and the kind of budget we're talking about putting behind Trident renewal, which is 200 billion pounds. The kind of peace project we could build with that kind of money and organisation, it's such a wasted opportunity. What will you do on a date after a drink, Nigel? I'm sure you've lived the purest life of anybody. Come on, it's not purity to you. I've never said a word wrong about anything, anywhere. Nicola Sturgeon. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I'm sure you're absolutely right. I Thank you very much on that. I have to add that it is unthinkable to use nuclear weapons, and I want to put my name down on that side of the aisle. Thank you. Sian Berry. Only the Greens are putting forward the investment plans that are really needed to transform our economy and solve the problem of runaway climate chaos. If you look at what the Liberal Democrats are putting forward, there's barely any investment in their plans. Even the Labour Party are putting forward barely a third of what's needed. And there's a reason we put forward this as an alternative to bailing out the banks, as an alternative to austerity 10 years ago. It's because our policies pay for themselves, they reach into every corner of the country and they reduce climate emissions. It's been a win-win for 10 years and I really, really hope whoever's in government at the end of this election will listen to the Green Party and start implementing some of these good ideas at last. That's one tenth of what we're putting forward. It's one tenth. Thank you. Sean Barry. I need to point out that... The Conservatives insisting that they've been managing the economy well is just not true. The credit reference agencies downgraded our credit scores based on the fact that the Conservatives were borrowing money but not saying how they were going to pay it back. They weren't making long-term investments. We saw the spending review delayed. Now the key thing about our plans is they're long-term and they pay for themselves. They bring money back in, in warmer homes, in cleaner air, in less burden on our health service, on healthier people. Now your plans don't do any of that. You're filling in the gaps that you left with 10 years of austerity now. You have to admit that was a mistake. Thank you. in response to that, Rishi Sunak. It really is a threat to the NHS from Brexit and from Trump. And our manifesto commits completely to refunding it and also reversing privatisation, as Caroline Lucas put forwards in her bill. But the really key thing that we would do is prevention. I talked before about some of the side effects of our Green New Deal investment, being warmer homes, healthier people, cleaner air. But also, we want to bring in more social care investment, a universal basic income, the next step in social security, the best thing we can do that follows the founding principles of the welfare state. When the NHS was set up, it was sent round to everybody, a leaflet that said, this is not a charity, you're all paying for it as taxpayers and it will relieve your money worries in times of illness. If you're talking about mental health, we need that universal basic income to relieve people's money worries throughout their lives. That's the big idea in our manifesto. Thank you so much for this question. I mean, this is the climate election partly because of the actions of young people who've done such a good job of bringing this to other politicians' attention. They can't ignore you when you speak up as a school-age person. It's fantastic. But this is how Greens work. You know, we're very rarely in power. This is how I work on the London Assembly. I try and get Sadiq Khan to carry out things that are in my manifesto, not his. The Greens in Scotland, they hold Nicola's government to account and get good things like the new tax structures that you talked about. We're used to doing this and we've tried in this election too to bring together parties to try and put together some kind of agreement that we can actually try and stop Brexit together. I think that was a constructive and grown-up thing to do as well. And we do, we run councils with Labour, and we tried to get Labour to talk to us as well, and they didn't. But I believe that we're trying to show the way. Yeah, I've no idea why you don't think that massive investment in new industries to retrofit all our homes, to sort out our transport system, wouldn't create lots of jobs. Because our plans do. They create 3 million jobs, our Green New Deal. We've had a Conservative government. Renewable energy is now the cheapest thing we can possibly invest in. Continuing to invest in renewable energy will make our energy cheaper. Investing in energy saving will save everybody money on their bills. Yes or no, would you all commit to working together on this issue? Do it. In our manifestos. Thank you. Sian Barry. It's easy to fear the future. The shadow of Brexit hangs over us. Runaway climate chaos is getting closer. And all around, there is inequality. But despite all this, Greens don't fear the future. We are the future. You can trust us to keep our promises and hold the other parties to account. Greens in Parliament will campaign for remain and let no government get away with more failure on climate chaos. We can't afford it. This election could be the last chance we have to transform Britain. It's time to change course. So vote Green, if not now, when?
deb_2019_12_01.PRICE: Politics was already in a dark place, and it feels as if it just got darker. But as Lukasz on London Bridge showed us, sometimes the worst times can bring out the best in us. So how can we rediscover our lost sense of hope? In Wales, it's difficult not to feel the opposite, the paralysis of despair. We're going backwards from health to the economy. And it's Labour that's been in power there for 20 years. They're not the answer. So what is? Well for us it's not more Westminster, it's more Wales. Elect MPs from the Westminster parties and they go there to fight each other. Elect MPs from Wales' party and they'll fight for us with every breath. Like my father when he was boxing champion, they'll punch above their weight. To Westminster, we are invisible. To us, Wales is invaluable because it's who we are. Where others put Wales last, we will put our people first because it's our future, our mothers, our fathers, our daughters, our sons, because we want them to feel hope again in those beating Welsh hearts. Well, I'd also like to express my deepest sadness that I feel for those that have lost their loved ones. Look, I think there are serious questions that need to be answered seriously. The 2016 report, the Aitchison report, which looked at this question of parole for convicted terrorists, it seems from the report authors' claims that the majority of those recommendations haven't been implemented. The former director of public prosecutions here in the Northwest says that he raised these issues directly with the Prime Minister. I don't know what happened as a result of that. and I think it would be wrong to speculate. We need sober reflection, not a rush to judgment in an election campaign. And, you know, I think that maybe I'm old-fashioned, but at a time when terrorists are trying to divide us, I want to be the... I want the Prime Minister, even in an election, to be the Prime Minister of all of us. And I think the language that he used this morning, talking about a lefty government, not only showed a huge disrespect to those that have lost their loved ones, it did a disservice to a democracy, and he showed yet again that he's unfit to be Prime Minister. Look, the reality of it is this. We were already a divided so-called United Kingdom. We were divided by a huge chasm of wealth between the North and the South, between the West and the East of the country. And many people, there will be many, many different reasons for voting Lee, but many people in the former Caulfield communities, like the one that I'm from, voted for leave because they were exasperated at a Westminster political establishment that has never delivered economic change for them. I have to be honest to people in the communities like the one that I represent. Look, Brexit will not deliver the economic justice that we want. It'll make matters worse. It'll take the division that's already there and make it what it'll do to our economy, what it's done to our politics, divided the rich from the poor. It will not be the answer to our problem. Certainly don't take power from Brussels. They're putting it in Westminster. We'll do the opposite of economic change for Wales. Thank you. Jo Swinson. It's a terrible choice facing the two main Westminster parties, a no-deal policy on the one hand and with the Labour Party, no policy at all. Here's a question, Richard, that possibly you can answer. You probably do admire Tony Benn. He used to talk about, didn't he, weathervane politicians that just go with the winds, et cetera, or the signpost politicians. He was actually clear. He was against the European Union. But what we've got in the Labour Party now are weathervane politicians. You won't even say, going into this general election, where you stand. You say you'll consult your members. You're not even able to be honest with the people. Where's the leadership in that when we're crying out for it in this country? Another thing Nigel Farage said before he had his Damascene conversion at the beginning of the campaign was you said that, actually, the deal on the table from the Conservative government is virtually worse than remaining within the European Union. Now, if that's true, surely the right thing to do is ask the people again, is this the Brexit that you really wanted, or not the fantasy Brexit, the actual Brexit on the table? Is this what you thought you were voting for? You know, these are incredibly worrying times, aren't they? We see the axis between Mr Farage, Mr Trump and Mr Johnson. And that's the really frightening thing. It's not just our NHS that is a threat. It's of fundamental values. The President stands accused in the impeachment process of trying to use his political influence in Ukraine. What are we seeing even this week? The Prime Minister of this country, when he doesn't turn up to a channel for debate, saying he's going to revoke their licence. It's the Trumpian future that's going to happen to this country unless we call it out at this election. That's not the values that we want to see at the heart of our politics, but it's what's coming as a result of the special relationship, not between two countries, but between two men. The truth is, the words we use matter, whether in public or in private. I mean, it can never be acceptable. And if you think it is, you should be ashamed of yourself. We have a Prime Minister who referred to gay men like myself, you know, as bum boys in tank tops, referred to Muslim women looking like letterboxes. You know, this is actually creating the climate, right, where abuse becomes acceptable, whether it's abuse of women or of minorities. And that's why neither Trump nor Boris Johnson are fit to be in public. OK. Well, I say to Richard, look, you can try and have it both ways on Brexit, but you can't have it both ways on this one. It's a moral question. You cannot support weapons of mass destruction, which means, if you use them, means deliberately killing tens of millions of innocent men, women, and children. We did unilaterally give up biological and chemical weapons. We showed the leadership, and that led OK, thank you very much. That should happen with nuclear weapons, too. Well, I think one of the things that we need to do is to rebalance the spending within the United Kingdom. So what we want to see is the UK overall increasing its level of infrastructure spending by at least 1% of GDP, but seeing that investment not going as it's as has happened decade by decade into the already affluent southeast of England, but into Wales, yes, into the north of England, Scotland, places that have been starved of investment so that we can be there at the ground level of this green jobs revolution rather than being where we've been for too long, which is at the wrong end, the long end of the queue for investment. We do it by having a fairer tax system, absolutely, but the crucial thing is that investment needs to get to the right places. It hasn't been coming to Wales. Well, we certainly don't fix it by putting our faith in Labour, because the NHS is in dire crisis in Wales. We've got the worst A&E waiting times on record. It's so bad that on Friday night, even the shadow Secretary for Business, the Labour Party, called for an inquiry into our own party's record in Wales, where we've got a crisis in terms of maternity services in the south, a crisis in terms of mental health services in the north. The truth is, we need different approach which first of all recognizes we have to diagnose the problem properly. We're not going to solve it by putting our faith in either Labour or Conservative parties. We've actually allowed one of our greatest treasures in these islands to waste away under the lack of leadership under both Labour and Conservative parties and that has to change in this election. But Richard, the most imminent threat to the NHS in Wales at the moment is not Trump, it's your party that is mismanaging the NHS in Wales. And how can you actually inspire confidence in people unless you're at least able to admit the responsibility for the failings at home in Wales? Thanks, Carly, for this question, because I think it goes to the heart of the dysfunction in a democracy at the moment. Brexit has brought it to the fore, but it was deeper than that. It was already there. And, you know, we've got a 19th-century political system which is unable to address the 21st-century problems. And, you know, no party has a monopoly on the truth. We'll all have some valid perspectives, and we have to find a more collaborative system. We have to adopt the sort of Citizens' Assembly approach positive in Ireland as they looked at the change of the constitution over rape. There are different mechanisms whereby we can have better conversations with each other and as a result of that we'll get better solutions for all of us. Well, I think this is one of the most important questions of this election. I see it in my own family. I see my 80-year-old mother having to try and negotiate the complexity of a means-tested system to care for, properly, for my 84-year-old father. It comes to the basic question of whether we are a decent society. And again, I don't mean to bang on about this, Richard, but why is it when you're in government in Wales, you're not implementing, we have free social care in Scotland, why can't we have it in Wales? You're talking about a national health and social care service, free at the point of need for social care in Wales. You could do it in Wales now, why don't you? You keep on saying, Richard, how disappointed you are in me. Not half as disappointed as I am in your government in Wales. I could stand here on this stage tonight because an earlier generation gave me a chance. Born in a council house, educated in a comp, free school meals kept me fed in the minor strike, and education was my route out of poverty. There are children in Wales that will go to school tomorrow hungry, some of them without even a decent pair of shoes on their feet. It doesn't have to be this way. We could start to end child poverty this winter, not drop the target as Labour have done in Wales. We could give all our children, the best Christmas present ever, the gift of a better future. If we do what we've always done and put our faith in the Westminster parties, that cycle of poverty will continue. But if pride breaks through, we break that cycle and then everything for Wales becomes possible.
deb_2019_12_01.STURGEON: This election is the most important in our lifetimes. At stake is not just Brexit, but what kind of society we want to be. The SNP wants Scotland to be an independent, internationalist country. And we are determined that the people of Scotland must have the right to make that choice. But we also want to play our part in locking Boris Johnson out of Downing Street. Make no mistake, if he wins a majority then the consequences will be dire. Brexit will not in any sense be done. Trade talks haven't even started and these issues will cause chaos for years to come. In the rush for a trade deal with Donald Trump, the Tories simply can't be trusted not to put our NHS on the table. They're also threatening to rip up rules on food standards, environmental protections and workers' rights. To build a better future, we need to stop Boris Johnson and the Tories. And in Scotland, that means voting SNP. Adam Price. Nicola Sturgeon. Well, firstly, my condolences and thoughts remain with the victims and all those who were affected by what was a horrific attack. Clearly, the responsibility of politicians is to do everything we can to keep people safe. He shouldn't have been out. I think we can all agree on that. There are lessons that will require to be learned. But I don't think we do any service to victims or to the wider public to rush to those conclusions or to have knee-jerk reactions and I frankly don't think Boris Johnson has done any service to victims in the crass way he has sought to politicise this issue during an election campaign. The most serious offenders like this one should be in jail for lengthy periods. In Scotland we have ended automatic early release for prisoners sentenced to more than four years in prison. I think that is right. It should be up to the parole board to determine the safety of releasing somebody. So there must be lessons learned here, but let's do it properly in a way that actually does do a service to those affected by this atrocity. A quick response from Rishi Sunak, then I'll come to Sean Barry and Adam Price. Nicola Sturgeon. He said this morning in the interview on the BBC that Parliament had blocked the proposals in his Queen's speech that would have led to tougher sentences. Parliament actually supported his Queen's speech. There is an obligation here on a Prime Minister, I agree with Adam, to try to bring people together in the aftermath of an attack like this. not to seek to politicise it for party political gain. But on these issues, of course, we have to reflect on what happens after an atrocity like this, but we also need to do more to prevent these things. And that's where good community policing is so important. The 20,000 cuts to police in England and Wales has been a disgrace. We've increased police numbers in Scotland. Let's get more police into our communities, helping to prevent these attacks, not just have us all standing here wondering how we react. Well, of course, the country I represent voted to remain in the European Union by more than 60%. Even some people who voted to leave, and I suspect this is true across the UK, are utterly fed up of the mess that Brexit has become, of the chaos that we've seen played out over the last three and a half years. My policy on Brexit, of giving people across the UK the opportunity to think again and to escape the Brexit mess, yes, I do think can help to bring people back together, because the biggest con in this election is the slogan from the Tories, that if they win this election, Brexit gets done. We only move into the next and probably more chaotic part of the process, and, of course, a no-deal Brexit would be back on the horizon next year. On the point about the union, it's not the sign of a strong union. where Scotland can be dragged out of the EU against our will. If Scotland is independent, we get to be in a partnership of equals with the rest of the UK, which is a much better position to be in. Thank you, Nicola Sturgeon. They are on. Brexit will cost people the length and breadth of the country. And the alternative to giving people the chance to escape it is just to continue with the chaos. I asked Rishi a question in the last debate we did. Can you guarantee that next year, if a trade deal hasn't been negotiated, that we won't have a no-deal Brexit? Will you take a no-deal Brexit off the table right now, yes or no? I'm asking you, if there is no trade, nobody thinks a trade deal can be negotiated by the end of next year, so if you're right, fine, but if you're not right, do you think a no-deal Brexit will happen or will you rule it out right now? If Scotland was independent, we wouldn't be getting dragged out of the European Union against our will. Well, look, as I said a moment ago, if the union is as strong as some people say it is, then how can Scotland be in a position of facing being dragged out of the European Union against our will? We stand at a crossroads right now. Down one path, Scotland has the prospect of our future being decided for us by Boris Johnson and Nigel Farage, or we can have the option of deciding a different future as an independent country where we're in charge of our own destiny. I think that is a much brighter prospect. On the question of Labour, I'm not asking Richard Burgon or Jeremy Corbyn to support independence but I would be asking them if the SNP hold the balance of power to respect Scotland's right to choose, to respect the fact that that is a decision not for Westminster but for the people of Scotland and I don't think they'd turn their back on the chance to govern just to block Scotland's right to choose. Well, I support a second Brexit referendum, because I actually think everybody in the UK should have the chance to escape the mess. But fundamentally, it's for Scotland to decide our own future, because it's not right. And perhaps you can explain why you think it's right for Scotland to face exit from the EU when we overwhelmingly voted against that. I respect the fact that people here don't support independence. That's legitimate to oppose it. But what's not legitimate is to deny the people of Scotland the right to be in charge of that decision. That's a basic issue of democracy, surely. issue. The relationship with America is important, of course it is, but when it comes to Donald Trump, we should sup with a very, very long spoon. His rhetoric and many of his policies are deeply dangerous. And the last thing the United Kingdom needs is a Prime Minister who's trying to be like Donald Trump. We certainly shouldn't let him anywhere near our National Health Service, and I think it is at risk in a future trade deal, the Tories, and Nigel Farage will say that's not true. Well, if that's not true, let us put it beyond doubt. Let's have an NHS Protection Act that makes sure our National Health Service can never, ever be on the table in a future trade deal, because Donald Trump and Boris Johnson, frankly, cannot be trusted with it. Thank you. Donald Trump, amongst many other things, demonises immigrants, so it's no wonder Nigel Farage really likes them. You never attacked Obama, not once. We've got strongman politics. Not once. We've got Nigel Farage, we've got Boris Johnson, we've got Donald Trump. That, in my view, is not what the UK, or any part of it, needs. Donald Trump's attitudes, I think, are wrong and they're dangerous. And in Boris Johnson, we've got somebody who's trying to be like him. That, I think, is the wrong way to go. And people in this country, people in Scotland, have a big choice to make. Are we going to allow our future to be dominated and decided by the Trump-Johnson-Farage axis, or are we going to choose something much better? for the future of our country, of this generation and those who come after us. You can't defend those comments. You don't bring about nuclear disarmament by spending £200 billion on new nuclear weapons. I would never press the nuclear button because it would kill tens of millions of people. I think nuclear weapons are a huge waste of money that would be better spent on health and other public services, but I think they're immoral. We don't countenance countries using chemical weapons any longer. Why on earth should we countenance weapons that could wipe out swathes of civilisation? They are morally wrong and it's time to get rid of them. Well, to be candid, my party's not going to be running the Treasury after this election, but we may well hold the balance of power. And therefore, we will be pressing for more investment in the National Health Service, an end to welfare cuts and austerity, more funding to tackle climate change. And we would bring to bear on that our experience in government in Scotland, where we have already reformed the income tax system. I, in Scotland, pay more tax as a high-paid person than I would do if I was living in England. But partly because of that, in Scotland, if you're a student, you don't pay tuition fees. If you're an older person, you don't have to pay for your personal care, we already have free prescriptions. So maybe it's a bit of that value-based approach to spending that we need to see right across the UK. Thank you. Joyce Winston? OK, Nicola Sturgeon and then Richard Bennett. Well, I'm still a bit taken aback hearing Nigel Farage accuse other people of lying about spending. Am I the only one who remembers the £350 million lie on the side of... The NHS will have £350 million a week more because of Brexit. I've just done it. The NHS will have £350 million more a week. I didn't say that. But there is a need for honesty here. Going back to Scotland, where I'm obviously the First Minister, we have cut taxes for those at the bottom, but we've asked those at the top of the income scale to pay a bit more. That's how we manage to protect public services. A bit of honesty and a bit of those values into this debate in the rest of the UK I think is long, long overdue. And incidentally, we reformed the income tax, we did what people conventionally would have said would never work for a political party, and we've proved that wrong. It does not say that. It says continue... The Resolution Foundation. said that the Tory manifesto could take levels of child poverty in the UK to record levels. How can you defend that? Already 4 million children living in poverty. It's rising despite what the Prime Minister said falsely this morning. Your policies will take more children into poverty. How can you stand here and defend a manifesto that would do that? Let me just bring in Nicola Sturgeon. Well, health services everywhere are under real pressure. That's true in Scotland. It's true right across the UK. Firstly, we've got to invest more in Scotland. We spend £136 more per head than elsewhere in the UK. We've got to have reform. We've already integrated health and social care. And we've got to keep recruiting professionals into the health service. One of the debates in England is about bringing back the nurse bursary. In Scotland we never abolished the nurse bursary in the first place, so that's why we've got 2,500 more nurses today than we had previously. So investment and reform and support for the health service is absolutely crucial. Well, some of these issues are the responsibility of my government in Scotland. So, on a cross-party basis in Scotland, we've already introduced and protected free personal care to help with the social care crisis. Just a few weeks ago, again, on a cross-party basis, we passed the most ambitious climate change targets anywhere in the world, and we're already making progress to meet those, although there's much, much more to be done, and it involves all of us working together. So, I will always try to work with others the political divide to make progress on these big issues that are so important, particularly for the next generation. But if we are to make progress on these issues, I think the priority in this election is to vote in a way that ensures that it is not Boris Johnson and Nigel Farage running the show as of the 13th of December, because they are the roadblock to the progress on so much of this. Thank you. You need a just transition, I agree with that. But offshore wind right now is about half the cost of nuclear power, and yet these two parties here are still investing in more expensive nuclear power. That doesn't make sense. I think working together is a really good thing, but you've got to be working together for the right things. So I don't think it was right for Joe Swinson and the Liberal Democrats to be in coalition with David Cameron's Conservatives and impose austerity and welfare cuts and vote for things like the bedroom tax. So let's work together to do the right things Will you restore support for onshore wind if the Tories win this election? I can stand here as the leader of a government that's already introduced free personal care. It can be done. Free personal care has existed in Scotland now for more than a decade. My government's just extended it to those under 65. These other parties here, they need to stop talking about it, and they need to get on and doing it. And to be positive, I'm happy to answer them now. Boris Johnson's Tories are wholly unfit for office. They prove that with every day that passes. But unless people vote to keep them out, we could be stuck with them and their policies for years to come, with damaging consequences for our economy and our public services, especially the NHS. So if you care about our health service and public services, it is vital that you make your voice heard by voting against Boris Johnson's Tories and for a better future. In Scotland, the SNP is the challenger in every Tory-held seat. So a vote for the SNP is a vote to lock Boris Johnson out of Downing Street, provide an escape route from the mess of Brexit and put Scotland's future into Scotland's hands so that we can build a better and a fairer country.
deb_2019_12_01.QUESTIONER_0: Before we start, each has precisely one minute to make an opening statement. They drew lots to decide the order, and we start this evening with Sian Berry, co-leader of the Green Party. Nigel Farage. Nicola Sturgeon. Richard Bergen. Thank you all very much indeed for your opening statements. Well, tonight's format is straightforward. Over 1,000 questions were sent in by ITV viewers, and some of the questioners are here with us this evening in the studio. I'll also read out some of the other questions we've had in during the course of the debate. Also here, the people who will listen to tonight's debate. Some of them have voted for these parties behind me in the past and are thinking of doing so again, and some are undecided. All are very welcome here this evening. Now, because of the tragic events in London on Friday, which saw two people killed in a terrorist attack, we've got a special question first related to security, which came in over the weekend from Darren Harris from Burnley. Thank you very much indeed, Darren. How is it that a convicted terrorist has been let out after only serving half of their sentence? It is, of course, the priority of every government to keep its people safe. OK, Richard Burgon, can I come to you to answer some of the points that have been raised there? We can't do security on the cheap. So the Conservative government have got this wrong in your view? OK, Rishi Sunak, respond to that point. On this point that Boris Johnson, in some people's view, politicised the subject this morning with the way he spoke about the tragedy, Ritchie Sunak, how do you respond directly to that point? Thank you very much indeed. Thank you very much indeed on that. We're going to move away from the dreadful events on London Bridge on Friday. And move to our main part of the debate this evening, just a brief reminder of the rules to which all the parties have agreed. After each question is put, each leader or party representative has a chance to respond with a 45 second answer and the timing needs to be respected. We'll then open it up for free-flowing debate. Our first question is the backdrop to the whole election and that of course is Brexit and it comes from Manitha Aravind who's a student from Bolton. Manitha. Thank you, Namitha. Can you honestly say your Brexit policy and your leadership will bring this deeply divided nation and union back together? Rishi Sunak. 45 seconds for each reply. Adam Price next. Thank you. Just a brief reminder of the question before we come to Richard Burgon. Can you honestly say your Brexit policy and your leadership can bring this deeply divided nation back together? Thank you very much, all of you. First, come to Richard Burgon. Will another referendum really bring this country back together, in your view? Just very briefly, we know that your leader wants to remain neutral on this issue. Where are you on this, Richard Burgon? Where will you campaign? Richard Burgon. You talked about a cliff edge at the end of next year. OK, answer that specific point, and then I'll come to both Sean Berry and Adam Frost. Adam Price. Sian Berry, you've been very keen to get in on this. All right. Thank you very much, all of you. Let's just do a quick reminder of the question. It was about, honestly, whether your Brexit policy, your leadership, will bring this deeply divided nation and union back together. Nigel Farage, if I can come to you. What do you think, then, of the Conservatives' proposed deal? You called it, at the beginning of the evening, a half-in, half-out fudge. Thank you, gentlemen. Well, I'm just going to ask Rishi Sunak to address that direct question. OK. Just briefly, Adam Price, and I want to come back to a different part of the question. OK, let's just return to the question because there's a very important point raised within it about Brexit policy and whether your leadership could bring this nation back together and indeed the union back together. It is on very many people's minds ahead of this election. Richard Burgon, there are those that would say by offering potentially a second independence referendum, which is what Nicola Sturgeon would request in return for some support if you needed it in government, how committed are you to the union? Let's allow Rishi Sunak to respond to that issue about Northern Ireland. OK, thank you, Nicola Sturgeon. We've also had a question from Gary in St Andrews on a subject of court that is very much part of this, and it is on the subject of immigration. Gary asks, many EU migrants work in the coffee shops and care homes of our nation. Will they still be welcome after we have left the EU? Rishi Sunak. It's a cruel system. Very briefly, will we get freedom of movement under a Labour government? Thank you very much indeed and we'll just draw this section of our debate to a close and we know that it is also the backdrop to very many other questions too, we'll have some more on it in a moment. It's very important. It will also come into some of the issues that we'll discuss very shortly. Thank you very much indeed for that. After the break, as President Trump is about to arrive in the UK, we'll be discussing the special relationship and Britain's place in the world. Welcome back to the ITV election debate. In a moment, our next question, but remember, you can join the debate at home by using the hashtag ITVdebate. A reminder that after each question, the seven politicians will have 45 seconds to answer it, and I'm hoping that they will stick to time. We've got plenty of time to debate one another after those. Now it is time for our third question on foreign affairs, and it comes from Lloyd Myatt from Stoke-on-Trent. Lloyd Myatt, thank you for that. A question about the special relationship. First off, Jo Swinson. Thank you. Sean Barry. Adam Price. Thank you. Richard Bergen. Nicola Sturgeon. So the question went to the heart of whether the special relationship should become more or less special. Nigel Farage, there are clear and very vocal concerns about the character of Donald Trump which have been raised by some on this platform. Jo Swinson initially raised them in her opening statement. How do you respond directly to that and are you comfortable being his friend? We're going to talk about that very shortly, but Joe Swinson wanted to come in on that. Thank you. I'm going to just bring in Adam Price, and then I'll bring in Richard Berger to respond. Let's respond directly to Adam Price. OK, can I... Just a moment, Jo Swinson, because I do need to bring in Richard Burgon on this. And a particular question about your leader's relationship with the President of the United States. Last time, when he visited the United Kingdom, he declined an invitation to attend a state dinner with Donald Trump. Would you attend a dinner with Donald Trump? Thank you, Richard Burgon. I just want to bring in another question that pertains to this issue from David in Stoke-on-Trent, has asked a question about Trident. Should we keep our nuclear deterrent and should we ever use it? Jo Swinson. Richard Bargain. OK, and just very briefly before we move on to our next question, against Trident, both Adam Price and Sean Berry. Thank you, Sian Berry. Let's move on now to another question, and this one is from James Lever. Thank you, James. It's a question about your spending plans and who is ultimately going to be worst off as a result. And to respond first, Richard Burgon. Thank you, Adam Price. Thank you. Nigel Farage. Thank you, Nigel Farage. Nicola Sturgeon. Thank you Jo Swinson. Rishi Sunak. It's what we need. OK, let's just have a reminder of the question. James works in the manufacturing industry. He's listened carefully to your spending promises, wants to know who's paying for all of these promises and who will be ultimately worse off as a result. Jo Swinson, it looked like you wanted to come in. Well, I mean, I just wanted to disagree. OK, on that point about credible spending plans, the IFS, the think tank, has looked at both Labour and Conservative proposals and said neither are credible. How do you respond to that, Richard Burgon? Nigel Farage had his head in his hands at that point. It's a must-have in response. OK, and of course there's devolved areas of spending in both Wales and Scotland, but I must just turn to Richard Burgon on some of the points that were raised by Rishi Sunak, because part of this question is very important. James wants to know who is paying for all of these promises. Rishi Sunak highlighted areas like marriage tax allowance and taxing on dividends. Just respond to that point. I just want to bring in another question on this point because this is also about spending priorities and then I'll come to you Adam Price. A very simple question from Lee who sent this in from Chorley. How do we fix our NHS? It's a devolved issue of course in Wales and Scotland. Adam Price. Thank you, Sian Barry. Yes, but on this point of the four-day week in the NHS, have you costed that in Labour? It has been one of those... Rishi Sunak? Thank you very much, all of our candidates on that issue. It is time now to take just another little breather. Don't go away, because in the final part of the programme, we'll be asking if these parties could possibly put their differences to one side and work together to solve some of the biggest issues facing this country. We'll be back very shortly. Welcome back to our final part of the ITV election debate. Debating tonight, Lib Dem leader Joe Swinson, Labour's Richard Burgon, Green Party leader Sean Berry, SNP leader Nicola Sturgeon, Nigel Farage, the Brexit Party leader, Conservative Rishi Sunak and Adam Price, the leader of Plaid Cymru. It is now time for our final question, and all the politicians will get the opportunity to respond, but they have to stick, of course, their 45 seconds. This question comes to us now from Carly from Airdrie. Thank you, Carly. So big issues there, climate change, social care. Can you work together? Adam Price. Thank you. Richard Burgon. Nicola Sturgeon. Okay, that's 45 seconds. We need to let Jo Swinson have her response first. OK, thank you. Let's pick up then with climate change. Sian Barry, you were very keen to get back to Nigel Farage. But on the issue of climate change then you were pointing to the gentleman either side of you, Ritchie Sooner. I want to touch on social care very quickly but a quick response to that please. I want to move on to a different question that was an element of Carly's question, that is about social care. John from Milton Keynes has been in touch. My wife has Alzheimer's and so far her care has cost over £105,000. Why hasn't tackling the crisis in adult social care been a bigger priority and will that honestly change on December the 13th? What is your plan, Rishi Sunak? Yes, and there has been an observation made by all the stakeholders in the social care industry that there has never been a government in the last 20 years that has put forward a workable proposal. Adam Price. Why isn't it possible, Richard Burgess? Thank you. We just need to be brief. Nigel Farage, and then a closing thought. It's extraordinary isn't it after nearly two hours that we're nearly out of time but actually on this point in the question about people working together it would be nice to offer all of us witnessing this election some hope that actually things could shift in politics and there could be some better ways of cross-party cooperation. What hope could you offer for us? OK, just on that, would you all here commit yourselves to working together on this issue? Can I say that actually another cross-party commission isn't needed? Thank you very much indeed, all of you, for taking part in those debates on these different subjects. It's been a pretty lively debate. And now it is time to have our closing statements from our politicians this evening. And first, we go to Nigel Farage. Thank you. Wish you good luck. Richard Burgon, thank you. Adam Price. Sian Berry of the Green Party, thank you. Jo Swinson. Thank you, Jo Swinson. Nicola Sturgeon. And that was our final closing statement and I must say a great thanks to all of the candidates on our platform this evening from Richard Bergen, Nicola Sturgeon, Rishi Sunak, Nigel Farage, Adam Price, Sian Berry and Jo Swinson. Thank you all very much indeed for taking part this evening.
