No. When Scotland is not playing, will you support England in the Euros? No. Will Donald Trump be welcome in Scotland if he wins the election? You just watched the head-to-head debate between Keir Starmer and Rishi Sunak. Who do you think won? OK. And you've made very, very clear that you think the worst thing for Scotland is a Conservative government. won't Scottish voters think, if we don't want a Conservative government, we need to vote Labour? They need to win 123 extra seats. If they can win 25 seats in Scotland, it's going to make their path easier. Well, you might try and reject this because of the polling. But if there is a hung parliament, that's a position in which you would have a very strong part. Would you do a deal with Keir Starmer? And if so, what would you demand? So the Labour Party wouldn't give you that. Is there something else that you would agree to do a deal over? Your deficit would require cutting if you were going to do that. That would be austerity, wouldn't it? But just on that point on the single market. And that is a list of policies that you wouldn't get in that situation, I think. And one of them you talked about was rejoining the single market. The European Union has very strict rules. Scotland's deficit is higher than the rules that would allow you to rejoin the EU, for example. Would you have to bring in pretty dire cuts to get to that position? You have talked about other issues and the importance of other issues than independence, than the EU. A recent YouGov poll suggested around six out of 10 people in Scotland have little or no confidence that you'll make the right decisions on the economy, public services, police, climate change. What has led to this? catastrophic loss in trust and what can you do to regain that trust? Do you accept that there has been a catastrophic loss in trust? Just one thing that really has damaged trust is the fact that you're going to have to hand back 450 million pounds of EU money that you failed to spend on key economic and anti-poverty projects. I mean would you apologise to voters for this? much higher percentage in Scotland than in Wales, England or Northern Ireland. Well, I think that people feel upset about how and if that money will be spent. And just finally, let me ask you about that football decision. You didn't hesitate there. Why do you really not want to support England in other games? Apparently, England and Scotland wouldn't even meet till the final. I look forward to that Scottish final without England. Stephen Flynn, thank you so much. Coming up, we'll speak to Richard Tice, the new chair of Reform UK, and Green Party co-leader Adrian Ramsey. Can they persuade you to lend them your vote? All that after this short break. Welcome back to the ITV election interviews. Well, we've already heard from the SNP and the Lib Dems. The Green Party is coming up, but now it's time for Richard Tice, the new chair of Reform UK. Let's take a quick look at their campaign promises. Richard Tice is chair of Reform UK, the rebranded Brexit party hoping to cause a major political upset this election. And in the most dramatic moment of the campaign so far, Nigel Farage took the reins of the party from Tice, vowing to lead a political revolt and make reform the official opposition in the UK. They say a vote for them is a common sense choice, and they have four main aims. Lower taxes, what they call net zero migration, cheaper energy and no waiting lists for the NHS. Reform gained its first MP in March after Lee Anderson, the former Tory deputy chairman, defected. Richard Tice says he wants to smash and destroy the Tories on July 4th and with the party set to field a candidate in every seat across England, Wales and Scotland, will it hurt the Conservative vote? And joining me now, the man himself, Richard Tice, Chair of Reform UK. Welcome, Richard. Good evening. Let's kick off with policy. Give me your pitch in a minute. And I'll come to those policies. But let me just start with you. Yesterday, you were leader of Reform UK. And you were doing well. The party was 12% in the polls, above the Lib Dems, above the Greens. Who told you you were out as leader? What's he got that you haven't then? And who calls the shots? I mean, do you march to Nigel Farage's tune? It's a job share? Who's the bigger figure? OK, so reform now led by a job share. You want net zero immigration. We have massive vacancies in the NHS and in the care sector. What would you do about them? That still leaves a gap in the meantime. I mean, you're talking about stuff that costs money, and yet you're talking about tens of billions of pounds in tax cuts. Well, let me just look at some of your costings. So you say you'll make £91 billion by cutting government waste. Tell me about government waste. How much are you going to make? It says £91 billion on your website. So how much are you going to shrink the civil service? How much money will you make from that? The entire civil service, that entire civil service salary is £15 billion a year. Do you realise there are six... Are you going to sack everyone? You are still talking about... not enough cuts to match the massive tax cuts that you're talking about. And you mentioned net zero. Let me ask you about net zero, because in order for net zero to save money, in the way you're saying it's going to, you have to assume that climate change isn't going to cost anything. Do you believe in man-made climate change? In man-made climate change? Every single person, every single kind of organisation that's locked into the cost of net zero says there is a massive cost if we don't act. No one else agrees with your £30 billion cost. And just on this before you go on, you say you believe in man-made climate change. On your own website, it basically says this isn't man-made, but the globe has been warming and cooling over many years. So you don't believe that man-made climate change is warming the planet? OK, let's talk about the debate. In 2021, a study came out that said 99% of thousands and thousands of studies on climate change say human activity is causing climate change. Why do you think you know better than the world scientists on this issue? But you don't basically think man-made is driving it? Let me do a quick fire on you. If you were to put money on it, how many seats do you think Reform UK are going to win? One word, how many? Would you be OK with Nigel Farage, if elected, going to the US to help Donald Trump get elected? Did you watch the head-to-head? And one of them, Keir Starmer. You talk a lot about, in your literature, you talk about Starmageddon. You basically think he is the most dangerous thing that can happen in Britain. So why are you trying to help him get elected? But you accept that Reform UK are doing well. is going to help Keir Starmer get elected. Let me ask you something your leader recently said. Nigel Farage said many young Muslims don't share his values. Do you know many young Muslims? Do you agree with Nigel Farage? Lots of people who are protesting are watching the scenes unfolding in Gaza and they find it heartbreaking. Do you find what you're seeing in Gaza heartbreaking? If you had the power, what would you be saying to Benjamin Netanyahu about this right now? What would you say to Israel about the situation in Gaza? Would you be asking them to slow down? Richard Tice, thank you very much. Next up, we have the Green Party and their co-leader, Adrian Ramsey. Let's find out a little bit more about the party and its promises. Why have one party leader when you can have two? Well, the Greens co-leaders Carla Denyer and Adrian Ramsey would agree. Elected in 2021, the pair have promised to bring a greater degree of professionalism to the party's organisation and campaigning. For the very first time, the Green Party is aiming to field a candidate at every count in England and Wales, but their focus is very much on four seats where they think they have the best chance. They say, therefore a fairer and greener country and predictably have a raft of environmental pledges. These include better insulated homes and cleaner rivers. But in economic policy they also want a pay ratio that makes sure the highest paid individuals in an organisation cannot earn more than 10 times the lowest. With the biggest parties accused of environmental policy U-turns, could the Greens capitalise and finally make gains at Westminster? Adrian Ramsey, co-leader of the Green Party, is here. Adrian, welcome. Let's start with your one-minute pitch. That's your time up. I mean, you are the Green Party. The environment is the number one issue. Friends of the Earth say they are sceptical about your aim to eliminate all emissions in 10 years. Shouldn't you just be realistic about what is achievable? But they said they're sceptical about being able to eliminate emissions within 10 years. Investment, even a group like Friends of the Earth thinks 10 years is probably too ambitious. And you do have to take people with you. I mean, you mentioned the cost of living crisis in your opening pitch. Think about heat pumps, for example. Yes, there are grants, but it's expensive for people to swap out heat pumps. You have to take people with you. And I'm going to come to your costings and all of this, but isn't there also a question of influence here? Like you mentioned, both the Conservatives and Labour have been accused of watering down environmental pledges. But you can't stop that happening. I mean, if I think about the Greens in Scotland or the Greens in Germany, they've had massive influence in government. You're gunning for four seats. Where's your ambition? I think that you're not going to be able to influence it because you're gunning for four seats. Whilst the Greens are a huge force in other countries. OK, I'm going to talk to you about that, but let's just do this quickfire section. I mean, you talk quite a lot in your policies about liberalising access to illegal drugs. Have you ever taken drugs? Would you press the nuclear button? No. What is the least green thing you do? Who do you think won tonight's head-to-head? Let me ask you about Gaza. We can't go into specifics about constituencies here, but you have been accused of circulating letters that look like they're from concerned neighbours saying, oh, I really like what the Green Party do, but actually they're your election leaflets. Tiny, tiny writing. It looked like it was from a neighbour. I mean, the Jewish Chronicle said it was stirring up division. So you're sorry if the Jewish Chronicle felt that that was stirring up division? Let's talk about costings. You've got quite a lot of big policies and I wondered if you thought about how much they cost. I mean how much do you think it costs to provide free school meals to all children, primary and secondary children in England? I'll tell you what the Institute for Fiscal Studies, because I asked them, £2.5 billion. We'll talk about the raising the money in a minute, but you say everyone should get access to an NHS dentist. Absolutely. Have you been to a dentist lately? I mean, that is an entire overhaul of the system. How much would that cost? When you speak to experts and they say, trying to tax the top 1% on their own is really difficult. People change their behaviour. They are pretty good at avoiding tax. You're not going to raise enough from that. Cancel all road building schemes. Well, actually, I mean, the IFS would say these are difficult taxes to raise, the amounts that you're talking about. I mean, there's things you talk about. You say you're going to scrap all road building schemes. I mean, how are you going to get around in your car? Just a couple more. Do you think the UK accepts enough refugees? Do you think we accept enough refugees? And as one of the richest countries in the world... You're not going to put a number on it but essentially you think that we should increase the numbers overall. Aidan Ramsey, thank you very much for your time. And there we have it. You've heard what the parties have to offer. It is now over to you to decide who gets your vote.
deb_2024_06_04_01.QUESTIONER_1: we could get the fair deal that people so deserve.
deb_2024_06_04_01.QUESTIONER_2: We are for Scotland. We will always put Scotland first.
deb_2024_06_04_01.QUESTIONER_3: The right thing for the UK to go for growth, to go for higher wages, is actually to freeze immigration.
deb_2024_06_04_01.QUESTIONER_4: So the Green Party is proposing practical policies that will help our economy and help the environment.

deb_2024_06_07.MORDAUNT: What happened was completely wrong and the Prime Minister has rightly apologised for that, apologised to veterans but also to all of us because he was representing all of us. I'm from Portsmouth, I've also been Defence Secretary and my wish at the end of this week is that all of our veterans feel completely treasured. And I'm hoping tonight to convince you of some things that are important to them, important to their legacy. And I couldn't do that if I wasn't straight with you on that issue. I didn't go to D-Day. I think what happened was very wrong. I think the prime minister has apologized for that. But what I also think is important is we honor their legacy. They fought for our freedom. Unless we are spending the right amount on defense, we can't honor that legacy. The baseline for our equipment program is 2.5 percent. If you're not committed to spending that over the period it needs to be spent, you are not funding defense. And I have to just mention also The cornerstone of our defence is our nuclear deterrent. And you need more than submarines, sailors and warheads to deliver that. You need credibility. And it's too late for Labour on credibility. Angela Rayner voted recently, along with the guy that wants to be your Foreign Secretary and half the Labour frontbench, to end our nuclear deterrent. If you are doubting that they would use that force, and she might be prime minister in four or five years' time, if you are doubting that she or David Lammy or Keir Starmer would use that, imagine what Putin is thinking. Without credibility, we become a target. If we become a target, you are less safe. It's too late for this generation of Labour politicians. That credibility is shot do not vote these people in. You stand next to me and have your photograph taken. If your foe does not believe that you will use these weapons, the deterrent is gone. And that's the position you're in. Look, I think what happened was very wrong and I'm glad the Prime Minister has said that and he's apologised. He's apologised to veterans, but I think he also has apologised to everyone because he was there representing us. And what I hope is that our veterans will feel treasured and their families will feel treasured at the end of this week. I don't want this issue to become a political football because I think that does them a disservice. Well, it already is. He's apologised. Let's get Penny Morton to respond to that. So, Manchester's second only to Portsmouth, and we're just about to have both a dental school and a medical school very shortly, and good luck with your studies. Your future colleagues are currently dealing with an increase in NHS workload of about 43%. That's the legacy of the pandemic that we have to deal with. So there are many things we need to do, but there are two really important things. We have to keep the budget strong. You need a strong economy to do that, and public spending is going to go up. It will go up, and for the NHS, very substantially. The only people on this platform who have ever cut the NHS budget are Labour in Wales. They've cut it four times, and that's why their waiting lists are four times longer than they are in England. Not only can you not afford a Labour government, the NHS can't afford it either. But the second thing we also need to do is increase the number of health care professionals we have. We have been doing that. We have 70,000 more nurses, for example. But we need to retain people in the service. We've got to take the stress off people, but we also have to remove barriers to them continuing in service. OK, you do acknowledge, don't you, that the pandemic is not completely part of waiting lists? Labour's plan to tax your future pension, senior nurses and doctors, is going to get health care professionals to leave the service, that is going to lead to more waiting lists. Even Liz Truss, on her worst day, still recognised that we need a nuclear deterrent in this country, Angela, so she's still got the edge on you. And so do I. But your songs do not add up. Well, look, this might be an immigration election, but it is definitely going to be a cost-of-living election. And one thing that we can do to help the gentleman who asked the question is about ensuring that you keep more of your money. We will cut your taxes. Labour will put your taxes up. Was that the question? If you're having more agency, being able to get onto the property ladder, that is the key in this general election. So immigration is too high. And what we need to do is have a balance between some of the issues that have been raised already. The best way to do this is to get Parliament, your Parliament, elected by you to have an annual cap. What that will do is take into account the economic needs, the needs of the workforce, but also the pressures that immigration puts on communities, as the question made very clear. That will be informed by experts, but it will be Parliament that decides. And because the numbers are too high, over the next few years, you can see it will come down. That is a clear plan, a clear guarantee. We've already ended free movement. We've done many things. That is the logical next step. That's what you get with us. Unlike Labour, who have no plan, they have no target, they have no clue, they haven't done any of the work on the workforce. They've had 14 years to work this out. They have nothing to offer you. Controlled numbers and a cap under us, or uncontrolled immigration under the Labour Party. How have you got the front to say that? Your leader campaigned for free movement. Keir Starmer has spent most of his life campaigning for free movement. These guys are not going to control immigration. So this is what I mean. This is what I mean when I say this election is about the cost of living. And what you have been going through and many other people have been going through, it's exhausting and we need to bring it to an end. We have supported people through some very difficult times. That has been expensive. But we are now in the recovery. The economy is doing much better. We're outperforming America on growth. We're outperforming Europe on inflation. Those are your achievements. But inequality continues to get worse. The only way you're going to keep the recovery going is to give you more money in your pockets. That is why this election must be about us cutting your taxes. We have already started doing that and you have already heard some announcements. You'll see more in our manifesto next week. We have got to cut people's taxes and we have got to alleviate burdens on business. Angela Rayner and the Labour Party, Keir Starmer confirmed this earlier this week, they are going to put up your taxes. No it won't. By 2,000 pounds. That's a lie. By 2,000 pounds per working household. And we've hated putting taxes up. We have now been cutting taxes. We will continue to cut taxes. The country can afford it. 2,000 pounds. We've heard overnight about 12 new taxes that Labour are going to bring in. It's not rubbish. That's rubbish. And our exports are at a record high. Well... LAUGHTER This is why the Prime Minister made the very clear pledges that he did, and you can measure the progress being made against them. We are making progress in this country. We have been through some very hard times, but the recovery is there. What we must ensure is that that is not choked off. And we must ensure that your taxes are being lowered. That is the pledge that we make to you. And that is what we have been doing with national insurance cuts that we have seen this year. That is what you need. It's not just your taxes that I'm worried about. I'm worried about my constituents being able to afford a Labour government. Angela mentioned GB Energy. Do you know what the GB stands for? It stands for Giant Bills. And more bills are coming with the net zero plans that Labour have. That is the most important thing because everything depends on you having that disposable income. And that is why tax is at the heart of this. The pledges that we are making to you on cutting your taxes and what Labour will not tell you about the taxes they are going to be putting up. Look, we have had some very difficult times. We have had to spend a lot of money on things like furlough. That was the right thing to do, to keep jobs and livelihoods going, not just hand out welfare payments. The pandemic has been expensive and we've also had terrible global shocks. We've had to step in and pay people's energy bills. But we are coming through that, thanks to your efforts. What you need now is disposable income in your pockets. You need taxes to be cut. We have announced already we're making cuts on your taxes regarding your home, your pension, work. We need to continue to do that. If you get us, we will reduce your taxes. That's in our DNA as conservatives. That's not what is in Labour's DNA. They always put up taxes. They're going to put yours up by £2,000. They're going to increase your energy bills, and you're going to get a whole lot of costs with their net zero plans. My constituents cannot afford that. Carla, Carla, Penny Morden, thank you. Carla Denier, you're next. Penny Mordaunt, done well, both should help the other. What is critical, though, is if you want to reach net zero by 2050, you have to do it with people. You have to ensure that people can afford to get there. If you don't do that, you will fail to reach your targets. We are going to do this at a pace that people can afford. 2050 sounds a long way off. And in this election, you're looking at the next five years. At the end of this parliament, if they get in, you will not be able to buy a petrol car. If your boiler goes, you'll have to spend tens of thousands of pounds on a new heat pump, other things that need to be done to your home to convert it. My constituents cannot afford that. You heard about GB Energy. You heard about GB Energy. This is an energy company that your money is going to be put into that doesn't produce any energy at all. It doesn't produce a drop of energy. Oil and gas, this is the insanity of Labour's policy. They are expecting a load of people to invest in oil and gas, at the same time saying they're not going to expand the sector. So they're not going to get the money in. And the final thing, if you do this too fast, All you do is destroy our supply chain. We should be building supply chains in this country to produce batteries, to do all sorts of things. Let's hear the response. You're flogging an energy company to these guys. You're expecting them to put their money in. Are your green policies going to be in your manifesto? Because you currently can't afford to pay for them. That's where that £2,000 comes from that you're going to have to pay. And I'm going to turn to... And I'm going to turn to the next question at this point. Unnecessary cost. Well, over the last 14 years, if you leave out computer crime and online fraud, we have more than halved crime, 55%. And we have now a record number of police. Violent crime is down by 44%, but we have hotspots. and knife crime in particular, London is top of the list. We have put more police on the streets. We have met our manifesto commitment of 20,000 more police officers. We have record numbers. And where you have conservative police crime commissioners, like my area in Hampshire, you have local bodies on the beach. Absolutely. That's a Labour police crime commissioner as well. if you are in, but he wasn't responsible for police. There was a police crime commissioner and it was Labour. If you live in a Labour area, you are 40% more likely to be a victim of crime. That is a shocking statistic. We need more police and we need police who are embedded in communities that can follow up with people. If kids are doing stuff, they follow up with the school, they follow up with the parents. That's what conservative police crime commissioners are doing. In Hampshire, we've got 600 extra officers. Every single one of my wards has a named Bobby. People know, build up intelligence. That is how we get crime down, and we have got it down by 55%. That's not true. Penny Mordaunt, we've come through tough times and now there is a choice to be made. You can choose Angela Rayner and Keir Starmer and get higher taxes, higher bills and have your pension raided. or you can stick with us and the plan that is working. We will cut your taxes. We will protect your pension. And we will defend this nation. For a more secure future, vote Conservative.
deb_2024_06_07.FARAGE: Well, Frances, you're right to ask the question. We're spending less on defence than we were in the last year of the Labour government, pro rata. The army has shrunk from 100,000 to 72,000. Recruitment is catastrophic. So rather than having a weird concept of national service, where 30,000 youngsters would do a year, we need to recruit 30,000 people into the army full-time. And yes, the veterans point's been made already. Respect our veterans, including those with the average age of 100 who were deserted by the Prime Minister in Normandy yesterday, which I think was a complete and utter disgrace and shows us we actually have a very unpatriotic Prime Minister. It was dreadful. No, hang on a second. I raised £100,000. I raised £100,000 for the London Taxi Charity to send veterans back to Normandy. That's why I was there. Thank you. It already is because the veterans themselves are speaking out saying he's let the country down. And to say that it's a mistake, if his instinct was the same as the British people, he would never have contemplated for a moment not being there for the big international ceremony. And it shows how disconnected he is with the people of this country. Whether it's Labour-run Wales or SNP-run Scotland or Conservative-run England, we all know that whilst you can get great care, the NHS model isn't working. You can't get GP appointments, things that we've all grown up taking for granted. And they can argue about, it's all money. I'm going to spend more money. You're going to spend more money. It doesn't work. Just a few years ago, we were spending 7, 7.5% of the national cake on the NHS. We're now spending over 11% of the national cake on the NHS. A fully functioning NHS? Well, I'm going to answer it. Because the more money we spend, the more money we spend, the less delivery we get. Which means the model is wrong. The model through which we fund health is wrong. A national treasure. You want to privatise it. That is what you want to do. And you in Scotland, of all places, I mean, look, the NHS is very badly run. Let's change the funding model. Let's change the model. Now, the NHS, it isn't funded through national insurance. That's a nonsense. It goes into the pot. There are countries right next door to us. There's one country, France, that has a very, very different way of funding the NHS. It's rather like those that can afford, through their taxes, pay into an insurance scheme. Those that can't afford it don't pay in so it's for the mutual benefit of everybody and it's managed as if it was a private company and their returns on stroke, heart and cancer are better than ours for exactly the same sum of money. The King's Foundation. We've got a fully functioning NHS. The King's Foundation. If we spend our money properly. Which is a respectable. And we're not spending our money properly and that's the No, no, no, no. As far as it works, let's learn from it. Nigel Farage. I'm on a platform tonight with six other people whose parties have been wholly unconcerned with the issue of immigration. They think that to even question it somehow makes you a bad person. Let's get back to Lorenzo's question. Let's deal with some logic, shall we? From the end of the 1940s... Probably a novelty for you. ..until Mr Blair got to power, listen to the numbers. You probably don't know them. For 50 years, net migration into Britain was 30,000 to 40,000 a year. Then Tony Blair gets in, decides to open the doors. We get net migration for the period of the Labour government of 2.7 million people come in that period. Then we get the Conservatives elected in 2010, 2015, 2017, telling us they'll reduce net migration to tens of thousands a year, 4.3 million the Conservatives have let in. Most of those that come in are not directly productive members of the economy. Most of those that come in are actually dependents. And Lorenzo, the truth of it is, this ought to be the immigration election. Because whether we talk about housing, whether we talk about the fact that rents are up between 20% and 30% in most of the country in the last four years, whether we talk about the roads, whether we talk about infrastructure, we are living through a population crisis, an increase of 10 million people since Mr Blair came to power and frankly it's making us poorer, it is diminishing our quality of life and it's nothing to do, nothing to do with race or any of those issues, it's to do with actually putting the interests of our communities first. We need to get net migration and that means by the way people come and people go. We need to get net migration down to an even figure for the next few years and maybe then we can hope to catch up with housing, with health and many of those things. No, about 50% of those that come are dependents. whether they're coming with work visas... ..family reunions. We've even had, under the Conservatives, students coming and bringing their mum. I mean, the whole thing's been unbelievable. Don't buy this. I have to say, I think a lot of working people like you feel quite resentful. The harder you work, the more taxes you pay, the less disposable income you have. And yet there are increasingly people opting just not to work. And that number has rocketed over the course of the last few years. So I understand how you feel. Your energy bills are too expensive. They have been for the last 20 years. Why? because we load money, we load tax onto your energy bill to give to wind farm companies. It's kind of the poorer in society giving to the richer in society. Rents, cost of housing, too high. That links back to the previous question. The population explosion has made housing, whether you buy them or rents, too expensive. Reducing net migration to zero would ease that. Getting rid of those subsidies on your energy bills would ease that. But the big one's taxes. Even during Tony Blair's time, the top rate of tax was 40p in this country, and it was paid by 1.5 million people. By the end of 2027, 8 million people will be paying 40p tax. So we're dragging more and more people doing middle-income jobs into higher and higher taxation. And that's why life is so tough. And to hear Penny Malden, whose government have put the tax burden up to the highest in this country since 1948, pretending they're a tax-cutting party, frankly, it is dishonesty on a breathtaking scale. Well, Linda, I have to say, I think this has been one of the worst general election campaigns so far between the two main parties I've ever seen in my lifetime. It isn't just that their leaders are dull, don't clearly... Well, very dull in the case of Labour. I mean, sort of Blair without the flair. I mean, the real leader of the Labour Party is here tonight on the stage. At least she's got some personality. We've got Rishi's slippery sunak, you know, he's fed all the lines to Penny Morton, don't believe any of it. I think it isn't just our public services that are broken, I think our political model is broken. The arguments that are going on on the other side of the room here, when the truth is, they don't really disagree on anything. fictitious numbers like £2,000, £3,000. And that's why reform has risen so quickly in the polls. There is a sense we need a revolt against this system. We need an electoral system where we get some proportional representation in Parliament. We need to break up the current model. If we get a new kind of politics in Britain, we might just get some real change. What we're being offered, frankly, are two parties promising the earth and nothing much will change, regardless of who gets in. Although, I think the breach of trust on manifestos with the Conservatives means they're probably sunk below the waterline. New politics, fresh start. We want a revolt from the British people. That's what I'm after. Well, it depends what those climate policies are. And at the moment we're pursuing completely unrealistic climate policies. I mean, the Labour pledge to decarbonise the grid by 2030 will not happen, just as the Conservative pledge to decarbonise the grid by 2035 will not happen. Not only is it impossible, it's actually unaffordable. So we have to be grown up and sensible. If we get new technologies that give us cheaper energy or better ways of manufacturing things, that's great. But what we're doing in this country is we're sacrificing economic growth. We'd be massacring British manufacturing. And we say to ourselves, isn't it marvellous? We've reduced carbon emissions more than any other Western country. But we haven't. All we've done is to export carbon emissions when our steel works go to India, or our car manufacture moves to China, or to Turkey, or wherever it is. We're living in a complete fool's paradise on all of this. I mentioned earlier to the lady at the back about the cost of living. You know, we've been chucking 20% on people's electricity bills now for 20 years. to pay for very inefficient wind energy. So what's really happening with net zero? What's happening with the attempt to deal with climate change? We could have well-paid jobs, secure jobs, and a welfare system that really acts as the safety net we need to catch people when they fall. A green economy would create jobs, well-paid jobs distributed all over the country, for example, on a nationwide home insulation program, which would bring down everyone's bills, help with the cost of living in the here and now, give us warmer, more comfortable homes, create hundreds of thousands of green jobs. and support local businesses. And the Greens would provide a real safety net, starting with a £15 minimum wage for all ages, removing the ageist cap on young working adults, an immediate uplift to universal credit, and, my final point, removing the cruel two-child benefit cap that both the Conservatives and Labour's party support, even though it holds 250,000 children down in poverty unnecessarily. Thank you, Paula Denyer. Thank you. Linda, I share your frustration. When I was younger, I had little to no faith that politics had the potential to make the country a better place. Now as several of the other panellists have already pointed out, as Labour drop pledge after pledge, policy after policy, slide further and further towards the Conservatives so that you can barely get a cigarette paper between them, the argument for voting for a bit of diversity in the House of Commons becomes ever stronger and in terms of answering your question Linda about parties keeping their promises, The Green Party, when I joined it in 2011, was a pretty small and young party, but we're coming of age. We have increased our number of councillors nearly five-fold over the last few local elections, and we're grateful to everyone who's put their trust in us. Well, Nigel's going to keep your fact checkers busy for a little while. Nigel Farage has been misleading you. I'm an engineer. I used to work in the renewable energy sector. Let her answer. One of my colleague's friends was one of those victims, so this strikes very close to home for me. Our manifesto will be out soon, but the broad points I can cover now. Youth centres are being closed. services that they need to support them. That's been all but wiped out in parts of our country. There's been cuts of 4,000, 4,500 PCSOs as well. And let me say about stop and search in particular. So where there is a suspicion of somebody, it can be used, but it has to be used in a targeted way based on evidence, not on profiling. We need to stand against it, we need to promote our economy, promote our public services and to do so by promoting migration. We know the damage that the Tories have caused to the economy as a result of Liz Truss's But I'm afraid the challenges facing the economy are much deeper than that. Firstly, there's austerity. 14 years of austerity. But there's also a conspiracy of silence on one other issue. And that's Brexit. Because Brexit has impacted the economy more than the COVID pandemic. I was talking about what I believe was right for our country. And that's who I am as a person. But what I was absolutely set about doing was making things that little bit easier. Well, look, let's go... And that's why inflation was a priority. Let's go through the pledges. What was it when you made the pledge? But we measure our debt according to... An independent body measures our debt. Let me be clear, Beth. I was never saying it would come down overnight. It's not even that expensive to lift, but both the Conservatives and Labour have ruled it out. Money in is less than money out. The government could choose policies that would fix this. It's choosing not to. So's the Labour Party. The Green Party thinks differently. Daisy Cooper, you wanted to come in. That's right. I've been a housing campaigner my whole life. It's something I'm very passionate about. And my God, do we have a housing crisis in this country. When I moved to Bristol about 15 years ago, I rented a one bed flat. The cost of a one bed flat in the 15 years since has doubled. And we can all agree that wages have not doubled in that time. And so, yes, the Green Party does have a plan. There is no one single bullet. There's no panacea. But there are a group of measures that we can do together. That includes creating more homes. I don't really feel like that answered my question. One in 30 people walking on the street out there has come in the last two years alone. The answer is we have to have net migration at zero. It means skilled workers can come, we can go work abroad, we have to have a freeze on the total numbers of those coming in. What's your response to that, Nigel Farage? I'm just talking numbers. We haven't even mentioned housing yet. But could you respond to that point in particular? Oh, no, I ignore that nonsense. Complete nonsense. I'm just talking pure facts. Housing. We have to build one new home every two minutes just to cope with legal net migration under this Conservative government. It is literally impossible. And as for the numbers coming in, I would respond to this. 50% of the three quarters of a million who are coming in a year are dependents. They're not wealth creators. And mass migration is making us poorer. You'll hear, oh, GDP's going up. Well, of course it is. There are more people. GDP per capita, our relative wealth, has declined for the last six consecutive quarters. You should bring your mum. You come to university in Britain, you can't bring your mum, can you? OK. All right. Nigel Farage. Christine, the answer to your question is, what are they going to do about it? Neither of them will do anything. These people have lied to us repeatedly. Says you, come on! And Labour... And Labour... And Labour... Repeatedly. I've actually always told the truth. Always said what I believe. And Labour, it's not even in your first six priorities for government. You haven't got the will to do anything about it. Nothing under these is going to change. And the vast majority, not on this panel, obviously, because they're not representative in that sense, the vast majority of Britons want net migration reduced significantly. No, we're free. Unfortunately, we're governed incompetently, but at least there are mistakes and not somebody else's. Yes, absolutely. because people are getting poorer. It's quite simple. I mentioned a few moments ago that GDP, wealth per capita, has fallen for the last six consecutive quarters. And it's very much... And it's been the same. It sort of began with Labour, but it's continued. It's very much a country in which the big corporates and the big banks and a certain group of people have become richer exponentially, And everybody else has fallen behind. And things that perhaps we could have expected when we were younger, our kids and grandkids now find a house unaffordable. Rents have risen by 20%, in some parts 30%, just in the last three years. And I'm sorry, but we are back. to the population explosion and the massive impact it's having on the lives of people. And we've got to try to get the population to steady, and we have to build more houses, and we have to take the poorer people out of tax, increase the tax rate... Thank you very much. ..for £20,000, get people back to work. Nigel Farage... We need to do something, something with vision. And if we increase the level at which people start to pay tax to £20,000, that takes 7 million people out of tax. That's up to a £1,500 benefit for people on lower wages. But it helps get people off the benefits trap. because the benefits trap is if you start to go back to work and earn money you lose benefits plus we'd rely less on unskilled foreign labour. That's the kind of vision that we need to help those who are lower end of the income scale or on benefits in this country. I really believe it would work. I think we should encourage people to have families. I think we should encourage people to have children. And I think we should also encourage people in marriage to have some tax benefits as well. I really do. So that's a yes? A lifting of the two tariffs? We've got to help people. My question is to Penny Morden. Given that your 2010 manifesto, your 2015 manifesto, your 2017 manifesto said you'd reduce net migration to tens of thousands, your 2019 manifesto said immigration would massively reduce, and that net 4.3 million people have come into the country since that time, why on earth should anybody believe the fifth manifesto that promises cuts to net migration? We've had... Oh, enough. That's fine. Nigel Farage, do you want to come back? I don't believe a single word that you say. You've deceived us with the last four manifestos. I don't believe you in the fifth. And as for being a Labour enabler, we are now ahead of you in the national polls. A vote for you is actually now a vote for Labour. I do agree that the first-past-the-post system has led to the two big parties. Well, one was big, not after today's polls, but there we are. They're both mushy sort of SDP parties in the middle. I would change the voting system. I think the House of Lords is an abomination. The fact that it's filled up with party donors is a complete disgrace. There must be an elected element. And finally, let's give the people the ability to call more referendums so they can decide the biggest issue of their lives. Unlike the rest, I don't need a script. I've come out of retirement, back into politics with passion in my heart. And I'll tell you, Britain is broken, everyone knows it, and Britain needs reform. The election is over under our system. We know that Keir Starmer's going to win and probably have quite a big majority. The question is, Who will lead the opposition? Because for democracy to work, there has to be a voice of opposition. And I put it to you that Ed Davey of the Liberal Democrats won't do that, because he actually agrees with Labour on most things. Rishi Sunak won't do it. Well, he'll probably be in California by then anyway. And the Tory party is split down the middle and about to implode in this election. I say to you, I will stand up against open borders. I will stand up and say those that come illegally across the channel should be deported. I will stand up and fight for the millions of men and women running small businesses. I have the courage to take on the mob. Please join the revolt.
deb_2024_06_13.FLYNN: I'm Stephen Flynn, leader of the SNP at Westminster, and we are here to always put Scotland's interests first. Dennis, thank you for your question. The NHS is quite an important topic for me because I physically wouldn't be standing here if it wasn't for the good work that the NHS did. I was a disabled man for 18 years. I know the importance of our public services but our NHS in particular. The thing the NHS requires more than anything is clear financial support from government and a plan for the future. And I'm afraid that Westminster doesn't offer that hope and that certainty. What's baked in under a future Labour government is £18 billion worth of public sector cuts. That's the equivalent of the entirety of Scotland's health and social care budget. You deserve better and the people of Scotland deserve better, which is why people should vote for the SNP at this election in Scotland. But what Angela's refusing to admit there is what was referred to earlier, which is the shadow health secretary essentially saying that he's going to hold the door open to the private sector. Now, that is not... He did say that, and that is not... That's not true. ..where the answer lies. The answer lies in investment. Well, I'll answer that directly. And when it comes to staffing... Angela, all of us are aware of what Wes has said. And you may deny it, but the reality is he said he wants privatisation. It goes beyond just hospitals as well. And when we talk about staffing, because staffing is an enormous issue, the elephant in the room is the fact that it's not migration is the problem. It's the fact that we need more people coming in to work in our hospitals and to work in our social care sector, which has been decimated by your Brexit. But Angela, your party is committed to continuing with £18 billion worth of public sector... Don't shake your head. This isn't my words, this is the IFS. The IFS have outlined the conspiracy of silence between the Labour Party and the Conservative Party on £18 billion worth of public sector cuts. When we look at education in the round, the biggest difference that exists between the SNP and what happens in Scotland and the Labour Party and the Conservative Party and what happens in Westminster in England is the fact that for our young people in Scotland, they go to university and they don't pay a single penny in tuition fees. Absolutely life-changing. And I don't know if the Lib Dems agree with tuition fees or not. They've got quite a record in that regard. I'm sure you can elaborate upon it for all of us. Do you still believe in free tuition, though? Thank you. Stephen Flynn. So Scotland has a migration problem. The problem is we don't have enough migrants because Scotland has a declining working age population. So we need people to come. And you know what? I am genuinely fed up going to speak to businesses. going to speak to health care workers, going to speak to people in our social care sector, in our schools and beyond, who say that they need more colleagues. Businesses who want to expand, who need people to come and work with them and for them. That's why I'm in favour of migration. And this race to the right that is happening in Westminster is so, so wrong. We can do better. We must demand better, not just from the Tories, but from the Labour Party too. And the SNP will always do that. The Westminster status quo. Of course it is. The four main parties... No, it isn't. The four main parties all with... I'll follow that. The reality is that Angela was being quiet because the leader of her party, Keir Starmer, just a couple of years ago said that we should be talking about the positive benefits of migration. And I actually believe that Angela Rayner probably agrees more with me on this topic rather than she does Nigel Farage or Penny Mordaunt, but the Labour Party has changed and it's changed in the wrong direction. And when Nigel Farage tells you all that migrants are making you poor and they're the problem. Don't believe him. Do you want to know the biggest problem facing the UK economy right now? His pet project of Brexit. £40 billion worth of tax receipts. And do you want to know what makes it worse? The Labour Party back Brexit, as outlined by their manifesto today. Shameful. Shameful. is an easy answer to your question, which is the Conservative Party is the problem. But to look more deeply into the policy aspect of this, we've had 14 years of austerity. Then we've had the damage of Brexit and what that's done to the economy, the shrinking of the economy, the £40 billion worth of tax receipts which have disappeared out of the economy. Then, of course, we had Liz Truss and her decision to crash the economy. And, of course, on top of that, we've had the mismanagement of primarily Scotland's energy resources, which has led to everyone in this room and everyone across these aisles' energy bills going through the roof. The thing that concerns me the most is what comes next. And the status quo consensus in Westminster is for £18 billion of public sector cuts, isn't it, Angela? You know what the public can't afford, Penny, is any longer, one single day more of a Conservative government which is completely out of touch with the reality on the ground. Let me respond to that directly. Ultimately, if we are going to move this conversation on to economic growth and what comes next, because that is probably where it is going to go, Angela said she wants economic growth but she didn't mention the single market or freedom of movement or the fact that the Labour Party have walked away from £28 billion of investment in net zero technologies, whilst the United States, Europe, and everyone else is going down that route to grow the economy. The Westminster consensus is alive and well. And it doesn't serve you well. And ultimately, it will be the people who need it most, the people who can't afford the shops, the people who are ultimately living in poverty who will feel the consequences of Westminster's priorities. Well, of course, in Scotland, it's the SNP that has seen the building as oversaw the building of 40% more affordable homes than in England and 70% more affordable homes in Wales. I don't necessarily think that's a tough question to answer. What we are looking at is the future and ultimately the Conservatives. have decided to cut the capital budget for Scotland, which impacts directly upon our ability to build more homes. But for the people who are living within those homes, what we've done in Scotland is introduced the Scottish Child Payment. It doesn't exist anywhere else on these isles. The Labour Party won't replicate it, the Tories certainly won't. 100,000 children are going to be lifted out of poverty thanks to that Scottish Child Payment. It is being called a game-changer. Will the Labour Party back it? Yes or no? It's like being back in the Commons. Angela. It took four and a half months and the death or injury of almost 100,000 civilians in Gaza for the Labour Party to back a ceasefire. On day one of a Labour government, will you end arms sales to Israel? Daisy Cooper, your question. Can I come back? In my view, you should always vote if you are doing well in life for the politician who will make sure that others can get on in life. And if you're not doing so well in life, you should always vote for the politician who's going to make things better for you. I think that's a nice and good starting point in politics. The Westminster system, the way it operates, is broken. It's been built on failure, Brexit being the most obvious one of recent times. Hold your politicians to high standards and vote for what you believe in. Well, I'm just surprised that Nigel Farage wants another referendum on Scottish independence. I'm sure there's a lot of folk back in Scotland who'd be... Oh, come on, you don't want to lose again, do you? Let's put power back in the hands of the Scottish people and take it out of politicians at Westminster. I'm sure the Labour Party would back that premise. The Tory government's forever by backing Scotland's independence. That's what we should do. Let's start with the good news. The Tories are finished. 14 years of Cameron, Clegg, May, Johnson, Truss and Sunak, that's finally coming to a close. So now our attention must turn to what comes next. And at a time when there should be hope and optimism, there is so little on display. Keir Starmer's status quo offers us yet more cuts and yet more Brexit. In the SNP, we don't accept that cosy Westminster consensus. We believe in better. Investment in our NHS. Action on the cost of living crisis. Rejoining the European single market. Economic growth by delivering net zero. Scotland's future in Scotland's hands. So on July 4th, vote for that future. Vote SNP to put Scotland's interests first.

Tonight I'll make the case for a fairer, more ambitious Wales, but also for a new kind of politics that can matter to you wherever you are. We can look at it from two sides in Wales, actually. That's why we say we want to recruit 500 extra GPs into the NHS. But two core principles. One, we absolutely have to pledge to keep the NHS free at the point of need, and also to keep privatisation out of the NHS. And it pains so many people to see Labour's West Streeting talking so warmly about bringing the private sector into the NHS. I chose my words carefully. It's the warm words which Wes Treating has used to describe the role he sees it for the private sector in the NHS. And the problem with that, you see, is that if you're allowing the scarce funding that we all agree there is in the NHS to be leaked out of the NHS in private profit, rather than doing things like setting up the cancer system that Plaid Cymru proposes in this election, you're not serving the people that we're meant to be serving. If I could make a bet, I had to draw attention to the analogy with the cats and the mice here. What we've seen under the Conservatives for 14 years is the fat cats. getting rich and getting the cream. And I fear that with privatisation in the NHS, we're going to see the same under Labour. This is the honesty that we need in this debate. We can't have a decent NHS. We can't have good schools unless we have a fair taxation system that brings the money in to pay for those services. The taxation system has to be fair. We have to have honesty. And the taxation has to be fair in that the weight is borne by the shoulders of those who have the broadest shoulders, be it corporates or the very, very wealthy. of the public sector. half an extra teacher in every school in England will make a difference to the standards of education. But I'll tell you one thing, we need, and both my parents were teachers, we need, just as in health, to make sure that our workforce feel supported. It's in good wages, but it's also in knowing that government is on their side, and neither in Wales nor in the rest, nor in England, can they say that that's the case. It's a very well-worded question, isn't it? I keep hearing that this is a problem, says Christine. And you know what? I think we need to heed and listen to people who have genuine concerns about the impact of the movement of population and pressures on public services, but we have to put that in the context of public spending cuts by the Conservatives. look at the numbers of immigrants that we need to bring in for certain sectors in health and care and parts of the economy. Listen, I've been on a whistle-stop tour of Wales in the past week. You know, I try to listen to people, but the problem we have is that Nigel Farage here has been on a dog-whistle tour of the UK for many, many years and exploiting the anxieties that people have, and we really need to change the way that we talk about this issue. It's making us poorer. That's the point. It's making us poorer. Thank you. I hear Nigel Farage talking a lot about dependence. The problem that we have in higher education now is that changes in visa requirements means that family members aren't able to come and study to accompany students to universities. That is causing major problems in terms of revenue for our higher education universities. I mean, come on. But we need people to come to study in our universe. Thank you. Thank you. Angela Rayner mentioned the border force. We're back to Brexit here. Of course, it's more difficult to control our borders and immigration because of Brexit and the fact that our relationship isn't what it was with the European Union. And on Brexit, £4 billion has been spent on controlling our borders when we used to have borders that were free. The question I ask myself time and time again in this election is, what has happened to Labour? After Brexit, I didn't want to leave the European Union, but once that vote happened, we had to think of ways, OK, of trying to protect our interest. I worked with Labour colleagues of yours on a document called Securing Wales' Future, which proposed getting back or staying in the customs union and the single market so at least we could look after our trade interests. What happened to you? Why won't you even talk about that in the Labour Party now? It's because you're afraid of the Conservatives, the Conservatives are afraid of Nigel Farage, and we end up in the trouble that we're in. Stephen Flynn. Thank you, Andrean. I have no doubt that Wales is best served by being in the European Union, but for now, let's get back in the customs union and the single market to help our economy. Yes, a very rich country. but one of the most unequal in the world. And it's because of that that we were so ill-prepared for the cost-of-living crisis. Some, of course, was exacerbated by war and global conditions, but, of course, we can never forget tressonomics and the crashing of the economy at the hands of the Conservatives. So, what we had was a cost-of-living crisis, yes, a cost of inequality crisis, a cost of lack of investing crisis, a cost of missed opportunities. Somehow, we have got to get government to recognise the importance of dealing with inequalities before they can make opportunities open to all. And that is what is so missing from what Labour is offering, tied at the hip as they are to the Conservatives. Thank you very much, Stephen Flynn. One of the biggest regrets of 14 years of mismanagement by the Conservatives is that they missed the opportunity to invest in economic growth in order to help our communities. During those years when interest rates were low, what they chose to do was have this ideological drive to cut public spending. that hit everybody, especially those who are poorest. And in this selection, you'll hear me talking a lot about ambition. Where is the ambition from either of the Westminster main parties to invest in allowing us in Wales to make the most of our natural resources through devolving the Crown Estate, allowing us to invest in transport and connectivity through giving us the billions we're owed as a result of HS2 rail in England? That's the ambition that we need. We're not hearing it from the Conservatives, and I regret we're not hearing it from them. And that is under the Tories' watch. Point out the embarrassment here for Angela Rayner. We have Nigel Farage wanting to get rid of the two-child limit, and Labour is refusing to. Again, I ask, what's happened to Labour? Well, again, we've said that we'd have action on child poverty, but we will not... But you won't lift the two-child limit? But we will not... A Conservative policy that you are determined to press ahead with, even though it damages some of our poorest families? Yeah, and I'd add that Plaid Cymru is proposing £20 extra for every child as well, because again, like the SNP, we recognise that we have to get to grips with the issue of child poverty. My question is also to Angela Rayner, given the convergence between Labour and the Conservatives on public spending. Maybe I should ask if Labour would accept Penny Mordaunt into your party, but I'll ask this. Gordon Brown said child poverty is a scar on the soul of Britain. Now, is it the likely incoming Labour government's two-child benefit cap or the £18 billion worth of cuts to public services which will make the situation worse? How about some honesty about the fact that freezing thresholds has meant higher taxes under the Conservatives whilst they've been cutting public spending? And this is the honesty and integrity that we're looking for. We need politicians to be truthful with people about the challenges that we face. We need good judgment. Yet we have a First Minister in Wales, a Labour First Minister, who accepted a £200,000 donation from a convicted environmental polluter. That's poor judgment, and I wonder if Angela Rayner would have accepted it. And just briefly, no wonder people are fed up. It's the PPE corruption, it's the Downing Street parties during Covid. We need to hold politicians to higher standards than we've seen in recent years. Thank you. Rhian ap Iorwes. I'm sure there'll be people who were thinking, why is this guy from Wales being invited on to a debate? It's being broadcast across the whole of the UK. Well, firstly, I hope some of what I have said has resonated with you, wherever you are, and that you agree we need to do politics in a different way. But the fact that some do think that reflects what Plaid Cymru is all about. We are here to offer a positive vision of what Wales could be, knowing that time and time again, our aspirations go unheard by Westminster governments, be that Labour or Conservatives. Now, the truth is, throughout the UK, There are communities who feel ignored and overlooked, and I hope you stand up for your community. And for me, it'll always be about standing up for Wales. Now, it's high time that the Conservatives were kicked out of power, but Labour will have to be held to account too. And Welsh voters can do that through Plaid Cymru MPs and their vision for fairness and ambition for Wales. Vote Plaid Cymru on July 6.

We're anchoring all of our debate tonight in the questions that have been so kindly provided by our audience, which we're very grateful for. Welcome back to the ITV election debate where seven of the country's political leaders are making the case for why they should get your vote with our next question. You could be facing a crisis on this within weeks of a new government. But will you stick with the early release scheme, which is so unpopular with them? THOMAS-SYMONDS: We will not release prisoners who are assessed to be a threat to the public on that scheme, which is what this government has been doing. In a moment, the biggest dividing line this election, immigration. Labour says it would scrap the Rwanda deportation scheme on day one. We'll fight it out with our seven parties. Welcome back to the First Sight Arts Centre in Colchester for this special extended edition of Channel 4 News. If you're just joining us, we've had half an hour of sometimes quite fiery debate on law and order with representatives from the seven main political parties fighting this election. Now we're going to hear from them on immigration. It is the one big issue with very clear divisions. Who can come to this country? Who is excluded? How do they get here? How are they sent home? What are the economic benefits and societal pressures? We have some polling on this too, but this time we've targeted the question around the two main parties as they are the only ones realistically who could be running the Home Office after the election. Both the Conservative Party and the Labour Party have pledged to cut net migration. That's the difference between those people coming here legally and those leaving to live abroad. It is largely determined by the government's immigration rules. So what did people make of the Labour and Tory pledge to bring legal migration down? Well, 28% said they only believe Labour. Just 15% said they only believe the Conservatives. 43% don't believe either of them. So a clear lack of trust in both those main parties. And that rises to 52% among the over 55 older voters. Secondly, we asked, both the Conservative Party and the Labour Party have pledged to stop small boats crossing the Channel if elected at the next general election. Which of the following is closest to your view? 27% said they only believe Labour, 16% believe the Conservatives, 7% believe both of them, but again, far more people, 44%, said they don't believe either party. Let's just get some reaction to that from Chris Philp and Nick Thomas-Simmons. Chris Philp, it is your broken policy pledges that has caused this massive lack of trust, isn't it? Now, I'm going to be... What proportion of net migration do they take up? What I wanted to talk about was the fact that both of you aren't believed by voters, Nick Thomas-Symonds. Is that a problem for you, or is it rather good because nobody believes you're going to solve the problem anyway? Of course you want to. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Of course you want to build trust, but it's no surprise that people don't have trust, having had four Conservative governments elected on manifestos that have all broken their promises. THOMAS-SYMONDS: So those figures don't come as a surprise. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Net migration is too high at 685,000, and what we need is a plan to bring it down. THOMAS-SYMONDS: So firstly, what we need to do is, where there are particular sectors, we need a proper THOMAS-SYMONDS: plan so that we can upskill our own workforce. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Take engineering for example. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Engineering visas for people coming from overseas have doubled, whilst engineering apprenticeships here in the UK have almost halved. But what is your actual pledge? Because they've taken the measures that will bring the immigration figures down. THOMAS-SYMONDS: If you get into government, you'll claim that that's because of you. THOMAS-SYMONDS: They haven't taken measures to bring immigration down. THOMAS-SYMONDS: You look at recent years, it's been at a record high. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And what they needed to do, and what the next Labour government, should we be privileged enough to form one, will do, is have a skills strategy right across those different sectors, like health and social care, for example, the IT sector, and not just move to that lever of many people coming in... What's the number? I want to bring in the audience on precisely this point. Let's get a question from Lily Lloyd-Smith. Read out yours. Hang on, Richard. I want to go a bit further into the economic argument around what we need. Piers Baker, you're the owner of three local businesses. Where are you? You rely on staff from abroad, don't you? So what do you make of this conversation going on about reducing the number of people coming to this country? OK, and Kate Wilson from the NHS, where are you? Are you not listening to what the audience is telling you? There's a difference between a people shortage and a labour shortage. I'm going to move us on. I'm going to move us on because we've spent quite a lot of time on that. I want to hear from a refugee now. Gwali Pasole. Now, you came here from Afghanistan as a 12-year-old. What's your question? OK, so we talked about legal migration. Let's now talk about asylum seekers and refugees, like our questioner, Keith Brown. Chris Philp, when you hear a man from Afghanistan asking for a little compassion in this debate, are you a little ashamed of where this has all got to? the tone of this debate if you get into government? Or is it going to carry on along the way? THOMAS-SYMONDS: I sincerely hope so. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And can I just say in relation to this question, and congratulations on your citizenship, some of the proudest moments I've had as a member of Parliament since 2015 THOMAS-SYMONDS: In Glenavon, Pontypool, Cumbrian, the places that I've been privileged to represent is welcoming people from Syria, welcoming people from Afghanistan and welcoming people from Ukraine and hugely proud as well of the welcome that they've had from local people in my home valley. THOMAS-SYMONDS: It's been remarkable to see. THOMAS-SYMONDS: But we do need to reform those resettlement routes. THOMAS-SYMONDS: You just mentioned your own journey of 15 years, which is clearly an unacceptable period for you to have waited. THOMAS-SYMONDS: We owe those people a moral duty. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And frankly, it's disgraceful that nearly three years on now, since we left Afghanistan, you still have thousands of people who helped us, who would be at massive risk of their safety as a consequence in Afghanistan, now stuck in neighbouring countries like in Pakistan. THOMAS-SYMONDS: We need that process speeded up because we owe those people a moral duty. People often talk about the ones who helped British troops. What about the rest of them? THOMAS-SYMONDS: I think that we have a very particular duty to groups of people. THOMAS-SYMONDS: I think that particular group of people, we call it the ARAP scheme, the interpreters who helped us, others who provided support to our troops. THOMAS-SYMONDS: And not the girls who can't go to school anymore. THOMAS-SYMONDS: Absolutely, and that THOMAS-SYMONDS: position is appalling as well. Right one of the big issues here of course is the existing system and what to do with it so let's get a question on that from Lorcan Furlong. Lorcan where are you? Where's Saira Javid? Saira, you've got a question on this as well. I want to hear the two main parties on this point. Nick Thomas-Symonds. THOMAS-SYMONDS: I'm just hugely concerned to hear that but I think what we need in terms of our immigration debate and indeed our immigration system is we just need a sense of fairness and we actually need to enforce the rules and I think it's those two things that gives the public confidence. THOMAS-SYMONDS: It's why, then, we have a very welcoming environment. Okay, I'm going to move us on to a broader topic now, which is about the bigger global picture when it comes to migration. Let's take a question from Hamza Eagle. Hamza. Thank you. Essex Police Annual Publication Performance Summary to October 2023. Of Colchester's near 15,000 victim-based crimes, half of those were violent against the person. Only 11.7% were solved.
