deb_2024_06_20.DAVEY: Well, thank you for your question, Alison. The answer is no. We put forward a very detailed costed manifesto, and it's got a big health and social care package at the centre, about £9 billion, and other things, for example, helping with free school meals, with costs of living. And we've shown how we'll pay for everything we've got in that. For example, we're thinking that some of the large corporates could pay more. For example, the oil and gas giants could pay more. They've made a huge fortune with oil and gas prices being so high. We've also said that the big banks, the tax cuts that the Conservatives have given the big banks, they should be reversed. That raises, for example, 4.3 billion. We've said to pay for our mental health programme to make sure that there's a mental health professional in every primary school and every secondary school. There should be an increase in the digital service tax on the social media giants, partly because some people think they're responsible for the mental health crisis for many young people. By the fifth year of the Parliament. Well, there are a number of tax changes we're making. One of the ones is to clamp down on tax avoidance and evasion. And although all the parties are saying that, I think we can say that more credibly because we want to invest £1 billion in her Majesty's revenue and customs. Yeah, well, we've said in the manifesto, in the costings, we've pointed to a number of tax rises, and I'm talking about the one of clamping down on people who aren't paying their fair share of tax. The way to do that is to have more tax inspectors. Her Majesty's revenue customs have been cut back dramatically, and they can't do the job. And so if you talk to the experts, they reckon for every pound you spend, in tax officials and the technology for HMRC, you can raise £18. Now, we haven't put that in our costings. We've just said an extra £7. So I think we've been quite cautious. and I think the money can be found. It's about the political will. And look what you can do with it. Our NHS is on its knees. It's on its knees. People can't get GPs. There's no NHS dentist. They're having to wait hours for ambulances. And on the care system, people are going without care. That is just wrong. It is in the bottom, of course it's not. And I think our proposals are responsible, they're fully costed. I think other parties, frankly, are not putting the money in we need to rescue our public services, particularly the NHS. But our schools need better funding too. I've talked about the mental health provision that we want to make, but I think if you look at the cost of living, I'm very worried that children are going to our schools with empty tummies. that they're hungry. They can't learn if they're hungry. And when we were in government, we provided free school meals for infant children. Actually, we used a pilot that Labour had done, performed to give credit where credit's due. But we put it in. The Tories didn't like us doing it. You know, George Osborne did not like giving free school meals to infants, I can tell you. But now we think we need to go further. OK. And the Kosselin crisis, any child that's in poverty really should be getting a free school meal. Well, listen, those were... I have to say it was very difficult governing with the Conservatives. We couldn't get everything we wanted. But we were faced with a choice as a minister. You either had to stay in and fight inside the government or leave. I think the easy choice for me would have been to leave the government, vote against it and tour the media studios and complain. The hard choice was to stay in, roll my sleeves up and really fight. And as explained earlier, the Tories didn't want us to bring in free school meals. We fought for that, and we got it. They wanted, actually, to cut spending on welfare far greater than happened. For example, in the second budget, when inflation was about 5.2%, they wanted to freeze benefits and pensions. Look, I'm not proud of some of the votes we did, Fiona. I'm not proud of them. and I want to overturn them, and a lot of our policies are changing some of the things that we had to agree to back then. OK. But look what happened when we left office. I lost my seat. The Conservatives were governing by themselves. The first budget with George Osborne, they cut welfare spending by £12 billion, hitting the poorest in our country. We stopped that, and people often... I don't blame people, but they forget what we stopped as well. Well, listen, I take... We're talking, obviously, falling off the paddleboard. I'll come to the paddleboard in a second. Going down a water slide. I'll come to the paddleboard. Going on a roller coaster. You name it, you've done it. Yeah, I'm happy to talk through all of them. And I don't know what they've got in store for me afterwards, I have to say. We did wonder if you were going to paraglide in here tonight. But it's a serious question, I'm grateful for it. I take your concerns and the concerns of voters very, very seriously. And if you look at our manifesto, we've got some very... well thought through policies to try to do things like rescue the NHS. But I don't think politicians should take themselves too seriously. I think that's part of the problem in politics actually. People are really disillusioned. They think politicians don't get them. And yes, it's been to grab a bit of attention. But when I did fall off that paddleboard in Lake Windermere, the serious message was about the sewage problem. The Conservative government, the water regulators, have allowed water companies to pump their filthy sewage into our precious environment, our lakes, our rivers, seas. And that's got to stop. I want to end the sewage scandal. The roller coaster. Well, that was the day we launched our manifesto, and I said, politics can be a roller coaster ride, and boy, it was. But there were some fun ones. But let me give you another one where I slid down a slide in Somerset. It was during half term, lots of families and kids having fun. And I had some fun, yes. But we launched that day the mental health policy. We said there's a crisis in mental health for our young people, our children. There's hundreds of thousands of children waiting to get care, to get counselling, to get therapy. That shouldn't be happening. You know, it's going to blight their whole future if we don't get that help. And so we were putting forward, on the day I came down that slide, a very serious policy. Well, I wish we had the resources to do that at the moment. People are talking about the costing manifesto. We can't do anything. We've said in our manifesto, when resources allow, we want to do that. We did that before. The Conservatives said you can't do that. And we said we're going to do that. We must do that. I don't think we can afford quite that yet. But one thing we can do to help families with children the ones you were talking about, sir, I think the two-child limit on universal credit is just wrong. I think it has to go. If you look and talk to the experts, they say the quickest and most cost-effective way of tackling child poverty is to get rid of that two-child limit on universal credit. And that's something that could happen really quickly. It's costed in our manifesto, because it is a bit pricey. But I think it's the best way. And hopefully, that means that family that you were talking about would have the money and wouldn't have to do that thing. Well, first I'd say, sir, with due respect, the election isn't over. I'm on here to try to persuade people in this audience and across the country viewing it to vote Liberal Democrat. Because I think we've got great ideas. And I think we've been getting great responses. Are you trying to persuade people that you can be Prime Minister? Listen, I'm realistic about our chances, right? We've had three very bad elections. Everyone knows that. And when I became leader, I said my top priority was to defeat as many Conservative MPs as possible. So do you think you can or cannot be Prime Minister? We might as well hear it from you. We know what the polls say. It will be challenging given the polls. OK. But I don't want to put a ceiling on our ambition. And, you know, this is a bit of time to go. Fiona, it's a very fair point. Can I take you through the GP policy? In a manifesto, we say we need to recruit 8,000 more GPs. And we've talked to health experts about how you could do that. It would take about four years, with a billion pounds a year. That's in our cost manifesto. And there's several ways you're going to do it. At the moment, actually, there are people who have been trained at taxpayer's expense and qualified as doctors. and they can't work as GPs because there's not the money there to employ them. How crazy is that? And we need to train more, we need to retain more. GPs are leaving. Some people in our country, they come out of our medical school and they go to Australia. Because in Australia they're better valued, they're better paid. No, it is absolutely not a wish list. It's a programme for government. And when I answered Alison earlier about the costings, and I would urge people, they're on the website, go and see them for yourself. We've put a lot of work into it to make sure we can afford something. Let me explain. One of my lessons from when I was in government before, and we didn't deliver everything, was that you shouldn't promise something you can't deliver. What, like cancelling tuition fees? Indeed. And what I learned from that process was two things, actually. First of all, don't promise something you can't deliver. And so when you have a cost of manifesto, make sure the sums add up. And I'm very confident that they do. Secondly, sorry, we lost some trust during that period. And we've had to rebuild it. And since I became leader, we've spent the last few years trying to rebuild that trust by listening to people. in communities that we seek to represent, trying to understand their concerns. And in our manifesto, we've tried to reflect those concerns. That's why I've put health and care right at the centre. I have some experiences of care, maybe we'll come on to that. But I've also put the cost of living as our key priority. I mentioned free school meals, talking about reducing people's energy bills through insulating people's homes. That's good for the environment, it's good to keep energy bills down, they're way too high. Well, listen, I'm... I understand why your generation lost faith in us. It was a difficult government to be in. Explained, had choices whether we came out of it and cut from the sidelines or try to get there and win things. We didn't win everything and we lost that one. And we got punished at the 2015 election. I lost my seat along with most of the Democrats actually. Well, as I was saying to the gentleman at the back, the thing I learned from that process, the whole process, fees but other things too, was you shouldn't promise what you can't deliver. And I think we did in 2010. Absolutely not. It was the big lesson I learned and I would urge people to look at our manifesto, look at the costings. We've said that money should come from all the people I've already listed in answer to Alison's question. There's another group that I didn't mention. I don't want to put tax up on ordinary people. People have seen huge tax rises under this government, to record levels, and with a cost-living crisis, I don't think people should be paying more tax. Except one group, the very, very wealthiest. They tend to get their income in capital gains. And we think capital gains tax on the billionaires and the multi-millionaires should go up. It's essentially a loophole. They're often paying a lower tax rate on their very high amounts of money they have than many people who are working. That's just not right. Well, it was very scarring. I lost my seat. The party was severely punished. In 2015, that's democracy. And we didn't do well in the two subsequent elections. And when I became leader, I said to my party, you've got to wake up and smell the coffee. We've got to really connect with people. And we've really tried to do that. And it's a work in progress. But I think the parliamentary by-elections we've won in this parliament, in Tory heartlands, the local elections results we've had. Just this May, we pushed the Tories into third place. It hasn't been done for 30 years. That suggests that some people are coming back and trusting us. Well, I'm hoping that you like our manifesto and the things I'm saying tonight. There are seats in Greater Manchester that I'm really sure we can win. Hazel Grove and Cheadle in Stockport are part of Greater Manchester. We're looking really good to beat the Conservatives in those seats. And there are parts of the Northern Ireland. I was in Sheffield earlier today with our candidate in Sheffield Hallam. I think we can actually beat Labour there. I'm going to Harrogate tomorrow. So there are parts of the North, but it is true that the vast majority of seats that I think we can win at this election are in the home counties against the Conservatives and in the West Country against the Conservatives and in some rural areas like Shropshire and Mid Wales and so on. I mean, I'd actually be interested to hear some of your views as well, but let me give you mine, but do come back and give me some of your ideas. I mean, when I talk to some young people, including my own constituency, they talk about their fear of knife crime. And I think that's really terrible, that young people are worried about going out of their homes to enjoy themselves, and some of them carry knives. Now, they shouldn't, but they're worried about that. And so we have to get beside those young people, obviously through education and schools, but also through community police. One thing that used to work really well until, I think it was Boris Johnson, when he was mayor of London, got rid of them, we had lots of community police in my area. and they were highly visible, they got the sight of young people, and they got the trust of young people. And it was very effective in tackling that sort of crime. Well, I'm making it clear that we're standing on the manifesto that we launched. I'm not focused on after the election, and excuse me, but I'm not, because I want to make sure that every minute that I get like this, and I often get it, is to put forward our Liberal Democrat values and our Liberal Democrat policies. And the more I get a chance to do that, I'm hoping I can persuade people to come over to us, and then the more Liberal Democrat MPs we have, the more influence we'll have. Look, what I'm being really clear to people about is that I want to make sure we maximise the Liberal Democrat number of MPs. We're basically going to beat lots of Conservative MPs, I hope, a few in Scotland, a few SNP MPs, but I'm determined to focus in on that. I've been in politics... OK, so that's not a question you're going to answer, just to be clear. Well, I'll explain why, Fiona. I've been in politics a little while. I've seen a lot of Lib Dem leaders. And sometimes they focus on what happens after the election. And I've noticed that when they do that, they don't focus on the job in hand, which is trying to win local champions and Liberal Democrats across the country. And I'm going to try and do that. Well, first of all, can I say that Horizon scandal is the biggest miscarriage of justice in our country's history, and my heart goes out to those sub-postmasters and their families who've suffered so much. I'm representing three in my constituency, and hearing their story is quite harrowing. I made two big mistakes during that time. It wasn't an issue that cropped up much, but it did crop up at the start, when Alan Bates wrote to me. I'd been in office about two weeks, and I wrote to him and said, no, this is a matter for the post office. We don't govern it. I'm not going to meet you. He wrote back furious. He did. And then I read his letter and said, well, I better meet him. And I met him in October, as you said, Fiona. I was the first post office minister to meet him. And I listened to his concerns, his arguments about the Horizon IT system, the fact that he thought there was remote access, but there wasn't, according to the post office. And I took all his questions, and I asked them to my officials and to the post office. And they gave me a categorical assurance that the points that Mr Bates was making were not true, that their system had been independently assured by IT experts, that the courts had looked at this, and they had found people guilty under a minute examination. And I wish I'd seen through that, but those were what I was told. Well, I wish I'd seen through the lies. I've said sorry for not seeing through those lies. There were many ministers of all political parties during this 20-year period who didn't see those lies. The courts didn't. And here's the issue, because I've obviously reflected on this, because it's such a scandal. What I've come to the conclusion is our system of government, whether you're a minister or you're in the courts, it depends on people telling the truth. And in a number of scandals, the contaminated blood scandal, The Hillsborough scandal, the Horizon scandal. We've seen people not telling the truth and there's been a conspiracy. Well, I think I did Fiona, because I did meet Mr Bates, the first person to meet him in that job, and I asked his questions to the post office, but I got those categorical assurance. And here's the point, what do we do when people aren't telling the truth? I think there are two big things to change the system. for whatever the scandal we've seen. And the first is to get protection for whistleblowers, because the Horizon scandal was actually only uncovered during a Panorama BBC programme in 2015 and then the High Court in 2019, because one guy who was an insider in Fujitsu blew the whistle. He took a lot of courage. and I think whistleblowers serve the public interest but they don't have the protections and we need to give whistleblowers far greater protections so these scandals are uncovered far more quickly. Man further back in the blue shirt. Well, I think there were serious mistakes made by Paul O'Reynolds and the post office executives who were in charge, some of the lawyers, some of the people in Fujitsu. There's a public inquiry which I campaigned for because the people who've done this are appalling thing need to be held to account, and there's a criminal investigation which I totally support. I don't think I should prejudice that investigation or the outcome of that inquiry. I think the evidence that we've heard does speak for itself, but I'll let the judge in charge of the inquiry and the police make those final decisions. But I do hope, I really hope that people go to prison for this. Well, it is in the manifesto costings. It's in the capital investment section. I looked at it. Yeah, it says six billion a year. So six billion each year over the lifetime of the Parliament. That's funded by borrowing because it's capital investment. So it is in a manifesto. So it's by borrowing. You're funding it by borrowing. OK. Yeah. because it's capital investment. And so we've said that there should be 150,000 new social homes built every year because they're affordable and they can be built in the right places. And our approach is a community-led approach because the current system isn't working. It's a developer-led approach. And they tend to build the wrong houses in the wrong places because they get more money for them. Well, it's not going to be done in the first two years, Fiona. We're not saying that. Well, I think it can. I think it can. It's happened in our country before. We're going to have to skill up. We're going to have to find... No, it hasn't happened, but it needs to happen. It absolutely needs to happen for young people. Well, I've been trying to explain that we want a very different approach, a bigger focus on social homes. That means the state investing in council properties. My wife, I met her on a Liberal Democrat housing policy working group. Mr. Romance. But to be fair to my wife, she's a social housing lawyer and she's now on the council in my area in charge of housing. And she's now in charge of the largest council house building programme in 40 years in Kingston. So it can be done. You know, my wife's doing it. I'm very proud of her. And I think it needs political will. The Conservatives aren't interested. It's really clear they're not interested in that. They want to play to the developers' tune. I think if you play to the community's tune, it's better. And here's another reason. You know, some people say people don't want homes built in the area. One of the reasons they don't, if you ask them, is they say, well, there's no GPs already. We want more people here. There's not enough school places. Their water infrastructure can't take it. So what you want is a community-led approach which deals with those issues. The developers don't want to do it. So if you have a community-led approach, you can deal with the infrastructure problems and people actually will welcome more homes because they can see there's something in it for them.
deb_2024_06_20.SWINNEY: I think the honest answer is that we've had a very tough time as a party in the last couple of years. And as a consequence of all of those difficulties, I'm here to lead the Scottish National Party. Shall we just sum up those difficulties for those who may not be aware? It has been a turbulent time and I would be the first to accept that and admit that. So my party has asked me to lead it through this election campaign and beyond the 2026 parliamentary elections to essentially do two things. to first of all rebuild trust between the electorate and the Scottish National Party and I've always worked throughout my political life to build trust and confidence with the electorate that have chosen me and secondly to provide the direction that's necessary in the Scottish Government to change people's lives and that's my primary duty as First Minister of Scotland. to make sure that we change lives and to improve lives of people in Scotland. And I've set that programme out by establishing a focus on eradicating child poverty, on achieving the transition to net zero, on strengthening the Scottish economy and improving our public services. And by doing that, I hope to rebuild the trust of people in Scotland, which I accept the SNP has got to earn on a constant basis. Where has it all gone wrong? There have been challenges about the issues you have set out and we have got to recognise the importance of delivering on the priorities of the public. When I look at some of the issues we have delivered on, for example, we have recently doubled the availability of early learning and childcare for families in our country so that every three and four-year-old and for many two-year-olds get access to full-time early learning and childcare which saves a family about five and a half thousand pounds a year per child and it's policies like that that make a real difference to the lives of children and young people and families in our country that we need to deliver on. We've established a Scottish child payment which puts about £26 every week into the family, into households of low-income families, to try to help with the cost of living and to eradicate child poverty. Now, these are examples where I think the SNP is delivering to individuals and to households around the country to make that difference. And we've got to do more of that as we take forward our agenda to transform lives within Scotland. to be a minority government again. So we don't have that overall control of the political system. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, I have to be able to do that. If I want to get a law, passed by the Scottish Parliament. I can't just instruct my colleagues to vote for it. I've got to win friends and allies. So on every piece of legislation, on every budget, we've got to win friends and allies. And one of the things that I think... What I want to make sure is that we don't have any whiff of that whatsoever and that we work hard, as I answered Andrew's question, to win the trust and confidence of the public. One of the things that really worries me about politics today, and I accept that I might actually have played a part in creating this, is the polarisation of debate. And I think there's a need to bring people together. And when I became First Minister just six and a half weeks ago, I pledged that I would reach out beyond my party to other parties, to other opinions, to try to bring people together. Because I think what's wrong with our society today is that we're not together, we are polarised, and we need to bring people back together again. Thank you for your question Sam and I accept it's an important question and one that's on the minds of many people. I think politics nowadays needs to have people setting out what they believe in and I honestly believe that Scotland would be a stronger country and would have better prospects if we were an independent country. That's been my That's been my honest view over my entire adult life. But I'm a Democrat, so I accept that we can't be an independent country until the people of Scotland vote for us to be an independent country. And I do believe in the right of the people of Scotland to decide their own future. I think, generally, there's an accepted view that self-determination of the right of people to decide how they should be governed is an important political principle. And I think what politics today needs is politicians who will set out what they actually believe in, set it out openly to the public, and ask the public to judge. And one of the frustrations I've got is that today, in the Scottish Parliament, there is a majority in favour of there being a referendum on independence, and that Scotland should be an independent country, but we're not being permitted by the United Kingdom government to put that idea and that concept to the public. And I think we should have the right to do so. Well, what I want to make sure, and it's really related to the point that I answered Sam about a moment ago, is that the legitimate decisions of the people of Scotland in the election in 2021, when a Scottish Parliament was elected with a majority of members favouring independence and a referendum, that that can actually be respected by the United Kingdom government. But I don't hear you saying that. What I want is people in Scotland to confidently vote for the SNP to make sure we can turn that manifesto into a reality. Just answer that question, John. And we can actually deliver on what people asked the Parliament to deliver on in 2021. Well, what I want to do during the election campaign is to encourage people to vote for the SNP. OK, so you're not answering that question. To enable us to deliver on that policy commitment, which is right at the heart of our manifesto. Are you going to answer that question or not? Well, what I'm saying is that I want people to vote for the SNP to make this happen. The first thing I would do is I would argue, as we argue in our manifesto, for us to rejoin the European Union, because that, I think, is the source of the problems in the manufacturing sector. I mean, that could be 10 years or something. But my point is that there's a couple of big issues in this election campaign that really aren't seeing the light of day. And one of them is the disastrous impact of Brexit on the United Kingdom economy. If I look at one of the things that we're taking forward in Scotland today, we've got what's called a tech scaler programme, which is designed to take new emerging companies that have emerged from our universities and partnerships with business, to essentially support them and to fuel the growth of those individual companies. And what that's about doing is taking new, fresh, dynamic ideas, the ideas of the future. There are things that are happening today in Scotland. We've got a facility up in Shetland which is now going to start launching satellites by rocket into space. Now, if you'd said to me a few years ago, Scotland will be a satellite-launching country, I would have said, OK, that's quite a big ask, but, you know, it'll be interesting if it happens. It's now happening. So innovation that comes from our universities can be fuelled with public investment and that's what the Scottish Government is doing to support that activity, to strengthen the manufacturing base of Scotland and to strengthen the economic opportunities that we face. Does that answer your question? That is happening now. Well, let me perhaps set out some of the things that we've done. So since we came to office, for example, GDP per capita in Scotland, the growth of the economy, has been faster in Scotland than in the rest of the United Kingdom. Productivity in the Scottish economy has grown faster under the SNP government than it has in the rest of the United Kingdom. I said a moment ago the measures were taken to tackle child poverty, which is keeping 100,000 children out of poverty in Scotland today. We've seen also the significant expansion of early learning and childcare, which is delivering the best start for children and young people in our society. So what I would say is that there's a whole range of decisions that have been made in Scotland that are good for Scotland, and that's an illustration of what an independent country could do if we had the powers, the broader range of powers, because I accept we need to improve economic performance in Scotland, we need to eradicate poverty, child poverty within our society and we can only do that if we have the broader range of powers and we've demonstrated with the limited powers that we've got how we can make a difference. So the first point I'd like to say in response to your question is that I accept there are some very significant challenges in the National Health Service in Scotland today. And some of that's a product of the impact of COVID and the necessity of trying to catch up on cases that were delayed. and we're making headway on the long waits that Fiona just put to me in the additional part of the question. So I accept, first of all, that there's a real challenge in the National Health Service, and we need to put more funding in to do something about that. There's two elements to the funding we have available to us. There's the block grant that comes from Westminster, which is affected by the same austerity and spending constraints that affect budgets in England and Wales. but there's also the money we can raise in Scotland. So we've taken decisions over the last three or four years to increase tax on higher earners so we could raise more money to invest in public services. And we now have one and a half billion pounds available to us that we invest in public services that wouldn't be there if we hadn't taken those decisions. That's an example of how we're using the powers that we have today to try to address the very issue that you fairly put to me about the challenges in the National Health Service. But the deeper problem we have, and this is a problem I think that we share with the rest of the United Kingdom, is a long-term effect of 14 years of austerity. and I'm not hearing coming out of the UK election debate between the Labour Party and the Conservative Party the type of financial solution to the challenges in the National Health Service that we need to hear because there's just not enough money proposed to be invested in improving the public services of the United Kingdom and that would have an effect on Scotland as well. Well, my answer to that is that we'll put in the resources to try to address the most immediate issues about long waits, for example. So we've put in £30 million to address some of the long waits issues, and that's currently being deployed. Well, I suspect what it probably has to do with is just about the nature of some of the complex health conditions that people are experiencing and how they are addressed. But there will be other indicators of performance. For example, Scotland has the best performing accident and emergency system in the United Kingdom and has had for the last 10 years. So there will be other aspects of looking at the health service to see how we're doing things differently that are performing better than other parts of the UK. And there will be lessons that we've got to learn from other parts of the United Kingdom. But my core point is that I just don't think the election debate in the UK is a realistic debate about the investment that is required in our public services. We all, I think, there is a crisis created by 14 years of austerity and we have to address that. I think it's about having a democratic debate and coming to those conclusions. And having that debate in a respectful fashion. I bring to my politics a respectful contribution about other parties. I think, I look at politics just now, I've been involved in politics a long time. I've never seen politics in as bad condition as it is today. It is very fractured, not just in Scotland or the United Kingdom, in a much wider sense. So I think it's incumbent on political leaders, and particularly on me as the First Minister of Scotland, to do all that I can to try to bring people together by respectful, courteous debate. And that's how I go about my business. Well, I think it's a very good point, because in 2016, Scotland voted emphatically to stay in the European Union. And one of the things we were told in the 2014 independence referendum in Scotland was that our European Union membership was guaranteed by voting no to independence. And two years later, the absolute opposite of what was promised actually came to pass. So, for me, that's a very, very material change in circumstances that merits another referendum on the question of independence, because one of the fundamental building blocks of the arguments against independence was about the continuity of EU membership. And we've lost that. And people in Scotland are really suffering because of the absence of freedom of movement, the absence of access to the single market, the absence of access to the customs union. All of these things are undermining our economy and our public services in Scotland. And the sooner we can get back into the European Union, in my view, the better. Yes, and that's always been our position, that any new licences have got to pass a climate compatibility assessment before they could be approved, and that's got to be a rigorous process. I think the Committee on Climate Change would accept, or they do accept, that there will be a necessity to use fossil fuels, oil and gas for some time to come. But the question is, we've got a multiplicity of issues to wrestle with here about energy security and about the journey to net zero. The key point I would make is that the journey to net zero is absolutely inescapable. We've got to do it for the sake of the planet. But the judgments we've got to make have got to be made on a case-by-case basis where we explore and examine whether any new activity could be compatible with our objectives on climate change. Well, if, for example, it passed an assessment in relation to energy security and also in relation to the sourcing of alternative products, that might well be a justifiable case. But that would have to be assessed rigorously in a climate compatibility assessment. And when you say energy security, it would be sold on the open market. It wouldn't make us any more secure, would it? We're also taking into account all of the issues about the utilisation of that oil supply, if we use oil as an example. So there would have to be a comprehensive assessment to do that. Now, the Prime Minister has said that he would authorise 100 new oil and gas licences. I think that's completely and utterly irresponsible. It is essentially to deny the climate emergency and I would have nothing to do with that. But what I do want to make sure is that we can work with the oil and gas sector to support it in making the transition to net zero because we will actually need lots of the skills in the oil and gas sector to be available to help us with the transition to net zero and that's a key part of how that agenda would be taken forward. Essentially, we are putting a process in place to enable us to come to that judgment. Throughout all of our analysis on this, we have said climate compatibility assessments are critical in that journey. But I do want to stress the point of the importance of maintaining the skills base which will help us to transition to net zero, because we will need those skills to help us in that journey. Well, we actually engage in a lot of dialogue with many Southern African countries, for example, about the loss and damage activity, where we became one of the first funders of a loss and damage program. I had a meeting yesterday with the president of Zambia, who was in Edinburgh. And we were discussing the developments of the Scottish-Zambia joint exchange program, which is heavily focused on improving energy sustainability in Zambia. It is part of the lost damage work that we are undertaking and also part of the international partnership work that we take forward. I accept we have a domestic responsibility for tackling climate change and net zero, but I also accept that we have a responsibility to reach to other countries and to work with them and to support them, given the fact that as a developed Western European country, we've created our fair share of the damage to the planet and we've got to address that in the loss and damage programmes with others. Christina Rivas Graver, you've got a question. Come on, John, just answer that one. You're asking me a very controversial question. And you're going to get an answer. Look, I think the Conservative government's been a total disaster and a calamity. So it can't be out of office quick enough, in my view. I think it's an absolute racing certainty that the Labour Party will win the election in England, and I hope people in Scotland will recognise the importance of having strong SNP voices in the House of Commons who will argue for decisions to be made in Scotland for Scotland, and that will put the interests of people in Scotland first. over my political life, a number of powers have been devolved to the Scottish Parliament. When I was born, the Scottish Parliament didn't exist. I was one of the founding members of it 25 years ago. So I've welcomed the development of that Scottish Parliament. I was also part of a commission that secured more powers for the Scottish Parliament in 2016. So we've strengthened the powers of the Parliament. So I'm never going to say no to more powers for the Scottish Parliament. But what I want to be able to do is to do the things that will transform people's lives. I want to live in a fairer, more equal country. I want to live in a country that prioritises the improvement of our economy and strengthening the rights and the responsibilities of people in our country. And I don't think that can be achieved for us in the United Kingdom, which is why I want Scotland to be like Denmark or Ireland or Sweden, as an independent country, And when you look at those countries, they are more prosperous, they're more equal, they're fairer than Scotland and the United Kingdom. And that's a prize that I think people in Scotland are attracted to and want to make sure happens for our country.
deb_2024_06_20.STARMER: In 2019 I campaigned for the Labour Party, as I've always campaigned for the Labour Party. I wanted good colleagues to be returned to Parliament. I knew we had a job and a half to do as a Labour Party because I didn't think we were going to win that election. Afterwards, because we got the worst results since 1935, the electorate clearly gave their verdict to us. And we did a lot of work on how we needed to change the party. And that started with what went wrong. And in all the analysis we did, people said they quite liked some of what was in our manifesto. But they thought it was too much. And they wanted to see something which was fully costed and fully funded. And that's why my manifesto going forward to this election is fully costed, fully funded. Everything that we say we're going to do, we say how much it's going to be and where the money is coming from. Because that's the sort of stability that we need. The other thing that's obviously happened since then is Liz Truss put forward a number of unfunded commitments, in her case, tax cuts, and look what it did to the economy. And there will be people in this audience, people who are watching, who are still paying the price for that. And I said it about Rishi Sunak's manifesto, because he's now included unfunded commitments in that. So he's repeating the mistake. That is a political point going into an election. I grant you that. But it is also really important, because if you lose control of the economy, It's ordinary people, working people who pay the price. So many people, for example, still pay more on their mortgages. But that's why I said it, because I think it was overloaded, it wasn't fully funded, and I think that, particularly now, in 2024, the base camp, the foundation, is economic stability. Did you mean it? It wasn't a question that really arose because I didn't think we were going to win the election. And now... And I'm glad I did. I was campaigning for the Labour Party. I was a Labour politician. I wanted to be elected or re-elected, and I wanted good colleagues to be re-elected. Just answer this, then. Some of the people around the shadow cabinet table were people whose seats were saved because we campaigned hard for the Labour Party. Well, look what we got, Boris Johnson. A man who made massive promises, didn't keep them, and then had to leave Parliament in disgrace. OK, you're still not answering the question. The more I look at the preparation we've now done, the more convinced I am about that. Well, in relation to taxation, I do need to be really clear. We are not going to increase income tax, national insurance or VAT. And the reason for that is because I think with the tax burden being as high as it is and a cost of living crisis, that is the wrong thing to do, particularly for working people. So we're not going to do that. We will put some immediate money into our public services. I ran a public service for five years. I believe in our public services and I know they need to be properly funded so we will put in money to the NHS straight away and the purpose of that will be to allow us to do 40,000 extra appointments and operations every week That's 2 million a year extra appointments to bring the waiting list down, because it's nearly 8 million now and we've got to get it down. And what about defence? And that will allow the NHS to be properly, you know, to get back on its feet. We'll have to then make it fit for the future. And what about defence? That's also in the question. Where are you going to find the money for that if you don't increase streams of taxation? Defence has to be the first priority of any government defending your country, and therefore we will always make the money available for defence. And you can see at the moment we live in a particularly volatile world. So we will make the money available for defence, we will put that extra money into the NHS, and I want to see our public services running properly. And my wife works in the NHS, my mum worked in the NHS, my sister worked in the NHS, and to see it in the state that it's in now, where I say it's on its knees, my wife says no it's not, it's flat on its face. is a complete disgrace. After 14 years of this government, we will have to pick it up, put it back on its feet, but also do something else, reform, to make sure that it's fit for the future. That will require a different model, a preventative model. I'll just give this one last example. Very brief, if you want, because there are a lot of hands up. I went to Alder Hey Children's Hospital. Some people will know it. It's a brilliant children's hospital. in Liverpool, where I saw heart surgery, or the wards where they do it, on children aged 0-2. It was very humbling and uplifting, but I also discovered that the single most common reason that 6-10 year olds are admitted to the children's hospital there, same across the country, is to have their teeth taken out because of teeth decay. That is a complete and utter disgrace. So we need to move the model and change the model for the NHS as well. Thank you. With Europe, I'm worried about some of the tendencies across Europe because I'm obviously leading a progressive party and I think that the answers to the problems we face globally, whether that's climate, whether that's conflict, whether that's extreme poverty, getting our economy going, are progressive answers to that. Now, I will have to work, if I'm elected into government, with all leaders across Europe, but I will always make that progressive argument that, in the end, you have to stabilise your economy, you have to get investment in to make sure that we get the jobs of the future, we have to take our climate obligations seriously and not see them as just a sort of challenge that we don't want, but as a brilliant opportunity for the next generation of jobs and play our full part in descaling conflict. If we're able to get over the line in two weeks' time, we have a platform for that, which is really important. If you look across Europe, I am genuinely worried by some of the political trends and moves that we see. Thank you. Yes, yeah. The first thing I'd say is, we treat you with respect. And I say that because we have a Prime Minister who, 18 months or so ago, said, I'm going to get the waiting list down. And he said, hold me personally responsible. And now he hasn't got them down, because they've gone up and knocked down. He blames you. He blames the NHS staff. We will never do that, because I know how hard you work all of the time. in Covid in particular than the waiting list. But we do have to take the pressure off the waiting list.
